topics.title: Introduction to Computer Numerical Control (CNC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computer Numerical Control (CNC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Computer Numerical Control (CNC)

**Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is a manufacturing methodology in which pre-programmed software and coded numerical data (coordinates such as X, Y, Z) dictate the movement of machine tools, fixtures, and accessories, enabling the automated production of complex parts with high precision, repeatability, and minimal human intervention.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PERUT412), CNC is formally defined as:
> The control of machine tool axes and auxiliary functions through a dedicated microcomputer (MCU) that executes a part program stored in memory, replacing the manual operator with an electronic numerical controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evolution of Control: NC → CNC**
> - **NC (Numerical Control):** Introduced in the 1950s (MIT), used hard-wired logic and punched tape. Bulky, hard to modify, single-task.
> - **CNC (Computer Numerical Control):** Emerged in the 1970s. Uses a dedicated **Microcomputer/Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** with stored software. Supports editing, subroutines, loops, compensation, and multi-axis interpolation.
> - **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** A network of multiple CNC machines connected to a central mainframe computer.
> - **HMC / VMC:** Horizontal/Vertical Machining Centers — multi-axis CNC machines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Automated Bakery Chef

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Master Chef who is a "recipe-robot":**
> - The **recipe card (part program)** contains the exact ingredient amounts and timings written in code.
> - The **robot chef (CNC machine)** reads the recipe and automatically dispenses flour, sugar, and water in **exact milligram quantities**.
> - The **sensors (feedback devices)** keep checking the temperature and weight at every instant, making sure nothing goes wrong.
> - The **chef's brain (MCU)** interprets the recipe and tells the robotic arms **what to do, when to do it, and how fast**.
>
> Just as the chef-robot can bake 1,000 identical cakes with sub-millimeter topping placement, a CNC machine can manufacture 1,000 identical gears, engine blocks, or surgical implants with micron-level precision — **24 hours a day, without coffee breaks!**

## 1.3 Key Terminology in CNC

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Part Program** | A sequence of coded instructions (G-code/M-code) that directs the CNC machine. |
| **MCU** | Microcomputer/Machine Control Unit — the "brain" of the CNC. |
| **CLP** | Continuous Loop Path / Closed-Loop Position control. |
| **APC** | Automatic Pallet Changer. |
| **TPI** | Tool Position Indicator. |
| **DRO** | Digital Read-Out — displays real-time axis position. |
| **ATC** | Automatic Tool Changer. |
| **G-Code** | Preparatory functions (geometry/motion codes, e.g., G01, G02). |
| **M-Code** | Miscellaneous functions (auxiliary, e.g., M03 spindle ON). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Coordinate Axes (Krishnamurthy Convention):**
> The **3 principal linear axes** in any CNC machine are:
> - **X-axis** — primary horizontal axis (longest traverse).
> - **Y-axis** — secondary horizontal axis, orthogonal to X.
> - **Z-axis** — axis parallel to the machine spindle (rotational axis of the tool).
> - **A, B, C** — rotational axes about X, Y, Z respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a CNC System

A CNC machine, at its core, is a **closed-loop feedback control system**. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates knowledge of the following sub-systems:

### 2.1.1 The Five Functional Sub-Blocks

1. **Part Program / Data Input Device**
   - The instructions that define the toolpath, feed, speed, and auxiliary functions.
   - **Input mediums (historically to present):** Punched paper tape → Magnetic tape → Floppy disk → USB → Ethernet / Wireless (Industry 4.0).

2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)**
   - The **digital brain** of the CNC. Contains:
     - **CPU + Memory (RAM/ROM/EPROM)** for program execution.
     - **Interpolator** — converts small line segments (linear/circular) into smooth axis motion.
     - **Controller logic** — for sequencing, decoding, and tool compensation.
   - Real-world utility: Found in Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain, Mitsubishi controllers.

3. **Drive System (Servo / Stepper Motors)**
   - Converts electrical pulses from the MCU into **mechanical rotation/translation** of the leadscrews or linear motors.
   - **Types:** DC servo, AC servo, stepper, hydraulic servo, linear motor.

4. **Feedback System (Transducers)**
   - **Position feedback:** Linear/rotary encoders, resolvers, Inductosyn, glass/steel linear scales.
   - **Speed feedback:** Tachogenerators.
   - Real-world utility: Achieves sub-micron accuracy in semiconductor lithography.

5. **Machine Tool (Mechanical Section)**
   - The structural body (bed, slideways, spindle, ATC) that physically removes material.

## 2.2 Working Principle — "How CNC Thinks"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CNC operates on a **decoded instruction cycle**, executed continuously in the following sequence:
>
> 1. **Read** the next block of the part program.
> 2. **Decode** the block (identify G/M codes, coordinates F, S, T).
> 3. **Interpolate** the desired toolpath between current and target positions (linear or circular).
> 4. **Generate** axis command pulses (one pulse = a small increment, e.g., **1 µm** or **0.001 inch** — this is the *least input increment*).
> 5. **Drive** the servo motors to move the slides.
> 6. **Measure** the actual position via feedback transducers.
> 7. **Compare** actual vs. commanded position (error signal).
> 8. **Correct** the error continuously until the actual position matches the commanded position.
> 9. **Repeat** for the next block.

This continuous process is called **Closed-Loop Control**, and it is the *fundamental reason* CNC machines are accurate to ±2 µm or better.

## 2.3 Classification of CNC Machines

| Basis | Type | Example |
|-------|------|---------|
| **By Motion Type** | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Drilling, punching |
| | Straight-Cut (1D) | Simple turning |
| | Contouring (2D/3D) | Milling complex dies |
| **By Axis Count** | 2-axis, 3-axis, 4-axis, 5-axis | Turret lathe → 5-axis VMC |
| **By Feedback** | Open-loop (stepper) | Low-cost engravers |
| | Closed-loop (servo + encoder) | Industrial VMCs/HMCs |
| **By Application** | Milling, Turning, Grinding, EDM, Laser, Waterjet | Machining Centers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Least Input Increment (LII)** | $\text{LII} = \dfrac{\text{Lead of screw}}{\text{Steps per revolution of motor}}$ | mm or µm |
| **Pulse Rate (f)** | $f = \dfrac{V_{\text{axis}}}{LII} \times 60$ | pulses/min |
| **Feedrate (F)** | $F = N \times f_z \times z$ | mm/min |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ | RPM |
| **MRR (Metal Removal Rate)** | $MMR = a_p \times a_e \times V_f$ | mm³/min |
| **Programming Resolution (PR)** | $PR = \dfrac{\text{Total travel}}{\text{No. of addressable positions}}$ | mm/pulse |
| **Axis Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ | mm/min |
| **Lead (L)** | $L = n \times p$ (n starts × pitch) | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in any table cell that requires absolute-value bars.** For example, write $\vert x \vert$ as `$\lvert x \rvert$` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages of CNC:**
> - High **repeatability** (±2 µm typical) and **accuracy**.
> - Complex 3D contours and undercuts possible.
> - Reduced lead time after first setup.
> - Lower long-term labor cost.
> - Integration with CAD/CAM and Industry 4.0 networks.
>
> **Limitations of CNC:**
> - High initial capital investment.
> - Requires skilled part-programmers and tool setters.
> - High cost of tooling and fixtures.
> - Not economical for very small batch sizes (1–5 pieces).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC is the backbone of:
- **Aerospace:** Machining titanium blisks, structural ribs.
- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, transmission housings (Maruti, Tata, VW lines).
- **Medical:** Patient-specific titanium implants, surgical guides.
- **Die & Mold:** Injection mold inserts with EDM-finished cavities.
- **Defence:** Missile casings, gun barrels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section builds every important CNC relationship **from first principles**, with each algebraic step explicitly shown. No skipped steps, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Least Input Increment (LII) of a CNC Axis

**Definition:** LII is the smallest linear displacement of an axis corresponding to **one electrical pulse** sent from the controller to the servo/stepper motor.

**Given data (typical Ball-Screw Drive):**
- Pitch of leadscrew, $p = 10 \text{ mm/rev}$ (single start)
- Step angle of stepper motor, $\theta_s = 1.8°$
- Microsteps per full step, $m = 10$
- Gear reduction ratio, $G = 1:1$ (direct drive)

**Step 1: Total angular steps per revolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Full steps per revolution} &= \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s} \\
&= \dfrac{360}{1.8} \\
&= 200 \text{ steps/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Include microstepping for finer resolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total microsteps/rev} &= 200 \times m \\
&= 200 \times 10 \\
&= 2000 \text{ pulses/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Linear travel of leadscrew per revolution**

Since the leadscrew has a pitch of $p = 10 \text{ mm/rev}$, one full revolution of the motor causes the nut (and thus the table) to translate by $10 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 4: Compute LII**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LII &= \dfrac{\text{Linear travel per revolution}}{\text{Pulses per revolution}} \\
&= \dfrac{p}{\text{Total pulses/rev}} \\
&= \dfrac{10 \text{ mm}}{2000} \\
&= 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse} \\
&= 5 \, \mu\text{m/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Every pulse from the MCU moves the slide by **5 µm**. To move 1 mm, the controller must send **200 pulses**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse Rate Required for a Given Axis Velocity

**Goal:** If the table must move at $V = 1200 \text{ mm/min}$ with the LII derived above, what pulse rate (in pulses/sec) must the MCU output?

**Step 1: Write the velocity per pulse**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{per pulse}} &= LII \\
&= 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute pulses required per minute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulses/min} &= \dfrac{V}{LII} \\
&= \dfrac{1200 \text{ mm/min}}{0.005 \text{ mm/pulse}} \\
&= 240{,}000 \text{ pulses/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to pulses per second (Hz)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{240{,}000}{60} \\
&= 4000 \text{ pulses/sec} \\
&= 4 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering note:** The MCU must toggle the step/direction lines at **4 kHz** to achieve 1200 mm/min. This is well within modern stepper-driver capability (often 200 kHz+).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spindle Speed for a Given Cutting Velocity

**Given:** A 50 mm diameter mild-steel workpiece is to be turned with a cutting speed $V_c = 80 \text{ m/min}$. Compute spindle speed $N$.

**Step 1: Recall the relation between $V_c$, $D$, and $N$**

$$
V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $N$**

$$
N = \dfrac{V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values (ensure unit consistency: convert $D$ from mm to m)**

$$
D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{80 \text{ m/min}}{\pi \times 0.05 \text{ m}} \\
&= \dfrac{80}{0.15708} \\
&= 509.3 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Round to the nearest standard value**

$$
N \approx 510 \text{ RPM (or 500 RPM depending on machine's spindle range)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Feedrate Computation for a Milling Cutter

**Given:**
- Spindle speed $N = 1000 \text{ RPM}$
- Number of teeth on cutter $z = 4$
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05 \text{ mm/tooth}$

**Step 1: Recall the standard feedrate formula**

$$
F = N \times f_z \times z
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 1000 \times 0.05 \times 4 \\
&= 200 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering utility:** This is the **table feedrate** programmed using the **F-address** in a milling part-program, e.g., `F200.`

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Metal Removal Rate (MRR) for a Turning Operation

**Given:** A turning operation has depth of cut $a_p = 2 \text{ mm}$, feed $f = 0.2 \text{ mm/rev}$, and cutting velocity $V_c = 80 \text{ m/min}$.

**Step 1: Recall the MRR formula for turning**

$$
MRR = a_p \times f \times V_c
$$

(Here, $V_c$ is in mm/min units when needed; we'll keep m/min and convert.)

**Step 2: Convert $V_c$ to mm/min**

$$
V_c = 80 \text{ m/min} = 80{,}000 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 2 \times 0.2 \times 80{,}000 \\
&= 32{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
&= 32 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open Loop vs. Closed Loop CNC

| Parameter | Open-Loop CNC (Stepper) | Closed-Loop CNC (Servo) |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Feedback** | None | Encoders/Resolvers |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 5$ to $\pm 20 \, \mu\text{m}$ | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5 \, \mu\text{m}$ |
| **Lost-step problem** | Yes (no correction) | No (auto-corrected) |
| **Speed** | Lower (resonance limits) | High (good high-speed torque) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Application** | Hobby, low-load engraving | Industrial machining centers |
| **Heat generation** | Higher (constant current) | Lower (only when correcting) |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU 2-mark comparison** and frequently appears as a "distinguish between" question in Part A.

---

## 3.7 KTU Pitfall: Axes vs. Directions

> [!WARNING]
> **Common confusion clarified:**
> - On a **vertical machining center (VMC)**: +Z is **upward** (tool moves up, away from table).
> - On a **turning center (lathe)**: +Z is **away from the chuck** (toward the tailstock).
> - On a **horizontal machining center (HMC)**: Z is along the spindle axis, but the spindle is horizontal.
>
> Examiners often test this with: *"For a VMC, the +Z direction is ___"* — answer is **upward / away from the workpiece**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a CNC Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a CNC system, showing signal flow from part-program input to finished workpiece output, with the closed-loop feedback path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Program Input - USB or Network] --> B[Machine Control Unit - MCU]
    B --> C[Interpolator and Decoder]
    C --> D[Axis Command Pulses - X, Y, Z]
    D --> E[Servo Amplifier and Drive]
    E --> F[Servo Motor - X Axis]
    E --> G[Servo Motor - Y Axis]
    E --> H[Servo Motor - Z Axis]
    F --> I[Ball Screw - X Slide]
    G --> J[Ball Screw - Y Slide]
    H --> K[Ball Screw - Z Slide]
    I --> L[Machine Tool Table and Spindle]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Workpiece - Material Removal]
    I --> N[Linear Encoder - X Feedback]
    J --> O[Linear Encoder - Y Feedback]
    K --> P[Linear Encoder - Z Feedback]
    N --> Q[Comparator - Error Signal]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> C
    L --> R[Coolant, ATC, APC Auxiliary Systems]
    B --> R
```

---

## 4.2 CNC Operating Cycle (Sequential Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start - Power ON] --> S2[Initialize MCU and Home All Axes]
    S2 --> S3[Load Part Program from Memory]
    S3 --> S4[Read First Block of Program]
    S4 --> S5{Decode Block - Identify G, M, F, S, T}
    S5 --> S6[Interpolate Path - Linear or Circular]
    S6 --> S7[Generate Axis Pulses to Servo Drives]
    S7 --> S8[Move Slides and Engage Tool]
    S8 --> S9[Read Feedback from Encoders]
    S9 --> S10{Error is Zero or Within Tolerance}
    S10 --> S11[Execute Auxiliary Functions - Coolant, Spindle]
    S10 --> S12[Generate Correction Signal to Drives]
    S12 --> S7
    S11 --> S13{End of Program}
    S13 --> S14[Tool Retract, Spindle Stop, Reset]
    S13 --> S4
    S14 --> S15[Cycle Complete - Part Ready for Inspection]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison: Conventional vs. CNC Machining Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV[CONVENTIONAL MACHINING]
        A1[Manual Sketch Drawing] --> A2[Manual Measurement and Marking]
        A2 --> A3[Operator Skill Required]
        A3 --> A4[Manual Handwheel Operation]
        A4 --> A5[Single Piece Production]
    end
    subgraph CNCM[CNC MACHINING]
        B1[CAD Model Design] --> B2[CAM Software Toolpath]
        B2 --> B3[Part Program G-code M-code]
        B3 --> B4[Automatic Axis Motion]
        B4 --> B5[Mass Production Identical Parts]
    end
    A5 --- A6[Lower Accuracy and Repeatability]
    B5 --- B6[High Accuracy and Repeatability]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Insight:**
> - The **interpolator** block is unique to CNC — it converts "discrete block" instructions into **continuous analog-like motion** by issuing thousands of pulses per second.
> - The **feedback loop** is the *defining feature* that separates CNC from simple stepper-based NC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define CNC. List any four advantages of CNC machines over conventional machines.**

**Model Answer:**

CNC stands for **Computer Numerical Control**. It is a system in which a dedicated microcomputer (Machine Control Unit, MCU) controls the motion and auxiliary functions of a machine tool by executing a stored part program composed of numerical codes (G-codes and M-codes).

**Four advantages over conventional machines:**
1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** — typically ±2 µm.
2. **Complex 3D contours** can be machined effortlessly.
3. **Reduced lead time** for batch production after the first setup.
4. **Lower long-term labor cost** due to automation; operates without fatigue.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Easy integration with CAD/CAM and Industry 4.0 networks.

*(End — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop CNC systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Open-Loop CNC | Closed-Loop CNC |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Uses stepper motors; no feedback device. | Uses servo motors with feedback (encoders/resolvers). |
| 2 | Accuracy is low (±5 to ±20 µm). | Accuracy is high (±1 to ±5 µm). |
| 3 | Suffers from "lost-step" problem. | Auto-corrects via comparator. |
| 4 | Lower cost, used in low-load applications. | Higher cost, used in industrial machining. |
| 5 | No error detection. | Continuous error detection and correction. |

*(3 marks: tabulated differentiation, key features highlighted)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. An **Internal Choice** is given between **Question A** and **Question B** of the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **block diagram of a CNC system** with all major components. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a closed-loop CNC system**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram of a CNC System — 7 marks**

A CNC machine consists of **five major functional blocks**:

1. **Part Program / Data Input Device:** Stores and feeds the coded instructions (G/M codes) to the MCU. Modern machines use USB, Ethernet, or DNC networks.

2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU):** The brain of the CNC. Contains:
   - CPU + RAM/ROM/EPROM
   - Interpolator (linear/circular/helical)
   - Decoding & sequencing logic
   - Tool length/diameter compensation routines

3. **Drive System (Servo/Stepper):** Converts electrical pulses into mechanical motion via ball screws or linear motors. Includes servo amplifier.

4. **Feedback System (Transducers):** Linear/rotary encoders, resolvers, Inductosyn, glass scales. Provides actual position to the comparator.

5. **Machine Tool:** The mechanical structure (bed, column, spindle, ATC, APC) that physically performs cutting.

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 for visual reference.)*
*(Award: 5 marks for listing + explaining components, 2 marks for diagram)*

---

**(b) Working Principle of a Closed-Loop CNC System — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** The part program is loaded into MCU memory.
**Step 2:** MCU reads one block at a time and decodes G-codes (motion), M-codes (auxiliary), F (feed), S (speed), T (tool).
**Step 3:** The interpolator computes incremental axis positions required to follow the programmed toolpath.
**Step 4:** Command pulses are sent to the servo amplifier → servo motor → ball screw → table.
**Step 5:** The **encoder** mounted on the ball screw continuously measures the actual table position.
**Step 6:** The **comparator** subtracts actual position from commanded position → generates an **error signal**.
**Step 7:** The error signal drives the motor until error = 0 (servo settles at commanded position).
**Step 8:** This loop runs thousands of times per second, ensuring micron-level accuracy.
**Step 9:** The cycle repeats for the next block until the part program ends.

**Key feature:** The *continuous correction* via feedback is what gives CNC its high accuracy.

*(Award: 5 marks for steps, 1 mark for closed-loop equation, 1 mark for accuracy statement)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** What are the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of CNC machines? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC milling machine has a leadscrew with pitch **5 mm/rev** and is driven by a stepper motor with step angle **1.8°** and **5 microsteps per full step**. Compute the **(i) Least Input Increment (LII)** and **(ii) the pulse rate required to move the X-axis at 900 mm/min**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Advantages, Limitations & Applications — 7 marks**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. High accuracy and repeatability.
2. Complex geometry achievable.
3. Reduced lead time, high productivity.
4. Lower operator fatigue and human error.
5. Easy storage and modification of part programs.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. High initial cost of machine and tooling.
2. Skilled programmers and tool-setters required.
3. Not economical for very small batches.
4. Maintenance is specialized and costly.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. Aerospace — turbine blades, structural ribs.
2. Automotive — engine blocks, gears.
3. Medical — implants, prosthetics.
4. Die & Mold — EDM-finished cavity inserts.
5. Defence — missile components.

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Pitch of leadscrew, $p = 5 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Step angle, $\theta_s = 1.8°$
- Microsteps per full step, $m = 5$
- Required axis velocity, $V = 900 \text{ mm/min}$

**Step 1: Calculate full steps per revolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Steps/rev} &= \dfrac{360°}{1.8°} \\
&= 200 \text{ steps/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Include microstepping**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total pulses/rev} &= 200 \times 5 \\
&= 1000 \text{ pulses/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Least Input Increment (LII)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LII &= \dfrac{p}{\text{Total pulses/rev}} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \text{ mm}}{1000} \\
&= 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse} \\
&= 5 \, \mu\text{m/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks awarded for LII computation.)*

**Step 4: Calculate pulse rate for $V = 900 \text{ mm/min}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulses/min} &= \dfrac{V}{LII} \\
&= \dfrac{900}{0.005} \\
&= 180{,}000 \text{ pulses/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to pulses/second (Hz)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{180{,}000}{60} \\
&= 3000 \text{ Hz} \\
&= 3 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(4 marks awarded for pulse rate computation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of pitch from mm to µm (or vice versa) — causes a 10× error.
> 2. **Confusing steps/rev with pulses/rev** — examiners specifically check whether the student has multiplied by the microstep count.
> 3. **Not mentioning "microstepping"** in the LII formula — KTU 2024 explicitly tests this concept.
> 4. **Skipping the comparator/feedback explanation** in closed-loop questions — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for the feedback path.
> 5. **Wrong axis convention** in VMC vs. lathe — students often write +Z as downward, which is incorrect for a VMC.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to CNC**

- **CNC** = Computer Numerical Control; uses an MCU to execute stored part programs.
- **NC → CNC → DNC** — evolution; CNC uses microcomputer, DNC networks multiple machines.
- **3 Principal Axes:** X (longest horizontal), Y (secondary horizontal), Z (parallel to spindle).
- **Rotational axes:** A (about X), B (about Y), C (about Z).
- **5 functional blocks of CNC:** Part program input, MCU, Drive system, Feedback system, Machine tool.
- **MCU sub-blocks:** CPU, memory, interpolator, decoder, compensation routines.
- **Drive types:** DC servo, AC servo, stepper, hydraulic, linear motor.
- **Feedback devices:** Linear encoder, rotary encoder, resolver, Inductosyn, glass/steel scale.
- **Open-loop:** stepper, no feedback, lost-step problem, low cost.
- **Closed-loop:** servo + encoder, comparator, high accuracy, high cost.
- **LII Formula:** $LII = \dfrac{\text{Lead of screw}}{\text{Steps/rev} \times \text{microsteps}}$.
- **Pulse rate:** $f = \dfrac{V}{LII \times 60}$ Hz (after converting V to mm/sec).
- **Spindle speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ RPM.
- **Feedrate (milling):** $F = N \times f_z \times z$ mm/min.
- **MRR (turning):** $MRR = a_p \times f \times V_c$ mm³/min.
- **PTP vs. Contouring:** Drilling = PTP; complex die-machining = contouring.
- **Industry 4.0 relevance:** Modern CNCs are Ethernet-connected and support MQL, IoT-based monitoring.
- **Axis convention for VMC:** +Z = upward; for Lathe: +Z = away from chuck.
- **Memorize these standard block-diagram names** — examiners reward labelled diagrams with 2 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Control - Functions of a Machine Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Control - Functions of a Machine Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Numerical Control (NC)?

**Numerical Control (NC)** is a form of programmable automation in which the processing equipment (the machine tool) is controlled by a series of coded alphanumeric instructions (numbers, letters, symbols) prepared in a specific format. These instructions are called a **part program** and they direct the machine tool to perform the required manufacturing operations on a workpiece automatically, with minimal human intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Numerical Control is a method of controlling the functions of a machine tool by means of numbers, where the numbers constitute a coded instruction set that dictates all aspects of the manufacturing process, including position, feed rate, spindle speed, tool selection, and auxiliary functions."*

The term was first coined by **John T. Parsons** in the late 1940s and early 1950s while working on helicopter rotor blade manufacturing. The first true NC machine was developed at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** in 1952 in collaboration with Parsons.

When the NC controller is implemented using a **microprocessor / computer** (instead of hard-wired electronic circuits), the system is called **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)**. Today's machines are almost universally CNC.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robotic Chef

Imagine teaching a chef to cook a dish. There are two ways:

1. **Manual Cooking (Conventional Machine):** You stand next to the chef, telling them what to do at every instant — *"turn the stove to medium... wait 30 seconds... add salt... stir clockwise..."* This is exhausting and error-prone.

2. **Recipe Card Cooking (NC/CNC Machine):** You write a **complete recipe** (program) on a card with **numbers and coded instructions** — `TEMP=180`, `TIME=20`, `RPM=300`. You hand it to the chef, and they execute it **automatically, accurately, and repeatedly**, every single time, without further verbal instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "recipe" = **Part Program (G-code & M-code)**
> The "chef" = **Machine Tool (Lathe, Mill, etc.)**
> The "kitchen timer & thermometer feedback" = **Servo feedback / Sensors**
> The "cookbook reader" = **MCU (Machine Control Unit)**

---

## 1.3 Functions of a Machine Tool in NC Context

Every machine tool, conventional or NC, must perform certain **basic functions** to manufacture a part. In NC, these functions are automated and precisely controlled. The primary functions are:

| Sl. No. | Function | Description |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Work-holding & Positioning** | Holding the workpiece firmly in a fixture or chuck so that it is located correctly relative to the tool. |
| 2 | **Tool Holding & Changing** | Holding the cutting tool rigidly, and (in modern CNC) automatically changing tools from a magazine. |
| 3 | **Cutting / Material Removal** | The actual operation that removes unwanted material (turning, milling, drilling, etc.). |
| 4 | **Feed & Speed Control** | Controlling the **relative motion** between tool and workpiece (feed rate) and the **rotational speed** (spindle speed). |
| 5 | **Coolant & Lubrication** | Supplying cutting fluid to reduce heat, improve tool life, and flush away chips. |
| 6 | **Chip Removal & Safety** | Evacuating chips and ensuring safe operation of the machine. |
| 7 | **Measurement & Inspection** | Verifying dimensions, sometimes in-process using probes. |

> [!TIP]
> In a **conventional machine**, these functions are performed manually by the operator using **hand wheels, levers, and dials**. In an **NC/CNC machine**, the same functions are performed by **servo motors, hydraulic/pneumatic actuators, and electronic sensors**, all orchestrated by the **Machine Control Unit (MCU)**.

---

## 1.4 Why NC / CNC is Important (Engineering Significance)

- **Repeatability:** The same part can be produced thousands of times with sub-micron accuracy.
- **Complex Geometry:** Curved surfaces (e.g., turbine blades, dies, moulds) that are practically impossible to produce manually can be machined easily.
- **Productivity:** Higher cutting speeds, less idle time, and reduced scrap.
- **Flexibility:** Part programs can be modified quickly for design changes.
- **Lead Time Reduction:** New parts can be produced rapidly without retooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **machine tool is the heart of manufacturing**. NC simply makes the machine tool "intelligent" by giving it the ability to read, interpret, and execute numerical instructions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Functional Roles of an NC Machine Tool

Every NC machine tool, irrespective of type (lathe, mill, grinder, EDMs, etc.), must execute **three primary functional roles** during a manufacturing cycle:

### A. **Positioning Function**
Moving the tool (or workpiece) to the correct location in space before, during, or after a cut.

- **Point-to-Point (PTP) Positioning:** Tool moves rapidly to a target location, but path taken is irrelevant (used in drilling, tapping, boring).
- **Straight-Cut Positioning:** Tool moves along one axis, performs a cut, retracts, moves to next point (used in simple milling).
- **Contouring (Continuous Path):** Tool follows a precisely defined continuous path (used in milling complex dies, turning profiles).

### B. **Machining Function (Metal Removal)**
The actual cutting action at the commanded location, where material is removed. Parameters controlled:
- **Cutting Speed ($V_c$)** — surface speed at the cutting edge
- **Feed Rate ($f$)** — rate of tool advance
- **Depth of Cut ($d$)** — penetration into the workpiece
- **Spindle Speed ($N$)** — rotational speed of the workpiece (lathe) or tool (mill)

### C. **Auxiliary / Supporting Functions**
Tool changes, coolant on/off, chip evacuation, pallet changes, safety interlocks, and in-process measurement.

---

## 2.2 Components of an NC System (Functional Architecture)

An NC system is composed of **six major sub-systems**:

1. **Part Program** — the coded instructions (the "language" of the machine).
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — the "brain" that decodes and executes the program.
3. **Servo Drive System** — converts MCU commands into physical motion of slides.
4. **Feedback System** — sensors (encoders, resolvers, LVDTs) that report actual position back to the MCU (**closed-loop** NC).
5. **Machine Tool** — the mechanical structure (slides, spindle, bed, ATC).
6. **Operator Interface (HMI)** — keyboard, screen, MDI panel for human interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MCU + Servo Drive + Feedback** together form the **closed-loop control system**. This is what makes CNC far more accurate than conventional or open-loop NC.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (NC Fundamentals)

The following equations are foundational to numerical control machine tools and will be used throughout this course.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Spindle Speed (Lathe) | $$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$ | rpm | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed (Milling) | $$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$ | rpm | $D$ = cutter diameter in mm |
| 3 | Cutting Velocity | $$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$ | m/min | Tangential surface speed |
| 4 | Feed Rate (Lathe) | $$f_n = N \cdot f_r$$ | mm/min | $f_r$ = feed per rev (mm/rev) |
| 5 | Feed Rate (Milling) | $$f_n = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$$ | mm/min | $z$ = no. of teeth, $f_z$ = feed/tooth |
| 6 | Metal Removal Rate (Turning) | $$MRR = f_r \cdot d \cdot V_c$$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| 7 | Metal Removal Rate (Milling) | $$MRR = f_n \cdot d \cdot w$$ | mm³/min | $w$ = width of cut (mm) |
| 8 | Power Required | $$P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 9 | Programming Resolution | $$\Delta = \dfrac{\text{Travel}}{\text{Pulses per rev} \cdot \text{Gear ratio}}$$ | mm/pulse | BLU (Basic Length Unit) |
| 10 | Contour Error (approx.) | $$e \approx \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}$$ | mm | $v$ = feed velocity, $a$ = acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **BLU (Basic Length Unit)** is the smallest addressable increment by the controller. For most modern CNC machines, BLU = **0.001 mm** (1 micrometre). This is also called **Least Count** or **Resolution** of the machine.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of NC

| Domain | Application |
|:--|:--|
| **Aerospace** | Machining of turbine blades, structural components, wing ribs from aluminium & titanium alloys. |
| **Automotive** | Engine blocks, cylinder heads, gear blanks, crankshafts. |
| **Die & Mould** | Complex 3D cavities, electrodes for EDM. |
| **Medical** | Orthopaedic implants, surgical instruments. |
| **Defence** | Missile casings, gun barrels, special alloys. |
| **General Engineering** | Shafts, flanges, brackets, hydraulic manifolds. |

> [!TIP]
> Any part that requires **repeatability, tight tolerance, or complex geometry** is a candidate for NC machining.

---

## 2.5 Classification Snapshot (Foundation for Future Modules)

| Basis | Types |
|:--|:--|
| Motion Type | Point-to-Point, Straight-Cut, Contouring (Continuous Path) |
| Loop Type | Open-Loop, Closed-Loop |
| Power Source | Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic |
| Application | Lathe, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, EDM, Punching, Bending |
| Control Unit | Hard-Wired NC, Soft-Wired (CNC), DNC (Direct NC) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

In Module 1, the focus is conceptual rather than on heavy algebraic derivations. However, **two foundational derivations** must be mastered because they appear in nearly every KTU question paper.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed Equation (Lathe)

**Given:** A turning operation on a mild-steel workpiece of diameter $D = 80$ mm with recommended cutting speed $V_c = 200$ m/min. Find the spindle speed $N$.

### Step-by-step Logical Build-up

**Step 1 — Definition of cutting speed:**
The cutting speed $V_c$ is the **tangential surface velocity** at the cutting edge of the tool, measured at the **workpiece diameter**.

$$V_c = \frac{\text{circumferential distance per rev}}{\text{time per rev}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the circumference in metres:**
For a workpiece of diameter $D$ mm, the circumference per revolution is:

$$C = \pi \cdot D \text{ mm per rev} = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{1000} \text{ m per rev}$$

**Step 3 — Time per revolution:**
The spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute, so time per revolution is:

$$t = \frac{1}{N} \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the definition of $V_c$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{\text{metres per rev}}{\text{minutes per rev}} \\[4pt]
V_c &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{1000}}{\dfrac{1}{N}} \\[8pt]
V_c &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values ($V_c = 200$ m/min, $D = 80$ mm):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 80} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{200{,}000}{251.327} \\[4pt]
N &\approx 795.77 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Round to nearest available spindle speed on the machine:**
The available spindle speeds of a typical lathe (e.g., 800, 710, 900) are selected, so:

$$\boxed{N_{\text{selected}} = 800 \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The spindle speed is **not** a free continuous variable on a conventional machine. On CNC, the spindle speed is **infinitely variable** within the machine's range and is set by **S-code** in the program (e.g., `S800`).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Metal Removal Rate (Turning)

**Definition:** MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time.

**Step 1 — Consider one revolution:**
In one revolution of the workpiece, the tool advances axially by $f_r$ mm (feed per revolution) and the depth of cut is $d$ mm.

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the chip:**

$$A = f_r \cdot d \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per revolution:**

$$V_{1 rev} = A \cdot (\pi \cdot D) \quad [\text{mm}^3]$$

> (Because the tool traverses the full circumference of diameter $D$ in one rev.)

**Step 4 — Volume per minute (multiply by $N$):**

$$MRR = N \cdot f_r \cdot d \cdot (\pi \cdot D) / 1000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> But since $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 1000$, we can substitute:

$$\boxed{MRR = f_r \cdot d \cdot V_c \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]}$$

**Step 5 — Quick sanity check (numerical example):**
Given $f_r = 0.3$ mm/rev, $d = 2$ mm, $V_c = 200$ m/min:

$$MRR = 0.3 \times 2 \times 200 = 120 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} = 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Conventional vs. NC Machine Tool Functions

| Function | Conventional Machine | NC / CNC Machine |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Speed & Feed selection | Operator turns levers/dials | Programmed via S & F codes |
| Slide movement | Hand wheel | Servo motor driven by MCU |
| Tool change | Manual | Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) |
| Tool path | Operator skill | Programmed G-codes (linear / circular) |
| Feedback | Visual / DRO (if any) | Encoders / resolvers in **closed loop** |
| Repeatability | Low (operator dependent) | High (program dependent) |
| Data storage | Operator memory | Part program on disk / network |
| Production rate | Lower | Higher (multitasking possible) |
| Human error | High | Minimal (after program verification) |
| Initial cost | Low | High |
| Operator skill required | Mechanical skill | Programming + tooling skill |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **functional capability** of a machine tool is identical in both cases — what changes is the **level of automation and the precision of control** of those functions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an NC Machine Tool

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete functional interaction of every sub-system of an NC machine tool, including the **closed-loop feedback** path that distinguishes CNC from conventional machines.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Part Program G-code M-code] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU Decoder Interpolator]
    B --> C[X Axis Servo Drive]
    B --> D[Y Axis Servo Drive]
    B --> E[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    B --> F[Spindle Drive Motor]
    B --> G[Auxiliary Control Coolant Tool Change Chuck]
    C --> H[X Axis Slide Linear Encoder]
    D --> I[Y Axis Slide Linear Encoder]
    E --> J[Z Axis Slide Linear Encoder]
    F --> K[Spindle Encoder]
    H --> L[Position Feedback to MCU]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> B
    H --> M[Machine Tool Slide Bed]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    F --> M
    G --> M
    M --> N[Workpiece Cutting Process]
    B --> O[HMI Operator Panel MDI Screen]
    O --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows (→) are **command / signal flow**.
- The loop from `H, I, J, K → L → B` represents the **closed-loop feedback** essential for contouring accuracy.
- The `MCU` block is the central node — every function of the machine tool is orchestrated by it.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Classification of NC Machine Tool Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Functions of a Machine Tool] --> B[Positioning Function]
    A --> C[Machining Function]
    A --> D[Auxiliary Function]
    B --> B1[Point to Point PTP]
    B --> B2[Straight Cut]
    B --> B3[Contouring Continuous Path]
    C --> C1[Speed Control Spindle]
    C --> C2[Feed Control]
    C --> C3[Depth of Cut]
    D --> D1[Tool Change ATC]
    D --> D2[Coolant Supply]
    D --> D3[Work Holding Chuck Fixture]
    D --> D4[Chip Evacuation]
    D --> D5[Safety Interlocks]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three top-level branches (Positioning, Machining, Auxiliary) are the **canonical functional roles** of any NC machine tool.
- The sub-branches list the specific tasks under each role — these are the items commonly tested in KTU Part-A questions.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop NC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN[Open Loop NC]
        O1[Part Program] --> O2[MCU Controller]
        O2 --> O3[Servo Drive]
        O3 --> O4[Stepper Motor or Hydraulic Actuator]
        O4 --> O5[Machine Slide]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED[Closed Loop CNC]
        C1[Part Program] --> C2[MCU with Interpolator]
        C2 --> C3[Servo Drive]
        C3 --> C4[Servo Motor AC or DC]
        C4 --> C5[Machine Slide with Encoder]
        C5 --> C6[Feedback Signal Position]
        C6 --> C2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-loop NC** (e.g., stepper-motor based machines) is cheaper but cannot correct for errors (backlash, thermal drift, load disturbance). **Closed-loop CNC** continuously compares commanded vs. actual position and corrects the error — essential for contouring and high-accuracy work.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Numerical Control (NC). List any four advantages of NC machines over conventional machines.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Numerical Control (NC) is a method of controlling the functions of a machine tool by means of numbers, where the coded numerical instructions (part program) dictate the sequence and parameters of all manufacturing operations with minimal human intervention.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Improved Accuracy & Repeatability:** Once the program is verified, the same part can be produced thousands of times with consistent dimensional accuracy.
2. **Complex Geometry:** Curved surfaces and 3D contours (impractical manually) can be produced easily.
3. **Reduced Lead Time:** Programming is faster than jig/fixture design for prototype production.
4. **Lower Scrap Rate:** Human error is reduced, leading to fewer rejections.
5. *(Bonus: Higher productivity due to higher cutting parameters and shorter set-up times.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between **Point-to-Point (PTP)** and **Contouring (Continuous Path)** NC systems. Give one example application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Path taken between points | Irrelevant (shortest / fastest) | Precisely defined and controlled |
| Cutting during motion | Usually no cutting during traverse | Cutting occurs during motion |
| Accuracy requirement | Only on final position | On the entire path |
| Control complexity | Simple (open-loop possible) | Complex (closed-loop mandatory) |
| Example | Drilling, tapping, boring | Milling a die cavity, lathe profiling |

**Examples:**
- PTP: Drilling holes on a workpiece plate.
- Contouring: Milling the airfoil of a turbine blade.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **Question A** *and* **Question B** are independent alternatives. KTU allows the student to answer either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **three basic functions of a machine tool** in the context of Numerical Control. Briefly describe the role of the **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** in coordinating these functions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the working of a **closed-loop CNC system**. Compare it briefly with the open-loop system. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**The three basic functions of an NC machine tool are:**

**1. Positioning Function:**
The machine must position the tool (or workpiece) at the correct location in 2D/3D space before machining. This is achieved through **servo-driven slides** along the X, Y, Z axes. In contouring systems, the path itself is also controlled.

**2. Machining Function:**
The actual metal removal takes place here. Parameters commanded:
- Spindle speed (S-code)
- Feed rate (F-code)
- Depth of cut (programmed in the tool-path)
- Coolant ON/OFF (M-code)

**3. Auxiliary / Supporting Function:**
- Automatic tool change (M06)
- Chuck open/close
- Coolant flow
- Pallet shuttle
- Chip evacuation
- Safety door interlock

**Role of the MCU:**
The **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** is the "brain" of the NC system. It performs:
- **Decoding** of the part program (G, M, S, T, F codes).
- **Interpolation** (linear, circular) to generate intermediate axis positions.
- **Distribution** of command signals to each axis servo drive.
- **Monitoring** of feedback signals from encoders.
- **Coordination** of auxiliary functions (coolant, ATC, etc.).
- **HMI handling** for operator input and display.

The MCU thus **orchestrates all three machine tool functions** in synchronised, real-time fashion.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Working of Closed-Loop CNC System (with block diagram — see Section 4.3):**

**Sequence of Operation:**

1. The part program is read by the MCU.
2. The MCU's interpolator generates target position values for each axis at every sampling instant (typically every 1–4 ms).
3. The **commanded position** is compared with the **actual position** measured by feedback sensors (linear encoders on slides, rotary encoder on spindle).
4. The difference (called the **following error** or **contour error**) is fed to a **PID controller** inside the servo drive.
5. The servo drive amplifies a corrective signal and drives the **servo motor** to reduce the error to zero.
6. This loop continues throughout the motion, ensuring accurate contour following.

**Comparison: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Feedback | None | Position sensor (encoder) |
| Actuator | Stepper motor / hydraulic | Servo motor (AC/DC) |
| Accuracy | Low (affected by load) | High (auto-corrected) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Speed | Limited (no slip) | High |
| Application | Light-duty, PTP | Heavy-duty, Contouring |
| Examples | PCB drilling, hobby mills | Industrial CNC mills/lathes |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** What are the main **components of an NC system**? Explain any five in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A turning operation is to be performed on a mild-steel workpiece of diameter **$D = 100$ mm** at a cutting speed of **$V_c = 250$ m/min** and feed of **$0.25$ mm/rev** with depth of cut **$d = 2$ mm**. Calculate:
   (i) Spindle speed in rpm.
   (ii) Feed rate in mm/min.
   (iii) Metal Removal Rate (MRR) in cm³/min. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

The main components of an NC system are:

**1. Part Program:**
A sequence of coded alphanumeric instructions (G-codes, M-codes) that define the complete machining operation. It is prepared offline (manually or via CAM) and loaded into the MCU. Example block: `N50 G01 X50.0 Y25.0 F150`.

**2. Machine Control Unit (MCU):**
The hardware/software that reads the part program, decodes it, performs interpolation, and generates command signals to the servo drives. It includes CPU, memory, I/O ports, and interpolator.

**3. Servo Drive System:**
Power amplifiers that drive the servo motors (or hydraulic actuators) on each axis. They receive low-power command signals from the MCU and produce high-power motion.

**4. Feedback System:**
Position and velocity sensors (encoders, resolvers, LVDTs) that continuously measure the actual position of each slide and feed it back to the MCU for error correction in closed-loop systems.

**5. Machine Tool:**
The mechanical portion of the system — bed, columns, slides, spindle, ATC, chuck, tool magazine. It must be designed with high static stiffness, low thermal expansion, and good dynamic damping to support precision NC operation.

**6. Operator Interface (HMI):**
A panel with keyboard, display, MDI, and rotary overrides for the operator to load programs, set zero points, monitor status, and override feed/spindle speed.

*(Any five of the above can be written.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:**
- Workpiece diameter: $D = 100$ mm
- Cutting speed: $V_c = 250$ m/min
- Feed per rev: $f_r = 0.25$ mm/rev
- Depth of cut: $d = 2$ mm

**(i) Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1000 \times 250}{\pi \times 100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250{,}000}{314.159} \\[4pt]
&\approx 795.77 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 796 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate $f_n$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_n &= N \cdot f_r \\[4pt]
&= 795.77 \times 0.25 \\[4pt]
&\approx 198.94 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{f_n \approx 199 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Metal Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= f_r \cdot d \cdot V_c \\[4pt]
&= 0.25 \times 2 \times 250 \\[4pt]
&= 125 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \quad \left[\because 1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3\right]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 125 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Spindle Speed Formula:** The constant 1000 in the numerator of $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$ is to **convert mm to metres**. Forgetting it (or using 1 or 10) gives a wildly wrong answer. Always check: $D$ in mm → constant = 1000; $D$ in m → constant = 1.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Feed Rate and Feed per Rev:** In turning, $f_r$ is mm/rev; in milling, $f_z$ is mm/tooth. Many students blindly multiply $N \times f_r$ without identifying which feed term the problem gives.
>
> 3. **MRR Formula Mismatch:** Use $MRR = f_r \cdot d \cdot V_c$ for **turning**, but $MRR = f_n \cdot d \cdot w$ for **milling**. Writing the wrong one costs full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing Open-Loop with Closed-Loop:** A common error is calling stepper motors "closed-loop". Stepper motors are inherently **open-loop** — they have no feedback. Closed-loop requires an encoder + servo motor + PID.
>
> 5. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** KTU strictly penalises answers without units. Always append `mm/min`, `rpm`, `cm³/min`, etc.
>
> 6. **Writing "CNC" and "NC" Interchangeably:** Although related, NC and CNC are not identical. Mention the role of the microprocessor to score the distinction mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Numerical Control & Machine Tool Functions**

- ✅ **NC Definition:** Automation of machine tool by coded numerical instructions.
- ✅ **Origin:** John T. Parsons + MIT (1952).
- ✅ **Three Primary Functions of a Machine Tool:** Positioning, Machining, Auxiliary.
- ✅ **Three Sub-Systems of NC:** Part Program, MCU, Machine Tool (+ Servo + Feedback + HMI for full CNC).
- ✅ **PTP vs. Contouring:** Path matters in contouring, not in PTP.
- ✅ **Open-Loop:** No feedback, stepper motor based, low cost, low accuracy.
- ✅ **Closed-Loop:** Encoders + servo motor + PID, high accuracy, used in modern CNC.
- ✅ **Spindle Speed Formula (must memorise):** $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$.
- ✅ **MRR (Turning):** $f_r \cdot d \cdot V_c$ in mm³/min.
- ✅ **Feed Rate (Turning):** $f_n = N \cdot f_r$.
- ✅ **BLU (Least Count):** Smallest programmable increment, typically 0.001 mm.
- ✅ **Axes Convention:** Z = spindle axis, X = longest horizontal travel, Y = third axis (right-hand rule).
- ✅ **Advantages:** Repeatability, complexity, productivity, lead time, low scrap.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** High cost, skilled programmer required, maintenance-intensive.
- ✅ **Typical Applications:** Aerospace blades, automotive engine parts, die/mould, medical implants.
- ✅ **Classification bases:** Motion type, loop type, power source, application, control type.
- ✅ **MCU Functions:** Decode, Interpolate, Distribute, Monitor, Coordinate, Display.
- ✅ **Feedback sensors used:** Linear encoder, rotary encoder, resolver, LVDT.
- ✅ **ATC:** Automatic Tool Changer — controlled by M06 (typical) in CNC programs.
- ✅ **Programming language:** G-code (geometry) + M-code (miscellaneous) + S, F, T codes.
- ✅ **Historical note:** MIT 1952 machine was a Cincinnati Hydrotel — the first NC mill.
- ✅ **Always carry units** through every calculation; KTU is strict on this.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Numerical Control: Functions of a Machine Tool**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Numerical Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Numerical Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Numerical Control (NC)

**Numerical Control (NC)** is a form of programmable automation in which the processing instructions for a machine tool are stored on some medium (such as punched paper tape, magnetic tape, or punched cards) as a series of numbers, symbols, letters, and decimal points. These instructions are then fed into the machine tool's control system, which interprets them and commands the machine to perform specific operations.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus definition, NC is:

> **Numerical Control (NC):** "An automated control system of a machine tool in which a sequence of operations is determined by coded instructions that are recorded on a suitable storage medium."

The three key characteristics that define NC:
1. **Programmed Instructions**: The sequence of operations is pre-defined and stored.
2. **Coded Format**: Information is given in symbolic/numeric codes (not manual operator input).
3. **Automated Execution**: The machine itself carries out the operations with minimal human intervention.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "GPS for a Machine" Analogy**
> 
> Think of Numerical Control like a **GPS navigation system for a machine tool**. 
> 
> Just as a GPS:
> - Takes a **pre-programmed route** (your part program)
> - Translates it into **directional commands** (X, Y, Z movements)
> - Guides the vehicle (the machine tool) **automatically** along the path
> - Requires **minimal driver input** (the operator)
> 
> An NC system does the same for a cutting tool — it follows a coded set of instructions to move the tool precisely along a predefined path, controlling position, speed, and feed without needing a human to manually turn handwheels.

## 1.3 Evolution Timeline of NC

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1947** | John T. Parsons conceived the idea of NC at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base |
| **1952** | First NC milling machine developed at MIT (with Parsons) |
| **1955** | Introduction of point-to-point NC machining |
| **1960s** | Development of Computer Numerical Control (CNC) |
| **1970s** | Adoption of microprocessors in CNC |
| **1980s** | Direct Numerical Control (DNC) became feasible |
| **1990s+** | Modern CNC with PC-based controllers and CAD/CAM integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** 
> - **NC** = The original concept (1950s, hardwired controllers)
> - **CNC** = Computer-based numerical control (modern, software-based)
> - **DNC** = Direct Numerical Control (multiple machines connected to a central computer)

## 1.4 Why Numerical Control Matters

The motivation for developing NC arose from the need to:
- Manufacture **complex aerospace parts** (post-WWII jet engine components)
- Reduce **human error** in repetitive production
- Achieve **tight tolerances** (within $\pm 0.01 \text{ mm}$)
- Increase **productivity** and reduce scrap rates
- Enable **mass production** of identical parts
- Lower **skilled labor dependency**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principle of NC

The working of an NC machine is based on a **closed-loop feedback system** that involves these sequential stages:

1. **Input Stage**: Part program (coded instructions) is fed into the controller.
2. **Decoding Stage**: The controller interprets the codes (G-codes, M-codes, coordinates).
3. **Data Processing**: The MCU (Machine Control Unit) calculates trajectories, velocities, and positions.
4. **Signal Conversion**: Digital signals are converted to analog signals (or stepping pulses for open-loop).
5. **Actuation**: Servo motors (or stepper motors) drive the leadscrews.
6. **Feedback (Closed Loop)**: Encoders/resolvers compare actual position to commanded position.
7. **Error Correction**: Any deviation generates a corrective signal to minimize error.
8. **Execution**: The cutting tool performs the operation accurately.

## 2.2 Block Diagram Components of an NC System

The NC system has three fundamental sub-systems:

### A. Program of Instructions (Input Medium)
- **Punched paper tape** (historical, 8-bit ASCII codes)
- **Magnetic tape** 
- **Punched cards**
- **Direct keyboard entry** (modern CNC)
- **CAD/CAM generated programs** (current)

### B. Machine Control Unit (MCU)
The "brain" of the NC system. Contains:
- **Data Processing Unit (DPU)**: Decodes part program
- **Control Loops**: Position and velocity control
- **Interpolator**: Calculates intermediate points between start and end of tool path
- **Servo-Drive Interface**: Sends commands to drive motors

### C. Machine Tool
- The physical machine (lathe, mill, drill, grinder) that executes the cutting operation.
- Modified with **positional feedback devices** and **servo drives**.

## 2.3 NC Coordinate System (The "Why" of NC Precision)

The NC machine tool uses a **Cartesian coordinate system** aligned with the principal directions of the machine slides.

For a standard 3-axis milling machine:

$$\hat{X} = \text{Longest travel (table movement)}$$

$$\hat{Y} = \text{Second longest travel (saddle movement)}$$

$$\hat{Z} = \text{Parallel to spindle axis (quill movement)}$$

A typical right-hand rule applies:
- **+X**: Tool moves away from operator
- **+Y**: Tool moves to the right
- **+Z**: Tool moves up (away from workpiece)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **absolute zero point** (workpiece origin) is established by the programmer to define all coordinates relative to a known datum on the workpiece.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for CNC)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the most frequently tested in KTU examinations on NC fundamentals.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Lead of screw}}{\text{Pulses per revolution}}$ | Smallest programmable increment | mm/pulse |
| 2 | $\text{Least Count} = \dfrac{\text{Total travel}}{\text{No. of divisions on scale}}$ | Minimum measurable distance | mm |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle speed from cutting velocity | rpm |
| 4 | $f_n = N \cdot Z \cdot f_z$ | Feed rate (milling) | mm/min |
| 5 | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_1 + L_2}{f_n}$ | Machining time (face milling) | min |
| 6 | $V_a = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$ | Average axis velocity | mm/min |
| 7 | $E_{\text{pos}} = P_{\text{commanded}} - P_{\text{actual}}$ | Position error (closed loop) | mm |
| 8 | $\text{Step angle} = \dfrac{360°}}{\text{No. of steps per rev}}$ | Stepper motor resolution | degrees |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars above are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Do **NOT** use `|` in any cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

NC technology is indispensable in:
- **Aerospace**: Machining of turbine blades, wing ribs
- **Automotive**: Engine blocks, transmission housings
- **Medical**: Orthopedic implants, surgical instruments
- **Mold & Die**: Complex 3D cavity machining
- **Electronics**: PCB drilling, semiconductor manufacturing

The economic break-even for NC vs. conventional machining typically occurs at batch sizes of **10–50 parts** (above which NC is more economical).

## 2.6 Classification of NC Systems

### By Control Loop Type
1. **Open-Loop System**: Uses stepper motors; no feedback (cheaper, less accurate)
2. **Closed-Loop System**: Uses servo motors + feedback (more accurate, more expensive)

### By Motion Type
1. **Point-to-Point (PTP)**: Tool moves to discrete positions; path between is irrelevant (e.g., drilling)
2. **Straight-Cut Control**: Tool moves in straight line only (limited application)
3. **Contouring (Continuous Path)**: Tool follows a continuous contour (most common in CNC milling/turning)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Operation of an NC System

The end-to-end execution of an NC command follows a strict, sequential pipeline. Each transition is detailed below.

### Step 1: Part Program Creation
The programmer writes a sequence of instructions in **standard NC code** (ISO/EIA). The program contains:
- Geometric data (coordinates)
- Technological data (feeds, speeds)
- Auxiliary functions (coolant on/off, tool change)

### Step 2: Input to the Controller
The program is loaded into the MCU. The data transmission medium determines the "generation" of NC:
- **1st Gen**: Punched paper tape (baud rate $\approx$ 300 characters/sec)
- **2nd Gen**: Magnetic tape
- **3rd Gen**: Computer-based CNC

### Step 3: Decoding
The **Data Processing Unit (DPU)** translates each block of the program into machine commands.

### Step 4: Interpolation
For a straight-line move from $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ to $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ in time $t$, the interpolator calculates intermediate points. The position of the tool at any time $\tau$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\tau) &= x_1 + \frac{(x_2 - x_1)}{t} \cdot \tau \\[6pt]
y(\tau) &= y_1 + \frac{(y_2 - y_1)}{t} \cdot \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

For circular interpolation, the parametric form is used:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\theta) &= x_c + r \cos(\theta) \\
y(\theta) &= y_c + r \sin(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(x_c, y_c)$ is the center of the circle and $r$ is the radius.

### Step 5: Position Control (Closed-Loop Operation)

The position error at any instant is:

$$
E_{\text{pos}} = P_{\text{commanded}} - P_{\text{actual}}
$$

This error drives the servo motor until $E_{\text{pos}} \rightarrow 0$.

### Step 6: Velocity Control

The feed velocity along a straight line is:

$$
V_{\text{feed}} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{dz}{dt}\right)^2}
$$

For a 2D straight-line cut from $(0,0)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ in time $t$:

$$
V_{\text{feed}} = \frac{\sqrt{x_2^2 + y_2^2}}{t}
$$

### Step 7: Actuation

The signal drives a servo motor, which rotates the ballscrew. The linear displacement $L$ of the slide due to $N$ revolutions of a leadscrew with lead $L_s$ is:

$$
L = N \cdot L_s
$$

### Step 8: Tool Movement and Machining
The tool moves precisely, and material removal occurs. Sensors (force, vibration) may provide real-time adaptive feedback in advanced systems.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Resolution Calculation (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A NC machine has a leadscrew with a lead of **5 mm**. The driving motor produces **1000 pulses per revolution**. Calculate the control resolution.

### Solution Steps:

**Step 1**: Identify the given values
- Lead of screw $L_s = 5 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Pulses per revolution $p = 1000 \text{ pulses/rev}$

**Step 2**: Apply the resolution formula

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{L_s}{p}
$$

**Step 3**: Substitute the values

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{1000 \text{ pulses}}
$$

**Step 4**: Final result

$$
\text{Resolution} = 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse} = 5 \text{ μm/pulse}
$$

**Interpretation:** The smallest position increment the controller can command is 5 micrometers, which is suitable for precision machining.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Spindle Speed Calculation

> **Problem:** A CNC turning operation requires a cutting speed of $v = 200 \text{ m/min}$ on a workpiece of diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the spindle speed.

### Solution Steps:

**Step 1**: Apply the spindle speed formula

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute (with $D$ in mm and $v$ in m/min, conversion factor 1000)

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 50}
$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate the denominator

$$
\pi \times 50 \approx 157.08 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4**: Final calculation

$$
N = \frac{200000}{157.08} \approx 1273.24 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5**: Round to nearest standard value (typical machine has fixed RPM steps)

$$
N_{\text{selected}} = 1273 \text{ rpm (approx.)}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop NC

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is **directly testable** in KTU exams (typical 7-mark question).

| Feature | Open-Loop NC | Closed-Loop NC |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Motor Type | Stepper motor | Servo motor (DC/AC) |
| Feedback | No feedback device | Encoder/Resolver |
| Accuracy | Lower (cumulative error) | Higher (error corrected) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Speed | Limited by stepper dynamics | Higher speeds achievable |
| Positioning | Approximate (may miss steps) | Exact (deviation corrected) |
| Application | Light-duty, low-speed | Heavy-duty, high-precision |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate to high |
| Example Use | Small drills, hobby CNC | Industrial mills, lathes |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NC vs CNC vs DNC

| Feature | NC | CNC | DNC |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| Full Form | Numerical Control | Computer Numerical Control | Direct Numerical Control |
| Controller | Hardwired logic | Microcomputer | Central computer + multiple CNCs |
| Program Storage | Punched tape | Memory (RAM/Flash) | Central host computer |
| Flexibility | Very low | High | High |
| Editing Program | Not possible | Possible at machine | Possible at host |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest (network setup) |
| Operators Needed | High (per machine) | Low | Lowest (one operator per network) |
| Use Today | Obsolete | Most common | Modern factories |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an NC System (Functional Architecture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a Numerical Control system. Each block represents a subsystem with a specific role in the closed-loop data flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Part Program Input Medium] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU]
    B --> C[Data Processing Unit DPU]
    C --> D[Interpolator Module]
    D --> E[Position Control Loop]
    D --> F[Velocity Control Loop]
    E --> G[Servo Amplifier Drive]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Servo Motor / Stepper Motor]
    H --> I[Ball Screw / Leadscrew]
    I --> J[Machine Slide / Tool]
    J --> K[Feedback Sensor Encoder]
    K --> L[Position Comparator]
    L --> E
    J --> M[Workpiece Machining Output]
    styleA fill:E1F5FF,stroke:0066CC,color:000000
    styleB fill:FFE1E1,stroke:CC0000,color:000000
    styleC fill:FFF4E1,stroke:CC6600,color:000000
    styleD fill:E1FFE1,stroke:009900,color:000000
    styleE fill:F0E1FF,stroke:6600CC,color:000000
    styleF fill:F0E1FF,stroke:6600CC,color:000000
    styleG fill:FFFFCC,stroke:CCCC00,color:000000
    styleH fill:FFCCFF,stroke:CC00CC,color:000000

    styleL fill:FFE1FF,stroke:CC0099,color:000000
    styleM fill:E1FFE1,stroke:009900,color:000000
```

## 4.2 Working Sequence Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Start: Operator Loads Part Program] --> A2[MCU Decodes Program]
    A2 --> A3{Interpolation Required?}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Calculate Intermediate Points]

    A4 --> A6[Compare Commanded vs Actual Position]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7{Error within Tolerance?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Send Correction Signal to Servo]
    A8 --> A6
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Drive Motor to Next Position]
    A9 --> A10{All Blocks Executed?}
    A10 -- No --> A2
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[End: Part Machined]
```

## 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of NC Systems

```mermaid

    B1[NC Systems] --> B2[By Control Loop]
    B1 --> B3[By Motion Type]
    B1 --> B4[By Generation]

    B3 --> B7[Point to Point PTP]
```

## 4.4 Feedback Control Loop (Closed-Loop) Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Reference Input: Commanded Position] --> C2[Comparator Sum Point]
    C2 --> C3[Controller / Amplifier]
    C3 --> C4[Servo Motor]

    C6 --> C7[Feedback Sensor: Encoder/Resolver]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q1. Define Numerical Control (NC). List any four advantages of NC machining.**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition:** Numerical Control is a form of programmable automation in which the processing instructions for a machine tool are stored as coded numerical data and executed automatically by the machine's control system.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Reduced lead time** for new product introduction.
2. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** (tolerances up to $\pm 0.005 \text{ mm}$ achievable).
3. **Lower skilled labor requirement** — once programmed, machine runs autonomously.
4. **Reduced scrap and rework** due to consistent machine behavior.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q2. Differentiate between point-to-point and contouring NC systems. Give one example application for each.**

#### Model Answer:
| Feature | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Tool path between points | Irrelevant (rapid traverse) | Precisely controlled |
| Accuracy | High at endpoints only | High throughout the path |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | **Drilling holes on a plate** | **Milling a curved profile on a die** |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following: Q1A **OR** Q1B.

---

### **Question 1A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Numerical Control system with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the role of the Machine Control Unit (MCU). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare open-loop and closed-loop NC systems in detail. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working Principle of NC System (7 Marks)**

The NC system executes a pre-programmed sequence of operations stored as coded data. The flow is:

1. **Input Stage (1 mark):** The part program — a list of coded instructions (G-codes, M-codes, coordinates) — is fed into the system through punched tape, magnetic tape, or direct keyboard.

2. **Decoding by MCU (2 marks):** The Machine Control Unit (MCU) is the brain. Its Data Processing Unit (DPU) reads each block of the program and converts codes into machine-understandable commands. The MCU performs:
   - Decoding
   - Interpolation (calculating intermediate tool positions)
   - Position and velocity control

3. **Signal Conversion and Actuation (2 marks):** The processed signals are amplified and sent to the servo motors (or stepper motors). These motors rotate the leadscrews, which move the machine slides (X, Y, Z axes).

4. **Feedback and Correction (2 marks):** In closed-loop systems, position encoders/resolvers measure the actual position and feed it back to a comparator. The difference (error) drives corrective signals to ensure the tool reaches the commanded position accurately.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 for the complete functional block diagram — should be drawn in the answer sheet for full marks.)

---

**(b) Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop NC Systems (7 Marks)**

(Refer to the Analytical Comparison Matrix in Section 3.4 for the complete table — the student should reproduce the key points in the answer.)

**Key points to mention:**
1. **Open-loop:** Uses stepper motors; no feedback; position is commanded by counting pulses; cheaper but subject to missed steps.
2. **Closed-loop:** Uses servo motors with feedback sensors (encoders/resolvers); continuously compares actual to commanded position; corrects any deviation; more accurate and reliable.
3. **Application differences:** Open-loop for low-cost hobby/light applications; closed-loop for industrial precision machining.

---

### **Question 1B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** What are the main components of a NC machine tool? Explain the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different types of NC systems based on the control loop and motion type with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Main Components of an NC Machine Tool (7 Marks)**

An NC machine tool consists of **three main sub-systems:**

1. **Program of Instructions / Input Medium (1 mark):** Stores the part program. Earlier: punched paper tape (1st generation). Now: USB, Ethernet, or direct keyboard entry in CNC.

2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU) (3 marks):** The "brain" comprising:
   - **Data Processing Unit (DPU):** Decodes input data
   - **Interpolator:** Calculates intermediate tool path points
   - **Feedback registers:** Store position data from sensors
   - **Servo control circuits:** Drive the motors

3. **Machine Tool (3 marks):** The physical machine (lathe, mill, drill) modified with:
   - Servo/stepper motors on each axis
   - Leadscrews for converting rotation to linear motion
   - Position feedback devices (encoders)
   - Automatic tool changer (ATC) in modern machines
   - Coolant and chip disposal systems

---

**(b) Types of NC Systems (7 Marks)**

**By Control Loop (4 marks):**
- **Open-Loop:** Stepper motor based, no feedback. Example: small engraving machine.
- **Closed-Loop:** Servo motor + encoder feedback. Example: industrial CNC vertical machining center.

**By Motion Type (3 marks):**
- **Point-to-Point (PTP):** Drilling machine, jig boring.
- **Straight-Cut Control:** Simple line cutting (rarely used standalone).
- **Contouring (Continuous Path):** CNC milling, lathe turning, EDM.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing NC with CNC:** Many students write "CNC" when the question asks for "NC." The 2024 KTU paper specifically tests this distinction. **Always read the question carefully** — NC = numerical control (general); CNC = computer-based NC.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the Block Diagram in 7-mark questions:** For any "explain with block diagram" question, a well-labeled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks**. Without it, you cannot score full marks.
> 
> 3. **Not mentioning the interpolator explicitly:** When explaining MCU, students often forget the **interpolator** function, which is a separate sub-module. Always list: DPU + Interpolator + Servo control.
> 
> 4. **Wrong units in spindle speed formula:** Use $N = 1000v/(\pi D)$ only when $D$ is in **mm** and $v$ is in **m/min**. Mixing units is the #1 reason for lost marks in numericals.
> 
> 5. **Confusing "Lead" with "Pitch":** Lead = distance moved per revolution (for single-start screws, lead = pitch; for multi-start, lead = pitch × number of starts). The formula uses **lead**, not pitch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam**

### Key Definitions to Memorize
- **NC**: Programmable automation using coded numerical instructions.
- **CNC**: NC where the controller is a dedicated computer.
- **DNC**: Multiple NC/CNC machines connected to a central host computer.
- **MCU**: Machine Control Unit — the brain of the NC system.
- **Interpolator**: Computes intermediate tool path points.
- **Part Program**: The set of coded instructions defining the operation sequence.

### High-Yield Facts
- NC was invented in **1952** at **MIT** (with John T. Parsons) for the **US Air Force**.
- Open-loop uses **stepper motors**; closed-loop uses **servo motors** with feedback.
- **Contouring** systems are also called **continuous-path** systems — most common in modern CNC.
- **Resolution** = (Lead of screw) ÷ (Pulses per revolution).
- **Spindle speed** $N = 1000v/(\pi D)$ where $v$ is in m/min and $D$ in mm.
- The **three subsystems** of NC: Program, MCU, Machine Tool.
- **Coordinate system**: Right-hand rule; Z is parallel to spindle axis.

### Must-Remember Comparisons
- NC vs CNC vs DNC
- Open-loop vs Closed-loop
