| **Workpiece Holding** | Vice, clamps, fixture on table | 3/4-jaw chuck, collet, mandrel |
| **Tool Holding** | Spindle taper (BT/CAT/HSK) | Turret station (VDI/bolt-on) |
| **Main Motion** | Tool rotates (spindle) | Workpiece rotates (spindle) |
| **Feed Motion** | X, Y, Z linear axes | X (radial), Z (axial) |
| **Number of Axes** | 3 to 5+ (X, Y, Z, A, B, C) | 2 to 5 (X, Z, C, Y, B) |
| **Tool Magazine** | 12 to 240 tools | 8 to 16 stations (turret) |
| **Tool Change Time** | 0.5 – 12 s | 0.2 – 0.5 s |
| **MRR** | High (deep cuts) | High (long passes) |
| **Typical Materials** | Castings, plates, forgings | Bar stock, forgings, castings |
| **Power Range** | 5 – 60 kW | 7 – 75 kW |
| **Cost Range (INR)** | ₹15 L – ₹3 Cr | ₹8 L – ₹2 Cr |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Tool Taper Standards Comparison

| Standard | Taper Ratio | Contact Type | Best For | Pull Stud |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BT (BT40, BT50)** | 7:24 | Taper + Flange | General purpose | Required |
| **CAT (CAT40, CAT50)** | 7:24 | Taper + Flange | High torque (US std.) | Required |
| **HSK-A63, A100** | 1:10 | Taper + Flange (dual) | HSM, aerospace | Not required |
| **HSK-E32, E50** | 1:10 | Taper only | HSM spindles | Not required |
| **Capto C6, C8** | Polygon | Face + taper | Multi-task machines | Not required |
| **KM4X** | 1:10 | Taper + Flange (4×) | Heavy-duty milling | Required |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between a CNC Machining Center and a CNC Turning Center in terms of construction and operations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Machining Center | Turning Center |
|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece motion** | Stationary on table; tool rotates | Workpiece rotates in chuck |
| **Axes** | X, Y, Z (with optional A, B, C) | X (radial), Z (axial) — with C, Y optional |
| **Tool storage** | Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) in chain/carousel magazine | Indexing turret (8–16 stations) |
| **Pallet system** | Automatic Pallet Changer (APC) available | Not typical |
| **Operations** | Milling, drilling, tapping, boring, reaming | Turning, facing, boring, threading, parting |
| **Tool holding** | Taper spindles (BT/CAT/HSK) | Square shank on turret; VDI for live tools |

*(Award 1 mark each for: workpiece motion difference, tool change system difference, and types of operations)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: What is the difference between BT and HSK tool taper systems? Why is HSK preferred in high-speed machining?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**BT Taper (7:24):**
- Standard steep taper (7:24 = approx. 16.26° half-angle)
- Contact is mainly on the **flange face**
- Requires a **pull stud** for retention
- Drawbar pulls the tool axially into the spindle

**HSK Taper (1:10):**
- Shallow taper (1:10 = approx. 5.71° half-angle)
- **Dual contact**: Both taper surface and flange face contact simultaneously
- **Hollow-shaft design** — clamping by axial force generates radial self-centering
- No pull stud required; clamping force is purely axial

**Why HSK for HSM (High-Speed Machining):**
1. Superior **radial stiffness** and **rotational accuracy** at high RPM (>15,000 rpm).
2. **Better balance** — no pull stud, hence less unbalance.
3. **Lower tool wear** on the spindle taper due to dual contact.
4. Faster tool change and reduced chip contamination.

*(1 mark for taper ratio distinction, 1 mark for clamping mechanism, 1 mark for HSM advantage)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Full Module Choice]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic structure of a CNC Vertical Machining Center (VMC). List the major subsystems and their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A CNC Vertical Machining Center is a multi-operation machine tool with a vertically oriented spindle, used for milling, drilling, boring, and tapping operations on prismatic workpieces, equipped with an Automatic Tool Changer (ATC).

**Major Subsystems (6 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Structure (Base, Column, Bed, Table):** Cast iron construction for vibration damping; the table moves in X and Y directions, while the spindle head moves in Z.

2. **Spindle Assembly:** Houses the AC spindle motor (typically 7.5 – 30 kW); the spindle taper accepts BT40/CAT40/HSK-A63 tool holders; provides the main rotary cutting motion.

3. **Feed Drive System:** Three servo motors (one per axis X, Y, Z) drive **preloaded ball screws** through couplings; feedback is provided by **rotary encoders** (semi-closed) or **linear scales** (full-closed).

4. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC):** A **chain-type magazine** typically holds 24 to 60 tools; tool change is performed by a swing arm (dual-arm) gripper. Tool-to-tool time: 0.8 to 2.0 seconds.

5. **CNC Controller:** Executes the part program; performs linear and circular interpolation; manages tool length and diameter offsets; provides operator HMI; commonly FANUC, Siemens, or Mitsubishi.

6. **Coolant & Chip Management:** Flood coolant (and through-spindle coolant) for chip evacuation; chip conveyor (scraper or auger) to remove chips from the work envelope.

7. **Safety & Auxiliary Systems:** Enclosure with interlocked doors, work-area lighting, tool length probe, and automatic lubrication system.

*(For a neat block diagram, the student should draw a labeled schematic showing Base, Column, Spindle, ATC Magazine, Table, and Controller.)*

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Q: A CNC turning operation on an EN8 steel shaft uses the following data: Cutting speed $v_c = 200$ m/min, Feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, Depth of cut $a_p = 2$ mm, Workpiece diameter $D = 80$ mm. Calculate (i) Spindle speed, (ii) MRR, and (iii) Cutting power. Assume specific cutting force $k_c = 1800$ N/mm² and machine efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Spindle Speed (2 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \dfrac{200{,}000}{251.327} \approx 795.77 \text{ rpm}$$

**Answer: N ≈ 796 rpm**

**(ii) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p = 200 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 100 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s} \times 60$$

$$MRR = 100 \times 60 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s} \times 1000$$

Wait — careful with units. Since $v_c$ is in m/min:

$$MRR = 200{,}000 \text{ mm/min} \times 0.25 \text{ mm} \times 2 \text{ mm} = 100{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Answer: MRR = 1,00,000 mm³/min or 100 cm³/min**

**(iii) Cutting Power (3 marks):**

Cutting force:
$$F_c = k_c \cdot f \cdot a_p = 1800 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 900 \text{ N}$$

Cutting power (output at tool):
$$P_c = F_c \cdot v_c = 900 \times \dfrac{200}{60} = 900 \times 3.333 = 3000 \text{ W} = 3 \text{ kW}$$

Required motor power:
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_c}{\eta} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.80} = 3.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer: Required motor power = 3.75 kW**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> - Students often forget to convert **m/min to m/s** when computing power. Always divide $v_c$ (m/min) by 60.
> - The formula $P_{motor} = P_c / \eta$ accounts for machine efficiency; omitting $\eta$ causes a **1-mark deduction**.
> - State the **assumed units** clearly; if you write $v_c = 200$ but the answer is 6000 mm³/min without explanation, marks may be deducted.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Q: With a neat block diagram, describe the basic structure of a CNC Turning Center. Explain the function of its key components.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A CNC Turning Center is a CNC lathe variant that performs turning, facing, boring, drilling, and (optionally) milling operations on a workpiece held and rotated by a chuck, with multiple tools available on an indexed turret.

**Key Components and Functions (6 marks):**

1. **Headstock with Main Spindle (1.5 marks):** Houses the spindle motor (AC servo) and the chuck. The spindle rotates the workpiece and provides C-axis positioning in modern machines for live-tooling.

2. **Chuck and Work-Holding (1 mark):** Hydraulic 3-jaw or 4-jaw self-centering chuck; optional collet chuck for bar work. Provides high gripping force (up to 100 kN).

3. **Slant Bed (1 mark):** Inclined cast iron bed (typically 30° or 45°) for better chip evacuation, higher rigidity, and easier operator access.

4. **Tool Turret (1.5 marks):** Indexable turret with 8 to 16 stations. Servo or hydraulic indexing in 0.2 to 0.5 seconds. Each station can hold turning, boring, or threading tools; live-tool stations include a driven spindle.

5. **Cross Slide and Saddle (1 mark):** The X-axis (cross) and Z-axis (longitudinal) slides carry the turret; driven by ball screws and servo motors with linear scales.

6. **Tailstock and Sub-Spindle (0.5 marks):** The tailstock supports long shafts; the optional sub-spindle enables back-side machining after part parting.

7. **Coolant, Chip Conveyor, and CNC Controller (0.5 marks):** Flood coolant with through-tool capability; chip pan and conveyor; CNC controller (FANUC/Siemens) with HMI.

**Neat Block Diagram (essential for full marks):** The student should draw and label: Spindle, Chuck, Workpiece, Cross Slide (X), Saddle (Z), Turret, Tool, Tailstock, Bed, and Controller.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Q: Compare BT, CAT, and HSK tool taper systems. With a sketch, explain the tool change procedure in a chain-type ATC.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | BT (MAS 403) | CAT (ANSI B5.50) | HSK (DIN 69893) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taper | 7:24 (16.26°) | 7:24 (16.26°) | 1:10 (5.71°) |
| Origin | Japan | USA | Germany |
| Contact | Taper + Flange (single) | Taper + Flange (single) | Dual (taper + flange) |
| Pull stud | Required | Required | Not required |
| Balance | Pull stud adds unbalance | Pull stud adds unbalance | Excellent |
| Speed range | ≤ 12,000 rpm | ≤ 12,000 rpm | Up to 40,000 rpm |
| Stiffness | Good | Good | Very high |
| Common sizes | BT30, 40, 50 | CAT40, 50 | HSK-A32, A40, A50, A63, A100 |

**Tool Change Procedure in Chain-Type ATC (3 marks):**

1. **Spindle Position Command:** CNC issues a tool change command (M06); the spindle is oriented to a specific angle (Z-axis up, tool at tool-change position).

2. **Magazine Index:** The chain-type magazine rotates to bring the **next tool** to the tool-change position adjacent to the spindle.

3. **Arm Engagement:** A **dual swing arm** (or "pot-style" changer) extends and engages both the **old tool in spindle** and the **new tool in the magazine** simultaneously via gripper fingers.

4. **Unclamping Old Tool:** The spindle drawbar retracts, releasing the old tool.

5. **Tool Swap:** The swing arm rotates 180° in 0.5 to 1.0 second, exchanging old and new tool positions.

6. **Clamping New Tool:** The drawbar extends and pulls the new tool into the spindle taper, securing it with a clamping force of 8 to 50 kN.

7. **Arm Retraction & Reset:** The swing arm retracts; the magazine returns to its holding position; the spindle resumes rotation. Total tool-to-tool time: **0.8 to 2.0 seconds.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - Students often confuse **BT and CAT** — they have the *same* taper geometry (7:24) but differ in flange dimensions, keyway position, and standards body. **BT40 ≠ CAT40** in interchangeability.
> - The tool change procedure must mention the **drawbar action** and the **arm rotation**; simply saying "tool is changed" is incomplete and loses **2 marks**.
> - The HSK taper is **1:10**, not 7:24. A common student error is to copy-paste the BT data here — carefully review the row.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2**

### A. Machining Center Basics
- ☐ A Machining Center is a CNC mill + ATC + (optionally) APC.
- ☐ Vertical Machining Center (VMC) has a vertical spindle; Horizontal Machining Center (HMC) has a horizontal spindle.
- ☐ Major subsystems: Base, Bed, Spindle, ATC, APC, Controller, Coolant.
- ☐ ATC types: Umbrella, Carousel, **Chain** (most common in industry), Matrix.
- ☐ Tool-to-tool change time: 0.5 – 12 seconds.
- ☐ Standard axes: X, Y, Z (3-axis); A, B, C added for 4 and 5-axis.

### B. Turning Center Basics
- ☐ A Turning Center is a CNC lathe with a turret and (optionally) live tooling.
- ☐ Slant beds (30°/45°) are preferred for chip evacuation.
- ☐ Turret: 8 to 16 stations, indexing time 0.2 – 0.5 s.
- ☐ Live tooling = driven tools on the turret for milling/drilling.
- ☐ C-axis = spindle positioning; Y-axis = cross-axis for off-center milling.
- ☐ Sub-spindle = secondary counter-spindle for back-side operations.

### C. Tooling Systems
- ☐ Taper standards: **BT, CAT (7:24)** and **HSK (1:10)**.
- ☐ HSK has **dual contact** (taper + flange) — superior for HSM.
- ☐ No pull stud in HSK → better balance.
- ☐ Collet systems (ER, OZ, DA) for small tools.
- ☐ Shrink-fit and hydraulic chucks for HSM precision.
- ☐ Turning tools use square shank (20×20, 25×25, 32×32 mm).
- ☐ VDI 30/40/60 standard for live tool holders on turning centers.

### D. Key Numerical Formulas
- ☐ Spindle speed: $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}$
- ☐ MRR (turning): $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$
- ☐ MRR (milling): $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$
- ☐ Feed rate: $v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot n$
- ☐ Power: $P_{motor} = \dfrac{k_c \cdot f \cdot a_p \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$
- ☐ Engagement angle: $\theta_e = 2\cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{a_e}{D}\right)$

### E. Common Exam Traps
- ☐ Do **not** confuse positioning accuracy with repeatability.
- ☐ Do **not** confuse BT and CAT tapers (same geometry, different standard).
- ☐ Do **not** forget the $1000$ and $60$ unit conversion factors in $N$ and $P$ formulas.
- ☐ Do **not** state the formula for MRR in milling as $v_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$ (that's for turning).
- ☐ Always include the **drawbar mechanism** when explaining ATC.
- ☐ Always state the **unit of final answer** in numerical problems.

### F. KTU 2024 Scheme Style Mark Distribution Reminder
- **Part A (3 marks):** Define, list, or short comparison — be precise in 4-5 lines.
- **Part B (14 marks):** Either (a) diagram-based structural question (7 marks) or (b) numerical/derivation question (7 marks). Answer any **ONE** of the two full Part B questions in the module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tool Holders and Tool Indexing, Axis Configurations and Fundamentals of CNC Codes, Datum and Tool Offset Settings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tool Holders

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Tool Holder** is a precision-engineered mechanical interface that rigidly clamps a cutting tool and mounts it onto the machine spindle via a standardized taper. The tool holder transmits torque, resists radial and axial cutting forces, and maintains positional accuracy (runout typically within **0.005 mm**) at spindle speeds up to **40,000 RPM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tool holders follow international taper standards — the most common being **CAT (CT)**, **BT (MAS 403)**, and **HSK (DIN 69893)**. The numerical suffix (e.g., CAT40, BT50) denotes the **large diameter of the taper in millimeters** measured at the gauge line.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a tool holder as the **"handshake"** between the machine and the cutting tool. Just as a power drill's chuck must grip a drill bit firmly without slipping at high speed, a CNC tool holder must do the same — but with micrometer-level precision, repeatability, and the ability to be changed 10,000+ times per day in mass production.

### Common Tool Holder Types

| Type | Application | Key Feature |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Collet Chuck (ER System)** | Drilling, light milling | Wide clamping range, popular for tool room |
| **End Mill Holder** | Heavy-duty milling | High gripping torque, low runout |
| **Drill Chuck** | Drilling only | Keyless, quick-change |
| **Boring Bar Holder** | Boring operations | Holds single-point boring tools |
| **Tapping Holder** | Thread tapping | Axial float to compensate for pitch errors |
| **Shell Mill Holder** | Face milling | Drives large diameter cutters via drive keys |
| **Morse Taper Adapter** | Lathe tailstock | Converts MT to spindle taper |

---

## 1.2 Tool Indexing

### Formal Definition
**Tool Indexing** is the automated process of retrieving, identifying, exchanging, and returning cutting tools between the machine spindle and a tool storage magazine during CNC operation, governed by a tool number (T-code) call in the part program.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **revolving door of Swiss Army knives** attached to your machine. The CNC controller says "I need knife #3 (a 10 mm drill) now," the ATC arm grabs it from the carousel, places it in the spindle, and the previous tool is sent back to its slot — all in under **5 seconds** in modern machining centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Components of an ATC System:**
> - **Tool Magazine** (drum, chain, or turret type)
> - **Tool Change Arm** (two-arm swing/gripper)
> - **Tool Identification System** (T-number, barcode, RFID)
> - **Spindle Orientation Mechanism** (for taper alignment)

---

## 1.3 Axis Configurations

### Formal Definition (ISO 841 Standard)
The **Axis Configuration** of a CNC machine is the geometric arrangement of linear (X, Y, Z) and rotary (A, B, C) axes defined by **ISO 841** and **EIA RS-267**, where linear axes follow the **Right-Hand Rule** and rotary axes rotate about the corresponding linear axis.

### Conceptual Analogy
If the CNC machine is a **3D printer on steroids**, then:
- **X-axis** = left/right (East-West)
- **Y-axis** = front/back (North-South)
- **Z-axis** = up/down (Sky-Earth)
- **A, B, C** = rotational motions about X, Y, Z respectively

---

## 1.4 Fundamentals of CNC Codes

### Formal Definition
**CNC Codes** are alphanumeric instructions (called *words*) that form an *address-based programming language* standardized by **ISO 6983** (internationally) and **EIA RS-244** (American). Each code controls a specific function: motion (G-codes), miscellaneous operations (M-codes), tooling (T-codes), or spindle/feed parameters (S, F).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of CNC codes as the **"recipe ingredients"** for machining. "G01" is the "stir slowly" instruction, "M08" is the "turn on coolant" instruction, and "X50.0" is the "go to 50 mm" coordinate — all written in a language the machine understands precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Program Word Format:** `N10 G01 X50.0 Y25.0 F150`
> - **N** = Sequence number
> - **G01** = Preparatory function (linear interpolation)
> - **X, Y** = Coordinate words (mm)
> - **F150** = Feed rate (mm/min)

---

## 1.5 Datum and Tool Offset Settings

### Formal Definition
A **Datum** in CNC is a precisely defined reference point or surface (zero coordinate origin) used to establish a consistent coordinate system between the part drawing, the workholding fixture, and the machine. **Tool Offsets** are pre-stored numerical values in the controller's offset register that compensate for the physical dimensions of each tool (length, radius, wear).

### Conceptual Analogy
A **datum** is like the **"ground zero"** or starting line in a race — every measurement in your program is referenced from this point. A **tool offset** is like adjusting the **starting position of each runner** based on their height, so they all cross the same finish line at the same time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Datums in CNC:**
> 1. **Machine Zero (M)** — fixed point determined by limit switches.
> 2. **Work Zero / Part Zero (W)** — defined on the workpiece (typically top-center of stock).
> 3. **Tool Zero (T)** — usually the spindle nose or a master reference tool.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory: Tool Holder Taper Standards

### Why Tapers Are Used
Tool holders use a **self-holding taper** (Morse-type geometry) that:
1. Provides **friction-locked grip** without needing a drawbar on the machine side for retention (except for BT/CAT which use a drawbar for *positive locking*).
2. Automatically centers the tool with high **concentricity** (typically TIR < 0.01 mm at 3×D from spindle).
3. Transfers **torque** through the taper surface, not just friction.

### Comparison of Major Taper Standards

| Standard | Origin | Contact | Drawbar | Common Use |
|----------|--------|---------|---------|------------|
| **CAT (CT)** | Caterpillar/ANSI B5.50 | Flange face + taper | Yes | American machines |
| **BT** | MAS 403 (Japan) | Taper only (45° angle on flange) | Yes | Asian machines, most common |
| **HSK** | DIN 69893 (Germany) | **Taper + face contact (Hollow Shank)** | Yes (hollow) | High-speed (HSK-A63, HSK-A100) |
| **SK** | DIN 69871 (Germany) | Taper only | Yes | European machines |
| **NT/NMTB** | Older American | Taper | No | Legacy machines |

### HSK — The High-Speed Champion
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HSK (HohlSchaft Kegel = Hollow Shank Taper)** uses **dual contact** (taper + flange face). At 20,000+ RPM, the centrifugal force expands the spindle taper, but the HSK's hollow design *contracts* symmetrically, maintaining contact and balance. This is why HSK is mandatory for **5-axis and high-speed machining**.

---

## 2.2 Tool Magazine & Indexing Mechanisms

### Magazine Types
1. **Drum / Carousel Magazine** — circular arrangement; capacity **12–30 tools**; tool change time ~2 sec.
2. **Chain Magazine** — conveyor loop; capacity **30–200+ tools**; common in horizontal machining centers.
3. **Turret Magazine** — used on **turning centers** (lathes); index by rotation; capacity 8–12 stations.

### ATC Cycle (Tool-to-Tool Time)
The complete automatic tool change follows this sequence:

1. **M06 T03** command is read by the controller.
2. Spindle is oriented to a specific angular position (e.g., Z-axis keyway alignment).
3. Spindle moves to **tool change position** (Z-axis retract).
4. Tool change arm swings in, **unclamps old tool**, **swaps with new tool**.
5. **Drawbar pulls new tool** into spindle taper (positive lock).
6. Arm retracts to magazine.
7. Spindle resumes machining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool Change Time** is the elapsed time between M06 execution and the next cutting motion — typically **1.5 to 8 seconds** in modern VMCs. This directly affects **spindle uptime percentage**.

---

## 2.3 Axis Configuration — The 3-2-1 Rule

### Right-Hand Rule (ISO 841)
The **positive Z-axis** is the direction of the **maximum spindle travel away from the workpiece** (i.e., the tool moves *away* from the part in +Z). The X-axis is the direction of **longest linear travel**, and Y completes the right-hand Cartesian system.

### Axis Classification

| Axis | Type | Description | Right-Hand Rule |
|------|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **X** | Primary linear | Longest linear travel | Index finger |
| **Y** | Secondary linear | Perpendicular to X-Z plane | Middle finger |
| **Z** | Spindle parallel | Parallel to spindle axis | Thumb |
| **A** | Rotary | Rotation **about X-axis** | Right-hand rule on X |
| **B** | Rotary | Rotation **about Y-axis** | Right-hand rule on Y |
| **C** | Rotary | Rotation **about Z-axis** | Right-hand rule on Z |

### Common Configurations

- **2-Axis Lathe**: X, Z
- **3-Axis VMC**: X, Y, Z
- **4-Axis HMC**: X, Y, Z + A (rotary table) or B (tilting)
- **5-Axis Machine**: 3 linear + 2 rotary (e.g., A+C head/table, B+C head/table)
- **Mill-Turn**: 9+ axes (multi-spindle, multi-turret)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous vs. Positioning 5-Axis:**
> - **3+2 Positioning** — rotary axes lock, tool moves linearly (still essentially 3-axis at angles).
> - **5-Axis Simultaneous** — all 5 axes move concurrently for complex surfaces (turbine blades, impellers).

---

## 2.4 CNC Code Architecture

### G-Code (Preparatory Functions) — Major Codes

| G-Code | Function | Modal/Non-Modal | Group |
|--------|----------|-----------------|-------|
| G00 | Rapid traverse | Modal | 01 |
| G01 | Linear interpolation (feed) | Modal | 01 |
| G02 | Circular CW interpolation | Modal | 01 |
| G03 | Circular CCW interpolation | Modal | 01 |
| G04 | Dwell (P=seconds) | Non-modal | 00 |
| G17/G18/G19 | Plane selection (XY/YZ/ZX) | Modal | 02 |
| G20/G21 | Inch / Metric | Modal | 06 |
| G28 | Return to reference point | Non-modal | 00 |
| G40/G41/G42 | Cutter comp OFF/LEFT/RIGHT | Modal | 07 |
| G43/G44 | Tool length comp +/− | Modal | 08 |
| G49 | Cancel tool length comp | Modal | 08 |
| G54–G59 | Work coordinate systems 1–6 | Modal | 12 |
| G80 | Cancel canned cycle | Modal | 09 |
| G81–G89 | Canned drilling cycles | Modal | 09 |
| G90/G91 | Absolute / Incremental | Modal | 03 |
| G94/G95 | Feed per min / per rev | Modal | 05 |
| G98/G99 | Canned cycle return to initial/R-point | Modal | 10 |

### M-Code (Miscellaneous Functions) — Major Codes

| M-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| M00 | Program stop (operator intervention) |
| M01 | Optional stop (with operator switch) |
| M02 | Program end |
| M03 | Spindle ON CW |
| M04 | Spindle ON CCW |
| M05 | Spindle OFF |
| M06 | Automatic tool change |
| M07/M08/M09 | Mist coolant / Flood coolant / Coolant OFF |
| M19 | Spindle orientation |
| M30 | Program end + rewind |

### Program Structure (Standard Format)
```
%
O1001 (PROGRAM HEADER / NAME)
N10 G21 G90 G17 G54 (SAFETY & SETUP BLOCK)
N20 T01 M06 (TOOL CHANGE)
N30 S1200 M03 (SPINDLE START CW)
N40 G00 X10.0 Y10.0 (RAPID TO POSITION)
N50 G43 H01 Z5.0 (TOOL LENGTH COMP)
N60 G01 Z-5.0 F100 (PLUNGE WITH FEED)
N70 G02 X30.0 Y30.0 R10.0 (CIRCULAR CW)
N80 G00 Z100.0 (RETRACT)
N90 M05 (SPINDLE OFF)
N100 M30 (PROGRAM END)
%
```

---

## 2.5 Datum & Tool Offset Theory

### Three Critical Datums (G and M)

1. **Machine Coordinate System (MCS)** — fixed; origin = machine home; established at power-on via **return-to-reference** (G28 / G53).
2. **Work Coordinate System (WCS)** — set on the workpiece using **G54–G59** (or extended G54.1 P1..P48 on Fanuc).
3. **Tool Length Offset** — stored in **H-offset register** (Fanuc) or geometry table.

### Tool Length Offset (G43/H01)
The controller adds the **H-offset value** to programmed Z-coordinates so the **programmed tip of tool #1 (T01)** is the reference. All other tools (T02, T03...) have their lengths measured relative to T01, stored in H02, H03, etc.

$$Z_{actual} = Z_{programmed} - H_{offset}$$

### Cutter Radius Compensation (G41/G42)
The controller offsets the tool path by the **cutter radius (D-offset)** to the left (G41) or right (G42) of the programmed contour.

$$C_{offset} = \pm D_{cutter} \cdot \hat{n}_{perpendicular}$$

### Work Offset Setting Methods
1. **Manual Edge Finder / Indicator** — most common in industry.
2. **Probing (Renishaw OMP60)** — automated with M-code triggers.
3. **Laser Tool Presetter** — off-machine for tool length pre-measurement.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Units | Application |
|-------------------|-------------------------|-------|-------------|
| Feed rate (linear) | $F = n \times f_z \times Z$ | mm/min | Milling feed calculation |
| Spindle speed | $n = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ | RPM | All rotating tools |
| MRR (milling) | $MRR = a_p \times a_e \times F$ | mm³/min | Productivity |
| MRR (turning) | $MRR = V_c \times f \times a_p$ | mm³/min | Lathe productivity |
| Power required | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | kW | Motor sizing |
| Cutting time | $t_c = \dfrac{L}{F}$ | min | Cycle time |
| Tool length comp | $Z_{machine} = Z_{programmed} - H_{offset}$ | mm | G43 calculation |
| Cutter comp offset | $\Delta = D_{cutter} / 2$ perpendicular to path | mm | G41/G42 |
| Indexing time | $T_{idx} = T_{ATC} \times N_{tools} + T_{rapid}$ | sec | Tool change analysis |
| Taper engagement | $\theta = 7/24 = 16.59°$ (BT/CAT) | degrees | Standard angle |

> **Where:**
> - $n$ = spindle speed (RPM)
> - $f_z$ = feed per tooth (mm/tooth)
> - $Z$ = number of effective teeth
> - $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min)
> - $D$ = tool diameter (mm)
> - $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm)
> - $a_e$ = width of cut (mm)
> - $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²)
> - $\eta$ = machine efficiency (typically 0.7–0.85)

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

### Problem Context
Given a workpiece material (Aluminum 6061, $V_c = 300$ m/min recommended) and a tool diameter ($D = 12$ mm end mill), find the required spindle speed.

### Derivation
The cutting speed is the tangential velocity at the tooth periphery:

$$V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot n$$

where $V_c$ is in m/min, $D$ is in meters, and $n$ is in RPM. Rearranging for $n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi \cdot D \cdot n &= V_c \\
n &= \frac{V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\
n &= \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D_{mm}} \quad \text{(to keep D in mm)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{300 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12} \\
&= \frac{300{,}000}{37.699} \\
&= 7957.7 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of 1000 converts meters to millimeters so the units cancel correctly (m/min ÷ mm = 1/min = RPM).

**Round to nearest controller-safe value:** $n = 8000$ RPM (S8000).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Feed Rate and MRR for Face Milling

### Given
- Cutter diameter: $D = 80$ mm (5-flute face mill)
- Spindle speed: $n = 600$ RPM
- Feed per tooth: $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth
- Depth of cut: $a_p = 3$ mm
- Width of cut: $a_e = 60$ mm

### Step 1: Feed Rate Calculation
$$\begin{aligned}
F &= n \times f_z \times Z \\
&= 600 \times 0.15 \times 5 \\
&= 450 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** $n$ (rev/min) × $f_z$ (mm/tooth) × $Z$ (teeth) = mm/min (each tooth advances $f_z$ mm per revolution, with $Z$ teeth engaged per revolution).

### Step 2: Material Removal Rate
$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= a_p \times a_e \times F \\
&= 3 \times 60 \times 450 \\
&= 81{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
&= 81 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Cross-sectional area engaged (mm²) × linear feed (mm/min) = volume removed per minute.

### Step 3: Power Requirement
For mild steel, $k_c = 2000$ N/mm², $\eta = 0.80$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{MRR \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta} \\
&= \frac{81{,}000 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.80} \\
&= \frac{1.62 \times 10^8}{4.8 \times 10^7} \\
&= 3.375 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Force (N) × distance (mm/min) = power (N·mm/min). Dividing by $60 \times 10^6$ converts to kW (1 kW = 60 × 10⁶ N·mm/min).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Tool Length Offset Compensation

### Problem
A part program has been written for **T01** (a 50 mm drill, master tool). You need to use **T02** (a 75 mm drill) at the same Z-depth. The H-offset for T02 was measured as $H02 = 25.0$ mm relative to T01.

### Concept
The tool length offset is the **difference in tip position** between any tool and the master tool. The controller subtracts this from the programmed Z so all tool tips reach the same Z-coordinate.

### Mathematical Model
$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{spindle} &= Z_{programmed} + (H_{tool} \times G43\_sign) \\
\end{aligned}$$

For **G43 (positive offset, +direction)**:
$$Z_{spindle} = Z_{programmed} + H_{offset}$$

### Numerical Example
Programmed depth: $Z = -10$ mm (10 mm below part top)
H02 value: $+25.0$ mm (T02 is 25 mm longer than T01)

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{spindle} &= -10 + 25 \\
&= +15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The spindle must be commanded to $+15$ mm in machine coordinates so that the *tip* of T02 sits at the desired $-10$ mm in work coordinates. The controller automatically handles this offset.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cutter Compensation Logic

### G41 vs G42 — How to Determine

| Tool Path Direction | Workpiece on... | Compensation | Code |
|---------------------|-----------------|--------------|------|
| Tool moves CCW around part | Right of tool | Left of tool | **G41** |
| Tool moves CW around part | Left of tool | Right of tool | **G42** |
| Climb milling (conventional) | — | Typically G41 | G41 |
| Conventional milling | — | Typically G42 | G42 |

> **Memory Trick:** "**41 Left, 42 Right**" — 1 < 2, so 41 = Left, 42 = Right.

### Lead-In / Lead-Out Requirement
Cutter compensation requires a **lead-in move** of at least the cutter radius for smooth engagement. This is a **2-line minimum**:
1. G41 (compensation activate)
2. Linear move of length ≥ D/2
3. Then the contour

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Tool Change Cycle Time

### Given
- 5 different tools in the program
- ATC tool-to-tool time: 4 sec
- Rapid traverse between tool changes: 1.5 sec
- Magazine index time per slot: 0.3 sec

### Calculation
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total\_tc} &= N_{changes} \times (T_{ATC} + T_{index} + T_{rapid}) \\
&= 5 \times (4 + 0.3 + 1.5) \\
&= 5 \times 5.8 \\
&= 29 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each tool change incurs a fixed mechanical cycle (ATC) + time to rotate the magazine to the next slot (index) + time to rapid back to cutting position.

---

## 3.6 KTU Common Mistake: Confusing G90 and G91

### Programmed Segment Analysis
**Same end point, different coding:**

**Absolute (G90) starting from X0 Y0:**
```
N10 G90 G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F100   (Move to 10,10)
N20 X30.0 Y30.0                  (Move to 30,30)
```

**Incremental (G91) starting from X0 Y0:**
```
N10 G91 G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F100   (Move +10 in X, +10 in Y → at 10,10)
N20 X20.0 Y20.0                  (Move +20 in X, +20 in Y → at 30,30)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G90 vs G91 is a MODAL group 03 setting.** Once set, it remains active until changed by the opposite code or reset. Most modern controllers default to **G90 (Absolute)** at power-on.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tool Holder Anatomy (Block-Level Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Spindle Nose] --> B[Taper Shank BT40/CAT40/HSK]
    B --> C[Flange / V-Flange Contact Face]
    C --> D[Tool Holder Body]
    D --> E[Collet ER32 / ER40]
    E --> F[Clamping Nut]
    D --> G[Drive Slots / Keyways]
    G --> H[Cutting Tool Shank Cylindrical]
    H --> I[Cutting Tool Cutting Edge]

    J[Drawbar / Retention Knob] -.Pulls taper into spindle.-> B
    K[Coolant Through / Center] -.Optional.-> D

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

### Node Description
- **A**: Spindle nose with mating taper, rotates at programmed RPM.
- **B**: Taper shank (BT/CAT/HSK) — friction-locked engagement.
- **C**: V-flange provides secondary contact for rigidity.
- **D-E-F**: Collet system clamps the cylindrical tool shank.
- **G**: Drive slots transmit torque (anti-rotation).
- **H-I**: Tool shank and cutting edge in contact with workpiece.
- **J**: Drawbar applies axial force to retain holder in spindle.
- **K**: Optional through-tool coolant supply.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between BT and HSK tool holder tapers. State two advantages of HSK over BT taper for high-speed machining.**

#### Model Answer (Key Points)

**BT Taper (MAS 403):**
- Single-contact taper (7/24 = 16.59° angle).
- Uses V-flange and drawbar for retention.
- As spindle speed increases, centrifugal force causes the spindle taper to *expand* (because the spindle is a hollow rotating mass), leading to loss of contact at the taper nose and reduced accuracy.

**HSK Taper (DIN 69893):**
- **Dual contact**: Taper surface + flange face simultaneously.
- Hollow shank design that *contracts* symmetrically with centrifugal force, maintaining contact and balance at high RPM.
- Better balance (typically G2.5 at 25,000 RPM).

**Two Advantages of HSK:**
1. **Higher rigidity and accuracy** at spindle speeds >15,000 RPM due to dual contact.
2. **Better dynamic balance** due to symmetric mass distribution (hollow shank); suitable for high-speed (>20,000 RPM) and 5-axis simultaneous machining.

## Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) system** of a CNC machining center. Discuss the **drum, chain, and turret-type tool magazines** with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **3-axis, 4-axis, and 5-axis configurations** of CNC machines. State the **right-hand rule** for axis identification as per ISO 841. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ATC System & Tool Magazine Types:**

**Working Principle of ATC:**
The Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) automatically exchanges tools between the spindle and the tool magazine based on **M06 T__** command. The system reduces non-productive time and enables unattended machining.

**Sequence of Operation:**
1. Controller decodes M06 and tool number T (e.g., T03).
2. Spindle is oriented to a fixed angular position (M19) so the **drive key** aligns with the tool change arm grip.
3. Z-axis rapid to **tool change position** (safe Z height).
4. Tool change arm (two-arm swing or single-arm gripper) rotates into position between spindle and magazine.
5. Spindle **unclamps** (drawbar retracts); arm grips old tool; arm swings and inserts new tool into spindle.
6. Drawbar clamps new tool; arm retracts; magazine indexes to next ready position.
7. Spindle returns to cutting position; program continues.

**Tool Magazine Types:**

| Type | Capacity | Tool Change Time | Application |
|------|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Drum / Carousel** | 12–30 | 1.5–3 sec | Small VMCs, tool rooms |
| **Chain** | 30–200+ | 3–8 sec | Horizontal machining centers, FMS |
| **Turret** | 8–12 | 0.4–1 sec | CNC turning centers (lathes) |

**Drum Magazine:** Tools arranged radially on a vertical or horizontal disc. The drum rotates to bring the requested tool opposite the spindle. Compact but limited capacity.

**Chain Magazine:** Tools mounted on a chain conveyor that can store 30–200+ tools. Suits high-mix production. Magazine can be loaded while machine is cutting (dual-arm systems).

**Turret Magazine:** Used exclusively on CNC lathes. The turret rotates (indexes) to bring a different tool to the cutting position in <1 second. Tools are pre-set in fixed pockets.

**(b) Axis Configurations & Right-Hand Rule:**

**Right-Hand Rule (ISO 841):**
- Point the **right-hand thumb** along the **+Z axis** (direction of greatest tool movement *away* from workpiece).
- The **index finger** points in the **+X direction** (longest linear travel).
- The **middle finger** points in the **+Y direction**.
- Rotary axes: **A = rotation about X**, **B = rotation about Y**, **C = rotation about Z** (all using right-hand rule with thumb along the axis, fingers curl in positive rotation).

**3-Axis Configuration (VMC):**
- Linear axes: X, Y, Z
- Tool moves in 3 linear directions; spindle orientation is fixed
- Application: Drilling, pocketing, 2.5D profiling
- Limitation: Cannot produce undercuts or complex sculpted surfaces

**4-Axis Configuration:**
- 3 linear (X, Y, Z) + 1 rotary (A or B)
- Typically realized by adding a **rotary table** (4th axis) on the X-Y bed
- Application: Cylindrical parts (cams, propellers), wrapping 2D profiles around cylinders
- Either **A** (table rotates about X) or **B** (table tilts about Y) is used

**5-Axis Configuration:**
- 3 linear + 2 rotary axes
- Two common arrangements:
  1. **Trunnion table** (A + C): Tilt + rotate — used for medium parts
  2. **Swivel head** (B + C): Spindle head tilts + rotates — used for large parts
- **Simultaneous 5-axis** enables tool to approach workpiece from any direction
- Application: Turbine blades, impellers, medical implants, aerospace structural parts
- **3+2 positioning** vs **true simultaneous 5-axis** distinction important for cycle time

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE OPTION **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **work coordinate system** (G54–G59) and **tool length offset** (G43/H-offset) in CNC programming with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **CNC part program** to drill **4 holes of Ø10 mm** on a plate of size **100 × 80 × 20 mm** at the corners (10 mm from each edge) using a Fanuc controller. Use absolute programming with G81 canned cycle. Assume suitable cutting data. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Work Coordinate System (G54–G59) and Tool Length Offset (G43/H):**

**Work Coordinate System (G54–G59):**
The WCS allows the programmer to define the **part zero** (origin) on the workpiece itself, rather than at the machine zero. The controller stores the **X, Y, Z distance** from the machine zero to the workpiece zero in the **G54–G59 offset registers**.

- **G54** = Work coordinate system 1
- **G55** = Work coordinate system 2
- **G56** = Work coordinate system 3
- **G57** = Work coordinate system 4
- **G58** = Work coordinate system 5
- **G59** = Work coordinate system 6

Setting is typically done by physically touching off the workpiece with the tool (or probe) and entering the values into the offset page. The advantage is that the same part can be **reproduced identically** on the same fixture without rewriting the program.

**Tool Length Offset (G43 / H-offset):**
Every tool has a different physical length. The controller must know each tool's length to position the **cutting tip** accurately. This is stored in the **H-offset register**:

- **H01** corresponds to T01
- **H02** corresponds to T02, etc.

The H-value is the **difference in length** from the master reference tool (typically the spindle face or first tool measured). When **G43 H01** is commanded, the controller adds H01 to every Z-axis command so the tool tip reaches the programmed Z-coordinate.

**Example:**
A drill program has Z = -15 mm (15 mm deep hole).
- T01 (50 mm drill) is master; H01 = 0
- T02 (75 mm drill) needs H02 = 25 mm (T02 is 25 mm longer)

Programming with T02:
```
N10 G43 H02 Z5.0   (Apply T02 length offset; rapid to safe Z)
N20 G01 Z-15.0 F80 (Drill to 15 mm depth — controller compensates automatically)
```

**Mathematical operation internally:**
$$Z_{machine} = Z_{programmed} + H_{offset} = -15 + 25 = +10 \text{ mm (machine coordinate)}$$

This means the spindle moves to +10 mm in machine coordinates so the *tip* of T02 reaches -15 mm in work coordinates.

**(b) CNC Part Program for 4-Hole Drilling:**

**Given:**
- Plate: 100 × 80 × 20 mm
- 4 holes Ø10 mm at corners, 10 mm inset from edges
- Hole positions in WCS: (10, 10), (90, 10), (10, 70), (90, 70)
- Tool: T01 = Ø10 mm HSS drill
- Cutting data: S = 800 RPM, F = 100 mm/min
- Assume tool length offset H01 = 100.0 mm

**Fanuc CNC Program:**

```
%
O0001 (4-HOLE DRILLING PROGRAM)
N10 G21 G90 G17 G54 (Metric, Absolute, XY plane, WCS-1)
N20 G91 G28 Z0.0    (Return to Z reference)
N30 T01 M06         (Tool change to T01 - 10mm drill)
N40 S800 M03        (Spindle CW at 800 RPM)
N50 G00 X10.0 Y10.0 (Rapid to first hole position)
N60 G43 H01 Z5.0    (Apply tool length comp, rapid to safe Z)
N70 M08             (Coolant ON)
N80 G99 G81 R2.0 Z-22.0 F100 (Drill cycle, R-point=2 above part, Z=-22 = 20+2)
N90 X90.0 Y10.0     (Hole 2)
N100 X90.0 Y70.0    (Hole 3)
N110 X10.0 Y70.0    (Hole 4)
N120 G80            (Cancel drill cycle)
N130 G00 Z100.0     (Retract to safe Z)
N140 M09            (Coolant OFF)
N150 G91 G28 Z0.0   (Return to Z ref)
N160 G91 G28 X0.0 Y0.0 (Return to XY ref)
N170 M05            (Spindle OFF)
N180 M30            (Program end + rewind)
%
```

**Code Explanation:**
- **N80**: G81 is the **drilling canned cycle**; R2.0 is the **R-plane** (2 mm above part top); Z-22.0 is the **final drill depth** (20 mm plate + 2 mm breakthrough for clean exit); F100 is feed in mm/min.
- **G99** = return to R-plane between holes (faster than G98 which returns to initial plane).
- **N90–N110**: Modal motion commands; since G81 is modal, only XY positions are needed for subsequent holes.
- **G80** cancels the canned cycle so subsequent moves don't trigger another drill.
- **M30** ends the program and rewinds to start.

---

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board 2024 Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing G90/G91**: Students mix absolute and incremental coordinates in the same block. **Loss: 2 marks** per program error.
> 
> 2. **Missing G80 after canned cycle**: Forgetting to cancel G81–G89 leads to unintended drilling on next motion. **Loss: 3 marks**.
> 
> 3. **G43 missing H-offset**: Writing `G43 Z5.0` without `H__` causes an alarm. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing G41/G42**: "41 = Left, 42 = Right" — get this backwards = **-3 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Wrong plane selection**: Using G02/G03 without G17/G18/G19 — the controller doesn't know which plane to circularly interpolate. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 
> 6. **Spindle commands before tool change**: Forgetting M03/M04 spindle start or S-value leads to spindle not rotating. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 
> 7. **M30 vs M02**: Only **M30 rewinds** the program. M02 stops without rewinding. The examiner often specifically checks for M30. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 
> 8. **No coolant command in drilling**: Drilling without M08 (or M07) on steel is considered unsafe and may lose marks for incomplete program.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔧 Tool Holders
- ☐ **BT, CAT, HSK** are the three main taper standards; HSK is best for high-speed.
- ☐ **Drawbar** mechanically pulls the holder into the spindle for positive retention.
- ☐ **Runout** < 0.01 mm is the standard for precision holders.
- ☐ Collet systems (ER) provide wide clamping range with high grip force.

### 🔄 Tool Indexing
- ☐ **M06 T__** initiates automatic tool change.
- ☐ **ATC** consists of: magazine, arm, spindle orientation, drawbar, controller.
- ☐ Tool change time: drum (fast, small) < chain (slow, large) < turret (fastest, lathe).
- ☐ **Tool-to-tool** vs **chip-to-chip** time distinction.

### 🧭 Axis Configuration
- ☐ **Right-hand rule**: Thumb = +Z, Index = +X, Middle = +Y.
- ☐ **A, B, C** = rotations about **X, Y, Z** respectively.
- ☐ **5-axis simultaneous** is required for sculpted surfaces (turbines).
- ☐ **3+2 positioning** is faster but less flexible than true 5-axis.

### 🔢 CNC Codes
- ☐ **G00** = rapid; **G01** = linear feed; **G02/G03** = circular CW/CCW.
- ☐ **G17/G18/G19** = plane selection; **G90/G91** = absolute/incremental.
- ☐ **G43** = tool length comp +; **G44** = tool length comp − (rarely used).
- ☐ **G41/G42** = cutter radius comp left/right; **G40** = cancel.
- ☐ **G54–G59** = work coordinate systems.
- ☐ **M03/M04/M05** = spindle CW/CCW/OFF; **M06** = tool change; **M08/M09** = coolant.
- ☐ **M30** = program end + rewind; **M00/M01** = stop / optional stop.

### 📏 Datum & Offsets
- ☐ **Machine zero (M)** = fixed by limit switches.
- ☐ **Work zero (W)** = set on the part (G54–G59).
- ☐ **Tool length offset** stored in **H-register**; activated by **G43 H__**.
- ☐ **Cutter radius offset** stored in **D-register**; activated by **G41/G42 D__**.
- ☐ Always perform **tool length measurement** on a presetter or at a known Z.

### ⚙️ KTU Numerical Must-Knows
- ☐ $n = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ RPM formula.
- ☐ $F = n \times f_z \times Z$ feed rate.
- ☐ $MRR = a_p \times a_e \times F$ (milling).
- ☐ $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \eta}$ power.
- ☐ $T_c = \dfrac{L}{F}$ cutting time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental and Absolute Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incremental and Absolute Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) part programming, the specification of tool position coordinates on the workpiece is governed by one of **two fundamental coordinate referencing systems**: **Absolute Programming** (also called *Incremental Coordinate System Mode OFF*, G90) and **Incremental Programming** (also called *Incremental Coordinate System Mode ON*, G91).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Programming (G90):** All coordinate values are referenced from a **single, fixed, pre-established origin** (the program zero point / workpiece zero point). Every commanded position is defined as a distance from this *one* absolute zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental Programming (G91):** All coordinate values are referenced from the **current tool position**. Each commanded position defines the *move distance* (signed displacement) from the *immediately preceding* tool position.

The choice between the two is made by issuing the preparatory function codes:
- **G90** → Activates Absolute Mode
- **G91** → Activates Incremental Mode

These G-codes are **modal**, meaning once activated, they remain in effect for all subsequent blocks until the other code (or a machine reset) is issued.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a friend in a city:

**Absolute Programming = "GPS Coordinates"**
You tell your friend: *"Go to Latitude 10.0261° N, Longitude 76.3125° E."*
Every destination you mention is referenced from the **same global origin** (the equator/prime meridian). No matter where your friend currently is, the coordinate **uniquely identifies** the spot.

**Incremental Programming = "Turn-by-Turn Navigation"**
You tell your friend: *"From where you are now, go 500 m straight, then turn right and go 200 m, then turn left and go 100 m."*
Every instruction is **relative to the current position** — distances and directions, not global addresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Think of absolute programming like mailing a letter using a postal address (always relative to the post office/origin). Incremental programming is like giving walking instructions: "10 steps forward, 5 steps right." Both get you there, but the *math* required by the CNC controller differs drastically.

## 1.3 Key Parameters (Standards)

- **Standard Reference Point:** Workpiece Zero (W) or Program Zero (set by G92 on older controls or automatic on modern ones).
- **Modal Nature:** Both G90 and G91 are **modal G-codes** belonging to **Group 03** on most controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Haas).
- **Default on Power-On:** Varies by controller — Fanuc defaults to **Absolute (G90)**, Siemens 840D defaults to **Absolute**.
- **Sign Convention:** In incremental mode, **positive (+) = direction of axis travel**, **negative (−) = opposite direction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Absolute Programming (G90) — Logic Steps

1. **Establish Origin:** The programmer (or operator) sets a single fixed datum — the **Workpiece Zero Point (W)** — on the part. For a turned component, this is typically at the **center of the finished diameter on the front face**. For a milled part, it is usually at the **lower-left corner of the workpiece** (or center of a hole).
2. **Measure From Zero:** Every X, Y, Z coordinate in every block is the **true distance from W**, not from the previous tool position.
3. **Cumulative Tracking:** The controller internally tracks tool position, but the *programmer* does **not** need to track it — coordinates are self-contained and self-checking.
4. **Block Independence:** Any single block can be read in isolation; its coordinates are *always* valid.

### 2.1.2 Incremental Programming (G91) — Logic Steps

1. **No Fixed Origin Needed (in Program):** Coordinates describe the **move vector** (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ) from the tool's *current* location.
2. **Sequential Dependency:** The current block's coordinates are **meaningless without the prior block's tool position**.
3. **Negative for Reverse Travel:** To move the tool in the negative axis direction, the programmer writes a **negative** dimension.
4. **Use Case:** Extremely useful for **repetitive patterns** (drilling a bolt-hole circle, tapping multiple holes) where the *spacing* is constant.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Concept

| Aspect | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference Datum** | Fixed workpiece zero (W) | Previous tool position (current) |
| **Programming Effort** | Easy for irregular shapes | Easy for equal-spacing patterns |
| **Error Propagation** | A typo affects only that block | A typo **carries forward** to all subsequent blocks |
| **Block-to-Block** | Independent | Sequential (cumulative) |
| **Coordinate Sign** | Always positive from W (typically) | Sign indicates *direction* of travel |
| **Tool Offset Handling** | Cleaner; offsets applied once | Can become complex with tool length |
| **Debugging** | Easier (read coordinates directly on DRO) | Harder (need to integrate positions) |
| **Typical Use** | Turning, complex contours, prismatic milling | Bolt-hole circles, peck drilling, repetitive subroutines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In **CNC turning of shafts**, absolute programming is the de facto standard because the X-diameter values are read directly from the engineering drawing. In **PCB drilling or flange bolt patterns**, incremental programming excels because every hole is the same ΔX, ΔY from the previous one.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{abs}(i) = X_W + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta X_k$ | Cumulative absolute position equals initial offset + sum of incremental moves | mm |
| 2 | $\Delta X_i = X_{abs}(i) - X_{abs}(i-1)$ | Incremental move equals difference of consecutive absolute positions | mm |
| 3 | $\Delta X_i = X_{abs}(i) - X_{abs}(i-1)$ | Same for Y-axis | mm |
| 4 | $\Delta Z_i = Z_{abs}(i) - Z_{abs}(i-1)$ | Same for Z-axis | mm |
| 5 | $L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \Delta X_i \vert$ (using `\vert`) | Total path length along X in incremental mode | mm |
| 6 | $D_{hole} = \sqrt{\Delta X^2 + \Delta Y^2}$ | Bolt-circle radial distance per hole (incremental) | mm |
| 7 | $D_{hole} = \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}$ | Bolt-circle radial distance (absolute, from center) | mm |
| 8 | Modal rule | G90/G91 remains active until the other is commanded | — |

## 2.4 Mathematical Relationship Between the Two Systems

If a tool travels through points $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$, then the conversion between the two systems is:

$$
X_{abs}(P_n) = X_{abs}(P_0) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta X_i
$$

and the inverse conversion:

$$
\Delta X_n = X_{abs}(P_n) - X_{abs}(P_{n-1})
$$

These two relations are the **exact mathematical basis** for converting programs written in one mode to the other — a frequent KTU examination question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Square Pocket (4 Points)

A tool must visit four points to mill a square pocket on a milling machine:
- **Point A** = (10, 10) mm
- **Point B** = (60, 10) mm
- **Point C** = (60, 60) mm
- **Point D** = (10, 60) mm

Starting position = **(0, 0) mm**. Generate both the **absolute (G90)** and **incremental (G91)** program blocks for the X-Y moves.

### 3.1.1 Absolute Program (G90)

In absolute mode, every coordinate is written from the fixed zero (0,0).

| Block | N | G | X | Y | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N10 | G90 G00 | X10.0 | Y10.0 | Rapid to point A |
| 2 | N20 | G01 | X60.0 | Y10.0 | Linear cut to B |
| 3 | N30 | G01 | X60.0 | Y60.0 | Linear cut to C |
| 4 | N40 | G01 | X10.0 | Y60.0 | Linear cut to D |
| 5 | N50 | G01 | X10.0 | Y10.0 | Return to A |

### 3.1.2 Incremental Program (G91)

For each block, compute $\Delta X = X_{new} - X_{old}$ and $\Delta Y = Y_{new} - Y_{old}$.

**Block N10** (0,0) → (10,10):
- $\Delta X = 10 - 0 = +10$
- $\Delta Y = 10 - 0 = +10$

**Block N20** (10,10) → (60,10):
- $\Delta X = 60 - 10 = +50$
- $\Delta Y = 10 - 10 = 0$

**Block N30** (60,10) → (60,60):
- $\Delta X = 60 - 60 = 0$
- $\Delta Y = 60 - 10 = +50$

**Block N40** (60,60) → (10,60):
- $\Delta X = 10 - 60 = -50$
- $\Delta Y = 60 - 60 = 0$

**Block N50** (10,60) → (10,10):
- $\Delta X = 10 - 10 = 0$
- $\Delta Y = 10 - 60 = -50$

| Block | N | G | X | Y | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N10 | G91 G00 | X10.0 | Y10.0 | Move +10, +10 |
| 2 | N20 | G01 | X50.0 | Y0 | Move +50 along X |
| 3 | N30 | G01 | X0 | Y50.0 | Move +50 along Y |
| 4 | N40 | G01 | X-50.0 | Y0 | Move −50 along X |
| 5 | N50 | G01 | X0 | Y-50.0 | Move −50 along Y |

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Conversion Derivation

**Problem:** A hole is to be drilled at absolute position (40, 30) from workpiece zero. The drill bit is currently at (10, 10). Find the incremental move.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Identify the current absolute position of the tool.
$$
X_{abs}(P_{old}) = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad Y_{abs}(P_{old}) = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the target absolute position.
$$
X_{abs}(P_{new}) = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad Y_{abs}(P_{new}) = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the incremental conversion formula.

$$
\Delta X = X_{abs}(P_{new}) - X_{abs}(P_{old})
$$

$$
\Delta X = 40 - 10 = +30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Y = Y_{abs}(P_{new}) - Y_{abs}(P_{old})
$$

$$
\Delta Y = 30 - 10 = +20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Write the incremental block.

```
N30 G91 G00 X30.0 Y20.0
```

**Verification:** Starting at (10, 10), adding (30, 20) yields (40, 30). ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bolt-Hole Circle (6 Holes, Incremental Advantage)

A circular flange has **6 equally spaced holes** on a bolt circle of diameter **100 mm**, centered at (50, 50). The starting tool position is at the **first hole (12 o'clock position)**. Compare program sizes.

The first hole (top, 12 o'clock) is at:
- $X_1 = 50 + 50 \cos(90°) = 50$
- $Y_1 = 50 + 50 \sin(90°) = 100$

Each subsequent hole is **60°** further around. The incremental chord distance is:
$$
\Delta = 2 R \sin(\pi / 6) = 2 (50) \sin(30°) = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

Since all 5 transitions between adjacent holes have the **same incremental distance**, the G91 program uses **the same block 5 times** — a sub-routine call or a simple repeated block. The G90 program requires **6 different absolute coordinates** (must compute each with trig).

This is a textbook KTU 2-mark or 7-mark example demonstrating **when G91 is the better engineering choice**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Coordinate Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Program Start] --> B[Read Current Modal State]
    B --> C{G90 or G91 Active?}
    C -- G90 --> D[Interpret X, Y, Z as distances from Workpiece Zero W]
    C -- G91 --> E[Interpret X, Y, Z as deltas from Current Tool Position]
    D --> F[Controller Sets New Tool Position absolute = X, Y, Z]
    E --> G[Controller Sets New Tool Position = Old + Delta]
    F --> H[Execute Motion Block]
    G --> H[Execute Motion Block]
    H --> I{Next Block G90 or G91?}
    I -- Same Mode --> C
    I -- Mode Change --> J[Update Modal Flag]
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 Visual Comparison: Absolute vs Incremental Path Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    O[Workpiece Zero W 0,0] -. Absolute Reference .-> A1
    O -. Absolute Reference .-> A2
    O -. Absolute Reference .-> A3
    O -. Absolute Reference .-> A4
    A1[Point A 10,10] -- deltaX=+50, deltaY=0 --> A2[Point B 60,10]
    A2 -- deltaX=0, deltaY=+50 --> A3[Point C 60,60]
    A3 -- deltaX=-50, deltaY=0 --> A4[Point D 10,60]
    A4 -- deltaX=0, deltaY=-50 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dotted lines (left side) emanate from a **single fixed origin W**, illustrating absolute references. The solid arrows (right side) flow **sequentially from point to point**, illustrating incremental references.

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to choose G90 or G91?] --> Q1{Is the part geometry\nmade of equally-spaced\nrepeated features?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Inc[Use G91 Incremental\nSimpler sub-routine/repeat]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are the dimensions\nread directly from\nthe engineering drawing?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Abs[Use G90 Absolute\nMatches drawing directly]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the tool path long\nand prone to cumulative error?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Abs
    Q3 -- No --> Inc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between absolute and incremental programming in CNC. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Fixed workpiece zero (W) | Previous tool position |
| Coordinate type | True position | Move vector (Δ) |
| Block dependency | Independent | Sequential |
| Error effect | Local to the block | Cumulative |
| Best for | Irregular contours, turning | Repetitive patterns, peck cycles |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for any two valid differences)**

### Q2. What is the function of G90 and G91 preparatory codes? State one application of each. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Model Answer:**
- **G90:** Activates **absolute coordinate system** mode — all subsequent X, Y, Z values are measured from the workpiece zero point. **Application:** CNC turning of stepped shafts where X-diameters are read from the drawing.
- **G91:** Activates **incremental coordinate system** mode — all subsequent X, Y, Z values are signed distances from the current tool position. **Application:** Drilling bolt-hole circles where the spacing between holes is constant.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the absolute and incremental programming systems used in CNC part programming with suitable block diagrams. List four advantages and two limitations of each system. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(b)** A milling cutter is required to machine the outline of the triangular profile with vertices at (20,20), (80,20), and (50,70). The tool starts at (0,0). Write the complete part program in:
- (i) Absolute programming mode **(3.5 marks)**
- (ii) Incremental programming mode **(3.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Absolute (G90):** Coordinates referenced from fixed datum (0,0). Programmer reads values directly from drawing. Error in one block does not affect others. Easy debugging with DRO.

**Incremental (G91):** Coordinates are move vectors from current position. Sequential dependency — a mistake compounds. Efficient for repetitive geometry.

**Absolute advantages (any 4):**
1. Coordinates match drawing — easy programming
2. No error accumulation
3. Blocks are self-contained, easy to edit
4. Tool offsets and work offsets integrate cleanly

**Incremental advantages (any 4):**
1. Repetitive patterns coded once
2. Sub-routine friendly
3. Useful when datum is hard to define
4. Smaller numbers for small relative moves

**Limitations (2 each — any 2):**
- *Absolute:* Long coordinate values, requires precise datum setting.
- *Incremental:* Cumulative errors, sequential dependency, harder to debug.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Vertices in order:** V1 = (20,20), V2 = (80,20), V3 = (50,70), return to V1.

**(i) Absolute Program (G90):**

```
O0001
N10 G90 G21 G17          (Absolute, metric, XY plane)
N20 G00 X20.0 Y20.0      (Rapid to V1)
N30 G01 Z-2.0 F100       (Plunge)
N40 X80.0 Y20.0 F150     (Cut to V2)
N50 X50.0 Y70.0          (Cut to V3)
N60 X20.0 Y20.0          (Return to V1)
N70 G00 Z5.0             (Retract)
N80 M30                  (End)
```

**(ii) Incremental Program (G91):**

Compute ΔX, ΔY for each move:

- Start (0,0) → V1 (20,20): ΔX = +20, ΔY = +20
- V1 → V2: ΔX = 80−20 = +60, ΔY = 20−20 = 0
- V2 → V3: ΔX = 50−80 = −30, ΔY = 70−20 = +50
- V3 → V1: ΔX = 20−50 = −30, ΔY = 20−70 = −50

```
O0002
N10 G91 G21 G17          (Incremental, metric, XY plane)
N20 G00 X20.0 Y20.0      (Move to V1)
N30 G01 Z-2.0 F100       (Plunge, delta Z)
N40 X60.0 Y0 F150        (V1 → V2)
N50 X-30.0 Y50.0         (V2 → V3)
N60 X-30.0 Y-50.0        (V3 → V1)
N70 G00 Z5.0             (Retract)
N80 M30                  (End)
```

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain how a bolt-hole circle of 8 holes on a 120 mm diameter (PCD) is drilled using incremental programming. The center of the PCD is at (60, 60) and the tool starts above the first hole at 12 o'clock position. List the program blocks. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(b)** A CNC lathe turns a stepped shaft. The tool starts at the workpiece zero (X0, Z2). The sequence of diameters to be turned (in X, with Z staying at 0 for the facing pass) is: X30 → X50 → X40 → X60 at Z = 0. Write the program in:
- (i) Absolute mode **(3.5 marks)**
- (ii) Incremental mode **(3.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Setup:** 8 holes, PCD = 120 mm → R = 60 mm, center (60, 60).
**First hole (12 o'clock):** $(X_1, Y_1) = (60, 60 + 60) = (60, 120)$.
**Angular step:** $\theta = 360°/8 = 45°$.
**Chord length (incremental Δ between adjacent holes):**

$$
\Delta = 2 R \sin(\theta/2) = 2 (60) \sin(22.5°) \approx 45.89 \text{ mm}
$$

**Program (G91 Incremental):**

```
O0003
N10 G91 G00 X0 Y60.0         (Move from center to first hole, +Y)
N20 G81 R2 Z-10 F80          (Drill first hole)
N30 G00 X45.89 Y-22.94       (Move to next hole — chord Δx, Δy)
N40 G80 G00 X... repeat 7 times
... (or use sub-routine / polar interpolation)
N50 M30
```

The Δ values for each 45° step are:
- $(\Delta X, \Delta Y)$ per step: $(+45.89, -22.94)$ for the first move; subsequent steps use the same pattern because the geometry is **rotationally invariant** (only the orientation changes — for proper incremental, all chord magnitudes are equal).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why incremental programming **shines** in repetitive, equally-spaced patterns.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Stepped shaft turning — sequence:** Move 1: X30, Move 2: X50, Move 3: X40, Move 4: X60 (all at Z0).

**(i) Absolute (G90):**

```
O0004
N10 G90 G21 G18              (Absolute, metric, XZ plane)
N20 G00 X30.0 Z0             (Rapid to X30 Z0)
N30 G01 Z-50.0 F200          (Plunge/turn)
N40 G00 X51.0                (Retract slightly)
N50 G00 Z2.0                 (Return)
N60 G00 X50.0 Z0             (Rapid to X50 Z0)
N70 G01 Z-50.0 F200
N80 G00 X51.0
N90 G00 Z2.0
N100 G00 X40.0 Z0
... (similar for X40 and X60)
N200 M30
```

**(ii) Incremental (G91):**

Starting at (X0, Z2). To reach X30, Z0: ΔX = +30, ΔZ = −2.
Each subsequent X move: ΔX = (X_new − X_old).

```
O0005
N10 G91 G21 G18
N20 G00 X30.0 Z-2.0          (Start → X30 Z0)
N30 G01 Z-50.0 F200          (Turn)
N40 G00 X1.0                 (Retract +1 mm)
N50 G00 Z52.0                (Retract Z back to start)
N60 X20.0 Z-52.0             (X30→X50 = +20, Z back to 0)
N70 G01 Z-50.0 F200
N80 G00 X1.0
N90 G00 Z52.0
N100 X-10.0 Z-52.0           (X50→X40 = −10)
... (and so on)
N200 M30
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in G91:** Writing +50 instead of −50 for a leftward move is the #1 mistake. Always sketch the arrow direction.
> 2. **Mixing G90/G91 inconsistently:** Once a mode is set, it stays modal — do not suddenly switch coordinates in mid-block without re-issuing the G-code.
> 3. **Forgetting to declare the mode in the first block:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the very first motion block does not have G90 or G91 explicitly stated.
> 4. **Forgetting tool-position context in G91:** Writing an incremental program without specifying the start point loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the F (feedrate) on cutting moves** in G01 — G00 is the only block allowed without a feedrate.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **G90 = Absolute** (from fixed workpiece zero W); **G91 = Incremental** (from current tool position).
- Both are **modal Group 03** codes — they remain in effect until cancelled.
- **Conversion formula:** $\Delta X_i = X_{abs}(i) - X_{abs}(i-1)$.
- **Absolute programming is preferred** for: turning, irregular contours, parts with dimensions directly read from a drawing.
- **Incremental programming is preferred** for: repetitive patterns (bolt-hole circles, peck drilling), when datum is hard to set.
- **Errors are local in G90** but **cumulative in G91** — a single typo can ruin the entire part in incremental mode.
- **Sign convention in G91:** Positive = direction of axis travel; negative = opposite direction.
- **Always declare the mode** in the first motion block of the program for clarity (good programming practice & examiner-friendly).
- **DRO (Digital Readout) debugging** is straightforward in G90 (just compare with drawing); in G91, you must mentally integrate all prior moves.
- **Sub-routines and macros** often exploit incremental moves to parameterize repetitive geometry with a single block.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Canned Cycles, Practical Programming (Simple) G
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Canned Cycles, Practical Programming (Simple) G

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Canned Cycle (Fixed Cycle):** A *canned cycle* is a single G-code command (or a small block of codes) that invokes a pre-programmed, standardized sequence of machine tool motions stored permanently in the CNC controller's firmware. It consolidates several repetitive positioning and machining moves—**approach → feed → retract**—into **one block** of code, drastically simplifying programs for hole-making, threading, and similar repetitive operations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PERUT412 – CNC Machines**), canned cycles are classified under **ISO 6983 (G-Code) standards** and are categorized as:

| Category | G-Code Group | Function |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| **Hole-Making Cycles** | G81 – G89 | Drilling, tapping, boring |
| **Lathe Cycles** | G70 – G76 | Roughing, finishing, threading |
| **Initialization** | G98, G99 | Return-plane control |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Photographer's Preset Button"

Imagine you are a **wedding photographer**. Instead of manually setting *aperture, shutter speed, ISO, white balance, focus mode* for every single indoor shot, you press a single **"Indoor Preset"** button. The camera instantly configures all settings optimally.

Similarly, instead of writing **5–7 lines of code** for every hole (rapid to clearance → feed to depth → dwell → retract), you write **one G81 block**. The CNC controller's *built-in macro* (the "preset") executes the entire sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A canned cycle remains **active (modal)** once called, meaning it will be repeated at every subsequent X-Y position until cancelled with **G80** or replaced by another G-code. This is a common source of errors in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Anatomy of a Canned Cycle Block

A standard canned cycle declaration contains **four** mandatory positional parameters:

$$
\underbrace{G81}_{\text{Cycle}} \; \underbrace{X\_ \; Y\_}_{\text{Hole Position}} \; \underbrace{Z\_}_{\text{Depth (Z-target)}} \; \underbrace{R\_}_{\text{Rapid Plane}} \; \underbrace{F\_}_{\text{Feedrate}}
$$

| Parameter | Meaning | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **X, Y** | Hole center coordinates | **mm** |
| **Z** | Final depth of cut (Z-target) | **mm** |
| **R** | Rapid plane (clearance above workpiece) | **mm** |
| **F** | Cutting feedrate | **mm/min** |

## 1.4 Return-Level Logic: G98 vs G99

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G98** — Tool returns to the **Initial Level** (the Z position before the cycle started).
> **G99** — Tool returns to the **R-plane** (rapid clearance plane).
> **Default** in most CNC controls is **G99** (more efficient, shorter traverse).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Canned Cycles — The "Big Nine" (G81 – G89)

The following table forms the **high-yield backbone** for KTU Module 2 questions:

| G-Code | Operation | Motion at Bottom | Spindle | Application |
|--------|-----------|------------------|---------|-------------|
| **G81** | Drilling | Rapid retract | CW | Simple shallow holes |
| **G82** | Drilling + Dwell | Rapid retract | CW | Counter-bore / spot-face |
| **G83** | **Peck Drilling** | Rapid retract | CW | Deep holes (>3×D) |
| **G84** | Tapping (RH) | Feed retract | **CCW** (reverses) | Threaded holes |
| **G85** | Boring | **Feed retract** | CW | Reamed / finished holes |
| **G86** | Boring + Spindle Stop | Rapid retract | **Stops at bottom** | Pre-bore for finish |
| **G87** | Back Boring | Manual | CW | Counter-bore from rear |
| **G88** | Boring + Manual Retract | Manual | CW | Precision boring |
| **G89** | Boring + Dwell | Feed retract | CW | Fine-finish bore |

> [!NOTE]
> The downward arrow ↓ indicates **spindle rotation reversal at hole bottom** for tapping (G84) — a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 2.2 Peck Drilling (G83) — Special Deep-Hole Logic

For **deep holes** (depth > 3 × drill diameter), continuous drilling causes:
- Chip clogging in flutes
- Excessive heat generation
- Tool breakage

**G83** breaks the total depth **Z** into multiple **pecks of depth `Q`**, retracting fully (or to R-plane) after each peck to **evacuate chips**.

$$
\boxed{Z_{\text{total}} = n \times Q + Q_{\text{last}}}
$$

where:
- $Z_{\text{total}}$ = total hole depth
- $n$ = number of full pecks
- $Q$ = peck depth per increment
- $Q_{\text{last}}$ = remaining depth of final peck

**Block Format:**
$$
\underbrace{G83}_{\text{Peck Drill}} \; X\_ \; Y\_ \; Z\_ \; R\_ \; Q\_ \; F\_
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Cutting Speed | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{V \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle Speed | rpm |
| 3 | $f_z = F / (N \cdot Z_t)$ | Feed per tooth | mm/tooth |
| 4 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{\vert Z \vert + R}{F_{\text{rapid}}} + \dfrac{\vert Z - R \vert}{F} + t_{\text{dwell}}$ | Time per hole | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{holes}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Total cycle time | seconds |
| 6 | $Z_{\text{peck count}} = \lceil Z_{\text{total}} / Q \rceil$ | Number of pecks | integer |
| 7 | $F_{\text{tap}} = N \times P_{\text{thread}}$ | Tapping feedrate | mm/min |
| 8 | $MRR = f \times a_p \times V_f$ | Material Removal Rate | mm³/min |

**Notation key:** $D$ = drill diameter, $N$ = rpm, $Z_t$ = number of teeth, $f$ = feed/rev, $a_p$ = depth of cut, $V_f$ = feed velocity, $P_{\text{thread}}$ = thread pitch.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Canned cycles are the **backbone of mass production** in:
- **Aerospace:** Thousands of rivet holes in wing skins
- **Automotive:** Engine block, gearbox housing machining
- **Die & Mold:** Deep cooling channels in injection molds
- **PCB Manufacturing:** Micro-drilling on CNC routers

The productivity gain over manual G00/G01 coding is typically **60–75%** in program length reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Time Calculation for a 4-Hole Pattern

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC milling machine must drill **4 holes** of depth **20 mm** in a 100 × 80 mm steel plate. The drill is **Ø8 mm HSS**, cutting speed **V = 25 m/min**, feed **f = 0.10 mm/rev**. The rapid plane **R = 2 mm** above the workpiece top (Z = 0). Rapid traverse = **3000 mm/min**, cutting feed = **F = N × f**.

**Find:** (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) Time per hole, (iv) Total cycle time.

---

### Step 1: Calculate Spindle Speed (N)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{V \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 8} \\
&= \frac{25000}{25.1327} \\
N &\approx \textbf{994.72 rpm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{use } N = 1000 \text{ rpm (controller round-up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The spindle speed is rounded to the nearest available controller value. This is standard KTU practice.

---

### Step 2: Calculate Feed Rate (F)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= N \times f \\
&= 1000 \times 0.10 \\
F &= \textbf{100 mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **modal feedrate** used inside the canned cycle for the Z-feed motion.

---

### Step 3: Time per Hole (T_cycle)

The G81 cycle has **three distinct motion phases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= \underbrace{\frac{R}{F_{\text{rapid}}}}_{\text{Rapid down to R}} + \underbrace{\frac{\vert Z - R \vert}{F}}_{\text{Cutting feed to depth}} + \underbrace{\frac{R}{F_{\text{rapid}}}}_{\text{Retract to R-plane}} \\
&= \frac{2}{3000} + \frac{\vert -20 - 2 \vert}{100} + \frac{20}{3000} \\
&= 0.000667 + 0.220 + 0.006667 \\
T_{\text{cycle}} &= \textbf{0.2273 seconds/hole}
\end{aligned}
$$

The dominant time component is the **cutting feed (97% of cycle time)** — this is why optimizing F is critical in production.

---

### Step 4: Total Cycle Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= n_{\text{holes}} \times T_{\text{cycle}} \\
&= 4 \times 0.2273 \\
T_{\text{total}} &= \textbf{0.909 seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Excludes tool changes, positioning between holes, and table clamping — those are added in Module 3 economics.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Peck Drilling G83 Calculation

**Problem:** Drill a **80 mm deep hole** (Ø10 mm) in mild steel using **G83** with peck depth **Q = 15 mm**. R-plane = 5 mm.

**Step 1 — Number of pecks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{pecks}} &= \lceil Z_{\text{total}} / Q \rceil \\
&= \lceil 80 / 15 \rceil \\
&= \lceil 5.33 \rceil = 6 \text{ pecks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Depth of final peck:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{last}} &= Z_{\text{total}} - (n_{\text{peck}} - 1) \times Q \\
&= 80 - 5 \times 15 \\
&= 80 - 75 = 5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Program excerpt (FANUC style):**

```
N10  G90 G99 G83 X0 Y0 Z-80.0 R5.0 Q15.0 F120.0
N20  X40.0 Y0              ; Repeats G83 at new XY
N30  G80                   ; Cancel canned cycle
```

**Verification of pecking logic:**

| Peck # | Cumulative depth (mm) | Action |
|--------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | 0 → −15 | Drill, retract to R |
| 2 | 0 → −30 | Drill, retract to R |
| 3 | 0 → −45 | Drill, retract to R |
| 4 | 0 → −60 | Drill, retract to R |
| 5 | 0 → −75 | Drill, retract to R |
| 6 (final) | 0 → **−80** | Drill to Z, retract |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **G83 (full retract)**, the drill retracts to the R-plane between pecks for maximum chip evacuation. In **G73 (high-speed peck)**, it retracts only **1 mm** — used for shallow holes where retraction time is expensive.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cycle Selection Logic

| Condition | Recommended Cycle | Justification |
