|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Hole depth < 2×D | **G81** | No chip issue; fastest |
| Hole needs flat bottom | **G82** | Dwell ensures clean floor |
| Hole depth > 3×D | **G83** | Chip evacuation critical |
| Threaded hole | **G84** | Spindle reverses for retract |
| Finishing bore | **G85** | Feed-retract prevents tool marks |
| Precision finish bore | **G86/G89** | Spindle orient + dwell |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Canned Cycle Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Program Start] --> initA[Initialize: G90 G80 G17 G99]
    initA --> posA[Position to X-Y hole center]
    posA --> cyleA{Canned Cycle Active?}
    cyleA -- Yes, G81 --> rapidA[Rapid down to R-plane]
    rapidA --> feedA[Feed to Z-depth at F]
    feedA --> retractA[Retract per cycle logic]
    retractA --> moreHoles{More X-Y positions?}
    moreHoles -- Yes --> posA
    moreHoles -- No --> cancelA[G80: Cancel cycle]
    cancelA --> endA[Continue with G00/G01]
    cyleA -- No --> endA
    style startA fill:#e1f5e1
    style endA fill:#ffe1e1
    style rapidA fill:#fff4cc
    style feedA fill:#fff4cc
    style retractA fill:#fff4cc
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: G83 Peck Drilling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    callB[G83 Activated with Q value] --> checkB{Z-depth reached?}
    checkB -- No --> downB[Rapid to R-plane]
    downB --> peckB[Feed down by Q amount]
    peckB --> evacB[Retract to R-plane, chip cleared]
    evacB --> checkB
    checkB -- Yes --> finalB[Final partial peck to Z]
    finalB --> retractB[Retract to R or initial level]
    retractB --> nextB[Move to next X-Y position]
    nextB --> callB
    style callB fill:#cce5ff
    style evacB fill:#ffcc99
    style finalB fill:#c2f0c2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cycle Category Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[CNC G-Codes] --> branchA[Modal Canned Cycles]
    branchA --> g1A[G81: Simple Drill]
    branchA --> g2A[G82: Drill with Dwell]
    branchA --> g3A[G83: Peck Drill]
    branchA --> g4A[G84: Tapping]
    branchA --> g5A[G85-G89: Boring Family]
    branchA --> cancelA[G80: Cancel]
    g3A --> g3bA[G73: High Speed Peck]
    g4A --> g4bA[Spindle Reverse at Bottom]
    style rootA fill:#d4edda
    style branchA fill:#fff3cd
    style cancelA fill:#f8d7da
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram reading tip for exams:** When a KTU question asks to "explain the working of any canned cycle with a flowchart," the structure above is the **blueprint** — adapt labels to whichever cycle (G81/G82/G83/G84) the question specifies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a canned cycle. List any four standard canned cycles used in CNC machining centers with one-line applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **canned cycle** is a pre-defined, modal G-code routine stored in the CNC controller that combines multiple machine movements (rapid approach, feed cut, dwell, retract) into a single block, reducing programming effort for repetitive hole-making operations. **(1 mark)**

| # | Cycle | Application (1/2 mark each) |
|---|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **G81** | Simple shallow drilling |
| 2 | **G82** | Spot-facing / counter-bore with dwell |
| 3 | **G83** | Deep-hole peck drilling |
| 4 | **G84** | Tapping (rigid) |

**(2 marks for the table — 1/2 mark per row × 4)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between G98 and G99 with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **G98 — Return to Initial Level:** After completing the cut, the tool retracts to the **Z-coordinate that was active before the cycle began** (i.e., the absolute initial plane). Used when **obstacles** exist between holes. **(1.5 marks)**
- **G99 — Return to R-Plane:** Tool retracts only to the **rapid clearance plane R** (typically 2–5 mm above workpiece). **Default mode** in most controllers. Faster and safer for flat parts. **(1.5 marks)**

```
        G98 retraction:        G99 retraction:
        |←——— Initial Z ———→|  |←— R-plane ——→|
        |   ↑                |  |   ↑         |
   ─────|───|·  ← Tool tip   ────|───|·  ← Tool tip
        |  R-plane           |  |
        |  Z-bottom          |  |  Z-bottom
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 📝 **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**a)** Explain the **G83 Peck Drilling Cycle** in detail with a neat sketch. Describe its necessity and the role of the `Q` parameter. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A CNC milling machine is required to drill **6 holes of Ø12 mm** and **30 mm deep** on a 150 × 100 × 20 mm mild steel plate. The holes are arranged in a **3 × 2 grid** with 60 mm spacing. Use G83 with peck depth Q = 10 mm, R = 3 mm, cutting speed V = 20 m/min, feed f = 0.15 mm/rev. Calculate the **spindle speed, feed rate, number of pecks, and total drilling time** (assume rapid = 2400 mm/min, dwell negligible). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — G83 Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
G83 is a **peck drilling canned cycle** that periodically retracts the drill to the R-plane to clear chips and coolant, used for deep holes where depth **> 3 × drill diameter**.

**Working Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Rapid from initial level → R-plane
2. Drill to first peck depth `Q`
3. Rapid retract to R-plane (chip evacuation)
4. Rapid back to just above last drilled depth
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until total Z-depth is reached
6. Final retract per G98 or G99

**Sketch (2 marks):**
```
Z-axis
  ↑     Initial Level (G98)
  |     ┌───────────┐
  |     │  R-plane  │  ← Rapid retract point
  |     │  ─────    │
  |     │ │  Q  │  │  ← First peck
  |     │ │  Q  │  │  ← Second peck
  |     │ │  Q  │  │  ← ...
  |     │ │Qlast│  │  ← Final (partial) peck
  |     │  ─────    │  ← Z-target depth
  └─────┴───────────┴──→ X-axis (time/depth)
```

**Role of Q (1 mark):** Q specifies **the incremental peck depth**. Smaller Q = better chip clearance but slower; larger Q = faster but risk of chip packing and tool breakage.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** D = 12 mm, Z = 30 mm, Q = 10 mm, R = 3 mm, V = 20 m/min, f = 0.15 mm/rev, n = 6 holes, F_rapid = 2400 mm/min.

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \frac{20 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12} \\
N &= \frac{20000}{37.699} = \textbf{530.5 rpm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{round to } 530 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed Rate (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= N \times f = 530 \times 0.15 = \textbf{79.5 mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Pecks (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{pecks}} &= \lceil Z / Q \rceil = \lceil 30 / 10 \rceil = 3 \\
Q_{\text{last}} &= 30 - (3-1) \times 10 = 10 \text{ mm (full peck)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Time per Hole (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= 2 \times \frac{R}{F_{\text{rapid}}} + \frac{Z}{F} \\
&= 2 \times \frac{3}{2400} + \frac{30}{79.5} \\
&= 0.0025 + 0.3774 \\
&= \textbf{0.3799 s/hole}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Drilling Time (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= n_{\text{holes}} \times T_{\text{cycle}} \\
&= 6 \times 0.3799 = \textbf{2.28 seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📝 **Question B (Choice 2):** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**a)** Explain the **G84 Tapping Cycle** with a block diagram. Why does the spindle **reverse direction** at the bottom of the tap? **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a **complete part program** (FANUC-style) to drill a **4-hole pattern** at coordinates **(20, 20), (80, 20), (80, 60), (20, 60)** on a workpiece. Use **G81** with R = 2 mm, Z = −15 mm, F = 80 mm/min. Assume tool change at T01, spindle start at S = 1200 rpm CW, and end with M30. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — G84 Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
G84 is a **right-hand rigid tapping cycle** that synchronizes spindle rotation with Z-axis feed so the thread pitch is maintained precisely. The tap feeds into the hole, dwells briefly, then **retracts while the spindle reverses**.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Rapid to R-plane above hole
2. Spindle CW + feed to Z-depth at F = N × pitch
3. Dwell at bottom (optional)
4. **Spindle reverses to CCW** + feed retract to R-plane
5. Spindle reverts to CW; tool moves to next hole

**Why spindle reversal? (2 marks):**
A standard right-hand tap has flutes cut in a helix that **pushes chips downward during cutting**. To retract without damaging the threads or breaking the tap, the spindle must rotate in the **opposite (CCW) direction** while the tap withdraws upward. This effectively **"unscrews"** the tap from the freshly cut threads.

**Block Format (1 mark):**
`G84 X_ Y_ Z_ R_ F_` where F = N × pitch.

---

### Part (b) — Part Program (7 marks)

```
O0001;                        (Program number)              [0.5 mark]
N10  G21 G90 G17 G99 G80;     (Metric, absolute, XY plane, R-ret, no cycle) [0.5 mark]
N20  M06 T01;                 (Tool change: T01 = Ø8 drill) [0.5 mark]
N30  M03 S1200;               (Spindle CW at 1200 rpm)    [0.5 mark]
N40  G00 X20.0 Y20.0;         (Rapid to first hole)        [0.5 mark]
N50  G81 R2.0 Z-15.0 F80.0;   (Activate G81 cycle)         [1 mark]
N60  X80.0 Y20.0;             (Hole 2)                     [0.5 mark]
N70  X80.0 Y60.0;             (Hole 3)                     [0.5 mark]
N80  X20.0 Y60.0;             (Hole 4)                     [0.5 mark]
N90  G80;                     (Cancel canned cycle)        [0.5 mark]
N100 G00 Z100.0;              (Retract to safe Z)          [0.5 mark]
N110 M05;                     (Spindle stop)               [0.5 mark]
N120 M30;                     (Program end, rewind)        [0.5 mark]
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 1–2 marks each in KTU evaluations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting G80 cancellation** — If omitted, the next G00/G01 move will still be in canned-cycle mode and the tool will unexpectedly drill at the next X-Y position. Always terminate cycles with **G80** before any non-cycle motion. **(−1.5 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing G98 vs G99** — Students often write G99 (default) but describe G98 (initial level) behavior in theory. Match code-to-explanation. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Spindle direction error in G84** — Tapping requires **spindle reversal at bottom**; simply retracting without reversing destroys the tap. Examiners specifically test this. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Units confusion** — Cutting speed V is in **m/min**; spindle N in **rpm**; the formula has the **1000 conversion factor**. Forgetting it yields a 1000× error. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 5. **In G83, mixing Q with full Z** — Q is the **increment**, not the total depth. Writing `Z-15 Q-15` is wrong; Q is the **per-peck** depth. **(−1.5 marks)**
>
> 6. **Missing tool change code M06** — A program that "begins" with a tool in spindle (T01) without M06 is technically incomplete for most KTU evaluations. **(−0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Canned Cycles**

- ✅ A **canned cycle** converts 5–7 motion blocks into **one line** of code.
- ✅ All hole-making cycles are **modal** — they stay active until **G80** cancels them.
- ✅ **G81** = simple drill; **G82** = drill + dwell; **G83** = peck drill; **G84** = tap (with spindle reverse).
- ✅ **G85–G89** = boring family, distinguished by retract method (rapid vs feed) and spindle state.
- ✅ **G98 = Initial Level**, **G99 = R-Plane** (default; faster).
- ✅ Q in G83 = **peck increment**, not total depth.
- ✅ Tapping feedrate **F = N × thread pitch** (synchronization).
- ✅ Cutting speed formula: $N = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ — **never forget the 1000**.
- ✅ Always include **safe tool retract (G00 Z100)** and **M30** at program end.
- ✅ Use **modal commands wisely** — set G90, G21, G17 once at program start to avoid repetition.

> 💡 **Final Exam Tip:** Memorize the G81–G89 table verbatim. Most KTU 14-markers are split: 7 marks for **theoretical explanation of one cycle** + 7 marks for **a numerical problem on a different cycle**. Practice cross-combinations (e.g., theory on G84 + numerical on G83).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: M Codes, APT Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** M Codes, APT Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions) — Formal Definition

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) programming, **M-Codes** (also called *Miscellaneous Functions* or *Auxiliary Functions*) are the set of non-geometric, non-cutting machine commands that control the **non-motion operational aspects** of a CNC machine tool. While G-Codes dictate the *path* and *geometry* of the tool, M-Codes control the *ancillary systems* such as spindle rotation, coolant flow, tool changes, and program flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> *"M-Codes are auxiliary preparatory commands beginning with the letter 'M' followed by a two-digit numerical index (e.g., M03, M08). They are modal or non-modal, are executed after the axis motion commands of a block, and govern machine-level operations not related to tool path geometry."*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor"

Imagine a CNC machine as a **symphony orchestra**:
- **G-Codes** are the **musicians** — they play the actual notes (linear/circular movements, feeds, speeds).
- **M-Codes** are the **conductor** — they don't produce music themselves, but they tell the musicians when to **start playing (M03 — Spindle ON)**, when to **turn the lights on (M08 — Coolant ON)**, when to **switch instruments (M06 — Tool Change)**, and when to **bow and exit the stage (M30 — End of Program)**.

Without the conductor, the musicians would play out of sync. Without M-Codes, the spindle, coolant, and tool changer would not synchronize with the cutting path.

### 1.2 APT (Automatically Programmed Tools) — Formal Definition

**APT** is a **high-level, English-like symbolic NC programming language** developed at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** in **1956** under the leadership of **Dr. Douglas T. Ross**. It was created to automate the tedious manual calculation of cutter-center paths for complex 5-axis contour milling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> APT is a *geometry-based, English-language NC programming system* that uses declarative statements to define part geometry, tool geometry, and tool motion independently. It is processed by a **pre-processor** (APT processor) and a **post-processor** to generate machine-specific G-Code/M-Code.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Blueprint Translator"

Think of APT as a **highly skilled architect** writing in plain English to describe a 3D sculpture. The architect doesn't worry about which CNC lathe or milling machine will carve it. The *APT processor* acts as a **universal translator** that converts the English blueprint into machine-specific code for any specific controller (FANUC, Siemens, Heidenhain, etc.). This makes APT **machine-independent** — a hallmark of modern CAM languages.

### 1.3 Standard M-Code List (Industry Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **standard ISO/EIA M-Code dictionary** accepted by KTU examiners. While specific CNC controllers (FANUC, Siemens) may extend this list, the codes below are **universally tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

| M-Code | Function | Category |
|:------:|:---------|:---------|
| **M00** | Program Stop (unconditional) | Program Control |
| **M01** | Optional Stop (operator-activated) | Program Control |
| **M02** | End of Program | Program Control |
| **M03** | Spindle ON, Clockwise (CW) | Spindle |
| **M04** | Spindle ON, Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | Spindle |
| **M05** | Spindle OFF | Spindle |
| **M06** | Automatic Tool Change (ATC) | Tool |
| **M07** | Mist Coolant ON | Coolant |
| **M08** | Flood Coolant ON | Coolant |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | Coolant |
| **M10** | Work Clamp ON (4th-axis fixtures) | Fixture |
| **M11** | Work Clamp OFF | Fixture |
| **M19** | Spindle Orientation (Rigid Tap) | Spindle |
| **M30** | End of Program + Rewind to Start | Program Control |
| **M98** | Sub-Program Call | Program Control |
| **M99** | Sub-Program Return / Loop | Program Control |

### 1.4 Modal vs Non-Modal M-Codes

- **Modal M-Codes** remain active until *cancelled* by another M-Code from the same group (e.g., M03 stays active until M05 is issued).
- **Non-Modal M-Codes** execute once and become inactive (e.g., M00, M06, M30).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule (Pitfall!):** A single block may contain **only one M-Code** on most standard CNC controllers. Writing `N50 M03 M08;` is **invalid** on a strict ISO controller — they must be placed in **separate blocks** unless the controller is explicitly non-standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Critical M-Codes

### 2.1.1 M00 — Program Stop (Unconditional)

- **Behaviour**: Halts the spindle, coolant, and axis motion **immediately** at the end of the executing block.
- **Reset Required**: Yes — operator must press **CYCLE START** to resume.
- **Use Case**: Operator inspection of the workpiece, manual deburring between roughing and finishing passes.
- **Modal/Non-Modal**: Non-modal (one-shot).

### 2.1.2 M01 — Optional Stop (Conditional)

- **Behaviour**: Identical to M00, but **only executes** if the operator has enabled the **OPTIONAL STOP** toggle switch on the machine control panel.
- **Use Case**: Production runs where stopping for inspection is *optional*. Allows a single program to serve both first-article inspection (with stop) and full production (without stop).

### 2.1.3 M03 / M04 / M05 — Spindle Control Triad

- **M03**: Spindle rotates **Clockwise (CW)** when viewed from the spindle nose. Used for **conventional milling** (right-hand cutter climbing a workpiece).
- **M04**: Spindle rotates **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)**. Used for **left-hand cutters** and certain turning operations.
- **M05**: Stops spindle rotation with **controlled deceleration** (ramp-down).
- **Safety Rule**: M05 **must** be issued before any tool change (M06) on most industrial controllers to prevent catastrophic spindle-tool collision.

### 2.1.4 M06 — Automatic Tool Change (ATC)

- **Behaviour**: Commands the machine's **automatic tool changer** (carousel, arm-type, or chain-type) to swap the tool currently in the spindle with the tool specified by the next `T` word.
- **Pre-Condition**: Spindle must be **OFF (M05)** and the tool must be at the **tool-change position** (typically Z-axis home).
- **Block Format Example**:
  `N30 T05 M06;`  → Loads Tool #5 into the spindle.

### 2.1.5 M07 / M08 / M09 — Coolant Control Triad

| Code | Coolant Type | Typical Application |
|:----:|:-------------|:--------------------|
| **M07** | Mist / Through-Spindle Air Blast | Drilling deep holes, aluminium machining |
| **M08** | Flood Coolant (high-volume fluid) | Heavy milling of steel, deep cuts |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | Dry machining, finishing passes, operator inspection |

### 2.1.6 M30 — Program End and Rewind

- **Behaviour**: Stops the spindle, coolant, and feed; **rewinds** the program to the first block; and **resets** register/macro variables.
- **Difference from M02**: M02 *stops* the program but does not rewind on most controllers. M30 is the **standard end-of-program** marker for production CNC.

### 2.1.7 M98 / M99 — Sub-Program Logic

- **M98 Pxxxx Lnnnn**: Calls sub-program `Oxxxx` and repeats it `nnnn` times.
- **M99**: Returns control from the sub-program to the main program (or loops to the start if used in the main program).
- **Block Example**:
  `N40 M98 P0500 L3;`  → Calls sub-program O0500 three times.

### 2.1.8 M19 — Spindle Orientation

- **Behaviour**: Rotates the spindle to a **precise angular position** (typically defined by a parameter) and locks it.
- **Use Case**: **Rigid tapping**, tool length measurement with optical probes, and automatic tool-change orientation.

---

## 2.2 APT Programming — Deep Theoretical Analysis

APT is structured into **four major statement categories**. Understanding this taxonomy is the most heavily tested KTU concept on this topic.

### 2.2.1 The Four APT Statement Families

1. **Geometry Definition Statements** — Define the *part* in 3D space.
2. **Motion Statements** — Define the *cutter's path* relative to the part.
3. **Cutter & Tolerance Statements** — Define the *tool geometry* and *accuracy*.
4. **Post-Processor / Auxiliary Statements** — Control machine actions, post-processing, and termination.

### 2.2.2 Geometry Definition Statements (Detailed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highest-Yield Topic:** Geometry statements are the foundation of APT. The examiner expects you to write *at least 3–4 of these* in any 14-mark part program question.

| APT Statement | Syntax | Meaning |
|:--------------|:-------|:--------|
| **POINT** | `POINT/x, y, z` | Defines a point in 3D space |
| **LINE** | `LINE/p1, p2` | Defines an infinite line through two points |
| **CIRCLE** | `CIRCLE/cen, r, ...` or `CIRCLE/p1, p2, p3` | Defines a circle (center+rad or 3-point) |
| **PLANE** | `PLANE/p1, p2, p3` | Defines a plane through three points |
| **VECTOR** | `VECTOR/x, y, z` | Defines a direction vector |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syntax Note:** APT uses the **slash `/`** as the separator between the keyword and its defining parameters. This is the most distinctive feature that examiners test.

### 2.2.3 Motion Statements (Detailed)

Motion statements are written **after** the geometry is fully defined. They describe *how the cutter moves*.

| Motion Statement | Behaviour |
|:-----------------|:----------|
| `FROM/p_start` | Sets the initial position of the cutter (must be the *first motion statement*). |
| `GO/TO, p_target` | Moves the cutter to a specified point (linear motion). |
| `GO/TO, LINE1` | Moves the cutter *onto* a line (perpendicular touchdown). |
| `GODLTA/dx, dy, dz` | Incremental move (delta from current position). |
| `GOLFT/L1, L2, ...` | Go *Left* of the listed surfaces. |
| `GORGT/L1, L2, ...` | Go *Right* of the listed surfaces. |
| `GOFWD/L1, L2, ...` | Go *Forward* (in the same direction as the previous motion). |
| `GOBACK/L1, L2, ...` | Go *Backward* (reverse direction). |
| `GOUP/plane` | Moves cutter *Up* to a defined plane. |
| `GODOWN/plane` | Moves cutter *Down* to a defined plane. |
| `TLLFT, TLRGT, TLOFPS` | Tool offset (left/right/offset) — modern APT extensions. |

### 2.2.4 Cutter, Tolerance & Auxiliary Statements

- **CUTTER/d**: Defines a **flat-end cylindrical cutter** of diameter `d` (in inches or mm depending on system convention).
- **TOLER/n**: Defines the **chordal tolerance** (in inches) for approximating curves with linear tool-path segments. Smaller tolerance = smoother (and slower) machining.
- **INTOL/n** / **OUTOL/n**: Defines the **inner/outer tolerance** for inside/outside offsets.
- **FEDRAT/n** / **SPINDL/n**: Feed rate and spindle speed (machine-specific).
- **COOLNT/ON, OFF, MIST, FLOOD**: Coolant control.
- **MACHIN/machine_name**: Tells the post-processor which machine is being targeted.
- **PARTNO/partname**: First statement of any APT program — identifies the part.
- **FINI**: **Mandatory last statement** — signals end-of-program to the APT processor.

### 2.2.5 The APT Processing Pipeline (Critical for KTU Theory)

A raw APT part program goes through **two stages** of translation before reaching the machine:

1. **Pre-Processor (CL File Generation)**: The APT processor converts the geometry-based English statements into a **Cutter Location (CL) file** — a numerical list of (X, Y, Z) coordinates that the cutter must follow. This file is **machine-independent**.
2. **Post-Processor**: Converts the CL file into the specific **G-Code/M-Code** required by the target CNC controller (FANUC, Siemens, etc.). This is **machine-specific**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why APT was revolutionary:** It separated *what to cut* (geometry) from *how to cut it* (machine code). This is the conceptual ancestor of every modern CAM system (Mastercam, Fusion 360, CATIA).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates all numerically-relevant rules, formats, and constants that KTU examiners have used in previous years. Memorize this verbatim.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Remarks |
|:--------|:---------------|:-----|:--------|
| Tool Offset (Cutter Radius) | $C_{\text{offset}} = \frac{d_{\text{cutter}}}{2}$ | mm or inch | APT automatically applies this during CL generation |
| Chordal Tolerance Approximation | $\Delta \approx \frac{r \cdot \theta^2}{2} \leq TOLER$ | inches | $r$ = radius, $\theta$ = step angle (radians) |
| Maximum Linear Step (arc approximation) | $L \approx \sqrt{8 \cdot r \cdot TOLER}$ | inches | Used by APT to discretize arcs |
| Spindle Power (Milling) | $P = \frac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| M-Code Group Limit | 1 M-Code per block | — | ISO/EIA standard; FANUC allows more on some models |
| APT Reserved Word Count | ~200 keywords | — | POINT, LINE, CIRCLE, GO, TLLFT, etc. |
| Modal M-Code Reset | M02 or M30 | — | M30 additionally rewinds program |
| Program Line Range | N01 – N9999 | — | Standard 4-digit line numbering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chordal Tolerance — Maximum Linear Step on an Arc

When a circular arc is approximated by a series of straight-line segments (as APT does internally), a **chordal error** $\Delta$ is introduced. APT lets the user specify a maximum acceptable $\Delta$ via the `TOLER/` statement. Below is the full mathematical derivation that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Consider a circular arc of radius $r$ subtending a small angle $2\theta$ (in radians) at the centre. A straight chord of length $L$ connects the two endpoints of the arc.

- Sagitta (perpendicular distance from arc midpoint to chord) = $\Delta$

### Step 2 — Express the Sagitta in Terms of $r$ and $\theta$

The distance from the centre of the circle to the chord is:
$$r \cos \theta$$

The distance from the centre of the circle to the arc is simply $r$. Therefore, the sagitta is:
$$\Delta = r - r \cos \theta = r(1 - \cos \theta)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Small-Angle Approximation

For small $\theta$ (which is the case for finely discretized arcs), we use the Taylor series expansion:
$$\cos \theta \approx 1 - \frac{\theta^2}{2} + \frac{\theta^4}{24} - \dots$$

Substituting and truncating after the first non-trivial term:
$$1 - \cos \theta \approx \frac{\theta^2}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta \approx r \cdot \frac{\theta^2}{2} = \frac{r \theta^2}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Maximum Step Angle

Setting the maximum sagitta equal to the user-specified tolerance $\varepsilon = TOLER$:
$$\frac{r \theta^2}{2} = \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon}{r}}$$

### Step 5 — Express the Chord Length $L$

The chord length for an angle $2\theta$ is:
$$L = 2 r \sin \theta$$

For small $\theta$, $\sin \theta \approx \theta$, so:
$$L \approx 2 r \theta = 2 r \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon}{r}} = \sqrt{8 r \varepsilon}$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Formula

$$\boxed{L_{\max} = \sqrt{8 \, r \, TOLER}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A tighter tolerance (smaller $TOLER$) forces APT to use **shorter linear segments**, producing a smoother surface but **more NC blocks**, larger CL files, and slower post-processing. This is the classic **accuracy vs. data-size** trade-off in APT.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An APT program must machine a circular pocket of radius $r = 50\,\text{mm}$. The user specifies `TOLER/0.025` (i.e., $0.025$ inches). Compute the maximum permissible linear step $L_{\max}$.

**Given:**
- $r = 50\,\text{mm} \approx 1.9685\,\text{inches}$
- $\varepsilon = 0.025\,\text{inches}$

**Calculation:**

First, convert radius to inches:
$$r = \frac{50}{25.4} \approx 1.9685\,\text{in}$$

Substitute into the formula:
$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{8 \times 1.9685 \times 0.025}$$

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{0.3937}$$

$$L_{\max} \approx 0.6274\,\text{inches} \approx 15.94\,\text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** APT will break the circular arc into chords of length $\leq 15.94\,\text{mm}$. The CL file will contain approximately $\frac{2\pi r}{L_{\max}} \approx \frac{2\pi \times 50}{15.94} \approx 19.7$ line segments per full revolution.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: M-Code Family — Decision Tree for Exam Use

The following matrix maps the *decision logic* an operator/programmer follows when selecting M-codes. KTU examiners use this exact decision tree in 14-mark programming questions.

| Stage of Operation | Required M-Code Sequence | Justification |
|:-------------------|:-------------------------|:--------------|
| **Program Initiation** | `M01` (optional) → Spindle warm-up | Allow operator verification |
| **Spindle Start** | `M03 S1500;` (with S = RPM) | Initiate CW rotation at programmed speed |
| **Coolant Engagement** | `M08;` (separate block) | Activate flood coolant for steel |
| **Tool Change** | `M05;` (in previous block) → `M06 T05;` | Spindle stop, then swap to tool #5 |
| **Peck Drilling** | `M08;` (coolant) | Deep hole coolant flooding |
| **End of Cut** | `M09;` (coolant off) | Dry finishing pass |
| **End of Program** | `M05; M09; M30;` | Spindle stop, coolant off, rewind |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often omit `M05` before `M06`. On industrial controllers (e.g., FANUC 0i), this triggers an **alarm 409 (Spindle not stopped)** and halts the program. Always halt the spindle before tool change.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: APT Statement Selection — Decision Tree

| Part Feature | Required Geometry Statement | Required Motion Statement |
|:-------------|:----------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Start point of cut | `POINT/p1, x, y, z` | `FROM/p1` |
| Straight wall of pocket | `LINE/p1, p2` | `GO/TO, L1` |
| Curved fillet | `CIRCLE/cen, radius` | `GO/TO, C1` or `GORGT/L1, C1` |
| Top surface of part | `PLANE/p1, p2, p3` | `GOUP/PL1` or `GODOWN/PL1` |
| Multi-pass milling | (re-use geometry) | Multiple `GO/TO, ...` with `GODLTA` for Z-stepdown |
| Finish operation | (re-use geometry) | `GO/TO, L1, PAST, L2` |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive APT Program — Worked Example (Pocket Milling)

**Problem (KTU-Style, 14 Marks):** Write a complete APT program to mill a rectangular pocket of dimensions $80 \times 60\,\text{mm}$ and depth $5\,\text{mm}$ on a vertical milling machine, starting from a clearance point above the part origin. Use a 10 mm diameter end mill.

### Step 1 — Define the Part

The pocket has four corners. Define each corner as a point:
- P1 = (0, 0, 0)  → origin
- P2 = (80, 0, 0)
- P3 = (80, 60, 0)
- P4 = (0, 60, 0)

### Step 2 — Define the Sides as Lines (Infinite Lines)

Each line passes through two corners. We need four lines.

### Step 3 — Define a Plunge Point Above the Pocket

P5 = (40, 30, 25) → clearance point 25 mm above the centre.

### Step 4 — Select Cutter, Tolerance, Feed & Speed

- CUTTER/10 → 10 mm diameter
- TOLER/0.01 → 0.01 inch chordal tolerance
- FEDRAT/100 → 100 mm/min
- SPINDL/1500 → 1500 RPM CW

### Step 5 — Full APT Program

```
PARTNO/RECT_POCKET_80x60x5
REMARK/ POCKET MILLING PROGRAM — KTU EXAMPLE
CLPRNT
N1 = POINT/0, 0, 0
N2 = POINT/80, 0, 0
N3 = POINT/80, 60, 0
N4 = POINT/0, 60, 0
N5 = POINT/40, 30, 25
L1 = LINE/N1, N2
L2 = LINE/N2, N3
L3 = LINE/N3, N4
L4 = LINE/N4, N1
PL1 = PLANE/N1, N2, N4
CUTTER/10
TOLER/0.01
FEDRAT/100
SPINDL/1500, CLW
COOLNT/FLOOD
FROM/N5
GO/TO, L1
GOLFT/L1, L2
GOFWD/L1, L3
GOFWD/L1, L4
GOFWD/L1, L1
GODLTA/0, 0, -5
GOLFT/L1, L2
GOFWD/L1, L3
GOFWD/L1, L4
GOFWD/L1, L1
GOUP/PL1
GODLTA/0, 0, 25
COOLNT/OFF
SPINDL/OFF
FINI
```

### Step 6 — Explanation of Critical Lines

- `CLPRNT` — Triggers printing of the CL file (auxiliary command).
- `CLW` — Spindle direction: **C**lock**W**ise (in `SPINDL/` statement, M03 equivalent).
- `FROM/N5` — Always the **first motion statement** in any APT program.
- `GO/TO, L1` — Plunges the cutter to the *first* line (L1, the bottom edge of the pocket).
- `GOLFT/L1, L2` — The cutter moves along the part keeping L1 to its left and L2 to its right (i.e., *around* the corner).
- `GOUP/PL1` — Retracts the cutter to the top plane (PL1) at the end of the pass.
- `COOLNT/OFF` and `SPINDL/OFF` — Equivalent to `M09` and `M05` in the post-processed G-Code.
- `FINI` — **Mandatory** last statement. Without `FINI`, the APT processor will throw an error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common marking scheme allocates **2 marks** for `PARTNO`, **2 marks** for proper geometry (POINT/LINE definitions), **4 marks** for the motion sequence, **2 marks** for cutter/tolerance/feed, and **2 marks** for `FINI` and proper sequencing. Total = 12/14. The remaining 2 marks are for *legibility, indentation, and comment statements*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Block Execution Flow (G-Code + M-Code Integration)

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid diagram illustrates how a single CNC block (e.g., `N50 G01 X100.0 Y50.0 F200 M08;`) is parsed and executed by the CNC controller. Notice the **strict order**: geometric commands first, then feed, then M-Code (auxiliary) — this is the ISO 6983 standard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CNC Block Read from Memory] --> B[Parse Line Number N]
    B --> C[Parse G-Code Words G]
    C --> D[Parse Coordinate Words X Y Z]
    D --> E[Parse Feed Rate Word F]
    E --> F[Parse Spindle Speed Word S]
    F --> G{M-Code Present?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Execute M-Code M function]
    G -- No  --> I[Execute Motion]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Update Tool Position Register]
    J --> K[Increment Block Counter]
    K --> L{More Blocks?}
    L -- Yes --> A
    L -- No  --> M[End of Program M30]
```

## 4.2 APT Language Processing Pipeline (Pre-Processor → Post-Processor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[APT Source Program] --> P2[APT Pre-Processor]
    P2 --> P3[CL File Cutter Location Data]
    P3 --> P4{Post-Processor Selection}
    P4 -- FANUC --> P5a[FANUC G-Code Output]
    P4 -- Siemens --> P5b[Siemens G-Code Output]
    P4 -- Heidenhain --> P5c[Heidenhain Klartext Output]
    P5a --> P6[CNC Machine Tool]
    P5b --> P6
    P5c --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Finished Part]
```

## 4.3 APT Statement Taxonomy (Hierarchy Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[APT Statement Families]
    Root --> Geo[Geometry Definition Statements]
    Root --> Mot[Motion Statements]
    Root --> Cut[Cutter and Tolerance Statements]
    Root --> Aux[Auxiliary and Post-Processor Statements]
    Geo --> Geo1[POINT]
    Geo --> Geo2[LINE]
    Geo --> Geo3[CIRCLE]
    Geo --> Geo4[PLANE]
    Mot --> Mot1[FROM]
    Mot --> Mot2[GO or TO]
    Mot --> Mot3[GOLFT and GORGT]
    Mot --> Mot4[GOFWD and GOBACK]
    Mot --> Mot5[GOUP and GODOWN]
    Mot --> Mot6[GODLTA Incremental]
    Cut --> Cut1[CUTTER]
    Cut --> Cut2[TOLER]
    Cut --> Cut3[INTOL and OUTOL]
    Aux --> Aux1[PARTNO]
    Aux --> Aux2[SPINDL]
    Aux --> Aux3[FEDRAT]
    Aux --> Aux4[COOLNT]
    Aux --> Aux5[FINI]
```

## 4.4 M-Code Decision Flow — Block Sequencing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Block Start] --> Q1{Tool Change Required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Issue M05 Spindle Stop]
    S1 --> S2[Issue M06 Tool Change]
    S2 --> Q2
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Coolant Needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S3[Issue M08 Flood ON]
    S3 --> Q3
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{End of Program?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S4[Issue M30 End and Rewind]
    Q3 -- No --> S5[Execute Geometric Motion G-Code]
    S5 --> End1[Block End]
    S4 --> End2[Program Halt]
```

## 4.5 Spindle Rotation Direction Reference

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a textual schematic of the ISO spindle convention. In **M03**, the spindle rotates *clockwise* when viewed from the **operator's station looking up at the spindle nose** — this is critical for proper tool engagement (especially in milling).

```mermaid
graph LR
    Op[Operator View] --> SpindleNose[Spindle Nose]
    SpindleNose --> R1[Clockwise M03 = Right Hand Cut]
    SpindleNose --> R2[Counter-Clockwise M04 = Left Hand Cut]
    R1 --> Note1[Standard for right-hand helical end mills in climb milling]
    R2 --> Note2[Used for tapping, left-hand tools, and conventional milling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Q:** List any **six** standard M-Codes used in CNC programming and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**

| M-Code | Function |
|:------:|:---------|
| **M03** | Spindle ON in clockwise direction |
| **M05** | Spindle OFF (with controlled deceleration) |
| **M06** | Automatic tool change (ATC) |
| **M08** | Flood coolant ON |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M30** | End of program and rewind to beginning |

> *(Any six correct codes with proper function descriptions will receive full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is APT? List the **four main categories** of APT statements with one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

**APT (Automatically Programmed Tool)** is a high-level, English-like symbolic programming language developed at MIT in 1956 for NC part programming, especially for complex 5-axis contouring.

The four categories of APT statements are:

1. **Geometry Definition Statements** — e.g., `POINT/x, y, z` defines a point in 3D space.
2. **Motion Statements** — e.g., `GO/TO, p_target` moves the cutter to a target point.
3. **Cutter and Tolerance Statements** — e.g., `CUTTER/d` defines a cylindrical cutter of diameter `d`.
4. **Auxiliary / Post-Processor Statements** — e.g., `COOLNT/FLOOD` controls coolant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are presented below with complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **M-Code triad** M03, M04, and M05. Discuss the safety implications and the role of M06 in tool changing. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** What is the **APT pre-processor and post-processor**? Explain the complete APT processing pipeline with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 Marks)

The M-Code triad **M03 / M04 / M05** governs spindle rotation control, which is the *most critical auxiliary function* in any CNC machining operation.

**1. M03 — Spindle ON, Clockwise (CW):**
- Causes the spindle to rotate in the **clockwise direction** when viewed from the **spindle nose** (operator's perspective looking up at the tool).
- Requires a **spindle speed word (S)** in the same block or a previous block. Example: `N20 S1500 M03;`
- Used for **standard right-hand helical end mills** in **climb milling** (most common configuration for CNC).

**2. M04 — Spindle ON, Counter-Clockwise (CCW):**
- Rotates the spindle **counter-clockwise** from the operator's view.
- Used for **left-hand tools**, **tapping operations**, and certain **conventional milling** configurations.

**3. M05 — Spindle OFF:**
- Stops the spindle with **controlled deceleration** (ramp-down, not free coast).
- The spindle must reach **zero RPM** before any subsequent tool change.

**Safety Implications:**

- **Direction Reversal:** Switching between M03 and M04 **without** an intermediate M05 causes a **mechanical shock load** on the spindle bearings and can damage the drive system. Always issue M05 before reversing direction.
- **Spindle Stop Before Tool Change:** On industrial controllers, an M06 issued without a prior M05 triggers **alarm 409 (Spindle not stopped)**. The tool change mechanism expects the spindle to be at rest to engage the ATC arm.
- **Coolant Interlock:** Many controllers interlock M08/M07 to require the spindle to be ON (M03/M04) — coolant without spindle rotation is a safety violation on heavy milling machines.

**Role of M06 in Tool Changing:**

- **M06** commands the **Automatic Tool Change (ATC)** sequence. The block must specify the tool number, e.g., `T05 M06;`
- The pre-conditions for M06 are:
  1. Spindle OFF (M05) at safe RPM (0).
  2. Tool positioned at the **tool-change height** (typically Z-axis home).
  3. Coolant OFF (M09) on most controllers.
- After M06, the controller updates the **active tool register** and applies the new tool's **length offset** and **diameter offset** (from the tool table).

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 Marks)

The APT programming language is **machine-independent**, but CNC machines require **machine-specific code** (G-Code/M-Code). This translation is performed by a **two-stage pipeline** consisting of the **pre-processor** and the **post-processor**.

**Stage 1 — APT Pre-Processor (Cutter Location File Generation):**

- **Input:** The APT source program (English-like statements like `POINT/`, `LINE/`, `CIRCLE/`, `GO/TO, ...`).
- **Function:** Performs **all geometric calculations** — solving cutter-path intersections, applying cutter offsets, generating linear approximations of curves based on the `TOLER/` value.
- **Output:** A **Cutter Location (CL) file** — a long list of (X, Y, Z) coordinates representing the *exact points* the cutter center must pass through, plus auxiliary data (feed, speed, coolant on/off flags).
- **Key Property:** The CL file is **machine-independent**. The same APT program can be used for any CNC machine that supports APT, with only the post-processor changing.

**Stage 2 — Post-Processor (CL → G-Code Translation):**

- **Input:** The CL file from the pre-processor.
- **Function:** Converts the numerical CL data into the specific **G-Code/M-Code dialect** of the target machine (FANUC, Siemens Sinumerik, Heidenhain, Mazak, etc.). It also handles:
  - Block sequencing (line numbering).
  - Feed rate and spindle speed word formatting.
  - Coolant M-Code selection (M07 vs M08).
  - Tool-change callouts.
  - Spindle rotation direction (M03 vs M04).
- **Output:** A complete **G-Code/M-Code NC program** ready to be transferred to the CNC controller.
- **Key Property:** The post-processor is **machine-specific** — different machines require different post-processors even for the same part.

**Block Diagram of the APT Processing Pipeline:**

```
┌──────────────────┐
│ APT Source Code  │  (English-like, geometry-based)
│  (PARTNO ...     │
│   POINT ...      │
│   LINE ...       │
│   CUTTER ...     │
│   GO/TO ...      │
│   FINI)          │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│ APT PRE-PROCESSOR│  (Geometric calculations,
│  (CL File Gen)   │   tool offsets, tolerances)
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  CL FILE         │  (X, Y, Z coordinates +
│ (Machine-Indep.)  │   feed, speed, coolant flags)
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  POST-PROCESSOR  │  (Machine-specific formatting)
│  (FANUC/Siemens/ │
│   Heidenhain)    │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  G-CODE / M-CODE │  (Ready for CNC controller)
│  NC PROGRAM      │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  CNC MACHINE     │
└──────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The pre-processor/post-processor split is the **conceptual ancestor of modern CAM systems** like Mastercam, CATIA, and Fusion 360. Modern CAM systems internalize APT's CL file generation as an intermediate step that the user never sees.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of **M00, M01, and M30** in CNC program control. Explain the difference between M02 and M30. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Write a complete **APT part program** to mill a **circular pocket** of diameter 60 mm and depth 4 mm using a 8 mm end mill. Use proper geometry, motion, cutter, and post-processor statements. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 Marks)

**M00 — Program Stop (Unconditional):**

- **Function:** Halts the program **immediately** at the end of the current block. The spindle, coolant, and axis drives are stopped.
- **Reset:** Operator must press the **CYCLE START** button to resume.
- **Use Case:** Operator inspection, manual deburring, dimensional verification between roughing and finishing passes.
- **Modal/Non-Modal:** **Non-modal** — it executes once and does not remain active.

**M01 — Optional Stop (Conditional):**

- **Function:** Behaves identically to M00, **but only if the operator has enabled** the **OPTIONAL STOP** toggle on the machine control panel.
- **Use Case:** Production environments where the same program is used for:
  - **First-article inspection** (toggle ON → stops for verification).
  - **Full production runs** (toggle OFF → runs continuously).
- **Advantage:** A single program can serve both modes, eliminating the need for two separate NC files.

**M30 — Program End and Rewind:**

- **Function:** Stops spindle (M05), coolant (M09), feed; **rewinds the program** to the first block; and **resets** register/macro variables to their default values.
- **Use Case:** The **standard end-of-program marker** for production CNC machining.

**Difference Between M02 and M30:**

| Feature | M02 (End of Program) | M30 (End + Rewind) |
|:--------|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| Stops spindle and coolant | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Rewinds program to start | ✗ No (on most controllers) | ✓ Yes |
| Resets macro variables | Partial | Full reset |
| Standard for production CNC | Rare | **Standard** (FANUC, Siemens) |
| Typical use | Old controllers, sub-programs | **Main programs** |

> **Mnemonic:** Think of M02 as *curtain close* and M30 as *curtain close + cast takes a bow and walks back to the dressing room*.

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 Marks)

**Problem:** Write an APT program to mill a circular pocket of diameter 60 mm (radius 30 mm), depth 4 mm, using an 8 mm diameter end mill. The pocket center is at the origin (0, 0, 0) of the part, and the clearance point is 20 mm above the center.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry required:**
- Center point: `CENTER = POINT/0, 0, 0`
- Clearance point above: `CLEAR = POINT/0, 0, 20`
- The pocket boundary: `POCKET = CIRCLE/CENTER, 30, 0, 0, 30` (center, radius, in XY plane, full circle)
- Bottom plane: `BOTPLN = PLANE/0, 0, -4, 0, 30, -4, 30, 0, -4` (defines a plane at Z = -4)

**Step 2 — Identify the motion sequence:**
- Position above part: `FROM/CLEAR`
- Plunge to pocket edge: `GO/TO, POCKET`
- Cutter offset (left/right): The cutter must orbit the circle with an 8 mm offset (4 mm radius).

**Step 3 — Full APT Program:**

```
PARTNO/CIRC_POCKET_D60_D4
REMARK/ CIRCULAR POCKET MILLING — 60MM DIA, 4MM DEPTH
CLPRNT
CENTER = POINT/0, 0, 0
CLEAR  = POINT/0, 0, 20
POCKET = CIRCLE/CENTER, 30, 0, 0, 30
BOTPLN = PLANE/0, 0, -4, 0, 30, -4, 30, 0, -4
TOPPLN = PLANE/0, 0, 0, 0, 30, 0, 30, 0, 0
CUTTER/8
TOLER/0.005
FEDRAT/80
SPINDL/1200, CLW
COOLNT/FLOOD
FROM/CLEAR
GO/TO, POCKET
TLLFT, POCKET
GO/TO, POCKET
GODLTA/0, 0, -4
GOFWD/POCKET
GORGT/POCKET
GOFWD/POCKET
GOLFT/POCKET
GOUP/TOPPLN
GODLTA/0, 0, 20
COOLNT/OFF
SPINDL/OFF
FINI
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of Key Lines:**

| Line | Purpose |
|:-----|:--------|
| `POCKET = CIRCLE/CENTER, 30, 0, 0, 30` | Defines a circle centered at origin, radius 30, in the XY plane. |
| `CUTTER/8` | Specifies an 8 mm diameter end mill. |
| `TOLER/0.005` | Chordal tolerance = 0.005 inches (very fine surface). |
| `SPINDL/1200, CLW` | Spindle speed 1200 RPM, clockwise (M03 equivalent). |
| `FROM/CLEAR` | Mandatory first motion — sets initial position 20 mm above origin. |
| `GO/TO, POCKET` | Plunges the cutter from clearance to the circle (perpendicular touchdown). |
| `TLLFT, POCKET` | Sets cutter offset to the **left** of the pocket boundary. |
| `GODLTA/0, 0, -4` | Incremental move **down** by 4 mm (plunge to depth). |
| `GOFWD/POCKET` and `GORGT/POCKET` | Cutter orbits around the pocket in a CCW/CW direction. |
| `GOUP/TOPPLN` | Retracts the cutter to the top plane (Z = 0). |
| `COOLNT/OFF` and `SPINDL/OFF` | Disengages coolant and stops spindle (M09, M05 equivalents). |
| `FINI` | **Mandatory** last statement. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - PARTNO + Header (1 mark)
> - Geometry definition (POINT, CIRCLE, PLANE) (2 marks)
> - Cutter + Tolerance + Feed + Speed (2 marks)
> - Motion sequence (FROM, GO/TO, GODLTA, GOFWD, GORGT) (3 marks)
> - Coolant/Spindle OFF + FINI (1 mark)
> - **Total: 9 marks** out of 7 (extra credit for neatness and use of comments)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on M-Code / APT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Omitting `M05` before `M06`** — Program execution halts with alarm 409. Always halt the spindle before tool change. *(Lose 1–2 marks per occurrence.)*
>
> 2. **Two M-Codes in a single block** — `N50 M03 M08;` is *invalid* on strict ISO controllers. M03 and M08 must be in **separate blocks**. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
>
> 3. **Forgetting `FINI` in APT** — The APT pre-processor throws an error and produces no CL file. `FINI` is **non-negotiable**. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
>
> 4. **Missing `FROM/` as the first motion statement** — APT requires `FROM/` to be the *initial* motion command. Starting with `GO/TO, ...` is a syntax error. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
>
> 5. **Confusing `TOLER/` units** — `TOLER/` is in **inches**, not millimeters. A common error is writing `TOLER/0.01` expecting mm, which is actually 0.01 inch ≈ 0.254 mm. *(Lose 1 mark if question is unit-sensitive.)*
>
> 6. **Confusing M02 and M30** — M02 does **not** rewind. For main programs, **always use M30**. *(Lose 1 mark in definitions.)*
>
> 7. **Not stating that APT is machine-independent** — The single most important conceptual point. APT's machine independence is achieved through the pre-processor/post-processor split. *(Lose 2–3 marks in theory questions.)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

### M-Code Essentials
- M00: **Unconditional** program stop (operator must press CYCLE START).
- M01: **Conditional** optional stop (requires operator toggle ON).
- M02: End of program (no rewind).
- M03: Spindle CW (right-hand tools, climb milling).
- M04: Spindle CCW (left-hand tools, tapping).
- M05: Spindle OFF (always before M06).
- M06: Automatic tool change.
- M07: Mist coolant; M08: Flood coolant; M09: Coolant OFF.
- M19: Spindle orientation (rigid tapping, probing).
- M30: End of program + rewind (standard for production).
- M98/M99: Sub-program call/return.
- **One M-Code per block** on strict ISO controllers.
- M05 is **mandatory** before M06.

### APT Essentials
- **APT** = Automatically Programmed Tool (MIT, 1956, Douglas T. Ross).
- **Machine-independent** language; uses **pre-processor + post-processor** architecture.
- **Four statement families:** Geometry, Motion, Cutter/Tolerance, Auxiliary.
- **Geometry keywords:** `POINT/`, `LINE/`, `CIRCLE/`, `PLANE/`, `VECTOR/`.
- **Motion keywords:** `FROM/` (always first), `GO/TO,`, `GOLFT/`, `GORGT/`, `GOFWD/`, `GOBACK/`, `GOUP/`, `GODOWN/`, `GODLTA/`.
- **Cutter/Tolerance:** `CUTTER/d`, `TOLER/n` (in inches!), `INTOL/`, `OUTOL/`.
- **Auxiliary:** `PARTNO/` (first statement), `SPINDL/`, `FEDRAT/`, `COOLNT/`, `FINI` (**mandatory last**).
- **Chordal tolerance formula:** $L_{\max} = \sqrt{8 \, r \, TOLER}$.
- **Separator:** APT uses the **slash `/`** between keyword and parameters (not parentheses or commas).
- `FROM/` is the **first motion statement** in every APT program.
- **CL File:** Output of pre-processor, machine-independent.
- **Post-Processor:** Converts CL to machine-specific G/M-Code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Turning and Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CNC Turning and Milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## CNC Turning — Formal Definition

**CNC Turning** is a subtractive manufacturing process performed on a **Turning Center** (a CNC lathe variant) where the workpiece rotates about the **Z-axis (spindle axis)** at a programmed spindle speed, while a single-point cutting tool, mounted on a powered tool turret, moves in a controlled linear path along the **X-axis (radial)** and **Z-axis (longitudinal)** to remove material and generate a rotationally symmetric part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PERUT412 / M3):** *CNC Turning & Milling — constructional features of CNC turning and milling machines, tooling, work holding devices, operations performed, and part programming basics.*

## CNC Milling — Formal Definition

**CNC Milling** is a subtractive manufacturing process executed on a **Machining Center** in which a multi-point rotating cutter (mounted on a spindle) removes material from a stationary or slowly rotating workpiece. The tool moves along the **X, Y, and Z axes** under NC control to produce prismatic parts, slots, pockets, contours, and holes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Potter vs. The Sculptor"**
> - A **CNC Lathe (Turning)** is like a **potter's wheel**. The clay (workpiece) **spins** while a fixed hand (tool) carves the shape — perfect for round, axisymmetric objects (shafts, bushes, threads).
> - A **CNC Milling Machine** is like a **sculptor chiselling a stationary block**. The block (workpiece) stays still while the spinning chisel (cutter) carves it from multiple sides — perfect for flat plates, slots, pockets, gears, and 3D contours.

## Standard Engineering Metrics (MUST remember)

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting speed | $v_c$ | m/min |
| Spindle speed | $N$ or $n$ | rev/min (rpm) |
| Feed per revolution (turning) | $f$ | mm/rev |
| Feed per tooth (milling) | $f_z$ | mm/tooth |
| Number of teeth | $z$ | — |
| Depth of cut (radial/axial) | $a_p$, $a_e$ | mm |
| Feed rate (table) | $v_f$ | mm/min |
| Material removal rate | $MRR$ / $Q$ | mm³/min |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CNC Turning — Architectural & Operational Breakdown

**Constructional Features of a CNC Turning Center:**

- **Headstock:** Houses the main spindle and chuck; provides variable spindle speed via AC servo motor.
- **Chuck (Work-holding):** 3-jaw self-centering (for round/hex) or 4-jaw independent (for irregular shapes). High-precision turning centers use **hydraulic/pneumatic power chucks**.
- **Tool Turret (ATC):** A rotating disc carrying 8–12 tools; indexes bi-directionally to bring the next tool into position. Modern machines use a **powered tooling turret** to enable C-axis (rotary) and live milling/drilling.
- **Linear Slides (Axes):** X-axis (cross-slide, radial), Z-axis (longitudinal), optional Y-axis (milling turn-mill centers), C-axis (spindle indexing for milling).
- **Tailstock:** Supports long slender shafts to prevent deflection.
- **Coolant System:** Flood coolant or high-pressure through-tool coolant (≥ 70 bar).
- **CNC Controller:** Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi, or Heidenhain — interprets G/M codes.

**Why each step matters:**
- The **chuck** must clamp without distortion → because deflection during cut causes taper/TIR errors.
- The **turret** enables zero tool-change time → critical for high-mix, low-volume production.
- The **C-axis** turns the lathe into a mill-turn machine → reduces setups, enables off-centre holes, slots, and polygonal turning.

**Operations Performed on CNC Turning Centers:**

| Operation | Description |
|---|---|
| **Facing** | Tool moves radially in X to create a flat end-face; cutting perpendicular to spindle axis. |
| **Straight Turning (OD/ID)** | Tool moves longitudinally in Z, reducing diameter; produces cylindrical surfaces. |
| **Taper Turning** | Simultaneous X and Z motion using a linear interpolation (G01 with X and Z in one block). |
| **Contour Turning** | Complex profiles using G02 (CW arc) / G03 (CCW arc) commands. |
| **Parting / Grooving** | Narrow tool plunges radially to cut off the part or form a groove. |
| **Threading** | Synchronized spindle-rotation and tool-feed creates helical threads; uses G32/G76 cycles. |
| **Knurling** | Patterned tool pressed radially to create a textured grip surface. |
| **Drilling/Boring/Reaming** | Performed with rotating tools (live tooling); drilling uses G81 canned cycle. |

## 2.2 CNC Milling — Architectural & Operational Breakdown

**Constructional Features of a CNC Vertical Machining Center (VMC):**

- **Column & Base:** Rigid cast iron / polymer-concrete structure to absorb vibration.
- **Spindle:** Direct-drive or belt-driven; speeds from **6,000 rpm** (general) to **40,000+ rpm** (high-speed HSC).
- **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC):** Umbrella-type, arm-type (carousel), or chain-type. Tool-change time: 1–5 seconds.
- **Tool Magazine:** Holds 16–200+ tools; can be drum, chain, or rack style.
- **Worktable & Pallets:** Linear roller guides; pallet changers enable unattended machining.
- **Linear Drives:** Ball screws (Positioning accuracy ±5 µm) or linear motors (HSC machines).
- **Axes:** X (table left-right), Y (table in-out), Z (spindle up-down). Optional 4th/5th axis rotary tables.

**Why each step matters:**
- **Rigid column** → resists deflection during heavy cuts → better surface finish and tool life.
- **ATC** → enables unmanned 3-shift operation → ROI of CNC milling.
- **Linear motors** → eliminate backlash → essential for high-speed milling of moulds.

**Operations Performed on a CNC VMC:**

| Operation | Tool | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Face Milling** | Inserted face mill | Flat large surfaces |
| **End Milling (Profile/Slot)** | Flat / ball end mill | Slots, pockets, contours |
| **Drilling** | Twist drill | Through / blind holes |
| **Tapping** | Rigid tap or tension/compression holder | Internal threads |
| **Boring** | Boring bar | Enlarge holes to high tolerance |
| **Reaming** | Reamer | Finish holes to ±0.01 mm |
| **Pocketing** | End mill + trochoidal path | Recessed cavities |
| **Engraving** | Small tapered mill | Letters, IDs, logos |

## 2.3 Work Holding — Turning vs Milling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison**

| Feature | CNC Turning | CNC Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Work-holding** | 3-jaw / 4-jaw chuck, collet, mandrel, between centres | Machine vice (most common), 3-jaw chuck, vacuum chuck, clamps, fixture plate, 4th axis rotary chuck |
| **Locating datum** | Spindle axis + face | Three orthogonal faces / precision pins |
| **Force direction** | Tool pushes workpiece — chuck resists radial/inward force | Cutter pushes workpiece sideways — vice resists 3D force vector |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: For any Machining Time or Parameter numerical — these 4 formulas carry the marks.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spindle speed (turning)** | $N = \dfrac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | $D$ = initial diameter (mm), $v_c$ in m/min |
| 2 | **Spindle speed (milling)** | $n = \dfrac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | $D$ = cutter diameter (mm) |
| 3 | **Feed rate — turning** | $v_f = N \times f$ | $f$ in mm/rev, $v_f$ in mm/min |
| 4 | **Feed rate — milling** | $v_f = f_z \times z \times n$ | $f_z$ in mm/tooth, $z$ = number of teeth |
| 5 | **MRR — turning** | $Q = \pi \times D \times a_p \times v_f$ | $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| 6 | **MRR — milling** | $Q = a_p \times a_e \times v_f$ | $a_e$ = width / radial engagement |
| 7 | **Cutting time — turning (straight)** | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{v_f} = \dfrac{L}{N \times f}$ | $L$ = table travel length |
| 8 | **Cutting time — milling (face)** | $t_m = \dfrac{L + D}{v_f}$ | Add cutter diameter for entry-exit |
| 9 | **Cutting time — drilling** | $t_m = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{v_f}$ | Plus approach allowance |
| 10 | **Power required** | $P_c = \dfrac{Q \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap (KTU examiners love this):** $v_c$ is in **m/min**, but $D$ is in **mm** → the multiplier **× 1000** converts mm → m. Forgetting it = 1 mark lost.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace turning:** Ti-6Al-4V shafts on Doosan/DMG MORI turn-mill centres using live tooling — single-setup "done-in-one" philosophy.
- **Mould milling:** Graphite electrodes on Makino / Röders high-speed VMCs at 40,000 rpm with linear motors.
- **Automotive:** Cylinder head aluminium face-milling with indexable inserts at >1000 cm³/min MRR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed (Turning)

**Given:** Cutting speed $v_c$ and workpiece diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of cutting speed.**
Cutting speed is the surface velocity at which the tool passes over the workpiece.

$$
v_c = \pi \times D \times N
$$

where $v_c$ has units of mm/min (using mm), $D$ is in mm, $N$ is in rev/min.

**Step 2 — Convert $v_c$ to m/min for industrial consistency.**

$$
v_c\,(\text{m/min}) = \dfrac{\pi \times D\,(\text{mm}) \times N}{1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c \times 1000 &= \pi \times D \times N \\[4pt]
N &= \dfrac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
A mild steel shaft of $\mathbf{50\,mm}$ diameter is to be turned at a cutting speed of $\mathbf{200\,m/min}$. Compute $N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{200 \times 1000}{\pi \times 50} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{200{,}000}{157.08} \\[4pt]
  &= \mathbf{1273.2\,rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The controller will round to the nearest available spindle step (e.g., **1300 rpm**).

## 3.2 Derivation — Feed Rate for Milling

**Step 1 — Per tooth engagement.**
Each tooth engages the workpiece for a feed of $f_z$ mm per spindle revolution.

**Step 2 — Per revolution of cutter.**
All $z$ teeth engage the workpiece in one revolution.

$$
\text{Feed/rev} = f_z \times z
$$

**Step 3 — Per minute.**
Spindle rotates $n$ times per minute.

$$
v_f = n \times f_z \times z
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
A 4-flute ($\mathbf{z = 4}$) end mill with $f_z = \mathbf{0.05\,mm/tooth}$ running at $\mathbf{1500\,rpm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= 1500 \times 0.05 \times 4 \\[4pt]
    &= \mathbf{300\,mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Practice Template

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to solve ANY 14-mark numerical on this topic.**

| Step | Action | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convert $v_c$ (m/min) → mm/min | $v_c \times 1000$ |
| 2 | Compute spindle speed | $N = v_c \times 1000 / (\pi \times D)$ |
| 3 | Compute table feed | $v_f = N \times f$ (turn) or $f_z \times z \times N$ (mill) |
| 4 | Compute MRR | $Q = \pi D a_p v_f$ (turn) or $a_p a_e v_f$ (mill) |
| 5 | Compute cutting time | $t_m = L / v_f$ |
| 6 | Compute power (if asked) | $P_c = Q k_c / (60 \times 10^6 \times \eta)$ |

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Example — Turning

> A **S45C steel shaft** of initial diameter **80 mm** is to be reduced to **74 mm** in a single pass on a CNC lathe. Length of cut $L = \mathbf{200\,mm}$. Tool feed $f = \mathbf{0.25\,mm/rev}$. Cutting speed $v_c = \mathbf{180\,m/min}$. Specific cutting force $k_c = \mathbf{2000\,N/mm^2}$. Machine efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Compute $N$, $v_f$, $Q$, $t_m$, and $P_c$.

**Step 1 — Average diameter** (for spindle speed, use initial D):

$$
D = 80\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{180 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{180{,}000}{251.33} \\[4pt]
  &= \mathbf{716.2\,rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Table feed rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= N \times f = 716.2 \times 0.25 \\[4pt]
    &= \mathbf{179.05\,mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Depth of cut (radial):**

$$
a_p = \dfrac{80 - 74}{2} = 3\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — MRR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \pi \times D \times a_p \times v_f \\[4pt]
  &= \pi \times 80 \times 3 \times 179.05 \\[4pt]
  &= 1{,}34{,}974\,\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \\[4pt]
  &\approx \mathbf{1.35 \times 10^5\,mm^3/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cutting time:**

$$
t_m = \dfrac{200}{179.05} = \mathbf{1.117\,min} = 67.0\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{Q \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta} \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{1{,}34{,}974 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.8} \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^8}{4.8 \times 10^7} \\[4pt]
    &= \mathbf{5.625\,kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Turning Center — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[G-Code Program from CAM or Manual Programming] --> B[CNC Controller Fanuc Siemens]
    B --> C1[Spindle Drive Motor]
    B --> C2[X-Axis Servo Motor Cross Slide]
    B --> C3[Z-Axis Servo Motor Longitudinal Carriage]
    B --> C4[Tool Turret Indexer]
    C1 --> D1[Spindle and Chuck Workpiece rotates]
    C2 --> D2[Cross Slide Tool moves radially in X]
    C3 --> D3[Longitudinal Carriage Tool moves in Z]
    C4 --> D4[Tool Turret 8 to 12 stations]
    D1 --> E[Workpiece with chips removed]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    F[Coolant System 70 bar] --> E
    G[Tailstock Optional for long shafts] --> E
    H[Feedback Encoders Linear and Rotary] --> B
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
```

## 4.2 CNC Vertical Machining Center — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[G-Code Program] --> K[CNC Controller]
    K --> SA[Spindle Drive]
    K --> XA[X Axis Servo Table left right]
    K --> YA[Y Axis Servo Table in out]
    K --> ZA[Z Axis Servo Spindle up down]
    K --> ATC[Automatic Tool Changer Controller]
    SA --> SP[Spindle 0 to 40000 rpm]
    XA --> TB[Worktable]
    YA --> TB
    ZA --> SP
    ATC --> MG[Tool Magazine 16 to 200 tools]
    MG --> ATC
    SP --> C[Cutting Zone]
    TB --> C
    C --> W[Workpiece Clamped on Vice or Fixture]
    CL[Chip Conveyor and Coolant Nozzle] --> C
    EN[Linear and Rotary Encoders] --> K
    SP --> EN
    TB --> EN
```

## 4.3 Work-Holding Selection Flow (Turning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Is the part axisymmetric] -->|Yes| B[Round hex bar]
    A -->|No| C[Use 4-jaw chuck or face driver]
    B --> D{Does the part need through machining on both ends}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use between centres with drive dog]
    D -->|No| F[3 jaw self centering chuck for lengths to 3x diameter]
    E --> G[For diameters under 65 mm use collet chuck ER32 ER40]
    F --> H[For long slender shafts add tailstock and steady rest]
```

## 4.4 Tool Path Logic — Milling a Rectangular Pocket

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Start Position Above Pocket] --> S1[Rapid Plunge Z to safe depth]
    S1 --> S2[Plunge to Z depth G01 with peck]
    S2 --> S3[Ramp in at 5 to 10 deg to reduce shock]
    S3 --> S4[Linear Cut G01 along X to corner 1]
    S4 --> S5[Linear Cut G01 along Y to corner 2]
    S5 --> S6[Linear Cut G01 along X to corner 3]
    S6 --> S7[Linear Cut G01 along Y to corner 4]
    S7 --> S8[Retract Z rapid and return to start]
    S8 --> S9[Repeat for second depth increment if multi pass]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this schematic matters:** KTU frequently asks students to **sketch a tool-path diagram** and list the G-codes at each node. The above flow is exactly what a 7-mark part (b) answer expects.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between CNC turning and CNC milling. List two operations unique to each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CNC Turning | CNC Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece motion** | Rotates about spindle axis | Stationary (cutter rotates) |
| **Tool motion** | Linear in X and Z | Linear in X, Y, Z |
| **Unique ops** | Threading, parting, knurling | Pocket milling, engraving, 3D contouring |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**State the functions of (a) tool turret and (b) tailstock in a CNC turning centre.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Tool turret** — Holds 8–12 tools, indexes bi-directionally under NC command to bring the required tool into cutting position with minimum tool-change time.
- **(b) Tailstock** — Supports the free end of long slender workpieces via a live centre to prevent deflection and vibration during turning.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ✅ CHOICE 1 — QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **constructional features of a CNC vertical machining centre**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **150 mm × 100 mm × 30 mm** mild-steel plate is to be face-milled on a VMC using a **50 mm diameter, 4-tooth** face mill. Cutting speed $v_c = 120\,\text{m/min}$, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.08\,\text{mm/tooth}$, depth of cut $a_p = 2\,\text{mm}$, width of cut $a_e = 100\,\text{mm}$. Calculate spindle speed $n$, table feed $v_f$, MRR $Q$, and cutting time $t_m$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A CNC VMC consists of:

1. **Column & base** — rigid cast-iron structure absorbing vibration.
2. **Spindle** — direct-drive AC motor, speeds 0–12,000 rpm (HSC: 40,000 rpm), with taper BT40 / HSK63A.
3. **Linear axes (X, Y, Z)** — driven by servo motors through ball-screws; positioned on linear roller guides.
4. **Automatic tool changer (ATC)** — arm-type with tool magazine (16–24 tools); changes tool in 2–5 s.
5. **Worktable** — supports vice/fixture; pallet changers for unmanned operation.
6. **Coolant and chip-management system** — flood coolant + chip conveyor.
7. **CNC controller** — Fanuc / Siemens with MDI, manual pulse generator, and DNC interface.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Spindle speed.**

$$
n = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{\pi \times 50} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{157.08} = \mathbf{763.9\,rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Table feed.**

$$
v_f = f_z \times z \times n = 0.08 \times 4 \times 763.9 = \mathbf{244.5\,mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — MRR.**

$$
Q = a_p \times a_e \times v_f = 2 \times 100 \times 244.5 = \mathbf{48{,}900\,mm^3/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Cutting time.**
For face milling, $t_m = (L + D) / v_f$.

$$
t_m = \dfrac{150 + 50}{244.5} = \dfrac{200}{244.5} = \mathbf{0.818\,min} = 49.1\,\text{s}
$$

---

### ✅ CHOICE 2 — QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain with a sketch the **work-holding devices used in CNC turning centres**. Compare **3-jaw chuck vs collet chuck**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **60 mm diameter S45C shaft** is to be turned to **55 mm** in one pass. Length of cut $L = 300\,\text{mm}$, $v_c = 150\,\text{m/min}$, $f = 0.2\,\text{mm/rev}$. Compute (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) cutting time, (v) cutting power if $k_c = 2100\,\text{N/mm}^2$ and $\eta = 0.85$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Work-holding devices in CNC turning:**

1. **3-jaw self-centering chuck** — scroll-type; all three jaws move synchronously; suited to round/hex bars.
2. **4-jaw independent chuck** — each jaw moves separately; for square, rectangular or off-centre work.
3. **Collet chuck (ER / 5C)** — high precision (±0.02 mm), for bars < 65 mm; ideal for small-diameter batch production.
4. **Mandrel** — holds finished bore workpieces for re-turning the outer diameter.
5. **Face driver / drive dog + centres** — for slender shafts; allows full turning of the journal.

**3-Jaw vs Collet Chuck:**

| Feature | 3-Jaw Chuck | Collet Chuck |
|---|---|---|
| Concentricity | ±0.05 mm | ±0.02 mm |
| Grip range | Wide (3–250 mm) | Narrow (1 mm increments) |
| Clamping force | Moderate (forgiving) | Very high (precision) |
| Best for | Heavy, rough parts | Small, precision shafts |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{150 \times 1000}{\pi \times 60} = \dfrac{150{,}000}{188.5} = \mathbf{795.8\,rpm}
$$

**(ii) Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = N \times f = 795.8 \times 0.2 = \mathbf{159.2\,mm/min}
$$

**(iii) MRR:**
Depth $a_p = (60-55)/2 = 2.5\,\text{mm}$.

$$
Q = \pi \times 60 \times 2.5 \times 159.2 = \mathbf{75{,}007\,mm^3/min}
$$

**(iv) Cutting time:**

$$
t_m = \dfrac{300}{159.2} = \mathbf{1.884\,min} = 113.0\,\text{s}
$$

**(v) Cutting power:**

$$
P_c = \dfrac{Q \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta} = \dfrac{75{,}007 \times 2100}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.85} = \mathbf{3.09\,kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the "× 1000"** when $v_c$ is in m/min and $D$ is in mm. Worth 1 mark — highest-frequency error.
> 2. **Mixing feed per rev (turning) and feed per tooth (milling).** Always re-read the problem statement.
> 3. **Using final diameter instead of initial** in the spindle-speed formula. For a single-pass roughing cut, use the **starting (largest) diameter** for safety.
> 4. **Not adding the cutter-diameter allowance** in face-milling time → answer is "too low" by 15–30 % → 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final answer — KTU awards marks **only for the numerical value with the correct unit**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Turning = work rotates; Milling = tool rotates.** Memorize this distinction — every other difference flows from it.
- ⭐ **Master the 4 master formulas**: $N = v_c \times 1000 / (\pi D)$, $v_f = N f$ (turning) or $v_f = f_z z n$ (milling), MRR, $t_m = L / v_f$.
- ⭐ **Live tooling + C-axis** turns a lathe into a mill-turn centre — must mention in any 7-marker on "advanced turning".
- ⭐ **ATC + tool magazine** distinguishes a CNC VMC from a manual mill — the single most important constructional feature.
- ⭐ **3-jaw chuck vs collet chuck** is a recurring 3-mark favourite — memorise the precision and grip differences.
- ⭐ **Canned cycles** (G81 drilling, G84 tapping, G76 threading) are time-savers — examiners expect at least one to be named.
- ⭐ **Units discipline**: mm ↔ m conversion is the #1 silly-mistake zone. Always write "v_c = 200 m/min" explicitly before plugging in.
- ⭐ **Tool material hierarchy for high speed**: HSS < Carbide < Coated Carbide < Ceramic < CBN / PCD.
- ⭐ **Coolant pressure**: 5–10 bar flood for general; ≥ 70 bar through-tool for deep-hole drilling and high-speed milling.
- ⭐ **High-speed cutting tip**: when $v_c$ exceeds 5,000 m/min (HSC), linear motors replace ball-screws — mention this for a "wow" line in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are CNC Machine "Basics"?

In the context of KTU PERUT412 (CNC Machines) — Module 3, the term **"Basics"** refers to the foundational vocabulary and rules of **CNC Part Programming**. Before a machine can cut metal, the part programmer must speak its language. This language is built upon four pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT412 Module 3)**
> The "Basics" of CNC programming cover the **NC coordinate system**, **axis identification**, **G-codes (Preparatory Functions)**, **M-codes (Miscellaneous Functions)**, **tool and work reference points**, and the **structure of a part program**. These are the universal building blocks used to command any CNC machine tool (turning or milling).

The four primary NC word types every KTU student must memorize:

| Symbol | Name | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **G** | Preparatory Function | Defines the *type* of motion (linear, circular, rapid) |
| **M** | Miscellaneous Function | Defines *auxiliary* actions (spindle on, coolant, stop) |
| **F** | Feed Function | Sets the **rate** of cutter movement (mm/min) |
| **S** | Spindle Function | Sets the **rotational speed** of the spindle (RPM) |
| **T** | Tool Function | Selects the **tool** and **tool offset register** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Mental Model

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Box: CNC Program = Cooking Recipe**
> Imagine you are teaching a robot cook to make Dosa:
> - **G-code** = the *action verb* (e.g., "G01 = pour slowly in a straight line")
> - **M-code** = the *kitchen helper command* (e.g., "M08 = turn on the stove/gas")
> - **S, F, T** = the *settings* (which pan? what heat? how fast to pour?)
> - **X, Y, Z** = the *coordinates* on the counter (where the batter lands)
>
> Without the basic grammar of these commands, the machine just sits idle — exactly like a chef who only knows the ingredients but not the verbs.

---

## 1.3 The Cartesian Coordinate System in CNC

Every CNC machine obeys the **right-hand rule** of a 3-axis Cartesian system. KTU examiners expect this drawn exactly:

- **X-axis** → Longest travel (typically left-right on a milling table)
- **Y-axis** → Cross travel (front-back on the table)
- **Z-axis** → Parallel to the **spindle axis** (always the axis that gives the most depth/rigidity)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Right-Hand Rule for CNC**
> Point your **right thumb along +Z**, your **index finger along +X**, and your **middle finger along +Y** — they form a mutually perpendicular triad. This convention is identical to **ISO 841** and is mandatory in KTU answer scripts.

For **Turning Centers (Lathes)**, the convention differs:
- **Z-axis** → Along the spindle (workpiece rotation axis)
- **X-axis** → Radial (tool infeed direction, toward the operator is often negative)

---

## 1.4 Absolute vs Incremental Programming — The GPS Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Two Ways to Tell a Machine "Where to Go"**
> - **Absolute (G90):** Every coordinate is measured from a **fixed origin point** (Program Zero). Like a Google Maps pin — you always give full addresses.
> - **Incremental (G91):** Every coordinate is measured from the **current position**. Like giving directions — "go 10 m forward, then turn left 5 m."

---

## 1.5 Key Reference Points (KTU High-Frequency)

- **Program Zero / Work Origin (W):** The (0,0,0) of the *part*. All part geometry is referenced here.
- **Machine Zero (M):** The fixed, repeatable home position defined by the machine manufacturer (limit switches).
- **Tool Length Offset (H):** Stores the difference between the tool tip and the spindle nose.
- **Tool Offset Memory / Work Offset (G54–G59):** Stores the *vector* from Machine Zero to Program Zero.

---

## 1.6 Standard NC Program Structure

A complete KTU-style part program has three sections:

```
%
O0001 (PROGRAM HEADER / IDENTIFICATION)
```
