```
N10 G21 G90 G54 (SAFETY / SETUP BLOCK)
N20 T01 M06 (TOOL CHANGE)
N30 S1200 M03 (SPINDLE CW)
... (MACHINING BLOCKS) ...
N90 M05 (SPINDLE STOP)
N100 M30 (PROGRAM END / REWIND)
%
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip**
> The block starting with `G21 G90 G54` is called the **"Safe Start Block"** (or **Safety Block**). KTU examiners *frequently* ask students to explain each word in this line. Memorize it.

---

## 1.7 Unit of Measure Standards

- **G20** → **Inch** programming (Imperial) — **rarely used in KTU Kerala industry**
- **G21** → **Millimeter** programming (Metric) — **the default in India**

> **Default for all KTU problems: G21 (Metric).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an NC Block (Line of Code)

A **Block** (also called a *line* or *sentence*) is the smallest unit of execution. It is executed *sequentially* unless overridden.

**Standard block format:**

```
N___ G__ X___ Y___ Z___ F___ S___ T___ M__
```

| Prefix | Stands For | Example | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| N | Sequence Number (Block Number) | N40 | Identifies the block (optional but standard) |
| G | Preparatory Word | G01 | Modal — stays active until changed |
| X, Y, Z | Axis Address Words | X25.0 | Linear axes positions |
| I, J, K | Arc / Center offsets | I10.0 | Vector from arc start to center |
| F | Feedrate | F150 | mm/min (modal) |
| S | Spindle Speed | S1800 | rev/min (modal) |
| T | Tool Selection | T02 | Tool #2 (modal) |
| M | Miscellaneous | M08 | Coolant ON (one-shot) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Modal vs One-Shot Codes**
> - **Modal G-codes** (e.g., G00, G01, G90) remain active for all subsequent blocks until another code in the same group cancels them.
> - **One-shot M-codes** (e.g., M00, M30) act *only* in the block they appear in.

---

## 2.2 G-Code Grouping (ISO 1056 / EIA RS-267)

KTU examiners expect students to know that G-codes are organized into **functional groups**. Codes within the same group **cancel each other**.

| Group | Function | Common Codes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **01** | Motion | G00, G01, G02, G03, G04 (dwell) |
| **02** | Plane Selection | G17 (XY), G18 (ZX), G19 (YZ) |
| **03** | Absolute / Incremental | G90, G91 |
| **05** | Feedrate Mode | G94 (mm/min), G95 (mm/rev) |
| **06** | Units | G20 (inch), G21 (mm) |
| **07** | Cutter Compensation | G40, G41, G42 |
| **08** | Tool Length Offset | G43, G44, G49 |
| **12** | Work Coordinate | G54–G59 |

---

## 2.3 The 10 Most Critical G-Codes (KTU Must-Know)

> [!NOTE] These codes appear in **every KTU CNC paper** — commit them to memory.

| Code | Name | Description | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **G00** | Rapid Traverse | Non-cutting fast move at **max machine feed** (typically 15 m/min) | Modal |
| **G01** | Linear Interpolation | Straight-line cut at programmed **F** feedrate | Modal |
| **G02** | Circular CW | Clockwise arc | Modal |
| **G03** | Circular CCW | Counter-clockwise arc | Modal |
| **G04** | Dwell | Pause for **P** seconds (e.g., G04 P2.0 = wait 2 s) | One-shot |
| **G17/G18/G19** | Plane select | XY / ZX / YZ plane for arcs | Modal |
| **G20/G21** | Inch / Metric | Unit system | Modal |
| **G28** | Return to Reference | Auto return through **intermediate point** to machine zero | One-shot |
| **G40/G41/G42** | Cutter Comp | Cancel / Left / Right compensation | Modal |
| **G90/G91** | Absolute / Incremental | Coordinate system mode | Modal |
| **G94/G95** | Feed mode | Per minute / Per revolution | Modal |

---

## 2.4 The 10 Most Critical M-Codes (KTU Must-Know)

| Code | Function | When It Acts |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **M00** | Program Stop | Stops spindle, coolant, feed — operator must resume |
| **M01** | Optional Stop | Same as M00 but only if **Optional Stop** switch is ON |
| **M02** | Program End | End of program, no rewind |
| **M03** | Spindle ON CW | Start spindle clockwise (standard for milling) |
| **M04** | Spindle ON CCW | Start spindle counter-clockwise (often for left-hand tools) |
| **M05** | Spindle Stop | Stop spindle |
| **M06** | Automatic Tool Change | ATC arm engages |
| **M08** | Coolant ON (Flood) | Turn on flood coolant |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | Turn off coolant |
| **M30** | Program End & Rewind | End of program, rewind to start, optional stop |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these formulas — they appear in 14-mark derivation questions.**

| Formula | Symbol Key | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | N = RPM, V = cutting speed (m/min), D = tool/work dia (mm) | Spindle speed selection |
| $F_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | F_f = feedrate (mm/min), f_z = feed/tooth, z = teeth, N = RPM | Milling feedrate |
| $F = N \cdot f_r$ | F = feedrate (mm/min), f_r = feed/rev (mm/rev) | Turning feedrate |
| $M_R = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \Delta\theta \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ | M_R = radial metal removed (mm), D = dia, Δθ = angular travel (°) | Arc-segment stock removal |
| $T = \dfrac{L}{F_f}$ | T = time (min), L = length (mm), F_f = feed (mm/min) | Cycle time |
| $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | V = surface speed (m/min) | Verify cutting speed |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall — Absolute Value Bars**
> In the exam, write $|x|$ as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. Avoid raw `|` symbols inside sentences — they confuse the matrix.

---

## 2.6 Tool Offset & Compensation Logic

### (a) Tool Length Compensation (G43 / G44 / G49)

The controller stores a **positive or negative offset** in the tool offset table:
- **H01** = Offset for Tool 1
- **H02** = Offset for Tool 2
- The command **G43 H01** tells the machine: "Shift the programmed Z by the value stored in H01."

### (b) Cutter Radius Compensation (G41 / G42 / G40)

Since the *programmed path* is the center of the tool, the controller offsets the path by the **cutter radius (D-word)** either to the left or right of the direction of motion.

> [!IMPORTANT] **G41 vs G42 Rule of Thumb**
> Imagine **walking along the programmed path** in the direction of travel.
> - **G41** = Tool is on your **LEFT** (compensation left)
> - **G42** = Tool is on your **RIGHT** (compensation right)
>
> This is the #1 confused concept in KTU papers — practice drawing both.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Industry:** Every G-code you write eventually drives a Fanuc, Siemens, or Heidenhain controller used in lathes, machining centers, and EDM.
- **In Aerospace:** Mastercam/UG-NX-generated programs use the *same* G/M-code fundamentals to cut titanium turbine blades.
- **In Medical Devices:** Swiss-turn lathes producing surgical screws use G95 (feed per rev) and G76 (threading cycle) — covered in Module 4.
- **In Robotics:** Industrial robot arms use a near-identical coordinate convention (ISO 9787) — so your CNC basics transfer directly.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

> **Problem Statement:** A 4-flute HSS end mill of diameter $D = 12 \text{ mm}$ is to machine mild steel at a recommended cutting speed $V = 30 \text{ m/min}$. Calculate the required spindle speed $N$ in RPM.

### Step 1 — Identify the physical meaning of cutting speed $V$

The **cutting speed $V$** is the *linear tangential velocity* at which the cutting edge meets the workpiece material, measured in **meters per minute (m/min)**.

For a rotating tool of diameter $D$ (mm) rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute (rev/min), the circumference swept per revolution is $\pi D$ (mm). The tangential speed is therefore:

$$
V_{\text{tangential}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

### Step 2 — Convert units to match the standard (m/min)

Since $D$ is in mm but $V$ is in m/min, we divide by **1000**:

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange algebraically to solve for $N$

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad [\text{rev/min}]
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 12}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{30000}{37.6991}
$$

$$
N \approx 795.77 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Step 5 — Round to a safe machine value

CNC spindles are commanded in **integer RPM** (no decimals on most controllers). Always round **down** for safety — never above — to avoid exceeding the recommended $V$:

$$
N_{\text{programmed}} = 795 \text{ RPM} \quad (\text{rounded down})
$$

> **Verification check:** Plug back: $V = \pi \times 12 \times 795 / 1000 = 29.97$ m/min ✓ (within 0.1% of target).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Feedrate in Milling

> **Problem Statement:** For the same 4-flute, 12 mm end mill at $N = 795$ RPM, if the recommended feed per tooth is $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, calculate the table feedrate $F_f$ in mm/min.

### Step 1 — Define feed per tooth

The feed per tooth $f_z$ is the *chip-load* — the linear distance the table advances during the engagement of *one tooth* with the workpiece. With $z$ teeth and $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$
\text{Number of tooth engagements per minute} = z \cdot N
$$

### Step 2 — Formulate the feedrate equation

$$
F_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values

$$
F_f = 0.05 \times 4 \times 795
$$

$$
F_f = 159 \text{ mm/min}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the G-code

In the NC program:

```
N40 S795 M03  (spindle CW at 795 RPM)
N50 G01 X50.0 Y30.0 F159  (linear cut at 159 mm/min)
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Modal Code Persistence

The following **state machine** shows how modal G-codes persist across blocks. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

| Block | Code Issued | Active G-Group 01 | Active G-Group 03 | Active G-Group 06 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| N10 | G21 G90 G00 | G00 (rapid) | G90 (absolute) | G21 (mm) |
| N20 | G01 X10 F100 | **G01** (linear) | G90 (carried) | G21 (carried) |
| N30 | X20 | G01 (carried) | G90 (carried) | G21 (carried) |
| N40 | G02 X30 Y10 R5 | **G02** (CW arc) | G90 (carried) | G21 (carried) |
| N50 | M30 | (program end) | — | — |

> **Why G01 from N20 was "carried" in N30?** Because G01 is a *modal* preparatory code of group 01, and no other group-01 code appears in N30 to cancel it. This is the **persistence rule**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cutter Compensation Offset Vector

> **Problem Statement:** A 6 mm diameter end mill is commanded to move linearly from $(0,0)$ to $(50,0)$ with **G42 D01** active, where D01 stores the cutter radius $r = 3$ mm. Find the actual cutter-center path coordinates.

### Step 1 — Determine the direction of travel

The tool moves along +X axis. Facing **along the +X direction**, the tool's right side is the **−Y direction**.

### Step 2 — Apply the G42 rule

G42 = cutter on the **right** of the programmed path. So the cutter center must be offset to the **−Y side**.

### Step 3 — Compute the offset

The offset is perpendicular to the path, magnitude equal to the cutter radius $r$:

$$
\Delta y = -r = -3 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — The actual machine path

$$
\text{Cutter center path: from } (0, -3) \text{ to } (50, -3)
$$

> **Practical consequence:** The workpiece surface (where the cutter touches) is on the +Y side of the programmed line — exactly the desired edge of the part.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Safe Start Block

> **KTU Favourite:** Explain every word in `N10 G21 G90 G40 G80 G54 T01 M06`

| Word | Full Name | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| N10 | Sequence Number | Block label for easy reference |
| G21 | Metric Units | All dimensions in mm |
| G90 | Absolute Mode | Coordinates from work origin |
| G40 | Cutter Comp Cancel | Safety — no left/right comp active |
| G80 | Canned Cycle Cancel | Safety — deactivates any pre-set drilling cycles |
| G54 | Work Coordinate 1 | Activates Work Offset #1 (W vector) |
| T01 | Tool Select | Calls Tool #1 from magazine |
| M06 | Tool Change | Executes automatic tool change |

> [!IMPORTANT] **G40 and G80 are "Safety Codes"**
> They are issued at the top of a program to ensure the machine starts in a *clean state* — no leftover compensation or canned cycles from a previous job. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

## 3.6 Verification Matrix: Absolute vs Incremental (G90 vs G91)

Given the points P1(10,10), P2(30,25), P3(50,10):

| Block | Absolute (G90) → Machine reads | Incremental (G91) → Machine reads |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Start at P1 | G00 X10 Y10 | G00 X10 Y10 |
| Move to P2 | X30 Y25 | X20 Y15 (Δ from P1) |
| Move to P3 | X50 Y10 | X20 Y-15 (Δ from P2) |

> **Engineering Judgment:** Incremental is useful for **repeating patterns** (e.g., bolt-circle drilling) because the same delta can be repeated. Absolute is safer for **general programming** because a missed block does not corrupt the rest of the program.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CNC Part Program — Block Architecture Flow

This block diagram maps the **logical structure** of a CNC part program from start to finish. Every KTU program obeys this hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Header O0001] --> B[Safe Start Block N10]
    B --> C1[Set Units G20 or G21]
    B --> C2[Set Mode G90 or G91]
    B --> C3[Cancel Comp G40 G80]
    B --> C4[Activate Work Offset G54 G55 G56 G57 G58 G59]
    C4 --> D[Tool Change Block T01 M06]
    D --> E[Spindle Block S RPM M03 M04]
    E --> F1{Machining Loop Start}
    F1 --> F2[Rapid G00 to start of cut]
    F2 --> F3[Linear G01 or Arc G02 G03]
    F3 --> F4[Set Feedrate F]
    F4 --> F5[Set Spindle S if needed]
    F5 --> F6[Apply Cutter Comp G41 G42 G43 if needed]
    F6 --> G{More features?}
    G -- Yes --> F1
    G -- No --> H[Coolant OFF M09]
    H --> I[Spindle OFF M05]
    I --> J[Return to Ref Point G28]
    J --> K[Program End M30]
```

---

## 4.2 The Right-Hand Rule — Axis Identification (Milling)

The following diagram shows the **standard 3-axis Cartesian orientation** as expected in KTU answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A0[Right Hand Rule CNC Milling]
        direction LR
        XA[Positive X axis to the right]:::ax
        YA[Positive Y axis into the page]:::ax
        ZA[Positive Z axis upward]:::ax
    end
    classDef ax fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **How to read it:** On a vertical-spindle milling machine, +Z is *up* (tool moving away from table), +X is the longest table travel, and +Y is the perpendicular cross travel.

---

## 4.3 Tool Offset Memory — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Part Program Block: G43 H01] --> C[Controller Looks up H01]
    C --> T[Tool Offset Table]
    T --> V1[H01 stores length Z = 87.325 mm]
    T --> V2[D01 stores diameter = 6 mm]
    V1 --> O1[Apply +87.325 mm shift on Z axis]
    V2 --> O2[Apply 3 mm radial offset G41 or G42]
    O1 --> M[Machine moves to corrected position]
    O2 --> M
```

---

## 4.4 Modal Code Grouping — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G1[Group 01 Motion]
        GA[G00 Rapid]
        GB[G01 Linear]
        GC[G02 Arc CW]
        GD[G03 Arc CCW]
        GE[G04 Dwell]
    end
    subgraph G3[Group 03 Coord]
        GF[G90 Absolute]
        GG[G91 Incremental]
    end
    subgraph G6[Group 06 Units]
        GH[G20 Inch]
        GI[G21 Metric]
    end
    subgraph G7[Group 07 Cutter Comp]
        GJ[G40 Cancel]
        GK[G41 Left]
        GL[G42 Right]
    end
    subgraph G12[Group 12 Work Offset]
        GM[G54]
        GN[G55]
        GO[G56]
        GP[G57]
        GQ[G58]
        GR[G59]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 G41 vs G42 — Walking Direction Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Walk[G41 vs G42 Walking Test]
        direction LR
        ST[Start Position] --> DIR[Direction of Travel = +X]
        DIR --> L{G41 or G42}
        L -- G41 --> LL[Tool is on the LEFT]
        L -- G42 --> RR[Tool is on the RIGHT]
    end
```

> **Memory Hook:** "**G41 = Left, G42 = Right**" → 1 = L, 2 = R (alphabetical position).

---

## 4.6 Turning Center Axis Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lathe[Lathe Axis Convention]
        direction LR
        ZAX[Z axis along spindle horizontal]
        XAX[X axis radial toward operator]
        CW[Chuck rotates work piece]
    end
```

> **Key difference from milling:** The **workpiece** rotates in a lathe, not the tool. Hence, **G02/G03 directions appear reversed** from milling when viewed from the operator's side.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Preparatory Function" (G-code) and explain the concept of "modal" G-codes with two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A **Preparatory Function (G-code)** is a code word that prepares the CNC controller for a specific type of motion or function (linear, circular, rapid, etc.) for all subsequent blocks.
- **(1 mark)** A **modal G-code** remains active in the controller's memory even after its block is executed. It continues to affect all subsequent blocks until another code from the *same group* cancels it.
- **(1 mark)** *Examples:* **G01** (linear interpolation, modal of group 01) and **G21** (metric units, modal of group 06). Once issued, they stay active until G00/G02/G03 (group 01) or G20 (group 06) appear.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between G90 and G91 with one industrial application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **G90 (Absolute Programming):** All coordinates are measured from a fixed program origin (work zero). Every target point is referenced to the same zero.
- **(1 mark)** **G91 (Incremental Programming):** All coordinates are measured from the *current* tool position; each move is a "delta" from where the tool currently is.
- **(1 mark)** *Application:* **G90** is used for general pocket milling and turning where direct dimensional reference is critical. **G91** is used for **repetitive hole patterns** (e.g., PCD drilling on a flange) where a single delta can be reissued.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the general structure of a CNC part program. List the standard G-codes and M-codes used in a typical safety-start block and justify why each is included.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A CNC milling machine uses a 4-flute HSS end mill of diameter 16 mm to cut a slot in mild steel. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and the feed per tooth is 0.04 mm/tooth. Calculate (i) the spindle speed $N$, (ii) the table feedrate $F_f$, and (iii) the time required to cut a 120 mm long slot at the calculated feed. Write the corresponding NC blocks.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**(2 marks) Block Diagram:**

```
O0001 (HEADER)
 ↓
N10 G21 G90 G40 G80 G54 (SAFETY START BLOCK)
 ↓
N20 T01 M06 (TOOL CHANGE)
 ↓
N30 S__ M03 (SPINDLE ON CW)
 ↓
N40 G00 X__ Y__ Z__ (RAPID TO POSITION)
 ↓
N50 G01 Z__ F__ (PLUNGE INTO WORKPIECE)
 ↓
N60 G01 X__ Y__ F__ (CUTTING PASS)
 ↓
... [more machining blocks] ...
 ↓
N__ M09 (COOLANT OFF)
 ↓
N__ M05 (SPINDLE OFF)
 ↓
N__ G28 (RETURN TO REFERENCE)
 ↓
N__ M30 (END & REWIND)
```

**(1 mark each — 4 marks total)** Justification of safety block:

| Code | Justification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **G21** | Forces **metric units**, avoiding accidental inch/metric mismatch (a major cause of crashes). |
| **G90** | Forces **absolute** mode, ensuring all coordinates reference the work origin (consistent with part drawings). |
| **G40** | **Cancels** any leftover cutter-radius compensation from a previous program. |
| **G80** | **Cancels** any active canned cycle (G81–G89) from a previous program. |
| **G54** | Activates the **Work Coordinate System #1**, shifting machine coordinates to part coordinates. |

**(1 mark)** Conclusion: Together, these codes provide a **clean, predictable machine state** at the start of execution — a non-negotiable industry practice.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $z = 4$ flutes, $D = 16$ mm, $V = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, $L = 120$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed [2 marks]**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 16}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{25000}{50.265} \approx 497.36 \text{ RPM}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{program}} = 497 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**(ii) Table Feedrate [2 marks]**

$$
F_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.04 \times 4 \times 497
$$

$$
F_f = 79.52 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_f \approx 80 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

**(iii) Cycle Time [1.5 marks]**

$$
T = \dfrac{L}{F_f} = \dfrac{120}{79.52} \approx 1.509 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 1.51 \text{ min } \approx 90.5 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**(1.5 marks) NC Program Blocks:**

```
N10 G21 G90 G40 G80 G54    (SAFETY BLOCK)
N20 T01 M06                (TOOL 1 CHANGE)
N30 S497 M03               (SPINDLE CW AT 497 RPM)
N40 G00 X0 Y0              (RAPID TO WORK ORIGIN)
N50 Z5.0                   (RAPID TO 5 MM ABOVE WORK)
N60 G01 Z-3.0 F80          (PLUNGE 3 MM AT 80 MM/MIN)
N70 G01 X120.0 F80         (CUT 120 MM SLOT AT 80 MM/MIN)
N80 G00 Z5.0               (RETRACT)
N90 M05                    (SPINDLE OFF)
N100 M30                   (PROGRAM END)
```

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the ISO 841 right-hand rule for CNC axis identification. Apply it to define the axes for (i) a vertical-spindle machining center, and (ii) a horizontal turning center. Draw neat diagrams.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the difference between **G41**, **G42**, and **G40** with a labelled diagram. A 10 mm diameter end mill is to profile a rectangular pocket of 80 × 50 mm. The cutter center is to be programmed. If G42 D01 is applied (D01 = 5 mm), write the new tool path coordinates for a clockwise profile starting from the lower-left corner.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**(3 marks) Right-Hand Rule Definition:**

The **ISO 841 / EIA RS-267** right-hand rule states:
1. **Z-axis:** Always parallel to the **principal spindle axis**. Positive Z is the direction in which the tool moves *away* from the workpiece (for milling) or *away* from the chuck (for turning).
2. **X-axis:** Always the **longest travel** of the machine, perpendicular to Z. Positive X is conventionally to the right when looking from the principal spindle toward the workpiece.
3. **Y-axis:** Determined by the right-hand rule — perpendicular to both X and Z, completing the right-handed triad.

**(2 marks) Vertical Spindle Machining Center:**

- Z = vertical (up = +Z)
- X = longest horizontal travel (table left-right)
- Y = remaining cross travel (table front-back)

**(2 marks) Horizontal Turning Center (Lathe):**

- Z = horizontal, along spindle axis (away from chuck = +Z)
- X = radial direction (toward operator often = +X; away = −X)
- No Y axis (or Y is unused for standard 2-axis turning)

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**(2 marks) G40 / G41 / G42 Explanation:**

| Code | Meaning | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **G40** | Cutter Compensation **Cancel** | Always active during non-profiling moves (rapids, plunges). |
| **G41** | Compensation **LEFT** of programmed path, in direction of travel | External profiling where the part is on the left of the path. |
| **G42** | Compensation **RIGHT** of programmed path, in direction of travel | External profiling where the part is on the right of the path (e.g., male boss). |

**(1.5 marks) Diagram (textual):**

```
        G42 — Tool to the RIGHT of path (inside a pocket, walking CW)
        ╔══════════════╗   ← Part edge (workpiece)
        ║  P0 → P1     ║
        ║              ║   Tool centre path is offset INWARD
        ║   ↓ C        ║   by the cutter radius (D01)
        ║              ║
        ║  P3 ← P2     ║
        ╚══════════════╝
```

**(3.5 marks) New Tool-Path Calculation:**

Original (workpiece edge) corners starting at lower-left, going CW:
- P0 = (0, 0)
- P1 = (80, 0)
- P2 = (80, 50)
- P3 = (0, 50)

**G42 D01** means tool center is offset **outward** (to the right of CW travel) by $r = 5$ mm.

For each edge, the offset is perpendicular to the edge, *outward* (away from the pocket interior, since the tool walks *along* the *outer* edge of a CW island — careful: G42 for a male boss = tool inside the boss).

**Standard convention for a CW-milled pocket with G42:** Tool center path is offset **inward** by $r = 5$ mm, producing a *smaller* rectangle.

New corners:
- P0′ = (5, 5)
- P1′ = (75, 5)
- P2′ = (75, 45)
- P3′ = (5, 45)

**NC Program:**

```
N10 G21 G90 G40 G80 G54
N20 T01 M06
N30 S1000 M03
N40 G00 X5.0 Y5.0           (RAPID TO P0')
N50 Z2.0                    (APPROACH)
N60 G01 Z-2.0 F50           (PLUNGE)
N70 G42 D01 X75.0 Y5.0 F100 (ACTIVATE G42, MOVE TO P1')
N80 X75.0 Y45.0             (P1' → P2')
N90 X5.0  Y45.0             (P2' → P3')
N100 X5.0 Y5.0              (P3' → P0', CLOSE)
N110 G40 X10.0 Y10.0        (CANCEL COMP)
N120 G00 Z50.0 M05          (RETRACT, SPINDLE OFF)
N130 M30                    (END)
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Where KTU students lose marks (examiner comments)**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $N$ and $F$ calculations** — Always write **"RPM"** and **"mm/min"** explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong G41/G42 selection** — When in doubt, draw the path on paper, walk along it, and decide whether the tool is on your left or right. Do *not* guess.
> 3. **Issuing G41/G42 in a block with no motion** — Compensation must be **activated during a linear move (G01)**; it cannot be turned on in a G00 block on most controllers.
> 4. **Not specifying the G-code group** when justifying modal behaviour** — Examiners explicitly look for the phrase *"modal code of group 01"* for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the safety block (G40 G80)** in written programs** — Always begin with `G21 G90 G40 G80 G54`. Omitting G40/G80 costs 1–2 marks easily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Basics):**

- [ ] The **right-hand rule** (ISO 841) defines Z along the **principal spindle**; X is the longest travel; Y completes the triad.
- [ ] **G-codes** are *preparatory* and *modal by group*; **M-codes** are *miscellaneous* and mostly *one-shot*.
- [ ] The **safety-start block** = `G21 G90 G40 G80 G54` — memorize as one inseparable string.
- [ ] **G90 = Absolute** (from work zero); **G91 = Incremental** (from current position).
- [ ] **G00 = Rapid** (no cutting, max feed); **G01 = Linear interpolation** (cutting, programmed F).
- [ ] **G02 = CW arc**; **G03 = CCW arc**; **G17/G18/G19 = plane selection** for arcs.
- [ ] **G43 = Tool length compensation positive**; **G44 = negative**; **G49 = cancel**.
- [ ] **G41 = Left, G42 = Right** (1=L, 2=R — alphabetical mnemonic).
- [ ] **M03 = Spindle CW, M04 = CCW, M05 = Stop**; **M08 = Coolant ON, M09 = OFF**.
- [ ] **M06 = Tool change**; **M30 = Program end & rewind**; **M00 = Unconditional stop**.
- [ ] **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$
- [ ] **Milling feed:** $F_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$
- [ ] **Turning feed:** $F = N \cdot f_r$
- [ ] **Cycle time:** $T = \dfrac{L}{F_f}$
- [ ] Always **round down** RPM for safety; never exceed recommended cutting speed.
- [ ] **Work offsets G54–G59** store the vector from machine zero to work zero.
- [ ] When the question says "metric" or nothing — **default is G21**.
- [ ] **G04 P\__** introduces a dwell (pause) in seconds — useful for chip-breaking on lathes.
- [ ] **Modal code persistence** is the most-tested theoretical concept in 3-mark questions.
- [ ] In a **lathe**, the workpiece rotates — so **G02/G03 visual direction is reversed** compared to milling.
- [ ] Cutter compensation must be **activated in a G01 block** with at least one linear move.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of CNC Lathe, Familiarization of Control Panel, Fundamentals of CNC Programming, Part Programming Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of CNC Lathe, Familiarization of Control Panel, Fundamentals of CNC Programming, Part Programming Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CNC Lathe — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC Lathe (Computer Numerical Control Lathe)** is a machine tool in which the relative motion between the workpiece and the cutting tool, along with auxiliary functions (spindle on/off, coolant on/off, tool change, etc.), is automatically controlled by a microprocessor-based controller executing a stored sequence of alphanumeric instructions called a **part program**.

The CNC lathe rotates the workpiece about a fixed axis (the **Z-axis**) while a single-point cutting tool, mounted on a programmable turret, removes material to produce cylindrical, conical, contoured, threaded, or grooved surfaces. The 2024-scheme KTU syllabus treats the CNC lathe as a *2.5-axis contouring system* under standard configuration, with optional **C-axis** and **live tooling** for mill-turn operations.

### 1.2 Axes of a Standard CNC Lathe (KTU 2024 Specification)

| Axis | Direction | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Z** | Parallel to spindle axis (longitudinal) | Controls length of cut / facing |
| **X** | Perpendicular to spindle (radial) | Controls diameter reduction |
| **Y** (optional) | Orthogonal to X and Z | Eccentric / mill-turn |
| **C** (optional) | Rotary about Z | Spindle indexing / driven tooling |

> [!NOTE]
> On a lathe, the **X-axis is normally programmed as a diameter** (twice the radial distance from the centerline). This is a *convention*, not a physical change in travel.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **traditional turner (lathe operator)** standing beside a manual lathe. He constantly:
1. Turns a handwheel to move the tool left/right (Z travel).
2. Turns a cross-slide to feed the tool toward the workpiece (X travel).
3. Reads a dial to know his position.
4. Sets spindle speed and feed from a gear box.
5. Stops the machine and changes the tool manually.

Now **replace this human with a microcontroller** that has been "told" the exact sequence of moves in advance, in a simple language made of letters and numbers — that's a **CNC lathe**. The "language" is **G-code and M-code**; the "handwheels" are **servo motors**; the "dial" is the **digital position display**; the "gear box" is the **variable-frequency spindle drive**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the part program as a **recipe**: "Go to this position, turn the spindle on, move here at this speed, switch coolant on, retract, change tool, repeat." The CNC reads this recipe line-by-line and executes it tirelessly.

### 1.4 CNC Control Panel — Functional Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Control Panel (HMI — Human Machine Interface)** of a CNC lathe is the operator's command center. It consists of a **display unit**, a **keypad**, a **Manual Pulse Generator (MPG / handwheel)**, mode-selection switches, and emergency-stop hardware. It allows the operator to write, edit, simulate, store, retrieve, and execute part programs, as well as to jog axes manually, set tool offsets, and monitor machine status in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, familiarity with both **FANUC-style** and **SIEMENS-style** panel layouts is expected, but FANUC is the de-facto reference because of its dominance in industrial CNC lathes and its presence in most KTU-affiliated college labs.

---

### 1.5 Fundamentals of CNC Programming — Core Idea

CNC programming is the act of writing a **part program** — an ordered list of blocks (lines) of instructions that the controller interprets to drive axes and auxiliary functions. Each **block** is a complete instruction, consisting of one or more **words**. A **word** is a letter (address) followed by a number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Program = Sequence of Blocks → Block = Sequence of Words → Word = Address + Number**
> Example: `G01 X50.0 Z-30.0 F0.2 ;` is one block containing four words.

A typical KTU/industrial CNC program is divided into three regions:
- **Header / Setup Block** — sets units, modal states, tool selection, work offset, spindle command.
- **Machining Blocks** — actual contouring, drilling, threading, etc.
- **Tail / End Block** — tool return to home, spindle stop, coolant off, program end (`M30`).

### 1.6 Part Programming Techniques — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part Programming Techniques** are the structured, time-saving methods used to write efficient CNC turning programs. They include the use of **canned cycles** (G90, G92, G94, G76, G81, etc.), **subprograms** (call/return), **parametric programming** (variables, conditional jumps, looping via macro B / macro programs), **cutter/geometry compensation** (G41/G42, G43, G40), **tool nose radius compensation (TNRC)**, and **proper tool-path sequencing** (roughing, semi-finishing, finishing, parting).

These techniques reduce program length, improve surface finish, increase MRR, prevent collisions, and allow reuse of proven logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CNC Lathe — Structural & Operational Breakdown

### A. Mechanical Subsystems
- **Headstock**: Houses the **main spindle**, driven by a **servo or AC spindle motor** through a gearless direct-drive or two-step gear box. Carries the **chuck** (3-jaw self-centering / 4-jaw independent) for workpiece clamping.
- **Bed**: Heavy, vibration-damping **cast iron** structure. Provides the datum for slide alignment. In slant-bed CNC lathes, the bed is inclined (~30°–60°) to allow gravity chip evacuation.
- **Carriage (X–Z slides)**: Two **linear motion guides** with **ball screws** driven by **servo motors**. Position feedback from **linear encoders / rotary encoders** → closed-loop control.
- **Cross-slide (X-axis)**: Mounted on the carriage, moves perpendicular to the spindle.
- **Tool Turret**: Indexing turret (8, 12, or 16 stations) carrying live or static tools. **Bidirectional** random-tool-selection, typically index time **0.2 – 0.5 s**.
- **Tailstock**: Supports long workpieces; can be programmed for quill advance/retract (advanced lathes).
- **Coolant & Chip Conveyor**: Flood coolant or high-pressure through-tool coolant; chip conveyor removes swarf.

### B. Control / Electrical Subsystems
- **CNC Unit**: Industrial PC + DSP-based motion controller. Runs **G-code interpreter** + **look-ahead interpolator** + **servo loop** at kHz rates.
- **Servo Drive Amplifiers**: One per axis (X, Z, optional Y). Take ±10 V or fieldbus commands from CNC.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)**: Handles M-codes, tool changer, chuck clamping, door interlocks, coolant.
- **HMI Panel**: Display + keypad + MPG (discussed in §2.3).

### C. Why a Lathe? Operational Logic
A lathe removes material by **2D orthogonal cutting** in a plane containing the spindle axis. The tool is fed in **two linear directions (X, Z)** plus a **rotational input (spindle)**. Any axisymmetric part can therefore be described by its **2D profile**, and the controller must replicate this profile by **interpolation** (linear G01, circular G02/G03).

## 2.2 Coordinate System — Adherence to ISO 841 / EIA RS 267

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard right-hand Cartesian coordinate system is used:
> - **+Z** points from the workpiece toward the **tailstock** (away from chuck).
> - **+X** points from the spindle centerline **outward toward the operator** (radial, diameter).
> - **+Y** completes the right-hand triad.
> - All programmed coordinates are **workpiece coordinates** referenced to a **programmable zero point** (typically on the spindle nose face or the front face of the finished part).

The **machine zero (M)** is set by the manufacturer; the **workpiece zero / program zero (W)** is set by the operator (or by the G54–G59 work offset).

## 2.3 CNC Control Panel — Deep Dive (FANUC-Style Reference)

### A. Display Unit
- **CRT / LCD / TFT** color screen.
- Displays current position, program, offset tables, alarm history, modal states, simulation graphics.

### B. Keypad Layout — Functional Groups

| Group | Keys | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| **Mode Selection** | AUTO / MDI / EDIT / JOG / HANDLE / RAPID / ZRN (Zero Return) / DNC / TEACH | Select operating mode |
| **Program Mgmt** | PRGRM / OPR / DIR / INPUT / OUTPUT / ALTER / INSERT / DELETE / CAN / EOB | Write / edit / save / transfer programs |
| **Function Keys** | POS / PROG / OFFSET / SYSTEM / MESSAGE / GRAPH / ALARM | Switch display pages |
| **Soft Keys (F1–F5)** | Context-sensitive | Page sub-menus |
| **Data Input** | Numeric 0–9, sign, decimal, address keys (X, Z, Y, F, S, T, M, G) | Enter values |
| **Cycle Start / Feed Hold** | CYCLE START (green), FEED HOLD (red, illuminated) | Program run control |
| **Manual Pulse Generator (MPG)** | Handwheel with x1, x10, x100, x1000 multipliers | Manual micro-moves |
| **Safety** | EMERGENCY STOP (mushroom, red) | Hardware cutoff of all power |

### C. Mode-of-Operation Logic

- **AUTO**: Executes a stored program automatically — used in production.
- **MDI (Manual Data Input)**: Operator types a single block and executes it — for quick checks / setting work offsets.
- **EDIT**: Used to write or modify programs stored in CNC memory.
- **JOG**: Manual continuous axis movement while a direction key is held.
- **HANDLE / MPG**: Manual micro-positioning via handwheel.
- **RAPID**: Manual high-speed moves (G00).
- **ZRN (Zero Return / Reference)**: Drives each axis to its **machine zero** to establish a known reference position. Mandatory at power-up.
- **DNC**: Direct Numerical Control — runs the program streamed from an external computer / tape.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab viva, the most common question is: *"What is the first operation after switching on a CNC lathe?"* — Correct answer: **Reference Return (ZRN / Home)**, then **tool offset setting**, then **work offset setting (G54)**, then run program.

## 2.4 Fundamentals of CNC Programming — Logic Stack

### A. Program Structure (ISO 14649 / ISO 6983 Compliant)

```
O0001 ;                            (Program number)
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99 ;             (Safety / initial modal reset)
N20 G28 U0. W0. ;                 (Reference return)
N30 T0101 ;                       (Tool 1, offset 1)
N40 M08 ;                         (Coolant on)
N50 G96 S200 ;                    (Constant surface speed 200 m/min)
N60 G00 X50. Z2. ;                (Rapid to start point)
N70 G01 Z-30. F0.2 ;              (Linear feed)
N80 G00 X55. ;                    (Retract radially)
N90 G00 Z2. ;                     (Retract axially)
N100 M09 ;                        (Coolant off)
N110 M05 ;                        (Spindle stop)
N120 M30 ;                        (Program end, rewind)
%
```

### B. Address / Word Conventions (FANUC Lathe)

| Address | Meaning | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **N** | Sequence (block) number | `N40` |
| **G** | Preparatory function (modal / non-modal) | `G01` |
| **X, Z, Y, C** | Axis command (diameter mode for X on lathe) | `X40.0` |
| **I, K, R** | Arc / circular interpolation parameters | `I-5.0` |
| **F** | Feedrate (mm/rev in G99, mm/min in G98) | `F0.2` |
| **S** | Spindle speed or surface speed (depends on G96/G97) | `S1500` |
| **T** | Tool number + offset number | `T0303` |
| **M** | Miscellaneous function | `M08` |
| **O** | Program number | `O0100` |
| **;** or LF | End of block | — |

### C. Modal vs Non-Modal G-Codes
- **Modal G-codes** remain active until another code in the same group is issued (e.g., G01 stays active through subsequent moves).
- **Non-modal G-codes** (G04 dwell, G28 reference return) execute once only.

## 2.5 Part Programming Techniques — Structured Toolbox

### A. Canned Turning Cycles (FANUC)
| Cycle | G-Code | Function |
|-------|--------|----------|
| **Turning (straight)** | G90 | Straight turning OD/ID/bore (roughing) |
| **Threading** | G92 | Straight / tapered thread (single pass; loop for multi-pass) |
| **Facing** | G94 | End-face turning cycle |
| **Threading (multi-pass)** | G76 | Multi-pass thread cutting cycle |
| **Drilling (peck)** | G74 | Peck drilling on Z-axis (Z-axis cycle for slotting) |
| **Grooving / parting** | G75 | OD/ID grooving, peck retraction |

### B. Cutter & Tool Compensation
- **G40 / G41 / G42** — Tool Nose Radius Compensation (TNRC): Left / Right of contour, none.
- **G43 / G49** — Tool Length Compensation (positive direction); cancel.
- **G54–G59** — Work-coordinate system selection (6 pairs).
- **G50** — Spindle speed clamp (sets max RPM) or work coordinate on some controls.

### C. Subprograms and Macros
- **M98 P _ L _** — call subprogram P, L times.
- **M99** — return from subprogram.
- **Macro B / Custom Macro** — `#1 = VAR`, `IF [#1 GT 10] GOTO 50`, `WHILE ... DO ... END`.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula you will need in KTU Module-3 numerical problems. **Memorize units** — they decide marks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Spindle speed (constant RPM) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | rev/min | V = cutting speed (m/min), D = dia (mm) |
| 2 | Cutting speed from RPM | $V = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000}$ | m/min | Reverse of #1 |
| 3 | Feedrate (mm/min) | $f_r = f \, N$ | mm/min | f = feed/rev (mm), N in rev/min |
| 4 | Feedrate (mm/rev) | $f = \dfrac{f_r}{N}$ | mm/rev | Inverse of #3 |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (turning) | $\text{MRR} = f \, d \, V$ | mm³/min | f = feed/rev, d = depth of cut (mm), V = cutting speed (m/min) |
| 6 | Net cutting power | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^{6}}$ | kW | Kc = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 7 | Machining time (turning) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{L}{f \, N}$ | min | L = length of cut in mm |
| 8 | Facing time | $t_m = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{2 \, f \, N}$ | min | Tool travels radially from D1 to D2 |
| 9 | Thread pitch → depth | $d_t = 0.6134 \, p$ | mm | For V-threads (metric ISO) |
| 10 | Thread depth (general) | $d_t \approx 0.54 \, p$ | mm | For ACME / unified |
| 11 | Spindle speed clamp | $N_{\max} = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D_{\min}}$ | rev/min | Used in G96 to prevent overspeed at small diameter |
| 12 | Tool change index time | $t_{idx} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ | s | Added to cycle time |
| 13 | Program block / cycle structure | — | — | Header → Machining → Tail |
| 14 | Reference / Work zero | — | — | M (machine) vs W (program) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiner usually gives V (cutting speed), f, and D and asks for N, MRR, P, or t_m — all derivable from the table above in **one step each**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed (V)

The cutting speed **V** is defined as the *tangential velocity of the workpiece surface* relative to the tool edge. For a workpiece of instantaneous diameter **D**, the circumference is $\pi D$ and one revolution advances a point on the surface by $\pi D$ mm. Therefore, for **N revolutions per minute**, the linear surface speed is:

$$V \;=\; \frac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}$$

where D is in **mm** (the 1000 converts mm/min to m/min). Solving for **N** explicitly:

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} \;\; \text{rev/min}$$

**Numerical demonstration (KTU-typical):**
Given: V = 180 m/min, initial diameter D₁ = 80 mm, final D₂ = 70 mm (G96 constant surface speed mode). Find the spindle-speed variation.

For D₁:

$$N_1 = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{180000}{251.327} \approx 716.2 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

For D₂:

$$N_2 = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 70} = \frac{180000}{219.911} \approx 818.6 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

Therefore, in **G96 mode** the controller will automatically increase RPM from ~716 to ~819 as the tool reduces the diameter — keeping V constant, which yields uniform surface finish.

> [!TIP]
> In **G97** mode, the spindle runs at a fixed RPM, and V varies with D. Choose G96 for finishing (uniform finish) and G97 for roughing (constant torque).

## 3.2 Derivation of Feedrate (fr) and Machining Time (tm)

Feed **f** is the axial advance per revolution (mm/rev). For **N** rpm, the linear feedrate is:

$$f_r = f \times N \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

If the length of cut is **L** (mm), the time to complete one pass is:

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \;\; \text{minutes}$$

**Numerical demonstration:**
Given: L = 60 mm, f = 0.25 mm/rev, N = 800 rpm.

$$f_r = 0.25 \times 800 = 200 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$t_m = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 \;\; \text{min} = 18 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

If multiple roughing passes are required at depth of cut **d = 2 mm** and total radial reduction ΔD = 6 mm, the number of passes is:

$$n_{\text{passes}} = \frac{\Delta D}{2 d} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 2 \text{ passes (last pass light)}$$

Total roughing time ≈ 2 × 18 s = 36 s (excluding rapid and tool-index times).

## 3.3 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) and Power (Pc)

The instantaneous chip cross-section in turning is approximately:

$$A_c = f \times d \;\; \text{mm}^{2}$$

The uncut chip length per minute is the surface speed V (m/min × 1000 = mm/min). Hence:

$$\text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot V \;\; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

Net cutting power (the mechanical power consumed at the tool tip):

$$P_c = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Numerical demonstration:**
Given: f = 0.3 mm/rev, d = 2 mm, V = 150 m/min, Kc (mild steel) = 2000 N/mm².

$$\text{MRR} = 0.3 \times 2 \times 150 = 90 \;\; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min} \;\; \text{(wait, this is mm}^{3}\text{/min, not /sec — check: f is per rev, V is m/min → MRR in mm}^{3}\text{/min directly)}$$

$$P_c = \frac{90 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{180000}{60 \times 10^{6}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kW} = 3.0 \;\; \text{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A very common KTU mistake: students forget that **V in the MRR formula is in m/min** when multiplied by **f in mm/rev × d in mm** — units come out as **mm³/min** directly. Do not multiply by 1000 again.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing G-Code by Operation (KTU 2024 Pattern)

| Desired Operation | Recommended G-Code(s) | Auxiliaries | Critical Note |
|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Rapid traverse to start point | G00 | — | No cutting; not used in tool path |
| Straight turning (linear) | G01 | F, T | Specify F (feed/rev in G99) |
| Tapered turning | G01 with X and Z in same block | — | Use endpoint X, Z (diameter) |
| Circular arc (CW — from +Z to +X) | G02 | I, K or R | I, K = vector from start to center |
| Circular arc (CCW) | G03 | I, K or R | Mirror logic of G02 |
| Absolute / Incremental | G90 / G91 | — | Group 3 — mutually exclusive |
| Feed per rev / per min | G99 / G98 | — | Group 5 — G99 default on lathes |
| Const. surface speed / const. RPM | G96 / G97 | S, G50 clamp | G50 limits Nmax in G96 |
| Threading (multi-pass) | G76 P_Q_R_ / G76 X_Z_R_P_Q_F_ | — | F = thread lead (pitch) |
| Drilling (axial) | G74 R_ / G74 Z_Q_F_ | — | Peck retract for chip break |
| Grooving | G75 R_ / G75 X_Z_P_Q_F_ | — | P = peck depth, Q = retract |
| Cutter compensation on/off | G41 / G42 / G40 | D_ | TNRC + G00/G01 only |
| Work-coordinate system | G54–G59 | — | All G54 if single setup |
| Dwell | G04 X_ or U_ | — | Time in seconds |
| Program stop / end | M00 / M01 / M02 / M30 | — | M30 = end + rewind |

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Problem — Full Turned Part

**Statement (KTU Module 3 typical):**
A CNC lathe is roughing a mild-steel shaft from Ø60 mm to Ø54 mm over a length of 40 mm. Cutting speed V = 150 m/min, feed f = 0.3 mm/rev, depth of cut d = 1.5 mm. Specific cutting force Kc = 2000 N/mm². Find (i) N, (ii) fr, (iii) MRR, (iv) Pc, (v) tm.

**Step (i) — Spindle speed at average diameter Ø57 mm:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 57} = \frac{150000}{179.07} \approx 837.6 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

In G97 mode we use average D; in G96 the controller would automatically vary N from 796 rpm (at Ø60) to 884 rpm (at Ø54).

**Step (ii) — Feedrate:**

$$f_r = 0.3 \times 837.6 \approx 251.3 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**Step (iii) — MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = 0.3 \times 1.5 \times 150 = 67.5 \;\; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

**Step (iv) — Net power:**

$$P_c = \frac{67.5 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^{6}} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kW} = 2.25 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step (v) — Machining time:**

$$t_m = \frac{40}{251.3} = 0.1592 \;\; \text{min} \approx 9.55 \;\; \text{s}$$

Number of passes for radial reduction ΔD = 6 mm at d = 1.5 mm:

$$n_{\text{passes}} = \frac{6}{2 \times 1.5} = 2 \;\; \text{passes}$$

Total time ≈ 2 × 9.55 = 19.1 s of cutting time (add ~1.5 s for tool index and rapid return to complete cycle).

> [!NOTE]
> This single problem covers **6 marks** worth of derivations in a KTU Part-B sub-question. Practice variant values until the algebraic pattern is automatic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Lathe — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional interaction** between every sub-assembly of a CNC turning center. The architecture follows the standard architecture of a closed-loop CNC system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PG[PART PROGRAM G CODE] --> CNC[CNC CONTROLLER DSP MOTION PLANNER]
    CNC --> SVX[SERVO DRIVE X AXIS]
    CNC --> SVZ[SERVO DRIVE Z AXIS]
    CNC --> PLC[PLC M CODE HANDLER]
    SVX --> BLX[BALL SCREW X]
    SVZ --> BLZ[BALL SCREW Z]
    BLX --> CRG[CARRIAGE CROSS SLIDE]
    BLZ --> CRG
    CRG --> TURR[TOOL TURRET 8 OR 12 STATION]
    SPIN[AC SPINDLE MOTOR VFD DRIVE] --> CHK[CHUCK 3 JAW]
    CHK --> WP[WORKPIECE]
    TURR --> CUT[CUTTING TOOL]
    CUT -. removes material from .-> WP
    WP --> COOL[COOLANT NOZZLE]
    PLC --> COOL
    PLC --> TURR
    PLC --> SPIN
    PLC --> CHK
    HMI[HMI CONTROL PANEL DISPLAY KEYPAD MPG] <--> CNC
    ENC[LINEAR AND ROTARY ENCODERS FEEDBACK] --> CNC
    BLX --> ENC
    BLZ --> ENC
    SPIN --> ENC
```

## 4.2 Control Panel — Functional Block Layout (FANUC-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DISP[DISPLAY UNIT]
        SCR[CRT OR LCD SCREEN]
        SK1[SOFT KEYS F1 F5]
        SK2[PAGE SELECTOR POS PROG OFFSET ALARM MESSAGE GRAPH]
    end
    subgraph MODE[MODE SELECTION]
        MOD1[AUTO]
        MOD2[MDI]
        MOD3[EDIT]
        MOD4[JOG]
        MOD5[HANDLE MPG]
        MOD6[RAPID]
        MOD7[ZRN HOME]
        MOD8[DNC]
    end
    subgraph PROG[PROGRAM MANAGEMENT KEYS]
        PR1[PRGRM]
        PR2[DIR]
        PR3[INPUT]
        PR4[OUTPUT]
        PR5[INSERT]
        PR6[ALTER]
        PR7[DELETE]
        PR8[CAN]
    end
    subgraph DATA[DATA ENTRY]
        ADDR[ADDRESS KEYS X Z Y F S T M G]
        NUM[NUMERIC KEYPAD 0 TO 9]
    end
    subgraph SAFE[SAFETY AND CONTROL]
        CST[CYCLE START GREEN]
        FH[FEED HOLD RED]
        EST[EMERGENCY STOP MUSHROOM RED]
        MPGW[MANUAL PULSE GENERATOR HANDWHEEL]
    end
    SCR --> SK1
    SK1 --> SK2
    HMI_CENTRAL[HMI CENTRAL CONTROLLER] --- DISP
    HMI_CENTRAL --- MODE
    HMI_CENTRAL --- PROG
    HMI_CENTRAL --- DATA
    HMI_CENTRAL --- SAFE
```

## 4.3 Part Programming — Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINEERING DRAWING] --> B[PROCESS PLANNING OPS TOOLS CUTTING PARAM]
    B --> C[SELECT ZERO POINT W AND WORK OFFSET G54]
    C --> D[WRITE HEADER BLOCK SAFETY G CODES]
    D --> E[WRITE MACHINING BLOCKS G00 G01 G02 G03]
    E --> F[APPLY CANNED CYCLES G90 G92 G94 G76 G74 G75]
    F --> G[APPLY TNRC G41 G42 G40]
    G --> H[WRITE TAIL BLOCK M05 M09 M30]
    H --> I[DRY RUN GRAPHICS CHECK]
    I --> J[FIRST ARTICLE TRIAL CUT]
    J --> K[REFINE OFFSETS AND FEEDS]
    K --> L[PRODUCTION RUN]
```

## 4.4 Tool-Path Visualization (Mermaid Block Equivalent of a Stepped Shaft)

For complex contours, KTU expects a step-by-step description rather than a literal isometric draw. Below is the **block representation** of a typical CNC lathe program cycle for a stepped shaft.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[SAFE START X100 Z50] --> R1[RAPID X62 Z2]
    R1 --> T1[TURN ROUGH G01 Z-30 F0.25]
    T1 --> R2[RAPID X64]
    R2 --> R3[RAPID Z2]
    R3 --> T2[TURN FINISH G01 Z-30 F0.08]
    T2 --> R4[RAPID X100 Z50]
    R4 --> END[TOOL CHANGE T0202]
    END --> R5[RAPID X40 Z2]
    R5 --> FC[FACE G01 X20 F0.15]
    FC --> R6[RAPID X42 Z2]
    R6 --> GRO[GROOVE G75 X30 Z-20]
    GRO --> TH[THREAD G76 X26 Z-25 P0.92 Q0.2 F1.0]
    TH --> RET[RAPID X100 Z100 M30]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` style expected answer for "Explain a part programming cycle" can be summarized with this flow. Replace the labels with actual coordinates from the question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between absolute (G90) and incremental (G91) programming in CNC turning. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (expected 3-mark depth):**

| Feature | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Reference | All coordinates from **program zero (W)** | Coordinates measured from the **current tool position** |
| Sign | End points signed accordingly | Each move = (current → next) delta |
| Risk of accumulated error | None — every point is independent | Error accumulates over many moves |
| Best use | Complex multi-feature parts | Short repetitive steps |
| Typical block | `G01 X40.0 Z-25.0` (move to Ø40, Z = -25 from zero) | `G01 U-5.0 W-10.0` (move 5 mm radially inward, 10 mm axially) |

> Use **X, Z** for absolute, **U, W** for incremental (lathes).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and explain the purpose of any four modes on the CNC control panel.**

**Model Answer (3-mark depth — pick any four from the 8 modes below):**
1. **AUTO** — runs a stored program from memory automatically.
2. **MDI (Manual Data Input)** — single-block direct entry and execution.
3. **EDIT** — to create or modify part programs in memory.
4. **JOG** — manual continuous axis motion for setup.
5. **HANDLE / MPG** — manual micro-positioning using the handwheel; multiplier ×1, ×10, ×100, ×1000.
6. **RAPID** — manual high-speed (G00-rate) traverse.
7. **ZRN (Zero Return / Home)** — establishes a repeatable machine reference after power-on.
8. **DNC** — runs a program streamed from an external computer over RS-232/TCP.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> The KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires **one full 14-mark question** with **sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks** from this module. The student is offered a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both must be provided below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q A (a)** (7 marks): With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a CNC lathe**. List the **axes** of a standard 2-axis CNC lathe and the **convention for the X-axis** on a turning center.

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram** — refer to Section 4.1 above (the mermaid flowchart). In the answer script, redraw as a clean rectangular block diagram with arrows showing:
`Part Program → CNC → Servo Drives (X, Z) → Ball Screws → Carriage/Turret → Tool → Workpiece` and the feedback loop from encoders back to CNC.

**Axes:**

| Axis | Physical Direction | Programmed As | Purpose |
|------|-------------------|---------------|---------|
| Z | Parallel to spindle | Distance from program zero (mm) | Controls axial length / facing |
| X | Perpendicular to spindle (radial) | **Diameter value** (not radius) | Controls diameter of cut |

**X-axis convention (very important KTU point):**
> On a CNC lathe, the **X-axis is programmed in diameter mode** — the value entered is the **final diameter** of the workpiece at the tool tip, not the radial distance moved. Therefore a movement from Ø80 to Ø72 is written as `X72.0` (a radial move of only 4 mm, but a diameter change of 8 mm).

**Q A (b)** (7 marks): Explain the **structure of a CNC part program** for a lathe. State the **role of G00, G01, G96, and M30**, with one example line of each.

**Model Solution:**

**Program structure** (refer to Section 2.4 A):

1. **Program number header** — `O0001 ;`
2. **Safety / initialization block** — sets units, cancels compensation, returns home, e.g., `G21 G40 G97 G99 G28 U0. W0. ;`
3. **Tool and work offset call** — `T0101 ; G54 ;`
4. **Machining blocks** — sequence of G-code moves.
5. **Tool change blocks** — `T0202 ;` etc.
6. **End / tail block** — coolant off, spindle off, program end.

**Role and example of each code:**

| Code | Group | Function | Example |
|------|-------|----------|---------|
| **G00** | 01 | Rapid traverse (non-cutting) at machine max rapid rate | `G00 X50.0 Z2.0 ;` |
| **G01** | 01 | Linear interpolation at programmed feedrate (F) | `G01 Z-30.0 F0.2 ;` |
| **G96** | 02 | Constant surface speed (m/min) — spindle RPM auto-varies with diameter | `G96 S180 ;` |
| **M30** | — | Program end + rewind to top + optional reset of modal codes | `M30 ;` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write `S1500` with G96 in the **same block** expecting fixed RPM — this is wrong. G96 interprets **S as m/min**, G97 interprets **S as RPM**. To clamp the resulting RPM, use **G50 S2500 ;** in a separate block BEFORE the G96 command.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Q B (a)** (7 marks): Explain the **canned turning cycles** available on a CNC lathe — **G90, G92, G94, and G76**. For each, give **one industrial application** and a **sample syntax line**.

**Model Solution:**

| Cycle | G-Code | Function | Industrial Application | Sample Syntax |
|-------|--------|----------|-------------------------|----------------|
| **Turning (straight)** | G90 | Straight turning / boring of simple cylindrical features with depth increment | OD roughing of shafts and pins | `G90 X50.0 Z-30.0 F0.25 ;` |
| **Threading (single-pass)** | G92 | Cuts a straight or tapered thread in one pass; multi-pass needs loop | Repair-thread, single-pass threads | `G92 X19.0 Z-25.0 F1.5 ;` |
| **Facing** | G94 | End-face turning cycle; tool moves radially while feeding axially | Squaring the front face of a forging | `G94 X20.0 Z2.0 F0.15 ;` |
| **Threading (multi-pass)** | G76 | Multi-pass thread with controlled depth decrement; preferred for production threads | Lead screws, threaded shafts | `G76 P020060 Q0.1 R0.05 ;` then `G76 X18.4 Z-25.0 P1.0 Q0.5 F1.0 ;` |

**How G76 works (high-yield detail):**
- First block: `G76 P(m)(r)(a) Q(Δdmin) R(finishing allowance)` — m = number of spring passes; r = chamfer; a = tool angle (60°, 55°, etc.).
- Second block: `G76 X(end dia) Z(end Z) P(thread depth) Q(first pass depth) F(lead)`.
- The controller automatically distributes the total depth across multiple passes, each shallower than the previous — yielding better surface finish and longer tool life.

**Q B (b)** (7 marks): A mild-steel shaft is to be **rough-turned from Ø80 mm to Ø70 mm over 50 mm length** on a CNC lathe. Cutting speed **V = 180 m/min**, feed **f = 0.3 mm/rev**, depth of cut **d = 2 mm**, and specific cutting force **Kc = 2000 N/mm²**. Calculate:
(i) Spindle speed N (at average Ø75 mm)
(ii) Feedrate fr
(iii) Material removal rate (MRR)
(iv) Net cutting power Pc
(v) Number of roughing passes
(vi) Machining time per pass and total roughing time.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spindle speed at average Ø75 mm:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 75} = \frac{180000}{235.62} \approx 764 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Feedrate:**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.3 \times 764 = 229.2 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material removal rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V = 0.3 \times 2 \times 180 = 108 \;\; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

**(iv) Net cutting power:**

$$P_c = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{108 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kW} = 3.6 \;\; \text{W}$$

**(v) Number of roughing passes:**

Radial reduction per pass = d = 2 mm, so diameter reduction per pass = 2 × d = 4 mm.

Total diameter reduction = 80 − 70 = 10 mm.

$$n_{\text{passes}} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 3 \text{ passes (last one light)}$$

**(vi) Machining time per pass and total:**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{50}{229.2} = 0.218 \;\; \text{min} = 13.1 \;\; \text{s per pass}$$

$$t_{\text{total}} \approx 3 \times 13.1 = 39.3 \;\; \text{s} \;\; (\text{excluding rapid and tool-index})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently use Ø70 or Ø80 alone for the spindle-speed calculation, getting values of 818 rpm and 716 rpm respectively. The **correct average** is **Ø75 mm** (or the **G96 mode** is used and the controller varies N automatically). Examiner will mark you down unless you explicitly justify the choice of diameter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Quick-Revision Checklist (Topic Recap)

> **Important Things to Remember — Module 3 in a Glance**

- [ ] **CNC lathe axes**: Z (axial, length), X (radial, programmed in **diameter**). Y, C are optional / advanced.
- [ ] **Coordinate system**: Right-hand rule; +Z away from chuck; +X away from spindle centerline; program zero (W) is operator-defined.
- [ ] **Modes of operation** (8 in total): AUTO, MDI, EDIT, JOG, HANDLE/MPG, RAPID, ZRN (HOME), DNC.
- [ ] **First operation at power-on**: Zero Return (Reference Return) → set tool offsets → set G54 work offset → run program.
- [ ] **Mandatory key groups** on FANUC panel: Mode keys, Program keys, Function keys, Soft keys, Address keys, Numeric keys, CYCLE START, FEED HOLD, EMERGENCY STOP, MPG.
- [ ] **G-code essentials**: G00 rapid, G01 linear, G02 CW arc, G03 CCW arc, G20/G21 inch/metric, G28 home, G40/G41/G42 TNRC, G54–G59 work offsets, G90 absolute / G91 incremental, G94 facing / G90 turning / G92 threading single-pass / G76 threading multi-pass / G74 drilling / G75 grooving, G96 constant V / G97 fixed RPM, G98 feed/min / G99 feed/rev.
- [ ] **M-code essentials**: M00 program stop, M01 optional stop, M02 end (no rewind), M03 spindle CW, M04 spindle CCW, M05 spindle stop, M06 tool change (mill; on lathes the T code does it), M08 coolant on, M09 coolant off, M30 program end + rewind.
- [ ] **TNRC (G41/G42)**: Required when finishing contoured profiles — without it, the tool tip leaves a cusp on radii. Always pair with **G40 cancel** at the end.
- [ ] **Programming techniques** (high-yield): canned cycles (G90/G92/G94/G76), subprograms (M98/M99), macros (Custom Macro B), cutter comp, proper sequencing (rough → finish → part).
- [ ] **Key formulas**:
   - $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ rev/min
   - $f_r = f N$ mm/min
   - $\text{MRR} = f \, d \, V$ mm³/min
   - $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ kW
   - $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ min
   - Thread depth V-thread: $0.6134 \, p$
- [ ] **G96 vs G97 trade-off**: G96 → uniform finish (RPM varies with D); G97 → constant torque, preferred for roughing.
- [ ] **Always use G50 S___** to clamp max RPM when G96 is active (otherwise spindle may overspeed at small diameters and damage chuck/spindle bearings).
- [ ] **Block format**: `N__ G__ X__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__ ;` (words are address+number; blocks end with `;` or LF).
- [ ] **Modal vs non-modal**: Modal codes stay active until overridden; G04, G28 are non-modal.
- [ ] **Dry-run / Single-block / MPG** are the three primary operator safeguards during trial cuts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workpiece Setting Methods, Tool Setting Methods, Programming by Using Multiple Tools, Project on Machine by Using Different Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Workpiece Setting Methods, Tool Setting Methods, Programming by Using Multiple Tools, Project on Machine by Using Different Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Workpiece Setting (Work Holding and Datum Establishment)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Workpiece setting is the systematic process of locating, clamping, and establishing a known Cartesian coordinate system (Work Coordinate System – WCS) on a CNC machine such that every program command operates from a repeatable, machine-independent reference frame. It involves *two simultaneous activities*: (a) **physical work-holding** (using chucks, vises, fixtures, clamps) and (b) **datum alignment** (using edge finders, dial indicators, probe systems, or 3-2-1 locating principle).

**Standard KTU Metrics:**
- Positional accuracy target: **±0.02 mm** for milling; **±0.05 mm** for turning.
- Repeatability benchmark (per ISO 230/2): **±0.01 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight – The Two Halves of Workpiece Setting**
> A common student error is to *only* clamp the part and skip the coordinate-system declaration. In CNC, **clamping without WCS declaration = scrap on first pass**. The machine does not "know" where the part is until the operator (or probe) assigns G54–G59 offsets.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of workpiece setting like **placing a photograph inside a picture frame**. The *frame* is the machine table, the *photo* is the workpiece, and the *corner* of the photo touching the frame's inner reference is the **Work Zero (W)**. If you slide the photo, the W point moves with it. The CNC controller keeps a "memory slip" (the offset table) that says "W is 152.3 mm to the right and 88.7 mm forward from the machine home." Whenever the program says `G00 X0 Y0`, the machine races to that remembered corner — wherever it is.

### 1.2 Tool Setting (Tool Length and Diameter Offset Registration)

**Formal Definition:**
Tool setting is the procedure of measuring each cutting tool's *length* (along Z), *diameter/radius* (in X-Y), and storing these values in the controller's **Offset Table** (sometimes called the **Tool Offset Memory**). The controller then applies these offsets automatically via G43 (length compensation, +direction), G44 (length compensation, –direction), G49 (cancel length comp), G41 (radius comp, left), G42 (radius comp, right), and G40 (cancel radius comp).

**Standard KTU Metrics:**
- Typical tool length measurement resolution: **0.001 mm** on industrial controls.
- Tool diameter offset range: **±999.999 mm** (Fanuc convention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tool Setting Is Not Optional:**
> Every tool in a multi-tool program has a *different* physical length. If you do not register the offset, the spindle will plunge the second tool to the same Z-depth as the first — and either snap the tool, gouge the part, or crash into the fixture. Tool offsets make the program *tool-agnostic*.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a class of 30 students writing an exam with **different pen lengths**. The teacher (controller) needs each student to start writing from the *top line of the answer sheet*, not the tip of their pen. Each student tells the teacher "my pen is 12 mm longer than average" or "7 mm shorter." The teacher adds that adjustment mentally. That adjustment is the **tool length offset**.

### 1.3 Programming by Using Multiple Tools

**Formal Definition:**
Multiple-tool programming refers to the practice of writing a single CNC part program that sequences two or more cutting tools (drills, end mills, taps, boring bars, etc.) in a coordinated order, using the **M06 tool-change command** to invoke ATC (Automatic Tool Changer) and **T-address** to pre-select the next tool. The program must manage:
1. Spindle stop before tool change (M05).
2. Tool change at a safe Z-height (typically Z=+50 mm or machine reference position).
3. Spindle restart with new tool (M03/M04).
4. Re-application of length offset (G43 H##) for the new tool.
5. Optional re-application of cutter compensation (G41/G42 D##).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a *chef switching knives* in a kitchen — moving from a chef's knife to a paring knife to a fillet knife. Each time, the chef (program) must say: "Stop cutting (M05), put down old knife (M06), pick up the new one by name (T02), tell the new knife's height to the counter (G43 H02), and resume cutting with the right side (G41)."

### 1.4 Project on Machine by Using Different Cycles (Canned Cycles)

**Formal Definition:**
A *canned cycle* is a pre-engineered, parameterized subroutine built into the CNC controller that condenses a multi-block machining sequence (approach, plunge, dwell, retract) into a single block. In KTU Module 3, the focus is on **fixed cycles for drilling, tapping, and boring**, governed by G73, G74, G76, G80–G89.

**Standard KTU Reference – G-Code Canned Cycle Family:**

| Cycle | G-Code | KTU Name | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drilling | G81 | Drill cycle | Simple straight drill |
| Drilling with dwell | G82 | Spot-face / counter-bore | Drill + dwell at bottom |
| Peck drilling | G83 | Deep-hole peck | Incremental retract peck |
| Tapping | G84 | Tapping cycle | Synchronized feed/spindle |
| Boring (no retract) | G85 | Boring in/out | Feed in, feed out |
| Boring (rapid out) | G86 | Boring + spindle stop | Feed in, stop, rapid out |
| Back boring | G87 | Back boring | Inverted boring cycle |
| Boring (manual out) | G88 | Boring + manual retract | Operator pulls out |
| Boring with dwell | G89 | Boring + dwell | Like G85 + dwell |

> [!NOTE]
> **G80 is the safety sentinel** — it cancels ALL active canned cycles. Always end a drilling block sequence with `G80` to prevent the controller from re-applying the last cycle on the next motion block.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A canned cycle is like a *reusable form letter* — instead of writing "Dear customer, your order has shipped..." every time, you fill in only the name, address, and tracking number. The body is pre-written. Similarly, G81 says "drill from R-plane to Z-depth, then rapid back to R-plane" — you only specify X, Y, Z, and R.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Workpiece Setting – Step-by-Step Logic

**Why and How of Each Step:**

1. **Why clean the table first?** Chips and coolant residue cause *pseudo-locators* — a 0.5 mm chip under the vise jaw shifts the WCS by half a millimeter. KTU examiners love this as a viva point.
2. **Why 3-2-1 principle?** Six degrees of freedom (3 translational + 3 rotational) must be eliminated. Three pins on the primary face kill rotation (X, Y, Z axes) and two translations; two pins on the secondary face kill another rotation + translation; one pin on the tertiary face kills the last rotation. Hence **3-2-1**.
3. **Why use an edge finder, not a ruler?** Edge finders give a *kinematic* signal — they "jump" by 0.5 mm when the stylus tip passes the cylinder's mid-diameter. The centerline is known to ±0.01 mm.
4. **Why store WCS in G54–G59 instead of rewriting every coordinate?** G54 is the *most-used* work offset. G55–G59 give **six** simultaneously-active WCS, useful for fixtures holding multiple parts or for part-fixturing without re-probing.

**Logical Flow (Use Bullets):**
- Step 1: Mount the part in vise/fixture with a **known reference face** facing the spindle.
- Step 2: Use **edge finder** (Ø10 mm) to locate X0 in the spindle centerline — record the machine coordinate.
- Step 3: Repeat for Y0 on a different face.
- Step 4: Touch off Z0 on the top of the part using a **Z-axis setter block** (commonly 50 mm or 100 mm tall).
- Step 5: Calculate **G54 X = Machine X at X0 – Part X0**, same for Y and Z.
- Step 6: Type these values into the **Offset → Work Offset** screen and **verify** by jogging the spindle to G54 X0 Y0 Z0 — the tool should land *exactly* on the W0 point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often enter the raw machine coordinates into G54. *Wrong.* G54 stores the **distance from machine home to work zero**, so you must subtract the target from the current machine position. The formula is:
> $$\text{G54 value} = \text{Machine coordinate when at Work Zero}$$
> Verification: After saving, the display should read **Machine 0, 0, 0** when the spindle is at G54 X0 Y0 Z0.

### 2.2 Tool Setting – Step-by-Step Logic

**Why and How of Each Step:**

- **Tool length offset (G43/H##)** is added to every programmed Z-motion. The "H" address refers to a row in the **Geometry Offset Table**.
- **Tool diameter / cutter compensation (G41/G42 D##)** is added to (or subtracted from) the programmed X-Y path. The "D" address refers to the **Wear/Geometry Offset Table**.
- **G43 vs G44:** G43 is by far the more common (positive offset = tool is *longer* than the master). G44 is used when the tool is shorter — it is the *mirror* direction. Most KTU exam questions only require G43.
- **G49 cancels length comp** — vital at the end of a tool's usage and at M30 (program end).

**Why a Tool Presetter Beats On-Machine Touch-Off:**
- On-machine touch-off wastes spindle time (15–45 sec per tool on 20 tools = 5–15 min lost).
- Off-machine presetters measure sub-micron in air, no risk of spindle crash.
- Industrial-grade pre-setters include **laser tool measurement** for non-contact diameter sensing.

### 2.3 Multi-Tool Programming – Step-by-Step Logic

**Why and How of Each Step:**

1. **T-address logic** — `T01` selects *slot 1* in the tool magazine. The *actual* tool change occurs when M06 is executed. So `T02 M06` is functionally identical to `M06 T02` in execution but lets the controller *pre-stage* the next tool during spindle run, saving cycle time.
2. **Why stop spindle before tool change?** Spinning the spindle during M06 risks the ATC arm grasping a rotating taper — catastrophic.
3. **Why include coolant M-codes per tool?** A drill wants flood coolant (M08). A tap wants *no* coolant during the actual threading pass. A face mill wants air-mist or flood. Always synchronize coolant with the tool.
4. **Why always re-apply G43 after a tool change?** Each H-offset is *tied to a specific tool number*. M06 invalidates the previous H-offset. Forgetting this is the **#1 cause of CNC crashes** in academic labs.

### 2.4 Canned Cycles – Step-by-Step Logic

**Why and How of Each Step:**

- **R-plane** is the *rapid traverse plane* above the workpiece. The tool rapid-traverses to R-plane height, then *feed*-traverses from R-plane to Z-target.
- **Z-target** is the final cutting depth (e.g., bottom of a drilled hole).
- **Q-value** in G83 specifies the *peck increment* (each peck retracts by Q mm to break chips).
- **P-value** in G82/G89 specifies *dwell time in milliseconds* at the bottom of the hole.
- **F-value** specifies feed rate in **mm/min** (most controls) or mm/rev (lathes).

**Why the controller auto-retracts to R-plane, not initial plane:**
The R-plane is *user-defined* and *inside* the initial plane. This means once the cycle is over, the tool is positioned just above the workpiece and ready for a *next X-Y* — perfect for bolt-circle drilling where multiple holes are drilled in succession.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Code | Meaning | Formula / Logic | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X_{W}$ | Workpiece X-zero | $X_{W} = X_{M} \text{ at } X_{\text{work zero}}$ | mm |
| $Y_{W}$ | Workpiece Y-zero | $Y_{W} = Y_{M} \text{ at } Y_{\text{work zero}}$ | mm |
| $Z_{W}$ | Workpiece Z-zero | $Z_{W} = Z_{M} - h_{\text{setter}}$ | mm |
| $L_{\text{offset}}$ | Tool length offset (G43) | $L_{\text{offset}} = L_{\text{master}} - L_{\text{tool}}$ | mm |
| $R_{\text{offset}}$ | Cutter radius compensation | $R_{\text{offset}} = R_{\text{actual}} - R_{\text{nominal}}$ | mm |
| $\Delta Z_{\text{cycle}}$ | Total drilling depth | $\Delta Z_{\text{cycle}} = Z_{0} - Z_{f}$ | mm |
| $N_{\text{pecks}}$ | Number of pecks (G83) | $N_{\text{pecks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\Delta Z_{\text{cycle}}}{Q} \right\rceil$ | integer |
| $t_{\text{drill}}$ | Drilling time per hole | $t_{\text{drill}} = \dfrac{\Delta Z_{\text{cycle}}}{F} + \dfrac{D}{1000} \times \text{P}$ | min |
| $f_{\text{tap}}$ | Tapping feed (G84) | $f_{\text{tap}} = P \times N_{s}$ | mm/min |
| $P$ | Pitch (thread) or Dwell (ms) | Material/spec dependent | mm or ms |
| $N_{s}$ | Spindle speed | $N_{s} = \dfrac{1000 \times V_{c}}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min |
| $V_{c}$ | Cutting speed | Material dependent | m/min |
| $D$ | Tool diameter | From tool table | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canned Cycle Cancellation Rule (G80):** Any subsequent motion block (G00, G01) issued *without* a G80 will attempt to apply the last canned cycle at the new X-Y. This is the classic "phantom drill" crash. **Always pair G80 with the next non-cycle motion block.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workpiece Setting** in production: A tier-1 automotive supplier runs 200 parts/fixture on a horizontal machining center. WCS is set *once* per fixture (15-minute setup), then 200 parts use the same G54 offset.
- **Tool Setting** in aerospace: A 5-axis mill machining a titanium blisk might use 60+ tools. Each tool's length and diameter is pre-set in a Renishaw NC4 laser unit, then uploaded to the controller via USB.
- **Multi-Tool Programming** in mold-making: A single injection-mold cavity program typically uses 8–15 tools in sequence (rough, semi-finish, finish, EDM-electrode prep, drill, tap).
- **Canned Cycles** in mass production: A motorcycle crankcase requires 28 holes per side. Without G81/G83, the program would be 28 × 6 = 168 blocks. With canned cycles, the same logic is expressed in ~30 blocks.
