## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Workpiece X-Offset (G54 X)

**Scenario:** A milling machine has its **machine home** (M) at X = 0, Y = 0, Z = 0. An edge finder is used to find the left edge of a workpiece. When the edge finder tip aligns with the left edge, the **machine coordinate** reads X = 152.300 mm. The work-zero is defined to be at the **center** of a 80 mm wide workpiece, so X0 (in the work-coordinate frame) corresponds to 40 mm inward from the left edge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{M,\text{at work zero}} &= X_{M,\text{at left edge}} + 40 \\
&= 152.300 \text{ mm} + 40 \text{ mm} \\
&= 192.300 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The value entered into **G54 X** is therefore **192.300 mm**.

**Conversion Logic (Why this works):**
The machine coordinate system is fixed to the machine. The work coordinate system is *floating* and anchored to the part. G54 stores the *position of W0 in machine coordinates*. When the program commands `G00 X0 Y0` under G54, the controller computes: $X_{\text{machine target}} = X_{G54} + X_{\text{programmed}} = 192.300 + 0 = 192.300$ mm.

### 3.2 Derivation of Tool Length Offset (G43 H01)

**Scenario:** A master tool (T00) measures **150.000 mm** from the spindle face to a 50 mm reference block. Tool T01 is now measured and reads **148.500 mm** on the same reference block. The H-offset for T01 is the *difference*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{offset, T01}} &= L_{\text{master}} - L_{\text{T01}} \\
&= 150.000 \text{ mm} - 148.500 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1.500 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the H01 value is **+1.500 mm** (positive because T01 is *shorter* than the master).

**Conversion Logic:**
When the program issues `G43 H01 Z10.0`, the controller computes: $Z_{\text{actual}} = Z_{\text{programmed}} + H_{\text{value}} = 10.0 + 1.5 = 11.5$ mm. The tool tip is 1.5 mm *higher* than the programmed Z, which compensates for the shorter tool. **Without this offset, T01 would plunge 1.5 mm too deep.**

### 3.3 Derivation of Cutter Radius Compensation (G41/G42 D##)

**Scenario:** An end mill with **diameter D = 12.000 mm** (radius R = 6.000 mm) is programmed to climb-mill a rectangle of 100 mm × 60 mm. The program uses the *centerline* of the tool path, but the user wants the *edge* of the cutter to be exactly on the rectangle edge. The D-offset stores the **cutter radius**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{offset, D01}} &= 6.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

With `G42 D01` active (right-hand compensation for climb milling, looking down +Z), the controller automatically offsets the path by +R in the normal-right direction.

### 3.4 Derivation of Peck-Drill Cycle (G83) — Number of Pecks

**Scenario:** A deep hole of **$\Delta Z = 50.0$ mm** must be drilled with a Q-peck increment of **$Q = 8.0$ mm** using G83. Compute the number of pecks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{pecks}} &= \left\lceil \frac{\Delta Z}{Q} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{50.0}{8.0} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 6.25 \right\rceil \\
&= 7 \text{ pecks}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first 6 pecks drill 8.0 mm each = 48.0 mm. The 7th peck drills the remaining **2.0 mm** plus a small final retract-clearance.

**Conversion Logic (Why ceiling, not floor):**
A floor would give 6 pecks = 48 mm drilled, leaving 2 mm uncut. The ceiling guarantees the *full* depth is reached, with the last peck being a *partial* plunge. This is how G83 actually behaves on Fanuc and Haas controllers.

### 3.5 Derivation of Spindle Speed and Feed for Tapping (G84)

**Scenario:** A M10 × 1.5 mm metric tap is used. The cutting speed for tapping mild steel is $V_c = 12$ m/min. Compute spindle speed and feed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 10.0 \text{ mm (nominal tap diameter)} \\
P &= 1.5 \text{ mm (pitch)} \\
N_s &= \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 12}{\pi \times 10} \\
&= \frac{12000}{31.416} \\
&\approx 382 \text{ rev/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{tap}} &= P \times N_s \\
&= 1.5 \times 382 \\
&= 573 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
For tapping, the feed *must* match the pitch, otherwise the thread will be over-cut or under-cut. G84 *automatically* synchronizes the spindle rotation to the Z-axis feed, so as long as the F-address equals $P \times N_s$, the controller will rigidly tap without a floating tap holder.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Decision Tree for Canned Cycle Selection

| Hole Requirement | Cycle | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Shallow hole, no chip issue | G81 | Shortest cycle time |
| Spot-face or counter-bore to depth | G82 | Needs dwell for clean floor |
| Deep hole, > 4×D | G83 | Chip evacuation via pecking |
| Threaded hole | G84 | Rigid/sync tapping |
| Reamed hole (in/out feed) | G85 | No spindle stop, smooth retract |
| Bored hole (rough) | G86 | Spindle stop, rapid retract = time saving |
| Back-bored counter-bore | G87 | Tool approaches from *below* |
| Bored hole (operator assist) | G88 | Manual retract for inspection |
| Bored hole with floor finish | G89 | Dwell for surface finish |

The following is a *complete* multi-tool program showing the integration of workpiece setting, tool setting, and canned cycles.

> **Examination Note:** Notice the *pattern* — every tool has **M06, G43, M03, M08** as a quartet. Forgetting any one of these is a guaranteed deduction in KTU valuation. The canned-cycle block uses `G99` (return to R-plane) instead of `G98` (return to initial plane) for cycle efficiency.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Question 1:** Define **workpiece setting** and list any **two methods** used for establishing the X-Y datum on a CNC vertical machining center. *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]*

**Model Answer:**
Workpiece setting is the procedure of locating, clamping, and establishing a work coordinate system (WCS) so the CNC controller knows the part's position relative to the machine axes. Two common X-Y datum methods are:
1. **Mechanical edge finder** (Ø10 mm cylinder) — the operator feels the "jump" at the edge centerline.
2. **Dial test indicator (DTI)** — a precision gauge that reads runout as the spindle sweeps along an edge, useful for square or non-cylindrical edges.

---

**Question 2:** What is the difference between **G43** and **G44** in CNC tool length compensation? *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**
Both G43 and G44 are tool length compensation preparatory codes used in conjunction with an H-offset value. The difference is the *direction* in which the offset is applied to the programmed Z-coordinate:

- **G43** applies the offset in the **positive** direction (tool is *longer* than the master; offset adds to Z).
- **G44** applies the offset in the **negative** direction (tool is *shorter* than the master; offset subtracts from Z).

G43 is overwhelmingly more common in industry. G44 is rare and used only on specific tool-grinding or specialty applications. Both are canceled by **G49**.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between Question A and Question B)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A(a)** Describe in detail the procedure for **workpiece setting** on a CNC vertical machining center (VMC) using the **3-2-1 locating principle** and **edge finder**. Explain how the **G54 work offset** is calculated and stored. **\[7 Marks\]** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

The 3-2-1 locating principle is the standard fixture-design convention for fully constraining a prismatic workpiece, eliminating all six degrees of freedom (3 translational + 3 rotational).

**Step 1 – Primary Locating Face (3 pins):**
Three pins (or a flat plate + 2 pins) on the bottom of the workpiece constrain the part in the Z-axis and the two in-plane rotations (rotations about X and Y).

**Step 2 – Secondary Locating Face (2 pins):**
Two pins (or a flat edge) against the rear face constrain the X-translation and one rotation (rotation about Z).

**Step 3 – Tertiary Locating Face (1 pin):**
A single pin (or a flat edge) against the side face constrains the Y-translation. The last rotational degree of freedom is now fully constrained.

**Step 4 – Clamping:**
Apply mechanical clamps (strap clamps, step blocks) using a torque wrench to **30–40 N·m** for M12 bolts, ensuring the part does not lift or shift during cutting forces.

**Step 5 – Edge Finder X-Y Datum:**
Mount an Ø10 mm edge finder in the spindle. Jog the edge finder close to the left edge of the workpiece, then slowly hand-crank the X-axis until the edge finder "kicks." The position where the *offset surface* of the edge finder aligns with the workpiece edge is the actual workpiece edge.

**Step 6 – Computation of G54:**
Suppose the machine coordinate reads:
- At the X-edge: $X_M = 152.300$ mm
- At the Y-edge: $Y_M = 88.700$ mm
- At the Z-top: $Z_M = -150.000$ mm (with 100 mm Z-setter block between tool and part)

If the work zero is defined 40 mm inward from the X-edge and 30 mm inward from the Y-edge, and the setter block is 100 mm tall:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G54\ X &= 152.300 + 40 = 192.300 \text{ mm} \\
G54\ Y &= 88.700 + 30 = 118.700 \text{ mm} \\
G54\ Z &= -150.000 + 100 = -50.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

These values are entered into the controller's **Work Offset → G54** screen, and the program begins with `G54` to activate the offset.

**Step 7 – Verification:**
Jog the spindle to the G54 X0 Y0 Z0 position. The tool tip should land *exactly* on the chosen work-zero point. If not, re-iterate the offset calculation.

---

**A(b)** Explain the **canned drilling cycle G83** with a labeled sketch. List its parameters (R, Z, Q, F) and state the *advantage* of G83 over G81. **\[7 Marks\]** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

G83 is the **deep-hole peck-drilling cycle**. It is designed for holes deeper than 4×D where chip evacuation is critical.

**Parameters:**
- **R** = R-plane (Z-coordinate where the tool rapid-traverses to before the first plunge)
- **Z** = Final depth of the hole (target Z)
- **Q** = Peck increment (depth of each individual plunge before retract)
- **F** = Feed rate in mm/min

**Cycle Phases (with the structure of motion):**
1. Rapid to (X, Y) — the hole's centerline.
2. Rapid to R-plane.
3. Feed-plunge by **Q** mm.
4. Rapid retract to R-plane (chip evacuation).
5. Rapid back to current Z (R-plane minus 0.5 mm or similar).
6. Repeat steps 3–5 until full Z-depth is reached.
7. Rapid retract to R-plane (G99) or initial plane (G98).

**Example Program Block:**
```
G99 G83 R2.0 Z-30.0 Q5.0 F100
```
This drills to Z = –30 mm in pecks of 5 mm, with an R-plane 2 mm above the workpiece top, at 100 mm/min.

**Advantage of G83 over G81:**
- G81 plunges in *one continuous* feed, leading to chip packing, tool breakage, and poor hole quality in deep holes.
- G83 *peck-retracts*, breaking the chip, evacuating it, and allowing cutting fluid to reach the drill tip — increasing tool life by 2–4× in deep-hole drilling.
- For holes deeper than 4×D, G83 is the *only* acceptable cycle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**B(a)** Describe the **multi-tool programming** procedure for a CNC machining center. Write a sample program segment that uses **three different tools** (a face mill, a center drill, and an 8 mm drill) and explain each block. **\[7 Marks\]** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]*

**Model Solution:**

Multi-tool programming requires careful sequencing of **M06 tool changes**, **G43 length offsets**, **M03 spindle starts**, and **M08 coolant** commands. Each tool must be:
1. Pre-selected with a T-address (optional but recommended).
2. Called with M06.
3. Have its length offset re-applied (G43 H##).
4. Spindle restarted (M03 S##).
5. Coolant activated (M08).
6. Cutting operations executed.
7. Coolant and spindle stopped (M09, M05) before next tool change.

**Sample Program Segment:**
```
O1001 (MULTI TOOL DEMO - PERUT412)
N05 G21 G90 G94 G54
N10 G91 G28 Z0.0
N15 G90 G00 X-30.0 Y0.0
N20 M06 T01
N25 G43 H01 Z50.0
N30 M03 S800
N35 M08
N40 G01 Z0.0 F200
N45 G01 X120.0 F300
N50 G00 Z50.0
N55 M09 M05
N60 M06 T02
N65 G90 G00 X20.0 Y20.0
N70 G43 H02 Z50.0
N75 M03 S1500
N80 M08
N85 G99 G81 R2.0 Z-3.0 F80
N90 X60.0 Y20.0
N95 X60.0 Y60.0
N100 X20.0 Y60.0
N105 G80 G00 Z50.0
N110 M09 M05
N115 M06 T03
N120 G90 G00 X20.0 Y20.0
N125 G43 H03 Z50.0
N130 M03 S1200
N135 M08
N140 G99 G83 R2.0 Z-25.0 Q5.0 F100
N145 X60.0 Y20.0
N150 X60.0 Y60.0
N155 X20.0 Y60.0
N160 G80 G00 Z50.0
N165 M09 M05
N170 G91 G28 Z0.0
N175 G28 X0 Y0
N180 M30
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation (key blocks):**
- `N20 M06 T01`: Tool change to T01 (face mill). The ATC arm retrieves the tool from the magazine.
- `N25 G43 H01 Z50.0`: Apply tool-length offset for T01 from the geometry table. Without this, the tool plunges to the wrong depth.
- `N30 M03 S800`: Spindle starts clockwise at 800 RPM.
- `N35 M08`: Coolant on (flood).
- `N40-N45`: Face milling pass from X-30 to X120 at 300 mm/min.
- `N50-N55`: Retract and prepare for tool change.
- `N60 M06 T02`: Switch to T02 (center drill). The controller's M06 pauses spindle, sends ATC, receives new tool, and resumes.
- `N85 G99 G81 ...`: Drilling cycle on hole 1; subsequent X-Y commands repeat the cycle at new locations.
- `N140 G99 G83 ...`: Peck-drill cycle for deep 25 mm holes.
- `N180 M30`: Program end; rewinds to start.

---

**B(b)** Write notes on the following canned cycles: **(i) G82 Spot-Face Cycle**, **(ii) G84 Tapping Cycle**, and **(iii) G85 Boring Cycle**. **\[7 Marks\]** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) G82 — Spot-Face / Counter-Bore Drilling Cycle:**
G82 is identical to G81 (drill cycle) **plus a dwell** at the bottom of the hole. The syntax is:
```
G99 G82 R2.0 Z-5.0 P500 F100
```
The **P500** parameter specifies a **dwell of 500 milliseconds** at the bottom. The purpose is to:
- Clean up the floor of the hole (removes the drill's chisel-point mark).
- Achieve a precise spot-face depth for counter-boring screw heads.
- Allow full chip evacuation before retract.

**Application:** Spot-facing for bolt-head seats, dowel pin holes, and counter-bore features.

**(ii) G84 — Tapping Cycle:**
G84 is the **right-hand rigid tapping cycle**. The controller synchronizes spindle rotation to the Z-axis feed such that one spindle revolution corresponds to one pitch of advance. Syntax:
```
G99 G84 R5.0 Z-15.0 F300
```
At the bottom of the hole, the spindle *automatically reverses* and retracts at the same synchronized feed, unscrewing the tap. The F-value *must* equal the pitch times the spindle speed (F = P × N).

**Application:** Threaded holes in mild steel, aluminum, and cast iron. Rigid tapping is preferred over floating tap holders for higher speed and accuracy.

**(iii) G85 — Boring Cycle:**
G85 is the **boring-in-boring-out cycle**. The tool feeds *in* to Z-depth and feeds *out* at the same feedrate (no spindle stop, no rapid retract). Syntax:
```
G99 G85 R2.0 Z-20.0 F80
```
This produces a **smooth, mark-free bore** because the tool's cutting edge stays engaged throughout the retract. The retract is *feed* retract, not rapid, so no scoring mark is left.

**Application:** Finishing bores where surface finish is critical. Combined with a fine boring bar, G85 achieves **Ra 0.8 μm** or better.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (CNC Canned Cycles):**
> 1. **Confusing G80 cancellation** — students forget to issue G80, causing the *next motion block* to re-apply the canned cycle at an unintended location. Marks deducted.
> 2. **Wrong F-value in G84** — students compute F = feed/min but forget that for tapping, F *must* equal pitch × RPM. Marks deducted.
> 3. **Forgetting to re-issue G43 after M06** — the most common crash cause. The examiner specifically tests if you know *when* length offset must be re-applied.
> 4. **Mixing G98 and G99** — G98 retracts to initial plane, G99 retracts to R-plane. In multi-hole bolt patterns, *G99 is preferred*; students using G98 with M06 in the middle of a pattern crash the tool. Marks deducted.
> 5. **Not writing M09 M05 before M06** — spindle running during tool change is unsafe. KTU examiner deducts 1 mark per missing safety M-code.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Workpiece Setting** = physical work-holding + WCS declaration. Without *both*, the program is unusable.
- **G54–G59** store *six* independent work offsets. G54 is the default.
- **Edge finder** gives ±0.01 mm accuracy. Always use it for X-Y datum.
- **Tool length offset** (G43) is *added* to programmed Z. Each tool has its own H-row.
- **Cutter radius comp** (G41/G42) is *added* to programmed X-Y. Each tool has its own D-row.
- **M06** is the tool-change command. Always preceded by M05 (spindle stop) and followed by M03 (spindle restart) and **G43 H##** (re-apply length offset).
- **G80 cancels all canned cycles**. Place it *before* the next non-cycle motion block.
- **G81** = drill, **G82** = drill + dwell, **G83** = peck, **G84** = tap, **G85** = bore, **G86** = bore+rapid retract, **G87** = back-bore, **G88** = manual retract, **G89** = bore+dwell.
- **R-plane** is the rapid-traverse plane above the workpiece. **Z-target** is the final depth. **Q** is the peck increment (G83). **P** is the dwell in milliseconds (G82, G89).
- **G99 (return to R)** is preferred over G98 (return to initial) for cycle efficiency.
- **For a complete multi-tool program**, follow the *quartet*: M06, G43 H##, M03 S##, M08 — and at the end, M05, M09, *then* M06 for the next tool.
- **For tapping (G84)**, the F-address = pitch × spindle RPM — never a free-form feedrate.
- **For peck drilling (G83)**, the number of pecks is the *ceiling* of $\Delta Z / Q$, not the floor.
- **Always end a program** with `G91 G28 Z0.0` (safe Z), `G28 X0 Y0` (machine zero), and `M30` (rewind/end).
- **Three KTU-typical 14-mark question patterns** to expect: (a) workpiece setting procedure + G54 calculation, (b) multi-tool program with three tools, (c) canned cycle notes covering G82, G83, G84, G85.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of CNC Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of CNC Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machine** is a fully automated, multi-axis manufacturing system in which the relative motion between the cutting tool and the workpiece, the selection of operating parameters, and the sequencing of auxiliary functions are governed by a stored program of alphanumeric instructions (typically conforming to **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267** standards) executed in real-time by a dedicated computer-based controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "CNC machines may be broadly classified based on (a) the type of motion control strategy (point-to-point, straight-line, contouring), (b) the number of simultaneously controlled axes, and (c) the specific manufacturing operation they perform (milling, turning, grinding, EDM, etc.)."

### 1.2 Classification of CNC Machines — Master Taxonomy

The classification of CNC machines can be approached from **four orthogonal perspectives**:

1. **Based on Motion Control Strategy** → Path / Trajectory Generation Approach
2. **Based on Number of Control Axes** → Degrees of Freedom under Simultaneous Control
3. **Based on Type of Manufacturing Operation** → Process Specificity
4. **Based on Special / Hybrid Configurations** → Dedicated or Composite Machines

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are driving a car from one city to another:
> - **Point-to-Point (PTP) control** is like parking the car at specific landmarks (Kochi → Bangalore → Chennai). The exact path between cities doesn't matter — only the destination.
> - **Straight-Line Control** is like driving strictly on a single straight highway between two towns — the path is constrained to a line.
> - **Contouring Control** is like driving a winding hill road that hugs the contour of a mountain — the path must follow a complex, continuous curve at all times.

Just as the GPS must know **what kind of route** you are taking, a CNC controller must know **what kind of path** the tool must trace relative to the workpiece.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Axis nomenclature:** Always designated as **X, Y, Z** (primary linear axes) and **A, B, C** (rotary axes about X, Y, Z respectively)
- **Controller resolution:** Typically **0.001 mm** (1 μm) for precision machines
- **Standard reference code:** **G-code (ISO 6983)** and **M-code** for miscellaneous functions
- **Repeatability:** Modern CNC machines achieve ± **0.0025 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Motion Control Strategy

This is the **most fundamental** classification from the controller's algorithmic standpoint. It defines *how* the interpolator calculates intermediate tool positions.

#### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (PTP) Control

- **Definition:** The cutting tool is positioned at a discrete (X, Y) coordinate, performs an operation (typically drilling, tapping, boring), retracts, then moves to the next point.
- **Path Between Points:** The trajectory during rapid traverse is **NOT controlled** — only the start and end coordinates matter.
- **No Machining During Motion:** Cutting action is **zero** during the rapid move.
- **Typical Applications:** Drilling, boring, tapping, punching.

#### 2.1.2 Straight-Line Control (1-D Contouring)

- **Definition:** The tool moves in a controlled linear path along **one axis at a time** between two points.
- **Contouring Capability:** Limited to producing **rectilinear profiles** (squares, rectangles, simple slots).
- **Typical Applications:** Face milling of rectangular pockets, simple slot cutting.

#### 2.1.3 Contouring Control (Continuous Path Control)

- **Definition:** The controller continuously interpolates the tool path so that the tool traces a **smooth, predefined 2D or 3D curve** while machining.
- **Interpolation Modes:** **Linear (G01)**, **Circular (G02/G03)**, **Helical**, **Spline**, **NURBS**.
- **Typical Applications:** Complex die/mold cavities, turbine blades, 3D sculptured surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:**
> "Contouring control is the highest level of motion control — it is **mandatory** for any machine that produces curved surfaces, and the controller's interpolator must compute axis velocities in real time to maintain a constant tool-path feed rate (F) along the resultant vector."

### 2.2 Classification Based on Number of Axes

| Number of Axes | Motion Capability | Typical Configuration | Common Applications |
|----------------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **2-Axis** | Tool moves in only 2 axes simultaneously | Simple lathe (X, Z) | Plain turning, facing |
| **2.5-Axis** | 2 axes continuous + 1 axis incremental (indexed) | Milling machine with rotary table that locks during cut | Pocket milling, 3-sided machining |
| **3-Axis** | X, Y, Z simultaneous contouring | Vertical Machining Center (VMC) | Die cavities, prismatic parts |
| **4-Axis** | 3 linear + 1 rotary (typically A around X) | VMC with 4th axis rotary table | Helical features, impellers |
| **5-Axis** | 3 linear + 2 rotary simultaneous | 5-axis VMC / Horizontal MC | Aerospace blades, medical implants |
| **Multi-Axis (>5)** | 3 linear + 3+ rotary | Special purpose machines | Propellers, turbine assemblies |

> [!NOTE]
> **2.5-Axis Explanation (Common Confusion Clarified):**
> A 2.5-axis machine can do **full contouring** in 2 axes (say X and Y) but the **third axis (Z)** moves only in **discrete steps** — not simultaneously. It is the bridge between PTP and true 3-axis contouring.

### 2.3 Classification Based on Manufacturing Operation

| Type of CNC Machine | Primary Process | Number of Axes | KTU Example Question Tag |
|---------------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **CNC Lathe / Turning Center** | Turning, facing, threading | 2–4 | "Explain 4-axis turning center" |
| **Vertical Machining Center (VMC)** | Milling, drilling, tapping | 3–5 | "Draw block diagram of VMC" |
| **Horizontal Machining Center (HMC)** | Heavy milling, pallet changing | 3–4 | "Compare VMC vs HMC" |
| **CNC Grinding Machine** | Surface, cylindrical, tool grinding | 3–5 | "Types of CNC grinders" |
| **CNC EDM (Die-Sink / Wire-EDM)** | Electrical discharge machining | 2–5 (typically) | "Wire-EDM working principle" |
| **CNC Router** | Wood, plastic, soft metal cutting | 3 | Wood working industry |
| **CNC Punching / Shearing** | Sheet metal operations | 2 | Press brake applications |
| **CNC Laser / Water-Jet Cutter** | Thermal / abrasive cutting | 2–5 | Sheet metal industry |
| **Additive + Subtractive Hybrid** | 3D printing + milling in one setup | 3–5 | Research / Industry 4.0 |

### 2.4 Machining Centers vs. Turning Centers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - A **Machining Center (MC)** has a **spindle** that holds the **rotating tool** and the **workpiece is stationary** (mounted on a table). Operates on **Milling** principle.
> - A **Turning Center (TC)** has a **chuck** that holds the **rotating workpiece** and the **tool is stationary (or traverses)**. Operates on **Turning** principle.

### 2.5 Special Purpose CNC Machines

1. **CNC Swiss-Type Lathe (Sliding Headstock Lathe):** Used for long, slender, high-precision parts (watch components, medical bone screws). The bar stock moves through a guide bushing while tools cut close to the bushing — minimizes deflection.
2. **CNC Cylindrical Grinder:** For high precision OD/ID grinding of shafts, rollers.
3. **CNC Surface Grinder:** For flat surface finishing.
4. **CNC Tool & Cutter Grinder:** For resharpening / manufacturing of cutting tools (drills, end mills).
5. **Wire-EDM:** Uses a thin brass/ molybdenum wire (typically 0.1–0.3 mm) as an electrode to cut intricate 2D profiles in hardened conductive materials.
6. **Die-Sinking EDM (Sinker EDM / Ram EDM):** Uses a shaped electrode to "sink" 3D cavities into hardened dies.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** 5-axis CNC machines for turbine blades, structural components.
- **Automotive Industry:** Mass production of engine blocks, cylinder heads on transfer-line VMCs/HMCs.
- **Medical Implants:** Patient-specific titanium implants via 5-axis simultaneous contouring.
- **Die & Mold Industry:** Wire-EDM and 5-axis milling for injection mold cavities.
- **Electronics:** CNC routers and laser cutters for PCB prototyping and enclosures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Classification Walkthrough & Comparative Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Selection of CNC Machine for a Given Component

Let us consider the **decision matrix** that an engineer follows when selecting a CNC machine type for manufacturing a component. The analysis is purely engineering-based (no programming).

#### Step 1 — Identify the Primary Manufacturing Process

**Logic:** Match the **manufacturing operation required** to the **machine category**.

$$\text{Process Required} = f(\text{Component Geometry, Material, Tolerance, Volume})$$

For a rotational, axisymmetric part (e.g., a shaft) → **CNC Turning Center**.
For a prismatic part with flat surfaces and holes (e.g., a bracket) → **VMC**.
For a complex 3D sculptured surface (e.g., a turbine blade) → **5-axis VMC**.
For a hardened die cavity → **Die-Sink EDM** or **5-axis MC + Wire-EDM**.

#### Step 2 — Determine the Required Motion Control Strategy

**Logic:** Based on geometry, decide between PTP, straight-line, or contouring.

$$\text{Motion Strategy} = \begin{cases} \text{PTP}, & \text{if only discrete holes} \\ \text{Straight-line}, & \text{if only rectangular pockets} \\ \text{Contouring}, & \text{if curved 3D surfaces} \end{cases}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Minimum Number of Axes Required

For a free-form surface, the **number of simultaneous axes** determines the capability:

$$n_{\text{required}} = n_{\text{DOF of surface}} + n_{\text{tool orientation DOF}}$$

For a turbine blade with a 3D sculptured surface and tool-normal vector control:

$$n_{\text{required}} = 3_{\text{positional}} + 2_{\text{orientational}} = 5 \text{ axes}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Effective Feed Rate Vector (for Contouring Machines)

When a tool moves in 3-axis contouring mode at programmed feed $F$ (mm/min), the individual axis feed rates are resolved by the controller's interpolator:

$$F_x = F \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{L}, \quad F_y = F \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{L}, \quad F_z = F \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{L}$$

where the resultant path length for one move is:

$$L = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$$

**Derivation logic:** The vector sum of the three axis velocity components must equal the resultant feed velocity along the tool path. Since velocity is a vector:

$$\vec{V}_{\text{resultant}} = \vec{V}_x + \vec{V}_y + \vec{V}_z$$

Taking magnitudes (assuming the components are perpendicular to each other in X, Y, Z):

$$\vert \vec{V}_{\text{resultant}} \vert = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2 + V_z^2}$$

This is the **fundamental kinematic equation** used by every CNC interpolator (linear, circular, and helical).

#### Step 5 — Realize Why 5-Axis is Necessary for Blade Machining

A 4-axis machine (3 linear + 1 rotary) can only orient the tool around **one rotational axis**. For a turbine blade, the tool-normal vector changes in **3D space**, requiring rotation about **two non-parallel axes** simultaneously — hence **5 axes minimum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis Equivalence Rule (KTU):**
> 3 linear axes (X, Y, Z) + 2 rotary axes (typically A and C, or B and C) = **5 simultaneous axes**
> The 6th axis (rare) is used in special applications like propeller machining.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — VMC vs HMC

| Parameter | Vertical Machining Center (VMC) | Horizontal Machining Center (HMC) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Spindle Orientation** | Vertical (Z-axis along gravity) | Horizontal (Z-axis perpendicular to gravity) |
| **Workpiece Mounting** | Top of a fixed table | Side of a pallet / indexer |
| **Chip Fall** | Gravity-assisted — falls away from workpiece | Requires auger / chip conveyor |
| **Tool Access** | 3 sides accessible (X, Y, Z) | 4 sides accessible — better for box-like parts |
| **Coolant** | Flood / through-spindle | Through-spindle + high-pressure flood |
| **Typical Part** | Flat plates, dies, medium enclosures | Engine blocks, gearbox housings |
| **Productivity** | Lower — single fixture | Higher — pallet changer allows off-line setup |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (1.5× – 2× of VMC) |

### 3.3 Comparative Matrix — Wire-EDM vs Die-Sink EDM

| Parameter | Wire-EDM (WEDM) | Die-Sink EDM (SEDM) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Electrode** | Continuous traveling wire (brass / Mo) | Shaped copper / graphite electrode |
| **Geometry Produced** | 2D through-cuts (slots, profiles) | 3D blind cavities |
| **Material Hardness** | Any conductive material (HRC 60+ easily) | Any conductive material |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 0.2 – 1.6 μm | 0.4 – 3.2 μm |
| **Kerf Width** | 0.02 – 0.3 mm (wire diameter dependent) | N/A (no kerf) |
| **Dielectric Fluid** | Deionized water | EDM oil / kerosene |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CNC MACHINE - MASTER CLASSIFICATION"] --> B["BASED ON MOTION CONTROL"]
    A --> C["BASED ON NUMBER OF AXES"]
    A --> D["BASED ON OPERATION TYPE"]
    A --> E["BASED ON SPECIAL PURPOSE"]

    B --> B1["POINT TO POINT PTP"]
    B --> B2["STRAIGHT LINE CONTROL"]
    B --> B3["CONTOURING CONTROL 2D 3D"]

    C --> C1["2 AXIS - LATHE"]
    C --> C2["3 AXIS - VMC"]
    C --> C3["4 AXIS - 3 LINEAR 1 ROTARY"]
    C --> C4["5 AXIS - 3 LINEAR 2 ROTARY"]
    C --> C5["MULTI AXIS - 6 OR MORE"]

    D --> D1["MACHINING CENTER MILLING"]
    D --> D2["TURNING CENTER LATHING"]
    D --> D3["GRINDING CENTER"]
    D --> D4["EDM WIRE AND SINKER"]
    D --> D5["ROUTER AND LASER"]

    E --> E1["SWISS TYPE LATHE"]
    E --> E2["TOOL CUTTER GRINDER"]
    E --> E3["HYBRID ADDITIVE SUBTRACTIVE"]
    E --> E4["PALLET CHANGER HMC"]
```

### 4.2 Axis Configuration Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINEAR_AXES["PRIMARY LINEAR AXES"]
        X["X AXIS - LONGITUDINAL"]
        Y["Y AXIS - CROSS"]
        Z["Z AXIS - VERTICAL ALONG SPINDLE"]
    end
    subgraph ROTARY_AXES["SECONDARY ROTARY AXES"]
        A["A AXIS - ROTATION ABOUT X"]
        B["B AXIS - ROTATION ABOUT Y"]
        C["C AXIS - ROTATION ABOUT Z"]
    end
    X --- Y --- Z
    A --- B --- C
    Z -. "SPINDLE NOSE" .-> Z
    C -. "CHUCK ROTATION ON LATHE" .-> C
```

### 4.3 5-Axis Machine — Kinematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE["MACHINE BASE / BED"] --> X1["X AXIS SLIDE"]
    X1 --> Y1["Y AXIS SLIDE"]
    Y1 --> Z1["Z AXIS SLIDE RAM"]
    Z1 --> A1["A AXIS TILT HEAD"]
    A1 --> C1["C AXIS ROTARY TABLE"]
    C1 --> WP["WORKPIECE"]
    SP["SPINDLE WITH TOOL"] -. "5 AXIS SIMULTANEOUS CUT" .-> WP
    CTRL["CNC CONTROLLER FANUC SIEMENS"] -. "FEEDBACK CLOSED LOOP" .-> X1
    CTRL -. "FEEDBACK CLOSED LOOP" .-> Y1
    CTRL -. "FEEDBACK CLOSED LOOP" .-> Z1
    CTRL -. "FEEDBACK CLOSED LOOP" .-> A1
    CTRL -. "FEEDBACK CLOSED LOOP" .-> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows represent the **mechanical chain** (kinematic tree) of the machine. The **dashed arrows** from the **CNC CONTROLLER** to each axis represent the **closed-loop servo feedback** (encoder signals) used for accurate position correction. The **dashed arrow** between the **SPINDLE** and the **WORKPIECE** represents the actual material-removal contact.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate clearly between Point-to-Point (PTP), Straight-line, and Contouring control in CNC machines. Give one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PTP Control | Straight-Line | Contouring |
|---------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| **Tool path between endpoints** | Uncontrolled (rapid traverse) | Controlled along a single straight line | Controlled along a continuous curve (linear, circular, spline) |
| **Machining during motion** | No | Yes (one axis at a time) | Yes (multiple axes simultaneously) |
| **Interpolator complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Typical application** | Drilling a bolt-hole pattern on a plate | Slot cutting on a rectangular pocket | Machining a turbine blade airfoil |
| **Controller cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Machining Center? How is it different from a Turning Center?**

**Model Answer:**
- A **Machining Center (MC)** is a CNC milling machine equipped with an **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** and a **coolant system**, capable of performing multiple operations (milling, drilling, tapping, reaming) in a single setup. The **tool rotates** and the **workpiece is stationary**.
- A **Turning Center (TC)** is a CNC lathe equipped with a **turret or multiple tool stations** and a **sub-spindle / live tooling option**, capable of performing turning, facing, threading, and (in advanced models) milling/drilling operations. The **workpiece rotates** and the **tool is held stationary or traverses**.
- Key differentiator: **rotating entity** — tool in MC, workpiece in TC.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Classify CNC machines based on (i) motion control strategy and (ii) number of axes. Draw a labeled block diagram of a 5-axis VMC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare in detail Vertical Machining Center (VMC) and Horizontal Machining Center (HMC) in terms of construction, chip evacuation, part accessibility, productivity, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & 5-axis VMC Block Diagram:**

- **Motion Control Classification:** PTP, Straight-line, Contouring (2D & 3D).
- **Number of Axes Classification:** 2-axis, 2.5-axis, 3-axis, 4-axis, 5-axis, multi-axis.
- **5-Axis VMC Block Diagram:** (Refer to the kinematic architecture shown in Section 4.3 of these notes). The 5 axes are X, Y, Z, A (tilt head), and C (rotary table on the bed). All five must be simultaneously controlled by the CNC controller for the tool to remain normal to the curved surface during cutting.

**(b) VMC vs HMC — Comparative Analysis:**

| Parameter | VMC | HMC |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Spindle axis** | Vertical (along Z) | Horizontal (along Z) |
| **Workpiece mounting** | Top of table | Side of pallet / indexer |
| **Chip fall** | Gravity | Needs auger/conveyor |
| **Accessibility** | 3 sides | 4 sides — better for box parts |
| **Productivity** | Single fixture, lower | Pallet changer, higher |
| **Typical parts** | Dies, plates, moulds | Engine blocks, gear boxes |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (1.5×–2×) |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Wire-EDM** and **Die-Sink EDM**. State at least four differences between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine is programmed to move from point **P1 (10, 20, 5)** to point **P2 (40, 60, 25)** in linear interpolation at a programmed feed rate of **F = 600 mm/min**. Calculate:
  (i) The resultant path length $L$.
  (ii) The individual axis feed rates $F_x$, $F_y$, and $F_z$.
  (iii) The time required to complete the move. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wire-EDM vs Die-Sink EDM:**

- **Wire-EDM (WEDM):** A thin continuously traveling brass/molybdenum wire (0.1–0.3 mm dia) acts as the electrode. Spark discharges between the wire and the workpiece (separated by deionized water dielectric) erode the material along a 2D contour.
- **Die-Sink EDM (SEDM):** A shaped copper/graphite electrode is plunged into the workpiece under servo control, with sparks eroding a 3D cavity that is a negative of the electrode shape.

| Difference | WEDM | SEDM |
|-----------|------|------|
| Electrode | Traveling wire | Shaped die |
| Geometry | 2D through-cut | 3D blind cavity |
| Kerf | 0.02–0.3 mm | None |
| Dielectric | Deionized water | EDM oil |
| Surface finish | 0.2–1.6 μm Ra | 0.4–3.2 μm Ra |

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution:**

Given:
- $P_1 = (10, 20, 5)$ mm
- $P_2 = (40, 60, 25)$ mm
- $F = 600$ mm/min

**Step (i) — Resultant path length $L$:**

$$\Delta x = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y = 60 - 20 = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta z = 25 - 5 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$L = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$$

$$L = \sqrt{(30)^2 + (40)^2 + (20)^2}$$

$$L = \sqrt{900 + 1600 + 400} = \sqrt{2900}$$

$$L = 53.8516 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (ii) — Individual axis feed rates:**

$$F_x = F \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{L} = 600 \cdot \frac{30}{53.8516} = 334.29 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$F_y = F \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{L} = 600 \cdot \frac{40}{53.8516} = 445.72 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$F_z = F \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{L} = 600 \cdot \frac{20}{53.8516} = 222.86 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step (iii) — Time to complete the move:**

$$t = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{53.8516}{600} = 0.08975 \text{ min} = 5.385 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in the final time answer: $0.08975$ min must be multiplied by 60 → **5.385 s**. Marks lost if unit not converted.
> 2. **Mixing up axis direction:** The deltas are always **final minus initial** — never the absolute difference. A student writing $L = \sqrt{10^2 + 20^2 + 30^2}$ will lose full marks.
> 3. **In comparison questions,** students often write one-sided answers. Always present a **two-column table** with both VMC and HMC for parallel scoring.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Three types of motion control:** PTP, Straight-line, Contouring.
- [ ] **PTP** = no controlled motion during traverse; **Contouring** = full continuous path with interpolation.
- [ ] **Axis naming convention:** X, Y, Z = linear; A, B, C = rotary about X, Y, Z.
- [ ] **2.5-axis** = 2-axis contouring + 1 axis stepped (NOT simultaneous).
- [ ] **5-axis** = 3 linear + 2 rotary simultaneous — needed for turbine blades, impellers.
- [ ] **MC vs TC:** Tool rotates in MC; Workpiece rotates in TC.
- [ ] **VMC** = vertical spindle, gravity chip fall, 3-side access.
- [ ] **HMC** = horizontal spindle, pallet changer, 4-side access, higher productivity.
- [ ] **Wire-EDM** = 2D through-cut with traveling wire in deionized water.
- [ ] **Die-Sink EDM** = 3D cavity with shaped electrode in EDM oil.
- [ ] **Resultant feed vector formula:** $F_{\text{resultant}} = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ — must be memorized.
- [ ] **Time for a CNC move:** $t = L / F$ — always state the final unit (seconds).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pick and Place Machine, CNC 3D Printer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pick and Place Machine, CNC 3D Printer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Pick and Place Machine & CNC 3D Printer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pick and Place Machine (PPM)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Pick and Place Machine* is a computer-controlled, multi-axis (typically 3 to 6 axes) mechatronic assembly system used in Surface Mount Technology (SMT) that automatically picks electronic components from a feeder, aligns them using a vision system, and places them with high positional accuracy onto a Printed Circuit Board (PCB). It is essentially a Cartesian/SCARA/Delta-style robotic gantry driven by CNC servo or stepper controllers executing G-code-like motion commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **CPH (Components Per Hour):** The throughput metric of a PPM.
> - **Placement Accuracy:** Usually **± 0.05 mm** for high-end machines and **± 0.01 mm** for chip shooters.
> - **Vision System:** Closed-loop optical feedback using upward/downward cameras for fiducial and component alignment.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are assembling a jigsaw puzzle, but instead of your hands, you are blindfolded. Now imagine someone removes the blindfold, gives you a tiny suction-cup stylus, and tells you to place 50,000 tiny chips in 8 hours — *without dropping one*. That is essentially what a Pick and Place Machine does. The *vision system is your "eyes"*, the *vacuum nozzle is your "suction-cup stylus"*, and the *CNC gantry is your "robotic arm"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PPMs are often called "Chip Shooters" when designed for high-speed passive component placement (resistors, capacitors) and "Flex Mounters" or "Fine Pitch Placers" for ICs and BGAs (Ball Grid Arrays).

---

### 1.2 CNC 3D Printer (Additive Manufacturing via CNC Control)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *CNC 3D Printer* is a computer-controlled additive manufacturing system that fabricates three-dimensional solid objects layer-by-layer from digital models (CAD/STL files) by interpreting G-code/M-code instructions through a multi-axis motion controller. The most common variants are **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)**, **SLA (Stereolithography)**, and **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering)** — all sharing the same fundamental CNC architecture: a digital interpolator driving stepper/servo motors on linear axes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is "CNC":** A 3D printer is CNC because it uses *Computerized Numerical Control* — the machine follows a numerical program (G-code) rather than manual hand-cranked wheels. The "controller" interprets G-code and energizes stepper drivers in a coordinated manner.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a 3D printer as a **glue gun mounted on a plotter pen**. The plotter moves the glue gun in X–Y, the bed moves down (Z) after each pass, and the glue gun extrudes molten plastic. Layer after layer, a 2D path becomes a 3D object — exactly like icing a cake, one thin layer at a time, where the shape of each layer is dictated by a numerical "recipe" (G-code).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A CNC 3D printer can be considered the *inverse* of a CNC milling machine — instead of *removing* material (subtractive), it *adds* material (additive), but the motion control principles (linear interpolation, G-code, stepper resolution) are identical.

### 1.3 Core Physical Metrics (Highlighted in **bold**)

| Parameter | Pick and Place | CNC 3D Printer (FDM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Positional Accuracy** | **± 0.01 mm to ± 0.05 mm** | **± 0.05 mm to ± 0.1 mm** (typical hobbyist) |
| **Resolution** | **20 µm optical** | **50–400 µm (layer height)** |
| **Throughput** | **20,000–200,000 CPH** | **20–200 cm³/hr (volumetric)** |
| **Axes** | 3–6 (X, Y, Z, θ, vision offsets) | 3–5 (X, Y, Z, dual extrusion) |
| **Drive System** | Servo + Linear motor | Stepper + Lead screw / Belt |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pick and Place Machine — Architecture & Operation

### 2.1.1 System Architecture (Block-Level)

A Pick and Place Machine is composed of the following functional subsystems:

1. **Gantry & Linear Axes (X, Y, Z, R):**
   - **X-axis:** Lateral movement across the PCB (typically a linear motor or servo-driven gantry).
   - **Y-axis:** Movement in the direction of the conveyor (PCB feed direction).
   - **Z-axis:** Vertical descent of the placement head to the PCB plane.
   - **R-axis (Theta):** Rotation of the head to correct component angular orientation.
   - **Why all four?** Components arrive at random orientations in tape reels; the R-axis rotates them to match the PCB pad geometry.

2. **Pick Head / Nozzle Assembly:**
   - Holds a **vacuum nozzle** (sizes from 0.4 mm to 10 mm) connected to a vacuum generator (venturi or vacuum pump).
   - Multi-nozzle turret heads (e.g., 12-spindle) are common in high-speed machines.

3. **Vision System:**
   - **Upward camera:** Inspects PCB fiducials to determine board position and compensate for skew.
   - **Downward camera:** Inspects the picked component from below, measures centroid offset ($\Delta x$, $\Delta y$) and rotational offset ($\Delta \theta$), and feeds these corrections back to the motion controller.
   - This is a **closed-loop feedback** mechanism.

4. **Feeder Bank:**
   - Tape reels, stick feeders, tray feeders — all indexed by stepper-driven sprockets.

5. **Conveyor & PCB Clamping:**
   - Brings the PCB into position with **± 0.1 mm** repeatability.

### 2.1.2 Why Vision Feedback is Critical

Without vision, an open-loop PPM would have no idea that the component was rotated 5° or shifted 0.2 mm on the nozzle tip. The vision system computes:

$$
\Delta x = x_{\text{pickup}} - x_{\text{PCB pad}}
$$

$$
\Delta y = y_{\text{pickup}} - y_{\text{PCB pad}}
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \theta_{\text{pickup}} - \theta_{\text{PCB pad}}
$$

These deltas are added to the placement coordinate before the Z-axis descent. This is mathematically identical to **G-code G92 (Coordinate System Offset)** in CNC.

### 2.1.3 Placement Cycle (Operation Phases)

A single placement cycle consists of:

1. **Indexing feeder** to expose next component.
2. **Pick:** Z-axis descends to feeder pocket, vacuum engages, Z retracts.
3. **Vision correction:** Downward camera captures component, computes offsets.
4. **Move to PCB:** Gantry interpolates X, Y, R to the corrected pad coordinate at high speed.
5. **Place:** Z-axis descends to PCB, vacuum releases, Z retracts.

The cycle time $T_{\text{cycle}}$ determines the CPH:

$$
\text{CPH} = \frac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Insight:** On the KTU exam, you may be asked to compute CPH given cycle time, or to compute cycle time given CPH. The relationship is *inverse* — always double the units.

---

## 2.2 CNC 3D Printer — Architecture & Operation

### 2.2.1 FDM 3D Printer Subsystems

1. **Frame & Motion Axes:**
   - **Cartesian (X, Y, Z):** Most common — three perpendicular linear axes.
   - **CoreXY / H-Bot:** Belt-driven X/Y for higher speed.
   - **Delta:** Three vertical linear arms moving a suspended effector.

2. **Extruder (Hot End):**
   - Heats thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) to **190–260 °C**.
   - Pushes filament via a **direct drive** or **Bowden** extruder.
   - The melted plastic is extruded through a brass nozzle of diameter typically **0.4 mm**.

3. **Build Platform (Bed):**
   - Heated bed (**40–110 °C**) to prevent warping.
   - Moves in Z-direction (layer-by-layer lowering).

4. **Controller Board:**
   - Common boards: **Arduino + RAMPS**, **Marlin firmware**, or **32-bit SKR boards**.
   - Executes G-code (G0, G1, G28, G29, M104, M140, M106, etc.).

5. **Filament Runout & Leveling Sensors:**
   - Provide safety and auto-bed-leveling feedback.

### 2.2.2 G-Code in 3D Printing (Critical for KTU)

The same G-code used in CNC milling is repurposed for 3D printing, with these commonly used commands:

| G/M Code | Function |
|---|---|
| `G28` | Auto home all axes |
| `G29` | Bed leveling probe sequence |
| `G0/G1` | Linear move (rapid / controlled) |
| `G92 E0` | Reset extruder position to zero |
| `M104` | Set hotend temperature |
| `M140` | Set bed temperature |
| `M106` | Set part cooling fan speed |
| `M109` | Wait for hotend to reach temp |
| `M190` | Wait for bed to reach temp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The major difference from milling G-code: in 3D printing, the **E-axis (Extruder)** is also interpolated alongside X, Y, Z. A typical line might be: `G1 X10 Y20 Z0.2 E0.05 F1500`.

### 2.2.3 Slicing: From STL to G-Code

A 3D model (`.STL`) is sliced by software (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D) into horizontal layers of thickness $h_L$ (layer height). For each layer, the slicer generates a **toolpath** consisting of:
- **Perimeters (outer & inner walls)**
- **Infill pattern (grid, honeycomb, gyroid)**
- **Top/bottom solid layers**
- **Travel moves (non-extruding)**

The total Z-motion equals the number of layers:

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H_{\text{object}}}{h_L}
$$

where $H_{\text{object}}$ is the object height.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derived from **first principles** in Section 3. Memorize the boxed ones for board exams.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPH} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Pick & Place throughput | components/hr |
| 2 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{pick}} + T_{\text{vision}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{place}}$ | Cycle time decomposition | seconds |
| 3 | $R_{\text{stepper}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{pitch}}}{\text{SPR} \cdot \mu}$ | Linear resolution of stepper-driven axis | mm/step |
| 4 | $\theta_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{360°}{\text{SPR} \cdot \mu}$ | Angular step size (with microstepping $\mu$) | degrees/step |
| 5 | $T_{\text{print}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}}}{Q_{\text{extrude}}}$ | 3D print time (volumetric) | hours |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{extrude}} = \dfrac{\pi d_n^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | Volumetric extrusion rate | mm³/s |
| 7 | $N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{object}}}{h_L}$ | Number of printed layers | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_f = f_{\text{rate}} \cdot \text{factor}$ | Feed-to-flow conversion (slicer) | mm/s |
| 9 | $A_{\text{placement}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{PCB}} \cdot W_{\text{PCB}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Placement productivity area | mm²/s |
| 10 | $P_{\text{acc}} = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | Placement positional error norm | mm |

> **Legend:** $d_n$ = nozzle diameter, $v_f$ = filament feed speed, $P_{\text{pitch}}$ = lead screw pitch, SPR = steps per revolution, $\mu$ = microstepping factor, $h_L$ = layer height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipe `|` symbols** are used inside any table cell — all absolute-value or "such that" notation uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ explicitly to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stepper Motor Linear Resolution (CNC Axis)

**Context:** A CNC 3D printer (or PPM) uses a stepper motor coupled to a lead screw. We want to know the *smallest linear displacement* the axis can produce.

**Given:**
- Step angle of motor: $\theta_s = 1.8°$ (typical for a 1.8° hybrid stepper)
- Microstepping factor: $\mu = 16$ (typical for A4988 or TMC2208 drivers)
- Lead screw pitch: $P = 8 \text{ mm/rev}$ (2 mm pitch × 4-start, or 8 mm single-start)

**Step 1 — Microstep angle:**
The driver subdivides each full step into $\mu$ microsteps. The angular resolution per microstep is:

$$
\theta_{\mu} = \frac{\theta_s}{\mu} = \frac{1.8°}{16} = 0.1125°\text{/microstep}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear displacement per microstep:**
For a lead screw, one full revolution advances the nut by $P$ mm. Therefore, the linear displacement per microstep is:

$$
R_{\text{stepper}} = \frac{P \cdot \theta_{\mu}}{360°} = \frac{P}{\frac{360°}{\theta_{\mu}}} = \frac{P}{\text{SPR} \cdot \mu}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
R_{\text{stepper}} = \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{200 \cdot 16} = \frac{8}{3200} = 0.0025 \text{ mm/microstep} = 2.5 \text{ µm}
$$

**Result:** The axis has a linear resolution of **2.5 µm** — this defines the *smallest controllable motion*. Layer heights must be integer multiples of this resolution to avoid rounding errors (e.g., 0.1 mm = 40 microsteps — exact).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pick and Place CPH from Cycle Time

**Context:** A mid-range PPM has the following cycle time breakdown (typical industry data):

- Pick time: $T_{\text{pick}} = 0.08 \text{ s}$
- Vision inspection: $T_{\text{vision}} = 0.05 \text{ s}$
- Move (X–Y) at $v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$ over 50 mm average: $T_{\text{move}} = 0.033 \text{ s}$
- Place: $T_{\text{place}} = 0.04 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Sum of sub-times:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.08 + 0.05 + 0.033 + 0.04 = 0.203 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CPH:**

$$
\text{CPH} = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{0.203 \text{ s/component}} \approx 17{,}734 \text{ components/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical adjustment (90% efficiency):**

$$
\text{CPH}_{\text{eff}} = 0.9 \times 17{,}734 \approx 15{,}961 \text{ CPH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** A common student error is forgetting to convert **seconds → hours**. If a question gives cycle time in *seconds* and asks for *CPH*, the **3600** conversion factor is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 3D Print Time (FDM)

**Context:** Estimate the print time for a 100 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm cube filled with **20% infill**.

**Given:**
- Cube volume: $V_{\text{outer}} = 100 \times 100 \times 50 = 500{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$
- Wall thickness: 1.2 mm on each side; 3 perimeters; so solid wall volume:

$$
V_{\text{wall}} = V_{\text{outer}} - (100 - 2 \cdot 1.2)^2 \cdot 50 = 500{,}000 - (97.6)^2 \cdot 50
$$

$$
V_{\text{wall}} = 500{,}000 - 476{,}288 = 23{,}712 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Effective infill volume (inside walls):**

$$
V_{\text{infill\_space}} = 476{,}288 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{infill}} = 0.20 \times 476{,}288 = 95{,}257.6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Total extruded volume (approx, ignoring top/bottom for simplicity):**

$$
V_{\text{material}} \approx V_{\text{wall}} + V_{\text{infill}} = 23{,}712 + 95{,}258 = 118{,}970 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Extrusion rate from slicer:**
Nozzle diameter $d_n = 0.4 \text{ mm}$, filament feed speed $v_f = 3 \text{ mm/s}$ (PLA), filament diameter $d_f = 1.75 \text{ mm}$.

Volumetric flow rate of extruded material:

$$
Q_{\text{extrude}} = \frac{\pi d_n^2}{4} \cdot v_{\text{print}}
$$

For a typical print speed of 60 mm/s and 0.2 mm layer height, the effective volumetric deposition rate is approximately:

$$
Q_{\text{deposit}} = L_{\text{per\_layer}} \cdot h_L \cdot w_{\text{line}} \approx \text{perimeter} \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.4
$$

Simplifying for board exam purposes, use the **average volumetric rate**:

$$
\bar{Q} = 2.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(typical for 0.4 mm nozzle at 60 mm/s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Total print time:**

$$
T_{\text{print}} = \frac{V_{\text{material}}}{\bar{Q}} = \frac{118{,}970 \text{ mm}^3}{2.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} = 47{,}588 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{print}} \approx 13.22 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Real slicers add ~20% time for travel moves and skirt/brim. Expected wall-clock time: **~15 hours**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PPM vs CNC 3D Printer (Side-by-Side Comparison)

| Analytical Dimension | Pick and Place Machine | CNC 3D Printer |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing Type** | Assembly (electronic component placement) | Additive (layer-by-layer deposition) |
| **Primary Axes** | X, Y, Z, R (4–6) | X, Y, Z, E (4) |
| **Drive** | Servo / Linear motor | Stepper (open-loop) |
| **Feedback** | Vision system (closed-loop on position) | None (open-loop) / optional BLTouch |
| **Code Standard** | Proprietary / G-code subset | Full G-code (Marlin, RepRap) |
| **Material Input** | Components in tape/tray | Filament spool / resin / powder |
| **Output Metric** | CPH (components/hr) | cm³/hr (volumetric) |
| **Key Sensor** | Up/down camera, vacuum pressure | Thermistor, endstops, BLTouch |
| **Accuracy Driver** | Camera resolution + motion | Stepper resolution + lead screw pitch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PPM is **closed-loop with vision**, while a typical FDM 3D printer is **open-loop with steppers**. This is the single most important architectural distinction. High-end 3D printers add closed-loop steppers or encoders, but are not standard.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Placement Positional Error (Vector Norm)

When a vision system measures a component offset $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$, the *magnitude* of the placement error the system must correct is:

$$
P_{\text{acc}} = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}
$$

**Example:** Vision detects $\Delta x = 0.03 \text{ mm}$, $\Delta y = 0.04 \text{ mm}$:

$$
P_{\text{acc}} = \sqrt{(0.03)^2 + (0.04)^2} = \sqrt{0.0009 + 0.0016} = \sqrt{0.0025} = 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

The controller then offsets the placement coordinate by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ so that the *actual* component centroid lands on the PCB pad centroid. This is mathematically equivalent to a **G92 (temporary coordinate system shift)** in CNC.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid v10+ syntax**. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA1`) and labels contain no markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pick and Place Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA1[Operator HMI Console] --> nodeA2[Central Motion Controller]
    nodeA3[PCB Conveyor and Clamp] --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA4[X Axis Servo Driver]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA5[Y Axis Servo Driver]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA6[Z Axis Servo Driver]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA7[R Axis Servo Driver]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA8[Linear Encoder X]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA9[Linear Encoder Y]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA10[Z Axis Vertical Slide]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA11[Head Rotation Motor]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA12[Feeder Indexer Controller]
    nodeA12 --> nodeA13[Tape Feeder Bank]
    nodeA13 --> nodeA14[Component Pickup Pocket]
    nodeA10 --> nodeA15[Vacuum Nozzle Tip]
    nodeA14 --> nodeA15
    nodeA2 --> nodeA16[Upward Vision Camera]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA17[Downward Vision Camera]
    nodeA16 --> nodeA18[Vision Processor FPGA]
    nodeA17 --> nodeA18
    nodeA18 --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA19[PCB Placement Location]
    nodeA15 --> nodeA19
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Central Motion Controller** is the brain (analogous to a CNC controller).
- The **Upward Vision Camera** reads PCB fiducials; the **Downward Vision Camera** reads the picked component.
- Both feed data into a **Vision Processor (FPGA)**, which closes the loop back to the controller.
- The **Vacuum Nozzle** moves through Pick → Vision → Move → Place in a continuous cycle.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FDM CNC 3D Printer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeB1[PC with Slicer Software] --> nodeB2[STL File to G Code Conversion]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[G Code File on SD Card or USB]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[32 Bit Controller Board SKR]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[X Stepper Driver TMC2209]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB6[Y Stepper Driver TMC2209]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB7[Z Stepper Driver TMC2209]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB8[E Stepper Driver Extruder Motor]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB9[X Axis Belt Drive]
    nodeB6 --> nodeB10[Y Axis Belt Drive]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB11[Z Axis Lead Screw]
    nodeB8 --> nodeB12[Direct Drive Extruder]
    nodeB12 --> nodeB13[Hot End with 0.4 mm Nozzle]
    nodeB11 --> nodeB14[Heated Bed Build Platform]
    nodeB13 --> nodeB15[Molten Filament Deposition]
    nodeB15 --> nodeB14
    nodeB4 --> nodeB16[Hot End Thermistor Input]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB17[Bed Thermistor Input]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB18[X Endstop Switch]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB19[Y Endstop Switch]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB20[Z Probe BLTouch Auto Leveling]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Slicer** is the offline CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) tool.
- The **Controller Board** (e.g., SKR) is the CNC controller — it interprets G-code.
- Four stepper drivers control X, Y, Z, E (extruder).
- Sensors (thermistors, endstops, BLTouch) provide *open-loop verification* — not true closed-loop position feedback.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Pick and Place Placement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC1[Start Cycle] --> nodeC2[Index Feeder Next Component]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Z Axis Descend to Feeder]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4[Vacuum On Pick Component]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5[Z Axis Retract]
    nodeC5 --> nodeC6[Downward Camera Capture]
    nodeC6 --> nodeC7[Compute dx dy dtheta Offsets]
    nodeC7 --> nodeC8[Interpolate X Y R to PCB Pad]
    nodeC8 --> nodeC9[Z Axis Descend to PCB Plane]
    nodeC9 --> nodeC10[Vacuum Off Place Component]
    nodeC10 --> nodeC11[Z Axis Retract]
    nodeC11 --> nodeC12[End of Cycle Increment CPH Counter]
    nodeC12 --> nodeC2
```

This state diagram mirrors a **CNC canned cycle** (e.g., G81 drilling cycle) — same philosophy: a series of defined sub-operations executed in a loop.

---

## 4.4 Process Flow — FDM 3D Print Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeD1[Start Print Job] --> nodeD2[M104 Set Hotend Target Temp]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3[M140 Set Bed Target Temp]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4[M109 Wait Hotend Ready]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5[M190 Wait Bed Ready]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD6[G28 Home All Axes]
    nodeD6 --> nodeD7[G29 Auto Bed Leveling Probe]
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8[Prime Extruder Skirt Line]
    nodeD8 --> nodeD9[Print Layer 1 G1 with E]
    nodeD9 --> nodeD10[Z Axis Increment by hL]
    nodeD10 --> nodeD11{Layers Remaining?}
    nodeD11 --> nodeD11a[Yes] --> nodeD9
    nodeD11 --> nodeD11b[No] --> nodeD12[Retract Filament M104 S0]
    nodeD12 --> nodeD13[Home Z then Disable Steppers M84]
    nodeD13 --> nodeD14[Print Complete]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decision block `nodeD11` uses a curly-brace label `{Layers Remaining?}` to avoid conflict with Mermaid's reserved word `end`. Always avoid the standalone keyword `end` as a node ID.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Differentiate between a Pick and Place Machine and a CNC 3D Printer based on (a) manufacturing process type, (b) feedback mechanism, and (c) primary output metric.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pick and Place Machine | CNC 3D Printer |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Process Type** | *Assembly* — places pre-made components onto a PCB | *Additive Manufacturing* — deposits material layer-by-layer |
| **(b) Feedback** | *Closed-loop* — uses upward and downward vision cameras to correct placement errors in real time | *Open-loop* — relies on stepper motors with no position feedback (advanced models use BLTouch / encoders) |
| **(c) Output Metric** | *CPH* (Components Per Hour) — measures assembly throughput | *cm³/hr* (Volumetric deposition rate) or *g/hr* of filament extruded |

**Key takeaway (1 line):** *A PPM is a high-speed assembly robot with vision, while a 3D printer is a slow additive fabricator with no vision feedback.*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**List and briefly explain any three G-codes commonly used in CNC 3D printing that are not part of standard milling G-code.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`G29` — Auto Bed Leveling:**
   Initiates a probing sequence where the BLTouch or inductive sensor measures the bed height at multiple points. The controller stores a mesh and compensates Z-height during printing to ensure first-layer adhesion.

2. **`M104 / M109` — Hotend Temperature Control:**
   `M104 S200` sets the hotend to 200 °C without waiting; `M109 S200` sets and *waits* until the temperature is reached. The **S** parameter is the target temperature in °C.

3. **`M140 / M190` — Heated Bed Temperature Control:**
   Analogous to M104/M109, but for the heated build platform. Critical for ABS/PLA adhesion and warp prevention.

*(Note: The E-axis extrusion in `G1` is also unique to 3D printing, where G1 commands move X, Y, Z, and E simultaneously.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, students answer **one full question** out of the choice provided. Each question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Pick and Place Machine used in SMT assembly. Highlight the role of the vision system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
A Pick and Place Machine (PPM) is a CNC-controlled robotic assembly system used in Surface Mount Technology (SMT) lines to mount electronic components onto PCBs. Modern PPMs achieve placement rates of **20,000 to 200,000 CPH** with accuracy of **± 0.05 mm**.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the labeled architecture.)*

**Key Subsystems Explained (3 marks):**
1. **Gantry (X, Y, Z, R axes):** Provides high-speed, multi-axis motion. X-Y are typically linear-motor driven for acceleration up to **5 g**.
2. **Vision System (Upward + Downward Cameras):**
   - *Upward camera* locates PCB fiducials (reference marks) to compute board position and rotation.
   - *Downward camera* inspects the picked component, measures centroid offset $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$, and feeds corrections to the controller.
   - This is a **closed-loop feedback** mechanism — mathematically equivalent to **G92 offset** in CNC.
3. **Vacuum Nozzle & Feeder Bank:** Picks components from tape/stick/tray feeders via negative pressure.

**Conclusion:** The vision system is what differentiates a PPM from a simple Cartesian robot — it enables *micron-level placement accuracy* despite component pickup errors.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A Pick and Place Machine has a cycle time of 0.18 s per component. The PCB is 250 mm × 200 mm with 1,200 components. Calculate (i) the CPH, (ii) the theoretical placement time for the entire PCB, and (iii) the placement productivity in mm²/s.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.18 \text{ s/component}$
- $N = 1{,}200 \text{ components}$
- $A_{\text{PCB}} = 250 \times 200 = 50{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$

**Part (i) — CPH:**

$$
\text{CPH} = \frac{3600}{0.18} = 20{,}000 \text{ components/hr}
$$

**Part (ii) — Total placement time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \times T_{\text{cycle}} = 1{,}200 \times 0.18 = 216 \text{ s} = 3.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Part (iii) — Placement productivity (mm²/s):**

$$
P_{\text{productivity}} = \frac{A_{\text{PCB}}}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{50{,}000}{216} \approx 231.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pick & Place Numericals):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Slip:** Students often forget to multiply by **3600** when going from seconds to hours. Always check: *if the question asks for CPH, the numerator must be 3600.*
> 2. **Confusing Cycle Time with Throughput:** CPH is *inverse* of cycle time. If cycle time doubles, CPH halves — students sometimes invert the relationship by mistake.
> 3. **PCB Area vs. Component Count:** The productivity metric $A_{\text{PCB}} / T_{\text{total}}$ gives mm²/s, not mm²/hr. Mind the units.

---

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the working principle of an FDM-type CNC 3D Printer with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of the slicer software.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
A Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printer is a CNC additive manufacturing system that fabricates parts by extruding thermoplastic filament layer-by-layer. Each layer is a 2D path interpreted from a G-code program, and the Z-axis lowers by the layer height $h_L$ (typically **0.1–0.3 mm**) after each layer completes.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The **STL file** (3D mesh) is sliced into horizontal layers by **slicer software** (Cura, PrusaSlicer).
2. The slicer generates **G-code** containing X, Y, Z, and E (extruder) coordinates plus feedrate `F` and temperature `M104/M140` commands.
3. The **controller board** (Marlin firmware) interprets G-code and energizes the four stepper drivers (X, Y, Z, E).
4. The **hot end** melts PLA/ABS filament (**190–260 °C**) and extrudes it through a **0.4 mm nozzle** along the X-Y toolpath.
5. After each layer, the **Z-axis** drops by $h_L$ and the next layer is deposited on top.

**Role of the Slicer (2 marks):**
The slicer is the **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) software** of a 3D printer. It performs:
- Layer slicing and toolpath generation
- Infill pattern selection (grid, gyroid, honeycomb)
- Support structure generation for overhangs
- Temperature, speed, and retraction settings
- Translation of all settings into executable G-code

**Conclusion:** Without the slicer, the 3D printer is "blind" — the slicer is the *intelligence layer* that converts 3D geometry into motion commands.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A CNC 3D printer uses a 1.8° stepper motor with 16× microstepping driving a lead screw of 8 mm pitch on the Z-axis. Calculate (i) the linear resolution per microstep, and (ii) the number of microsteps required to achieve a layer height of 0.2 mm. Comment on whether 0.2 mm is achievable without rounding error.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Step angle $\theta_s = 1.8°$
- Microstepping $\mu = 16$
- Lead screw pitch $P = 8 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Desired layer height $h_L = 0.2 \text{ mm}$

**Part (i) — Linear resolution per microstep:**

$$
R_{\text{stepper}} = \frac{P}{\text{SPR} \cdot \mu} = \frac{8}{200 \cdot 16} = \frac{8}{3200} = 0.0025 \text{ mm/microstep}
$$

$$
R_{\text{stepper}} = 2.5 \text{ µm/microstep}
$$

**Part (ii) — Microsteps per layer:**

$$
N_{\mu} = \frac{h_L}{R_{\text{stepper}}} = \frac{0.2}{0.0025} = 80 \text{ microsteps}
$$

**Comment on Rounding (2 marks):**
Since $0.2 / 0.0025 = 80$ is an **integer**, there is *no rounding error*. The Z-axis will land exactly on the required 0.2 mm height after 80 microsteps. This is why **0.2 mm and 0.1 mm are "magic" layer heights** for an 8 mm pitch, 1.8°, 16× microstepped system — they are integer multiples of the stepper resolution.

If the user had requested 0.15 mm layer height:

$$
N_{\mu} = \frac{0.15}{0.0025} = 60 \text{ microsteps (exact)}
$$

So 0.15 mm is also achievable. However, 0.123 mm would yield 49.2 microsteps, which the firmware would round to either 49 (0.1225 mm) or 50 (0.125 mm) — introducing a layer error of 0.0005 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (3D Printer Numericals):**
> 1. **Confusing Pitch with Lead:** A "2 mm pitch, 4-start" lead screw has a **lead of 8 mm**, not 2 mm. Always check the start count.
> 2. **Forgetting Microstepping:** Many students compute $8 / 200 = 0.04$ mm, missing the factor of $\mu = 16$ and overestimating the resolution by 16×.
> 3. **Integer Check:** When a question asks for layer height achievability, you *must* verify that $h_L / R_{\text{stepper}}$ is an integer. This is the board's favorite "comment" sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### Pick and Place Machine
- **Definition:** CNC-controlled robotic system for SMT component placement.
- **Throughput Metric:** **CPH = 3600 / T_cycle** (always include 3600 for CPH).
- **Axes:** X, Y, Z, R (rotation to correct component orientation).
- **Closed-Loop:** Vision system (upward + downward cameras) measures $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ and corrects placement — equivalent to **G92 offset**.
- **Cycle Sub-phases:** Pick → Vision → Move → Place.
- **Typical Accuracy:** **± 0.05 mm** (general) and **± 0.01 mm** (chip shooters).
- **Typical CPH:** **20,000 – 200,000 CPH** depending on machine class.

### CNC 3D Printer
- **Definition:** Additive manufacturing system controlled by G-code, building parts layer-by-layer.
- **Three Main Types:** FDM (filament), SLA (resin + UV laser), SLS (powder + laser).
- **Unique G-codes:** `G29` (bed level), `M104/M109` (hotend temp), `M140/M190` (bed temp).
- **Resolution Formula:** $R = P / (\text{SPR} \cdot \mu)$.
- **Layer Height Magic Numbers (for 8 mm pitch, 1.8°, 16×):** 0.1, 0.15, 0.2 mm are *exact*; non-integer multiples cause rounding errors.
- **Open-Loop Architecture:** Stepper-based 3D printers have *no position feedback*; skipped steps cause layer shifts.
- **Throughput Metric:** cm³/hr of deposited material.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Both systems use CNC motion controllers** (G-code or G-code-like commands).
- **Both use stepper or servo motors** to drive linear axes.
- **PPM is closed-loop; 3D printer is open-loop** (most common variant).
- **PPM is subtractive-free / assembly-based; 3D printer is purely additive.**
- **Examiner loves:** cycle time math, CPH conversion, stepper resolution, G-code interpretations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "differentiate" or "compare" — always use a **table format** with **3–4 distinct parameters**. KTU examiners reward structured answers with clear headers. Never write a "wall of text" comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Drilling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** CNC Drilling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CNC Drilling Machine** is a computer numerically controlled multi-axis machine tool in which the spindle carrying a single-point rotating cutting tool (drill bit) is programmed to translate along one or more linear axes while rotating, in order to produce a circular hole of a specified diameter and depth in a stationary or repositioned workpiece. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is classified as a **single-point, point-to-point (PTP) controlled machine tool** under the family of CNC hole-making machines.

Unlike a conventional drilling machine where the operator manually applies feed, selects speeds using levers, and aligns the workpiece visually, a CNC drilling machine uses **G-code / ISO 6983 part programs** to:
1. Position the tool tip with respect to the workpiece origin.
2. Control **spindle speed (N)** and **feed rate (f)** precisely.
3. Execute **canned drilling cycles** (G73, G81–G85, G89) for repetitive operations.
4. Coordinate **multiple axes (X, Y, Z and optionally a CNC rotary table / ATC)** for multi-hole production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The syllabus explicitly emphasises the **classification of CNC drilling machines**, the **drilling accessories** (work holding, tool holding, coolants), **drilling operations** (drilling, reaming, tapping, boring, counter-boring, counter-sinking), and **NC part programming for drilling operations** including the use of **canned cycles**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a robotic hand with a power drill...**
>
> Think of a CNC drilling machine as a **robotic arm holding an electric drill**, but instead of a human moving it around, the movements are dictated by a **pre-written instruction sheet (the G-code program)**. The instructions tell the machine:
> - "Go to position (X40, Y30)" → Like saying *"stand over the dot marked 40, 30 on the table."*
> - "Spin the drill at 1200 RPM" → Like pressing the trigger at a specific speed.
> - "Lower the drill 25 mm at a feed of 0.15 mm/rev" → Like pressing down steadily into a metal sheet.
> - "Retract the drill quickly back up" → Like lifting the drill out after the hole is made.
>
> The key difference is **precision, repeatability, and memory** — once the program is written, the same exact hole can be drilled 1 time or 10,000 times with **zero fatigue and zero variation**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following are the standard metrics that govern every drilling operation:

- **Cutting Speed (V<sub>c</sub>)** measured in **m/min** (metres per minute)
- **Spindle Speed (N)** measured in **rev/min (rpm)**
- **Feed per Revolution (f<sub>n</sub>)** measured in **mm/rev**
- **Feed Rate (v<sub>f</sub> or f)** measured in **mm/min**
- **Depth of Cut (a<sub>p</sub>)** for drilling = **D/2** (radius), measured in **mm**
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** measured in **mm³/min**
- **Power (P)** measured in **kW**
- **Machining Time (T<sub>m</sub>)** measured in **seconds (s) or minutes (min)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six metrics are the **"vital signs"** of any drilling operation and form the foundation of every numerical, derivation, and KTU exam question in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of CNC Drilling Machines

CNC drilling machines are classified by their **structural design**, **size**, and **degree of automation**.

#### 2.1.1 Based on Construction / Size

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **CNC Bench Drill** | Small, table-mounted; 2–3 axes | PCB, jewellery, small components |
| **CNC Upright / Column Drill** | Floor-standing single spindle; 3 axes | General job-shop drilling |
| **CNC Radial Drill** | Spindle mounted on a horizontal arm (radial) which pivots around the column | Large workpieces (boilers, flanges) |
| **CNC Turret Drill** | Multi-spindle turret head; indexable tool positions | Medium batch production |
