| **CNC Gang Drill** | Several vertical spindles in line | High-volume small parts |
| **CNC Deep Hole Drill** | Specialised BTA / ejector drills with high-pressure coolant | Barrels, gun barrels, shafts (L/D > 5) |
| **CNC Multi-Axis Drilling Centre** | 4–5 axes, often with ATC and rotary table | Aerospace, automotive, moulds |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, the most commonly asked classifications are **Bench → Radial → Turret → Multi-spindle → Drilling Centre**. A **radial drill** is the answer when the question says *"the drill head can swing on a horizontal arm."*

#### 2.1.2 Based on Axes Configuration

- **2.5 Axis CNC Drill:** X, Y positioning + Z feed (most common).
- **3-Axis CNC Drill:** X, Y, Z with linear interpolations.
- **4-Axis CNC Drill:** Adds a **rotary table (A-axis)** to drill on cylindrical parts.
- **5-Axis CNC Drill:** Adds **B-axis (tilting head)** for angled holes (e.g., aircraft wing ribs).

### 2.2 Major Sub-Systems of a CNC Drilling Machine

A CNC drilling machine is an integration of **mechanical**, **electrical**, and **hydraulic/pneumatic** subsystems:

1. **Base & Column** — Provide rigidity and absorb vibration.
2. **Work Table** — XY positioning table driven by **servo motors + ball screws**; typically with a **T-slot / fixture grid** for clamping.
3. **Spindle Head / Quill** — Houses the spindle and is moved in Z by a servo motor.
4. **Spindle** — Driven by an **AC servo / spindle motor** with encoder feedback; holds the drill via a **collet chuck, drill chuck, or Morse taper holder**.
5. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — For high-end machines, holds **8–40+ tools**; not mandatory in basic CNC drills.
6. **Coolant System** — Flood coolant or **through-spindle coolant (TSC)** for deep hole drilling.
7. **CNC Controller** — Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi, Haas, etc. Hosts the G-code program and interpolates axes.
8. **Feedback System** — Linear scales (optical) on X, Y, Z and rotary encoders on spindle.

### 2.3 Drilling Accessories

#### 2.3.1 Work Holding Devices
The workpiece must be held rigidly so that drilling forces (axial thrust + torque) do not deflect it.

- **Machine Vise** — Most common; hardened steel jaws; clamping force up to **25–50 kN**.
- **T-Slot Fixture Plate with Step Blocks & Clamps** — For irregular / large workpieces.
- **Vacuum Chuck** — For thin sheet / non-magnetic workpieces.
- **Magnetic Chuck** — For ferromagnetic workpieces only.
- **Rotary Chuck / Indexer (4th Axis)** — For circumferential hole patterns.
- **Pallet Changer** — Two-station system to load/unload offline, increasing spindle utilisation.

#### 2.3.2 Tool Holding Devices
- **Drill Chuck** — Three-jaw self-centring; for shank diameter 0–13 mm or 0–25 mm.
- **Collet Chuck (ER / 5C)** — For precision; clamping range 1 mm to 26 mm; high concentricity (<5 µm TIR).
- **Morse Taper Sleeve / Socket** — For taper-shank drills; sizes MT1–MT5.
- **Hydraulic / Shrink-Fit Holders** — For high-speed drilling (≥ 10 000 rpm).
- **Quick-Change Tap Holder** — For tapping operations with tension/compression.

### 2.4 Drilling Operations Performed on a CNC Drilling Machine

A CNC drill is a versatile **hole-making centre**. Beyond plain drilling, the following operations are routinely performed:

| Operation | Description | Typical Tool |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Drilling** | Producing a new hole in solid stock | Twist drill |
| **Reaming** | Finishing a previously drilled/bored hole to close tolerance and better surface finish | Straight / spiral / expansion reamer |
| **Tapping** | Cutting internal threads | Tap (straight, spiral, forming) |
| **Boring** | Enlarging an existing hole for size and concentricity | Boring bar with single-point insert |
| **Counter-boring** | Enlarging the entry portion of a hole to accommodate a socket-head cap screw | Counter-bore tool |
| **Counter-sinking** | Producing a conical seat for flat-head screws (82° or 90°) | Counter-sink tool |
| **Spot Facing** | Producing a flat seat perpendicular to a hole axis | Spot face tool |
| **Trepaning / Deep Hole Drilling** | Removing an annular core; high L/D ratio | Gun drill / BTA drill |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"List the operations performed on a CNC drilling machine,"* always present them in **alphabetical order** with one-line definitions. The minimum list expected is **Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Boring, Counter-boring, Counter-sinking**.

### 2.5 Canned Cycles for Drilling (G-Code Programming)

The most important G-codes (per ISO 6983 / Fanuc standard) for drilling are:

| G-Code | Name | Function |
|--------|------|----------|
| **G73** | High-Speed Peck Drilling Cycle | Shallow retract for chip breaking in shallow holes |
| **G74** | Left-Hand Tapping Cycle (some controls) | Rigid tapping with reversal |
| **G76** | Fine Boring Cycle | Precise bore with spindle orient & retract |
| **G80** | Cancel Canned Cycle | Exits any active canned cycle |
| **G81** | Drilling Cycle, Spot Drilling | Simple straight drill |
| **G82** | Drilling Cycle with Dwell | Drill + pause at bottom (for spot facing / deburr) |
| **G83** | Peck Drilling Cycle (Full Retract) | Deep-hole drilling with full Z retract between pecks |
| **G84** | Tapping Cycle (Right-Hand) | Rigid tapping with spindle reversal |
| **G85** | Boring Cycle, Feed In / Feed Out | Boring with smooth retract |
| **G86** | Boring Cycle, Spindle Stop | Boring + rapid retract |
| **G87** | Back Boring Cycle | Boring from the back side |
| **G88** | Boring Cycle, Manual Retract | Operator retracts |
| **G89** | Boring Cycle with Dwell | Like G82 but with feed retract |

> [!NOTE]
> **Format Example — G81 Simple Drilling Cycle:**
> `G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__;`
> - **X, Y** → Hole position
> - **Z** → Final hole depth (absolute, from R-plane)
> - **R** → Reference plane position (rapid traverse entry height)
> - **F** → Feed rate
> **To cancel:** `G80;` or `G00 Z__;`

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table verbatim — these are the "money formulas" for KTU drilling problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min (rpm) | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | **Feed Rate** | $v_f = N \times f_n$ | mm/min | $f_n$ is feed per rev |
| 3 | **Material Removal Rate (Drilling)** | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times f_n \times N$ | mm³/min | Equals $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times v_f$ |
| 4 | **Drilling Time per Hole** | $T_h = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{v_f}$ | minutes | $L$ = hole depth, $L_a$ = approach, $L_o$ = overtravel |
| 5 | **Thrust Force (empirical)** | $F_t = C_F \times D \times f_n^{0.86}$ | N | Machinability data table |
| 6 | **Drilling Power** | $P = \dfrac{F_t \times V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | kW | $\eta$ = efficiency (typically 0.80) |
| 7 | **Power from MRR** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | kW | $U_s$ = specific energy in J/mm³ |
| 8 | **Twist Drill Point Angle** | Standard: $118°$ | degrees | For general-purpose steels |
| 9 | **Hole Diameter Adjustment (Boring)** | $\Delta d = D_f - D_i$ | mm | Depth of cut for boring |
| 10 | **Peck Depth (G83)** | $Q = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \times D$ | mm | Reduce as hole gets deeper |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** $V_c$ is in **m/min** in the formula $N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$. If you forget the 1000 factor, you will get an answer off by **1000×**. KTU examiners deliberately test this in Part A numericals.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC drilling machines are the **backbone of mass production** in:
- **Automotive industry** — Engine blocks, gearbox housings, chassis nodes.
- **Aerospace industry** — Wing rib, spar drilling (often 5-axis).
- **Electronics industry** — PCB depanelisation, smartphone chassis.
- **Mould & die making** — Cooling channels, ejector pin holes.
- **Shipbuilding** — Large radial CNC drills for hull plates.

The **economic impact** of a CNC drilling machine versus manual drilling is a **5× to 10× productivity gain** in batch production, with **±0.05 mm** positional accuracy versus **±0.5 mm** for a manual radial drill.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

**Given:** A Ø20 mm HSS twist drill cutting mild steel with a recommended cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min. Determine the spindle speed $N$.

**Step 1 — Statement of the relationship:**

The cutting speed is the linear speed at which the **outer periphery of the drill** sweeps through the workpiece material. It is the product of the rotational speed and the **circumference** of the drill.

$$
V_c = \pi \times D \times N
$$

where the dimensions must be consistent. If $V_c$ is in **m/min** and $D$ is in **mm**, we must convert $D$ to metres by dividing by 1000:

$$
V_c = \pi \times \left(\frac{D}{1000}\right) \times N
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for N:**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
N = \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20}
$$

$$
N = \frac{25000}{62.8319}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 397.9 \approx 398 \text{ rpm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, KTU examiners expect you to round to the **nearest standard spindle speed** on the machine (e.g., 400 rpm) — the controller can directly set this.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Drilling

**Given:** Same drill as above, with feed per revolution $f_n = 0.20$ mm/rev.

**Step 1 — Conceptual setup:**

In drilling, the **chip cross-section** being removed per revolution is **NOT** a rectangle as in turning. Instead, it is the **area of a circle of diameter D** (the projected area of the drilled hole), advanced by $f_n$ mm per revolution.

**Step 2 — Area of the circular chip cross-section:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per revolution:**

$$
V_{rev} = A \times f_n = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times f_n
$$

**Step 4 — Volume removed per minute (MRR):**

Since the spindle turns at $N$ rev/min:

$$
MRR = V_{rev} \times N = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times f_n \times N
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numbers:**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} \times 0.20 \times 397.9
$$

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} \times 0.20 \times 397.9
$$

$$
MRR = 314.159 \times 79.58
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR \approx 25\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \quad (25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min})}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drilling Time for a Single Hole

**Given:** A Ø12 mm drill is to make a hole 30 mm deep in an aluminium plate. Approach distance $L_a = 5$ mm, overtravel $L_o = 2$ mm. Spindle speed $N = 1500$ rpm, feed $f_n = 0.10$ mm/rev.

**Step 1 — Express the feed rate:**

$$
v_f = N \times f_n = 1500 \times 0.10 = 150 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the time equation:**

The drill must travel from the R-plane (rapid) down through the approach, the actual depth, and the overtravel at the programmed feed rate. The **rapid approach and retract** are not counted in $T_h$ (only the cutting feed is counted):

$$
T_h = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{v_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
T_h = \frac{30 + 5 + 2}{150}
$$

$$
T_h = \frac{37}{150}
$$

$$
T_h = 0.2467 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds:**

$$
T_h = 0.2467 \times 60 = 14.8 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_h \approx 14.8 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drilling Power

**Given:** A Ø15 mm drill cutting cast iron, $V_c = 30$ m/min, $f_n = 0.15$ mm/rev, machine efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 15} = \frac{30000}{47.124} \approx 636.6 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = 636.6 \times 0.15 \approx 95.5 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — MRR:**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times 15^2}{4} \times 95.5 = 176.71 \times 95.5 \approx 16\,876 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy for cast iron:**

For grey cast iron, the specific cutting energy $U_s \approx 2.5$ J/mm³ (from standard tables).

**Step 5 — Power:**

$$
P = \frac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}
$$

$$
P = \frac{16\,876 \times 2.5}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.80}
$$

$$
P = \frac{42\,190}{48 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
P = 8.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW} \times 1000 = \text{...} 
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction note (exam scale):** $P$ from MRR using $U_s$ yields small numbers in kW for a single drill. More realistically, the result is interpreted in **kW** directly. For a 16 mm drill in cast iron, expected power is in the range of **0.9–1.2 kW** at the spindle. The unit check is $\frac{\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \times \text{J/mm}^3}{\text{s}} = \text{W}$ — divide by 1000 for kW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recalculation with consistent units:**
> $P = \dfrac{16\,876 \times 2.5}{60 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{42\,190}{48} \approx 879$ W $\approx \mathbf{0.88 \text{ kW}}$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drilling Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most important KTU comparison table** for the drilling module. Memorise it.

| Operation | Tool | Material Removed | Surface Finish (Ra, µm) | Tolerance (mm) | Typical MRR vs Drilling |
|-----------|------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Drilling | Twist drill | High (creates hole) | 1.6 – 6.3 | ±0.1 | 1.0× (baseline) |
| Reaming | Reamer | Very low (10–30 µm/radial) | 0.4 – 1.6 | ±0.025 | 0.05× |
| Tapping | Tap | Low (thread volume) | 0.8 – 3.2 | ±0.05 | 0.10× |
| Boring | Boring bar | Medium (size adjustment) | 0.4 – 1.6 | ±0.0125 | 0.30× |
| Counter-boring | Pilot + C-bore | Medium | 1.6 – 3.2 | ±0.05 | 0.50× |
| Counter-sinking | C-sink | Low | 1.6 – 3.2 | ±0.10 | 0.20× |
| Spot Facing | Spot face tool | Low | 3.2 – 6.3 | ±0.10 | 0.40× |
| Deep Hole (BTA) | BTA drill | High but slow | 0.8 – 3.2 | ±0.05 | 0.60× |

---

### 3.6 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Drilling Cycle (G-Code)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision flow when writing a CNC drilling program on the KTU exam:**

```
START: I need to make a hole.
   │
   ├── Is the depth less than 3 × D? 
   │       YES → Use G81 (simple) or G82 (with dwell)
   │       NO  → Use G83 (peck) or G73 (high-speed peck)
   │
   ├── Do I need internal threads?
   │       YES → Use G84 (rigid tap) or floating tap holder
   │
   ├── Do I need to enlarge an existing hole precisely?
   │       YES → Use G85 or G76 (fine boring)
   │
   └── Do I need a chamfered entry?
           YES → First use a G82 with dwell (spot face), then drill
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNC Drilling Machine

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** between the major sub-systems of a CNC drilling machine — from the part program on the controller down to the physical chip formation at the tool tip.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD/CAM Program] --> B[Part Program G-code]
    B --> C[CNC Controller Fanuc Siemens Haas]
    C --> D[Interpreter and Interpolator]
    D --> E[X axis Servo Drive]
    D --> F[Y axis Servo Drive]
    D --> G[Z axis Servo Drive]
    D --> H[Spindle Drive VFD]
    E --> I[X axis Ball Screw]
    F --> J[Y axis Ball Screw]
    G --> K[Z axis Ball Screw and Quill]
    I --> L[Work Table XY]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Spindle Head and Tool]
    H --> N[Spindle Motor]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Tool and Workpiece Interface]
    L --> O
    P[Coolant Pump] --> O
    Q[Feedback Linear Encoders] --> C
    N --> Q
    O --> R[Chips and Heat Generation]
    R --> S[Chip Conveyor]
    P --> T[Coolant Tank and Filter]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** (A, B, C) represents the **information flow** — from design intent to executable code.
- The **middle column** (D, E, F, G, H) represents the **drive and control layer**.
- The **right column** (I to O) represents the **mechanical power flow** — motors → ball screws → table/spindle → cutting zone.
- The **bottom row** (P, Q, R, S, T) represents the **auxiliary systems** (coolant, feedback, chip handling).

---

### 4.2 CNC Drilling Machine — Process Flow of a Single Canned Cycle (G81)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller reads G81 X Y Z R F] --> B[Move to XY at rapid G00]
    B --> C[Move to R plane at rapid G00]
    C --> D[Engage spindle at programmed RPM]
    D --> E[Plunge to Z depth at feed F]
    E --> F{Dwell at bottom G82 only}
    F -->|Yes| G[Wait D seconds]
    F -->|No| H
    G --> H[Retract to R plane at rapid G00]
    H --> I{More holes in program}
    I -->|Yes| B
    I -->|No| J[Cancel canned cycle G80]
    J --> K[Move to safe Z at rapid]
    K --> L[Program end M30]
```

---

### 4.3 Axes Layout of a Typical 3-Axis CNC Drill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[XYZ Axis Reference]
    direction TB
    X[X axis Longitudinal along table]
    Y[Y axis Transverse along table]
    Z[Z axis Vertical along spindle]
    end
    subgraph B[Origin Convention]
    direction TB
    O1[Workpiece Zero G54 usually at top left of stock]
    O2[Machine Zero G53 fixed on machine bed]
    end
    subgraph C[Motion Types]
    direction TB
    M1[G00 Rapid positioning]
    M2[G01 Linear interpolation]
    M3[G02 G03 Circular interpolation N A for drilling]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> For CNC drilling, **circular interpolation (G02/G03) is rarely used at the spindle** — the drill only plunges (G01 in Z). G02/G03 would only appear on a **milling-drilling centre (machining centre)** or a **4th-axis rotary table** moving around a circle.

---

### 4.4 Hierarchical Classification Diagram of CNC Drilling Machines

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[CNC Drilling Machines] --> S1[By Construction]
    Root --> S2[By Axes]
    Root --> S3[By Spindle Count]
    Root --> S4[By Application]

    S1 --> N1A[Bench Drill]
    S1 --> N1B[Upright Column Drill]
    S1 --> N1C[Radial Arm Drill]
    S1 --> N1D[Turret Drill]
    S1 --> N1E[Gang Drill]
    S1 --> N1F[Deep Hole Drill BTA]

    S2 --> N2A[2.5 Axis X Y Z]
    S2 --> N2B[3 Axis XYZ]
    S2 --> N2C[4 Axis XYZ plus A rotary]
    S2 --> N2D[5 Axis XYZ plus A plus B]

    S3 --> N3A[Single Spindle]
    S3 --> N3B[Multi Spindle 2 to 8]

    S4 --> N4A[Job Shop]
    S4 --> N4B[Mass Production]
    S4 --> N4C[Aerospace]
    S4 --> N4D[Automotive]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a CNC drilling machine. List any four drilling operations that can be performed on it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **CNC Drilling Machine** is a numerically controlled machine tool used for producing holes in a workpiece by means of a rotating multi-point cutting tool (drill bit), where the tool path, spindle speed, and feed rate are controlled by a stored part program executed on a CNC controller.

**Four drilling operations:**

1. **Drilling** — Producing a new hole in solid stock using a twist drill.
2. **Reaming** — Finishing a pre-drilled hole to close dimensional tolerance (e.g., H7) and better surface finish.
3. **Tapping** — Cutting internal threads in a pre-drilled hole for fastening (e.g., M8 × 1.25).
4. **Counter-sinking** — Producing a conical recess at the hole entry to seat a flat-head screw.

*(Any four of: Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Boring, Counter-boring, Counter-sinking, Spot Facing, Deep-hole Drilling — full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the functions of G81, G82, G83, and G84 canned cycles in CNC drilling programming.**

**Model Answer:**

| G-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **G81** | Simple drilling canned cycle — drill a hole to a specified depth at a programmed feed, then rapid retract. Used for shallow holes where chip evacuation is not a problem. |
| **G82** | Drilling with dwell — same as G81, but with a programmed pause (P seconds) at the bottom of the hole. Used for spot facing and ensuring full depth at the bottom. |
| **G83** | Peck drilling cycle (full retract) — the drill retracts completely to the R-plane between pecks to evacuate long stringy chips. Mandatory for deep holes (L/D > 3 to 5). |
| **G84** | Tapping cycle — right-hand rigid tapping with automatic spindle reversal at the bottom of the thread. The feed rate must be synchronised exactly to the spindle pitch. |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of CNC drilling machines with neat sketches. Discuss the work-holding and tool-holding devices used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC drilling machine is used to drill Ø20 mm holes in a mild steel plate (Recommended cutting speed = 25 m/min, feed = 0.20 mm/rev). The plate is 40 mm thick. Calculate:
  1. The required spindle speed in rpm.
  2. The feed rate in mm/min.
  3. The material removal rate in cm³/min.
  4. The drilling time per hole in seconds (assume approach = 5 mm, overtravel = 2 mm). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of CNC drilling machines (with sketches replaced by block diagrams in Section 4.4):**

1. **CNC Bench Drill** — Small footprint, mounted on a workbench, 2–3 axes, capacity up to ~13 mm drill diameter. Used for electronics, jewellery, small parts.

2. **CNC Upright / Column Drill** — Floor-standing vertical column, single spindle, 3-axis (X, Y table + Z head). General job-shop machine.

3. **CNC Radial Drill** — The spindle head is mounted on a horizontal arm that can **swing radially** around the vertical column. Used for **large workpieces** (flanges, boilers) where repositioning the part is impractical.

4. **CNC Turret Drill** — The spindle has a **multi-position turret** (typically 6–12 stations) allowing automatic tool changes without an ATC. Ideal for medium-batch production.

5. **CNC Multi-Spindle / Gang Drill** — Several spindles on a common bed, each operating on a different hole simultaneously. High productivity for small parts.

6. **CNC Deep Hole Drilling Machine** — Specialised for L/D > 10 using **BTA (Boring and Trepanning Association)** drills with high-pressure through-spindle coolant.

**Work-holding devices:**

- **Machine Vise** — Hardened jaws, fixed and movable; clamping force 25–50 kN.
- **T-Slot Fixture Plate** — Step blocks, strap clamps, studs, and nuts for irregular parts.
- **Magnetic Chuck** — For ferromagnetic workpieces; uniform holding force.
- **Vacuum Chuck** — For thin non-ferrous sheets.
- **4th-Axis Rotary Chuck / Indexer** — For cylindrical parts with circumferential hole patterns.

**Tool-holding devices:**

- **Drill Chuck (Key-Type or Keyless)** — Three-jaw self-centring; for 0–13 mm or 0–25 mm shanks.
- **Collet Chuck (ER32, ER40, 5C)** — High precision, clamping range 1–26 mm; concentricity < 5 µm.
- **Morse Taper Sleeves** — For taper-shank drills (MT1–MT5).
- **Hydraulic / Shrink-Fit Holders** — For high-speed drilling (≥ 10 000 rpm).
- **Tapping Chucks with Tension-Compression** — Compensate for spindle-feed mismatch during tapping.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $V_c = 25$ m/min
- $f_n = 0.20$ mm/rev
- $L = 40$ mm
- $L_a = 5$ mm
- $L_o = 2$ mm

**(1) Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20}
$$

$$
N = \frac{25000}{62.8319} \approx 397.9 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 398 \text{ rpm}}
$$

**(2) Feed Rate:**

$$
v_f = N \times f_n = 397.9 \times 0.20
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f \approx 79.58 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

**(3) Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times v_f
$$

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} \times 79.58 = 314.159 \times 79.58
$$

$$
MRR \approx 25\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 25\,\text{cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**(4) Drilling Time per Hole:**

$$
T_h = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{v_f} = \frac{40 + 5 + 2}{79.58} = \frac{47}{79.58}
$$

$$
T_h \approx 0.5906 \text{ min} \times 60 = 35.44 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_h \approx 35.4 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the major sub-systems of a CNC drilling machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a manual part-program (in standard G-code) to drill **four Ø10 mm holes** in a mild steel plate as per the layout below:
  - Plate size: 100 mm × 100 mm × 20 mm thick.
  - Hole positions (X, Y) measured from the **top-left corner of the plate** as origin: (20, 20), (80, 20), (80, 80), (20, 80).
  - Use **G81** simple drilling cycle, with the R-plane at +5 mm above the top surface and final Z at –20 mm.
  - Use **spindle speed = 800 rpm** and **feed = 200 mm/min**.
  - The plate is clamped on a machine vise with the top surface 50 mm above the machine table.

  Show all safety blocks, tool change, and proper program end. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A CNC drilling machine consists of the following major sub-systems (as detailed in Section 4.1):

1. **Mechanical Structure (Base, Column, Table, Head)** — Provides rigidity, vibration damping, and houses the moving components.
2. **Spindle Assembly** — Spindle motor (AC servo / VFD-controlled), spindle bearings, and tool-holder interface (BT, CT, HSK, or straight shank).
3. **Drive System (X, Y, Z Axes)** — Servo motors coupled to **ball screws** via flexible couplings; linear motion guided by **linear motion guides** (LM rails).
4. **CNC Controller** — Industrial PC + PLC; executes the G-code program; performs interpolation (linear / circular); sends command pulses to servo drives.
5. **Feedback System** — Linear scales (optical) on each axis, rotary encoders on spindle and servos — closed-loop control with position error of < 5 µm.
6. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — Optional; carousel / arm-type with 8–40+ tool positions.
7. **Coolant System** — Flood coolant nozzle + optional **Through-Spoke Coolant (TSC)** at 5–70 bar pressure for deep hole drilling.
8. **Work-Holding and Pallet System** — Vises, fixtures, chucks, pallet changers.
9. **Chip Management** — Chip pan, chip conveyor, coolant filtration.
10. **Safety System** — Enclosures, interlocks, e-stops, light curtains, pressure sensors.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Assumptions:**
- The workpiece coordinate system origin is at the **top-left corner of the plate (X0, Y0)** on the **top surface (Z0)**.
- The machine table is at Z = –50 mm in machine coordinates.
- The vise jaw height places the plate top at **Z = +50 mm** in machine coordinates → therefore **G54 Z-offset = +50 mm**.

**Complete Part Program:**

```text
%
O1001 (DRILL 4 HOLES ON 100x100 PLATE)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54 (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min, XY plane, Work offset)
G00 Z100.0              (Safe Z for tool change)
T01 M06                 (Tool 1: Ø10 mm twist drill, tool change)
S800 M03                (Spindle CW at 800 rpm)
G00 X20.0 Y20.0         (Position over hole 1)
G43 H01 Z50.0           (Tool length comp, rapid to R-plane +5 mm)
M08                     (Coolant ON)
G81 R5.0 Z-20.0 F200    (Drill cycle: R-plane +5, depth -20, feed 200)
X80.0 Y20.0             (Hole 2)
X80.0 Y80.0             (Hole 3)
X20.0 Y80.0             (Hole 4)
G80                     (Cancel canned cycle)
G00 Z100.0 M09          (Retract, coolant OFF)
G91 G28 Z0.0            (Return to reference Z)
G90 G28 X0.0 Y0.0       (Return to reference XY)
M05                     (Spindle stop)
M30                     (Program end, rewind)
%
```

**Program Logic Explanation:**

| Line | Function |
|------|----------|
| `G21 G90 G94 G17 G54` | Safety: metric units, absolute coordinates, feed per minute, XY plane, work offset 1 active. |
| `G00 Z100.0` | Rapid to safe tool-change height. |
| `T01 M06` | Tool 1 (Ø10 mm twist drill) loaded automatically. |
| `S800 M03` | Spindle ON clockwise at 800 rpm. |
| `G43 H01` | Tool length offset H01 applied (critical when multiple tools are used). |
| `G81 R5.0 Z-20.0 F200` | Canned cycle defined: rapid to R = +5 mm, feed to Z = –20 mm, feed 200 mm/min. |
| `X__ Y__` (next 3 lines) | Subsequent holes executed in the same cycle — no need to repeat R, Z, F. |
| `G80` | Mandatory — cancel the active canned cycle. |
| `G91 G28 Z0.0` | Return spindle to machine zero in incremental mode. |
| `M30` | Program end + rewind. |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in CNC Drilling questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ×1000 factor** in $N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$ when $V_c$ is in m/min and $D$ is in mm. This single error **costs 2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using G02/G03 instead of G81–G85** for drilling — G02/G03 are circular interpolation codes used in milling. Drilling uses **canned cycles**.
> 3. **Forgetting `G80` after the canned cycle** — the controller will keep drilling at every subsequent X/Y move. Major logical error in part programs.
> 4. **Not including a tool change block (T__ M06)** and **spindle direction (M03)** — even if not asked explicitly, the KTU key insists on a complete, executable program.
> 5. **Mixing up feed per revolution (f<sub>n</sub>) and feed rate (v<sub>f</sub>)** in MRR calculation. The MRR formula for drilling is $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times v_f$ (using feed rate), NOT $f_n$.
> 6. **Not specifying G90 (absolute) and G21 (metric)** at the start — leading to ambiguity in coordinate interpretation.
> 7. **Writing Z = +20 mm instead of Z = –20 mm** in G81 — drilling depth is always **negative** in the standard convention (downward into the workpiece).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print / Pin this checklist before every KTU exam on this module.**

- [ ] **CNC Drilling Machine** = point-to-point CNC machine for hole-making with rotating multi-point tools.
- [ ] **Six standard operations**: Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Boring, Counter-boring, Counter-sinking.
- [ ] **Five main classifications**: Bench, Upright, Radial, Turret, Multi-spindle, Deep-hole.
- [ ] **Radial drill** = spindle swings on horizontal arm; used for large workpieces.
- [ ] **Spindle speed formula**: $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$ — units: $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm.
- [ ] **Feed rate**: $v_f = N \times f_n$ — units: mm/min.
- [ ] **MRR for drilling**: $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times v_f$ — units: mm³/min.
- [ ] **Drilling time per hole**: $T_h = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{v_f}$ — units: minutes.
- [ ] **G81** = simple drill; **G82** = drill + dwell; **G83** = peck (deep hole); **G84** = tapping.
- [ ] **Standard drill point angle** for mild steel = **118°**.
- [ ] **Peck depth (G83)**: $Q = 0.5$ to $1.0 \times D$, reduce as depth increases.
- [ ] **Deep hole rule of thumb**: use G83 when **L/D > 3**; mandatory for L/D > 5.
- [ ] **Standard part program order**: Safety blocks → Tool change → Spindle ON → Rapid to hole → Coolant ON → Canned cycle → Cancel cycle (G80) → Coolant OFF → Spindle OFF → Home → M30.
- [ ] **Always include**: `G21 G90 G94 G17 G54` at the start; `G80` and `M30` at the end.
- [ ] **Through-spoke coolant (TSC)** is essential for drilling depths > 3×D and for tool life in tough materials.
- [ ] **Work-holding**: vise for prismatic, 4th-axis chuck for cylindrical, magnetic for ferromagnetic.
- [ ] **Tool-holding**: drill chuck (general), collet (precision), Morse taper (large drills), shrink-fit (high-speed).
- [ ] **Bore tolerance hierarchy** (best to worst): Boring < Reaming < Drilling < Punching.
- [ ] **KTU hot keywords**: "explain the working principle," "list the operations," "write a part program using G81," "calculate spindle speed, feed, MRR and time."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Lathe Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CNC Lathe Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CNC Lathe Machine

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Lathe Machine** is a precision automated turning center in which the relative motion between the cutting tool and the workpiece — along predefined axes — is controlled by a computer executing stored part programs written in standard **G-codes (ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267-D)** and **M-codes**. The machine performs turning, facing, contouring, threading, drilling, and parting operations on cylindrical, conical, and complex rotational components with minimal manual intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expected Answer):**
> A CNC lathe is a two-axis (X and Z) NC machine tool in which the workpiece rotates about the spindle axis (Z-axis) and the cutting tool, mounted on a programmable carriage/turret, traverses along the radial (X) and longitudinal (Z) directions. The geometry of the part is dictated by a part program stored in the CNC controller, which generates precise servo-driven motion commands to the ball-screw feed drives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Potter's Wheel"

Imagine a **master potter** sitting at a potter's wheel. In a **conventional (manual) lathe**, the potter's hands are the slide — every cut, every taper, every thread is shaped by the human hand guided by a manual lead screw. Now imagine replacing the potter's hands with a **robotic arm that follows a pre-written recipe card** (the part program). The arm knows exactly how deep to cut (depth of cut), how fast to move (feed rate), and which shape to carve. That robotic potter is essentially the **CNC Lathe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Things a CNC Lathe MUST do that a human cannot (as accurately):**
> 1. Maintain constant surface speed during diameter change.
> 2. Cut identical thread profiles on 500 parts without fatigue.
> 3. Combine taper, chamfer, and arc transitions in a single continuous toolpath.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Standard Defaults)

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Spindle Speed (N) | **rev/min (rpm)** | **50 – 4000 rpm** |
| Spindle Power | **kW** | **5 – 75 kW** |
| Maximum Turning Diameter | **mm** | **200 – 800 mm** |
| Maximum Turning Length | **mm** | **300 – 2000 mm** |
| Positioning Accuracy | **mm** | **±0.005 mm** |
| Repeatability | **mm** | **±0.0025 mm** |
| Number of Tools (Turret) | — | **8, 12, 16 stations** |
| Feed Drive | — | **AC Servo + Ball Screw** |
| Controller | — | **FANUC, Siemens, Mitsubishi, HMI** |

## 1.4 Classification of CNC Lathes (KTU High-Yield)

CNC Lathes are broadly classified based on **bed design, axis count, and tooling capability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Sub-Classification:**

- **Based on Bed:**
  - **Flat-Bed Lathe** — Horizontal flat ways, used for heavy-duty, long shafts.
  - **Slant-Bed Lathe** — Inclined bed (30°/45°/60°), chip flow is gravitational, best for modern turning centers.

- **Based on Axes:**
  - **2-Axis CNC Lathe** — X and Z only.
  - **3-Axis CNC Lathe** — X, Z, and C (spindle indexing for live-tooling/milling).
  - **4 / 5-Axis Mill-Turn Machine** — X, Y, Z + B (tool rotation) + C, used for prismatic + turned features.

- **Based on Tool Capacity:**
  - **Turret-Type (8/12 tools)**
  - **Chain-Type Magazine (20–40 tools)**

- **Special Type:**
  - **Swiss-Type (Sliding Headstock) Lathe** — For small, long, precision parts (watch, medical).
  - **Multi-Spindle Lathe** — For high-volume production.
  - **Vertical Turning Center (VTC)** — For large disc-shaped components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a CNC Lathe (Element-by-Element Breakdown)

A CNC Lathe is an integration of **mechanical, electrical, electronic, and hydraulic subsystems**. Each element plays a non-negotiable role.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Structural Elements

**1. Bed**
- **Function:** The base/column that supports all subassemblies and provides the reference surface for tool travel.
- **Why critical:** Deflection in the bed = loss of accuracy. Made of **high-grade cast iron (Grade FG 260)** with internal ribbing for vibration damping.
- **Two types:**
  - **Flat Bed** — Ways are horizontal, traditional layout.
  - **Slant Bed** — Ways are inclined; chips and coolant slide off by gravity. Higher rigidity, faster chip evacuation, modern preference.

**2. Headstock**
- **Function:** Houses the **main spindle**, **chuck**, and **gearbox**. Provides rotational drive to the workpiece.
- **Spindle:** Driven by **AC Servo/Spindle Motor** (typically 5–75 kW). Mounted on high-precision **angular contact bearings** (P4 / ABEC 7 grade) for run-out < **0.005 mm**.
- **Spindle Nose:** Standardized as **A2-5, A2-6, A2-8** (for chucks up to 8", 10", 12").
- **Spindle Bore:** Diameter through the spindle for bar-stock feeding (e.g., 65 mm, 80 mm, 105 mm).

**3. Chuck (Work-Holding Device)**
- **3-Jaw Self-Centering Chuck** — For round/hex workpieces, centripetal clamping.
- **4-Jaw Independent Chuck** — For square/irregular sections, individually adjusted jaws.
- **Collet Chuck** — For high-precision, small-diameter bar work (run-out < 0.005 mm).
- **Hydraulic / Pneumatic Chuck** — Clamping actuated by hydraulic cylinder for fast cycle times.
- **Mandrel** — For holding pre-machined (bored) workpieces from inside.

**4. Tailstock**
- **Function:** Supports the free end of long workpieces via a **quill** carrying a **dead/live center**.
- **On CNC Lathes:** Tailstock movement is often **servo-controlled (programmable quill advance)** to eliminate manual setup errors.
- **Live Center vs Dead Center:** Live center has internal bearings (rotates with workpiece); dead center is fixed (requires lubrication to avoid wear).

**5. Carriage Assembly**
- **Function:** Mounts the tool and traverses in X (radial) and Z (longitudinal).
- **Sub-components:**
  - **Saddle** — Mounts on Z-axis ways, moves along bed length.
  - **Cross Slide** — Mounts on saddle, moves in X direction (perpendicular to spindle axis).
  - **Compound Slide** — Optional, swiveling slide for angular cuts (rarely used on CNC).
  - **Tool Post / Turret** — Holds the cutting tool.
- **Drive Mechanism:** **Ball Screw + Linear Guideways** driven by **AC Servo Motors** with rotary encoders (closed-loop feedback).
  - **Ball Screw Lead:** Typical 5, 10, 12, 20 mm/rev.
  - **Linear Guideway Types:** Roller type (high load) or ball type (low friction).

**6. Automatic Tool Turret (Indexer)**
- **Function:** Holds 4, 6, 8, 12, or 16 tools; rotates (indexes) to bring the next required tool into the cutting position.
- **Indexing Time:** Typically **0.2 – 0.5 seconds** (tool-to-tool).
- **Repeatability:** ±0.001 mm to ±0.005 mm.
- **Tool Identification:** Tools are stored in a magazine with offset tables in the controller (geometry + wear offsets).

**7. Coolant System**
- **Function:** Flood coolant or high-pressure through-tool coolant (≥ **70 bar**) to evacuate chips and reduce heat.
- **Coolant Types:** Emulsion (water-soluble), Neat oil, Synthetic.
- **Why critical:** On a CNC lathe cutting at 200 m/min, dry cutting causes thermal distortion.

### 2.1.2 CNC Controller Subsystem

- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Operator panel with display + keyboard.
- **MCU (Machine Control Unit):** Interprets G-code → generates motion commands (pulse trains) → sent to servo drives.
- **Servo Drives + Motors:** Each axis has its own servo loop.
- **Feedback Devices:**
  - **Incremental Encoder** — Position feedback on each axis.
  - **Linear Scale (Glass / Magnetic)** — Optional, for ultra-precision.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Handles auxiliary functions (coolant on/off, chuck open/close, turret rotate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern (2024):**
> "Explain the function of any FIVE major elements of a CNC Lathe with neat sketch."
> Mark allocation: 1 mark per element + 5 marks for sketch = 10 marks (or 14 marks with explanation depth).

## 2.2 Axis Nomenclature of CNC Lathe (ISO 841 Standard)

The **ISO 841 / EIA RS-267** standard defines axes for machine tools unambiguously.

| Axis | Direction of Motion | Motion Description | Driven By |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Z** | Along the spindle axis | Longitudinal (parallel to spindle rotation) | Z-axis servo |
| **X** | Perpendicular to Z | Radial (cross-slide direction) | X-axis servo |
| **+ (C)** | About Z | Spindle rotational / indexing axis | Spindle motor |
| **+ (Y)** | (Mill-turn only) | Perpendicular to both X and Z | Y-axis servo (optional) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Right-Hand Rule for CNC Lathe:**
> Point your right thumb along the +Z direction (away from chuck toward tailstock). Your fingers curl from +X (toward you, operator side) to +Y (impossible in 2-axis lathe).

**Reference Points on a CNC Lathe:**

1. **Machine Zero (M)** — Manufacturer-defined origin; cannot be changed.
2. **Work Zero / Program Zero (G54–G59)** — Set by operator at workpiece face-center.
3. **Tool Offset (H/D codes)** — Stores tool tip geometry relative to spindle centerline.

## 2.3 Tool Geometry for Turning (KTU High-Yield)

A standard turning tool has **7 angles** defined by ISO 3002:

1. **Rake Angle (γ):** Relief for chip flow; **positive** for ductile materials, **negative** for hard materials.
2. **Clearance Angle (α):** Prevents rubbing; typically **6°–15°**.
3. **Cutting Edge Angle (κ):** Major edge orientation; affects chip thickness.
4. **Nose Radius (r):** 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6 mm; affects surface finish.
5. **Side Rake (λ):** Chip flow direction control.
6. **Side Clearance (α'):** Relief on the side flank.
7. **End Clearance (α''):** Relief on the end flank.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Rake-Clear-Edge-Nose"** → **R-C-E-N** is the order of the 4 most important tool angles.

## 2.4 CNC Lathe Operations (Comprehensive List)

| Operation | Description | Typical G-Code |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Straight Turning** | Reducing diameter along Z | G01 |
| **Facing** | Cutting a flat face perpendicular to Z | G01 (in X) |
| **Taper Turning** | Simultaneous X and Z motion | G01 with taper |
| **Contour Turning** | Multi-diameter profile | G01 with multiple points |
| **Thread Cutting** | Helical path synchronized to spindle | G32, G76, G92 |
| **Grooving** | Narrow recess cut | G01 with reduced feed |
| **Parting Off** | Cut-off operation | G01 with plunge in X |
| **Drilling** | Axial hole with drill in tailstock | G01 with G98 retract |
| **Boring** | Enlarging existing hole | G01 with bore bar |
| **Reaming** | Finishing drilled/bored hole | G01 with reamer |
| **Knurling** | Diamond/straight pattern on surface | G01 with knurl tool |
| **Chamfering** | Edge break at 45° | G01 with equal X,Z increment |
| **Form Turning** | Profile using shaped tool | G01 with form tool |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Essential — All Marks in Board Exam Come From This)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Memorize the units. 80% of CNC numerical problems = these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$ | rev/min (rpm) | V = cutting speed (m/min), D = workpiece dia (mm) |
| 2 | Cutting Speed | $$V = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$$ | m/min | Used to verify cutting parameters |
| 3 | Feed Rate (per rev) | $$f_r = f \times N$$ | mm/min | f_r = feed/rev (mm/rev), N = rpm |
| 4 | Feed Rate (per min) | $$f = f_r \times N$$ | mm/min | Used in G-code F word |
| 5 | Machining Time (Turning) | $$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r \times N}$$ | min | L = length of cut (mm) |
| 6 | Machining Time (Facing) | $$T_m = \dfrac{D_i - D_f}{2 \times f \times N}$$ | min | D_i = initial, D_f = final dia |
| 7 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $$Z_{MRR} = f_r \times d \times V$$ | mm³/min | d = depth of cut, V = cutting speed |
| 8 | Specific Cutting Energy | $$P_s = \dfrac{P}{Z_{MRR}}$$ | N·m/mm² | P = cutting power (W) |
| 9 | Cutting Power | $$P = \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$$ | kW | F_c = cutting force (N) |
| 10 | Taylor's Tool Life | $$V \times T^n = C$$ | m/min, min | n = exponent, C = constant |
| 11 | Thread Lead Calculation | $$P_{thread} = \dfrac{f_r \times N}{1}$$ | mm/min | Must equal pitch × N for single-start thread |
| 12 | Surface Roughness (Turning) | $$R_a \approx \dfrac{r \times f_r^2}{8}$$ | µm | r = nose radius (mm), f_r = feed/rev |
| 13 | Taper Ratio (KTU specific) | $$T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$$ | mm/mm | D = large dia, d = small dia, L = length |
| 14 | Constant Surface Speed (CSS) | $$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{current}}$$ | rev/min | Controller recalculates N as D reduces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taper Ratio vs. Taper per TPF (per turning practice formula):**
> In some KTU questions, taper is given per TPF (Taper Per Foot, in inches). The TPF to Taper-per-mm conversion is:
> $$\text{Taper (mm/mm)} = \dfrac{\text{TPF (in/ft)}}{12 \times 25.4}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC Lathes are the **backbone of mass production** in:

- **Automotive** — Crankshafts, brake drums, piston pins, gear blanks.
- **Aerospace** — Turbine hubs, engine shafts, landing gear pins.
- **Medical** — Bone screws, orthopedic implants, surgical instrument blanks.
- **Hydraulics/Pneumatics** — Valve spools, piston rods, cylinder liners.
- **Defence** — Missile casings, gun barrel blanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Data Point (KTU 2024 Context):** A modern **3-axis CNC turning center** can produce 150–300 turned parts/hour with a single operator supervising 3–5 machines (lights-out manufacturing).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed (Standard Formula Derivation)

The cutting speed **V** is the relative surface velocity between the tool and the workpiece at the point of contact. For a rotating cylindrical workpiece of diameter **D** rotating at **N** revolutions per minute:

**Step 1 — Circumference of the workpiece:**
$$\text{Circumference} = \pi \times D$$

where D is in **mm** (this is the linear distance travelled by the surface in **one revolution**).

**Step 2 — Linear distance travelled per minute:**
If the workpiece makes N revolutions in 1 minute, the total linear distance travelled by the surface in 1 minute is:

$$\text{Distance/min} = \pi \times D \times N$$

This distance is measured in **mm/min** (since D is in mm).

**Step 3 — Convert mm/min to m/min (since V is in m/min):**

$$V = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for N (spindle speed):**

Rearranging the equation:

$$\pi \times D \times N = 1000 \times V$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} \quad \text{[rev/min]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Dimensional Check:**
> - Numerator: 1000 × (m/min) = 1000 m/min.
> - Denominator: π × (mm) → must convert mm to m, but V is already in m/min and D in mm, so the 1000 in the numerator handles the unit conversion.

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A mild steel workpiece of diameter **80 mm** is to be turned on a CNC lathe with a recommended cutting speed of **V = 180 m/min**. Calculate the spindle speed.

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 80} = \dfrac{180\,000}{251.327} = 716.2 \text{ rev/min}$$

**Practical CNC Implication:**
The controller will display this as **N716** or rounded to **N700** (whichever is closer on the standard speed table). The actual commanded speed must lie within the machine's max spindle speed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Machining Time for a Straight Turning Operation

**Problem Setup:**
A workpiece of initial diameter **D₀** is to be reduced to final diameter **D_f** over a length **L** at feed **f_r** (mm/rev) and spindle speed **N** (rev/min).

**Step 1 — Depth of Cut:**

$$d = \dfrac{D_0 - D_f}{2} \quad \text{[mm]}$$

This is the radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece.

**Step 2 — Feed in mm/min:**

The feed per revolution is **f_r** (mm/rev). At N revolutions per minute, the linear feed rate is:

$$f = f_r \times N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 3 — Time to traverse length L:**

The tool travels distance L (in mm) at linear feed rate f (in mm/min). Therefore:

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f} = \dfrac{L}{f_r \times N} \quad \text{[min]}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A shaft of **Ø100 mm** is reduced to **Ø90 mm** over a length of **150 mm** at f_r = 0.2 mm/rev and N = 600 rpm. Find the machining time.

**Step 1:** 
$$d = \dfrac{100 - 90}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** 
$$f = 0.2 \times 600 = 120 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3:** 
$$T_m = \dfrac{150}{120} = 1.25 \text{ min} = 75 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap (Common Mistake):**
> Students often compute feed as f = N / f_r (inverted formula). Remember: feed per rev × rpm = feed per min. **Multiplication, not division.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

**Definition:** MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the chip:**
For a straight turning operation, the uncut chip cross-section is a rectangle (approximately) of:

- Width = depth of cut (**d**, in mm) — radial direction.
- Thickness = feed per rev (**f_r**, in mm) — axial direction.

$$A_c = d \times f_r \quad \text{[mm}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of material removed per revolution:**
The tool advances **f_r** mm per revolution, so the volume removed per rev is:

$$V_{rev} = A_c \times f_r = d \times f_r^2 \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/rev]}$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per minute:**
Multiplying by N (revolutions per minute):

$$Z_{MRR} = d \times f_r^2 \times N = d \times f_r \times (f_r \times N) = d \times f_r \times f \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

**Alternative Form (More Common):**
Since cutting speed V = π·D·N / 1000, and f = f_r·N:

$$Z_{MRR} = d \times f_r \times N = d \times f_r \times \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

Turning a **Ø60 mm** mild steel shaft with **d = 2 mm, f_r = 0.25 mm/rev, N = 1000 rpm**. Find MRR.

$$Z_{MRR} = 2 \times 0.25 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

In **cm³/min** (more readable): **0.5 cm³/min**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thread Cutting Pitch & Feed Synchronization

**Step 1 — Definition of Lead and Pitch:**
- **Pitch (P):** Distance between two adjacent thread crests, in **mm**.
- **Lead (L):** Axial advance of the tool in one spindle revolution. For single-start threads, L = P. For double-start, L = 2P.

**Step 2 — For Single-Start Threading (Most Common in KTU Problems):**
The tool must advance **P mm in one spindle revolution**. Therefore the feed rate must be:

$$f = P \times N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 3 — G76 Threading Cycle (Standard CNC Lathe):**

In **G76 canned threading cycle**, the programmer specifies:
- **Pitch (P)**
- **Thread depth (d)** — typically 0.613 × P for metric V-thread (60°).
- **Infeed angle** — radial / side / alternate.
- **Number of spring passes and finish passes.**

**Step 4 — Synchronization Error:**
The controller uses the **spindle encoder feedback** to ensure the Z-axis feed is perfectly synchronized. Any mismatch (lead error in ball screw) is compensated via **leadscrew error compensation tables** in the controller.

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

Cut an **M20 × 2.5** thread (pitch 2.5 mm) at N = 200 rpm. What feed should be programmed?

$$f = 2.5 \times 200 = 500 \text{ mm/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "Thread = Pitch × Spindle Speed". The CNC controller automatically keeps the feed in lockstep with spindle rotation.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Constant Surface Speed (CSS) — Why Spindle Speed Changes in Turning

In turning, as the tool moves radially inward, the **workpiece diameter decreases** while the controller tries to maintain a **constant cutting speed V**.

**Step 1 — Current spindle speed at diameter D:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{current}}$$

**Step 2 — As D decreases, N must increase** (inverse proportionality):

- At D = 100 mm, N = 1000 × 200 / (π × 100) = **637 rpm**
- At D = 50 mm, N = 1000 × 200 / (π × 50) = **1273 rpm**
- At D = 10 mm, N = 1000 × 200 / (π × 10) = **6366 rpm** ← this exceeds machine limit, so controller caps it.

**Step 3 — G96 (CSS ON) vs G97 (CSS OFF):**

| Code | Meaning | Use |
|------|---------|-----|
| **G96** | Constant Surface Speed (CSS) ON | Turning/facing — recommended |
| **G97** | CSS OFF, constant RPM | Drilling, tapping |

**Step 4 — G50 (Maximum Spindle Speed Clamp):**
To prevent the spindle from racing to its max limit at small diameters, the programmer uses:

`G50 S2500` → limits spindle to 2500 rpm max.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Type Question (Common):**
> "Calculate the spindle speed when the tool is at Ø40 mm during turning with V = 200 m/min." → Apply formula directly. The diameter at the moment of cut = D_current.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Cutting Parameter Selection

| Workpiece Material | Tool Material | V (m/min) | f_r (mm/rev) | d (mm) | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | Coated Carbide (CVD) | 180–250 | 0.2–0.4 | 1–4 | Flood |
| Stainless Steel | Coated Carbide (PVD) | 80–150 | 0.1–0.25 | 0.5–2 | High-pressure |
| Cast Iron | Uncoated Carbide / Ceramic | 120–200 | 0.2–0.5 | 1–5 | Dry / MQL |
| Aluminum | PCD / HSS | 300–600 | 0.1–0.3 | 1–4 | Flood |
| Titanium | Uncoated Carbide | 40–80 | 0.1–0.2 | 0.5–2 | High-pressure |
| Brass | HSS | 150–300 | 0.05–0.2 | 0.5–3 | Flood |
