# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: List any six major elements of a CNC Lathe and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Element | Function |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Bed** | Base structure supporting all components; provides reference surface for tool travel; absorbs vibration. |
| 2 | **Headstock** | Houses the spindle and main drive motor; transmits rotational motion to the workpiece via chuck. |
| 3 | **Chuck** | Work-holding device that grips and rotates the workpiece; can be 3-jaw (self-centering) or 4-jaw (independent). |
| 4 | **Tool Turret** | Automatic tool magazine that holds 8/12 tools; indexes the required tool into cutting position. |
| 5 | **Carriage** | Mounts the tool turret and provides X and Z axis motion via ball screws and linear guideways. |
| 6 | **Tailstock** | Supports the free end of long workpieces via a quill carrying a center (dead or live). |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between Flat-Bed and Slant-Bed CNC Lathes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flat-Bed CNC Lathe | Slant-Bed CNC Lathe |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Bed Orientation | Horizontal ways | Inclined at 30°/45°/60° |
| Chip Flow | Manual / drag-out (poor) | Gravitational (excellent) |
| Rigidity | Lower (longer ways) | Higher (shorter, more compact) |
| Footprint | Larger | Smaller |
| Coolant Drainage | Requires pumping | Self-draining |
| Application | Heavy-duty long shafts | Modern production turning centers |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — With Sub-Parts

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**A shaft of Ø80 mm × 200 mm length is to be reduced to Ø70 mm over a 150 mm length on a CNC Lathe. The recommended cutting speed is 180 m/min, feed is 0.25 mm/rev.**

**(a) Calculate the spindle speed (N), feed in mm/min, depth of cut, and machining time. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Explain with a sketch the various axes of a CNC lathe and list any 8 standard G-codes used in lathe programming. [7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Initial Diameter, D₀ = 80 mm
- Final Diameter, D_f = 70 mm
- Length of cut, L = 150 mm
- Cutting speed, V = 180 m/min
- Feed, f_r = 0.25 mm/rev

**Step 1 — Depth of Cut (d):**

$$d = \dfrac{D_0 - D_f}{2} = \dfrac{80 - 70}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_0} = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \dfrac{180\,000}{251.327} = 716.2 \text{ rpm}$$

Rounded to standard: **N = 716 rpm**.

**Step 3 — Feed in mm/min (f):**

$$f = f_r \times N = 0.25 \times 716 = 179 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time (T_m):**

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f} = \dfrac{150}{179} = 0.838 \text{ min} \approx 50.3 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer for Part (a):**
- d = **5 mm**
- N = **716 rpm**
- f = **179 mm/min**
- T_m = **0.84 min ≈ 50 s**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Axes of CNC Lathe (as per ISO 841):**

- **Z-axis:** Along the spindle axis (longitudinal), positive direction away from chuck toward tailstock.
- **X-axis:** Perpendicular to Z (radial direction), positive direction toward the operator.
- **C-axis:** Rotation about Z (spindle indexing for live tooling on advanced machines).
- **Y-axis (optional):** Perpendicular to both X and Z (only on mill-turn centers).

**Sketch (3 marks):**
[Box representing the lathe bed, arrow labeled +Z pointing toward tailstock, arrow labeled +X pointing toward operator side, spindle drawn with chuck, tool turret drawn on cross slide, with tool tip shown at the workpiece reference point.]

**Eight Standard G-Codes for CNC Lathe (4 marks):**

| G-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (positioning at high speed, not cutting) |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (straight cutting move at programmed feed) |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation, clockwise (CW) |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation, counter-clockwise (CCW) |
| **G90** | Absolute programming (coordinates relative to program zero) |
| **G91** | Incremental programming (coordinates relative to current position) |
| **G96** | Constant Surface Speed (CSS) ON |
| **G76** | Threading cycle (multi-pass automatic) |

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the functional subsystems of a CNC Lathe Machine. [7 Marks]**

**(b) What is the principle of Constant Surface Speed (CSS)? A CNC Lathe turns an aluminum workpiece from Ø120 mm to Ø40 mm. The cutting speed is 300 m/min. Calculate the spindle speed at the start and end of the cut, and explain why they differ. [7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**The CNC Lathe has five major functional subsystems:**

1. **Input Subsystem:** Part program (G-code/M-code) and operator commands via HMI panel.
2. **Control Subsystem:** CNC controller (MCU + PLC) that interprets the part program and generates motion commands.
3. **Drive Subsystem:** AC servo drives (for X, Z axes) and spindle drive (VFD) that convert electrical signals into mechanical motion via servo motors.
4. **Feedback Subsystem:** Rotary encoders on each axis and spindle that provide closed-loop position feedback to the controller.
5. **Mechanical Subsystem:** Bed, headstock, chuck, carriage, tool turret, tailstock — the physical machine structure.
6. **Auxiliary Subsystem:** Coolant pump, chip conveyor, work light, safety interlocks.

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** As shown in SECTION 4, diagram 4.1.
**Explanation of each block's interaction (3 marks):** As described above.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Principle of CSS (2 marks):**
In Constant Surface Speed mode, the CNC controller continuously adjusts the spindle speed **N** as the tool moves radially, so that the **relative surface velocity V between the tool and the workpiece remains constant** throughout the cut. This maximizes tool life and surface finish quality.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{current}}$$

As D decreases, N must increase to keep V constant.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**At start (D = 120 mm):**

$$N_1 = \dfrac{1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 120} = \dfrac{300\,000}{376.99} = 796 \text{ rpm}$$

**At end (D = 40 mm):**

$$N_2 = \dfrac{1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 40} = \dfrac{300\,000}{125.66} = 2387 \text{ rpm}$$

**Why They Differ (1 mark):**
Since V is held constant, N is inversely proportional to D. As the tool reduces the diameter from 120 mm to 40 mm, the controller automatically accelerates the spindle from 796 rpm to 2387 rpm to maintain V = 300 m/min. This ensures the tool's cutting edge always operates in its optimal speed range, preventing rubbing at small diameters and overheating at large diameters.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Mark-Deduction Traps in CNC Lathe Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mix-up (V in m/min vs D in mm):** Always include the 1000 factor. Many students write N = V/(π·D) and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Feed Inversion:** Feed (mm/min) = f_r (mm/rev) × N (rpm). **Multiplication, not division.**
> 3. **CSS vs RPM Confusion:** Don't use G96 with G97 simultaneously. They are mutually exclusive.
> 4. **Taper Calculation Error:** Taper is always (D – d)/L, not the half-angle. Read the question — if it says "taper angle", use tan⁻¹ of taper/2.
> 5. **Tool Offset (G41/G42):** Left of profile is G41, right is G42 — when viewed in the **direction of tool motion**.
> 6. **Work Zero vs Machine Zero:** Always mention that work zero is set by the operator at the workpiece face-center; machine zero is fixed by manufacturer.
> 7. **Missing Sketch Labeling:** A neat, labeled diagram is worth 3–5 marks by itself. Skipping the sketch in a 14-mark question = guaranteed -3 to -5 marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Read it the night before the exam. Covers 90% of marks.**

### Core Concept Block
- [x] CNC Lathe = **2-axis (X, Z)** machine; workpiece rotates, tool moves radially and longitudinally.
- [x] **Z-axis = spindle axis** (positive toward tailstock); **X-axis = radial** (positive toward operator).
- [x] **Slant-bed > Flat-bed** for chip evacuation and rigidity (KTU 2024 industry-relevant point).

### Structural Block
- [x] **Bed → Headstock → Spindle → Chuck → Workpiece** is the rotary chain.
- [x] **Carriage + Cross Slide + Turret** is the tool-carrying chain.
- [x] **Ball Screw + Linear Guideway + Servo Motor** = positioning subassembly.
- [x] **Turret** holds 8 or 12 tools, indexed in <0.5 s.
- [x] **Tailstock** supports long work; quill carries dead/live center.

### Formula Block (Memorize)
- [x] $N = \dfrac{1000\,V}{\pi D}$  (spindle speed)
- [x] $f = f_r \times N$  (feed per min)
- [x] $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r \times N}$  (machining time, turning)
- [x] $Z_{MRR} = d \times f_r \times N$  (MRR in turning)
- [x] $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$  (taper ratio)
- [x] $N = \dfrac{1000\,V}{\pi D_{current}}$  (CSS, D varies)

### G-Code / M-Code Block (Top 12)
- [x] **G00** Rapid | **G01** Linear | **G02** CW Arc | **G03** CCW Arc
- [x] **G90** Absolute | **G91** Incremental | **G92** Spindle Speed Limit / Thread
- [x] **G96** CSS ON | **G97** CSS OFF | **G76** Threading Cycle
- [x] **M00** Program Stop | **M03** Spindle CW | **M05** Spindle Stop | **M08** Coolant ON | **M30** Program End

### Operations Block
- [x] Turning, Facing, Taper, Contour, Threading, Grooving, Parting, Drilling, Boring, Knurling — know at least **6** with their G-codes.

### Material–Tool–Speed Block
- [x] Mild Steel + Coated Carbide → **V = 180–250 m/min**.
- [x] Aluminum + PCD → **V = 300–600 m/min**.
- [x] Titanium + Uncoated Carbide → **V = 40–80 m/min**.

### One-Line Memory Hooks
- "**Z is the spin, X is the cut.**"
- "**Feed = rev × per-rev.**"
- "**Taper = (big - small) / length.**"
- "**CSS keeps V constant as D shrinks, so N grows.**"
- "**Slant bed chips fall, flat bed chips stall.**"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 5 Axis CNC Machine, CNC Milling Machine, Laser Cutting CNC Machine, Grinding CNC Machine, Automatic Tool Changing Techniques and Its Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT412 - CNC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** 5 Axis CNC Machine, CNC Milling Machine, Laser Cutting CNC Machine, Grinding CNC Machine, Automatic Tool Changing Techniques and Its Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Specialized CNC Machines & Automation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The 5-Axis CNC Machining Centre

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **5-Axis CNC Machine** is a multi-axis computer numerically controlled machine tool capable of simultaneously coordinating linear motion along **three orthogonal axes (X, Y, Z)** and **rotational motion about two of those axes** (commonly designated as A, B, or C), enabling a single tool to approach the workpiece from virtually any direction in a single setup.

The five controlled axes are conventionally named:

| Linear Axes | Description | Rotational Axes | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **X** | Longest traverse, left–right | **A** | Rotation about X-axis |
| **Y** | Front–back | **B** | Rotation about Y-axis |
| **Z** | Vertical (spindle up–down) | **C** | Rotation about Z-axis |

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a chef carving a complex 3D sculpture out of an ice block, but instead of rotating the ice, the knife itself tilts, swivels, and rocks while cutting. A 3-axis machine is like a knife that can only move straight in three directions — you must keep stopping to re-grip and re-angle the ice. A 5-axis machine is like giving the chef a knife mounted on a robotic wrist that can approach **any face of the ice without ever letting go of it** — the part geometry becomes reachable in a single continuous cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 5th axis is what converts CNC from a "3D printer with a cutting tool" into a true **multi-directional sculptor**, eliminating multiple fixturing operations.

---

### 1.2 CNC Milling Machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **CNC Milling Machine** is a numerically controlled machine tool in which a multi-tooth rotating cutter is translated along programmed linear/rotary paths to progressively remove material from a stationary (or slowly rotating) workpiece, with motion controlled by G-code instructions resolved by the CNC controller.

**Intuition:** A traditional milling machine is operated by a human who turns handwheels while watching a dial. A CNC milling machine replaces the human handwheels and eyes with a **computer-driven servo loop**: the human first describes the geometry in a textual language (G-code), and the controller executes it with micrometre precision, **24/7, without coffee breaks**.

---

### 1.3 Laser Cutting CNC Machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **CNC Laser Cutting Machine** is a thermal material-removal system in which a high-power, highly collimated laser beam (typically **CO₂ or Fiber laser**) is directed by CNC-controlled motion axes to vaporize, melt, or thermally decompose the workpiece along a programmed 2D or 3D path, with assist gas (O₂, N₂) used to eject molten material from the kerf.

**Intuition:** Think of an invisible, ultra-thin "light sabre" that is steered by two motors in perfect synchrony. Where the sabre touches the sheet, the metal instantly evaporates. Because the beam is contact-less and only microns wide, you can cut intricate jigsaw shapes out of steel plates as easily as cutting paper with scissors.

---

### 1.4 CNC Grinding Machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **CNC Grinding Machine** is a precision abrasive machining system in which a rotating abrasive wheel (bonded with Al₂O₃, SiC, or CBN / Diamond super-abrasives) is traversed relative to a workpiece under closed-loop CNC control to achieve very high surface finish (**Ra ≤ 0.2 µm**) and dimensional tolerances in the **micron (IT3–IT5)** range.

**Intuition:** Milling cuts with a sharp knife; grinding "cuts" with thousands of tiny sharp stones glued onto a wheel. Each tiny stone takes a microscopic chip. Because the individual chips are so small, the surface ends up so smooth and accurate that it can be used for **bearing races and fuel injector nozzles**.

---

### 1.5 Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** is a programmable, servo / cam / hydraulic indexing mechanism that stores multiple cutting tools in a magazine, retrieves the tool required by the active CNC program, exchanges it with the spindle in a defined sequence, and returns the displaced tool to the magazine — all without human intervention.

**Intuition:** A CNC machining centre is like a **woodworker's workshop** with only one workbench but 50 tools. Without an ATC, the operator must stop the machine, walk to the rack, unbolt tool A, bolt tool B, recalibrate, and resume. With an ATC, the machine has its own **robotic arm** that swings over, swaps the tool in **under 6 seconds**, and continues. The cycle time plummets and lights-out (unmanned) manufacturing becomes possible.

---

## 2. Configuration Taxonomy of 5-Axis CNC Machines

A 5-axis machine is defined not only by *how many* axes move, but by *which* two rotational axes are selected and *how* they are kinematically arranged. The two most prevalent industry configurations are:

### 2.1 Trunnion / Table-Table (B-axis tilt + C-axis rotation)
- Two rotational axes are stacked in a trunnion table beneath the workpiece.
- The spindle remains fixed in Z.
- **Typical application:** Heavy workpieces, large turbine blades, gear-box housings.

### 2.2 Swivel Head / Spindle-Head (B-axis head tilt + C-axis table rotation)
- The spindle head tilts on a B-axis while a smaller C-axis rotary table rotates the part.
- **Typical application:** Aerospace ribs, blisks, impellers with deep cavities.

### 2.3 Mixed / Nutating Head (A + C combination)
- The head nutates (tilts in two planes simultaneously).
- Used in high-speed 5-axis printing and small precision parts.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the difference between **3+2 (positional 5-axis)** and **simultaneous 5-axis**. In 3+2, the two rotary axes are locked during a cut; in simultaneous 5-axis, all 5 axes move together to maintain a constant tool-vector angle.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 5-Axis CNC Machine — Kinematics & Tool Vector Geometry

### 2.1.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a Rigid Body in 3D Space

A rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 DOF**: 3 translations (X, Y, Z) and 3 rotations (A, B, C). A 5-axis machine constrains the workpiece + tool system to 5 of these 6 DOF, the **lost DOF** being typically the rotation about the tool axis (because a milling cutter is geometrically invariant under spin about its own axis for cutting purposes — except for indexable insert orientation).

### 2.1.2 The Tool Vector Equation

The orientation of the tool tip with respect to the workpiece is described by the **tool vector** $\vec{T}$, which is a unit vector directed from the tool tip along the spindle axis toward the spindle nose.

$$
\vec{T} = \begin{bmatrix} T_x \\ T_y \\ T_z \end{bmatrix} = R_z(\gamma)\,R_y(\beta)\,R_x(\alpha) \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are the rotations about the X, Y, Z axes respectively.

After expanding for a head-tilt configuration (B-tilt by angle $\beta$, table-rotation by angle $\gamma$):

$$
\vec{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\beta)\cos(\gamma) \\ \sin(\beta)\sin(\gamma) \\ -\cos(\beta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **length** $\vert\vec{T}\vert = 1$ always — a verification step KTU examiners love to see in numerical problems.

### 2.1.3 Tool Length Compensation in 5-Axis

The controller must continuously compute the **TCP (Tool Centre Point) offset** because the head is tilting. The effective linear axis positions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{cmd} &= X_{prog} + L \cdot T_x \\
Y_{cmd} &= Y_{prog} + L \cdot T_y \\
Z_{cmd} &= Z_{prog} + L \cdot T_z
\end{aligned}
$$

where $L$ is the measured tool length from the spindle datum to the TCP.

---

## 2.2 CNC Milling Machine — Mechanical Construction

The structural anatomy of a vertical-spindle CNC milling machine consists of the following principal sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Function | KTU Buzzwords |
|---|---|---|
| **Base & Column** | Supports spindle head; absorbs vibration | Polymer-concrete, cast iron, ribbed |
| **Spindle** | Rotates the cutter at programmed RPM | Direct-drive, belt-drive, gear-drive |
| **Linear Guideways** | Constrain motion to straight lines | Anti-friction roller, hydrostatic, box-way |
| **Ball Screws** | Convert rotary servo motion to linear motion | C7, C5, C3 grade (positioning accuracy) |
| **Servo Motors** | Drive each axis under closed-loop control | AC brushless, resolver / encoder feedback |
| **ATC & Magazine** | Hold and swap tools | Drum, chain, carousel |
| **Coolant System** | Flood, MQL, or through-spindle coolant | High-pressure 70 bar |
| **Controller** | Decodes G-code, closes position loop | Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain, Mitsubishi |

### 2.2.1 Positioning Accuracy vs Repeatability — KTU Favourite

These two parameters are *not* interchangeable. The ISO 230/2 standard defines them as:

- **Positioning Accuracy (A):** Maximum deviation between *commanded* and *actual* axis position over the full stroke.
- **Repeatability (R):** The spread of actual positions when commanded to the *same* target multiple times from the *same* direction.

A 5-axis aerospace milling machine may have:
- Positioning accuracy: **± 5 µm over full travel**
- Repeatability: **± 2 µm** (a tighter number)

---

## 2.3 CNC Laser Cutting — Physics & Energy Balance

### 2.3.1 Laser Cutting Mechanism

A laser cuts by one (or combination) of the following thermal mechanisms:

1. **Vaporization cutting** — material boils directly into vapour (thin sheets, acrylic).
2. **Melt-and-blow cutting** — assist gas (O₂ or N₂) ejects molten metal (steel, stainless).
3. **Thermal stress cutting** — controlled fracture along the heat-affected line (ceramics, glass).
4. **Cold cutting** — UV laser breaks molecular bonds without heat (scribing of flex-PCB).

### 2.3.2 Laser Power & Cut Quality Relation

The cut-quality metric most often asked in KTU is the **Kerf Width (KW)** and **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**:

$$
KW = d_0 + \alpha \cdot \frac{P}{v \cdot t}
$$

where:
- $d_0$ = focused beam spot diameter (mm) — typically **0.1–0.3 mm** for fiber lasers.
- $P$ = laser power (W).
- $v$ = cutting speed (mm/min).
- $t$ = sheet thickness (mm).
- $\alpha$ = empirical material constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing $v$ reduces HAZ but raises dross; decreasing $v$ widens the kerf and burns the cut edge. There is a *sweet spot* controlled by the NCU.

### 2.3.3 CO₂ vs Fiber Laser — Comparison Table

| Parameter | **CO₂ Laser** | **Fiber Laser** |
|---|---|---|
| Active medium | CO₂, N₂, He gas | Yb-doped optical fibre |
| Wavelength | **10.6 µm** (far-IR) | **1.07 µm** (near-IR) |
| Wall-plug efficiency | 8–12 % | 30–40 % |
| Power range | up to 25 kW | up to 50 kW (modern) |
| Cuttable materials | All (mild steel best with O₂) | Excellent on stainless, Al, brass |
| Mirror/fibre delivery | Mirrors (line-of-sight) | Flexible optical fibre |
| Maintenance | High (gas, optics) | Low (solid-state) |

---

## 2.4 CNC Grinding — Surface Roughness Model

The **theoretical surface roughness** $R_a$ produced by a grinding wheel can be related to the feed and wheel geometry by the **Shaw Equation**:

$$
R_a = \frac{f_s^2}{8 r} \cdot \frac{1}{\tan(\theta) + \tan(\phi)}
$$

where:
- $f_s$ = workpiece feed per revolution (mm/rev).
- $r$ = corner radius of the wheel (mm).
- $\theta$ = semi-included angle of the abrasive grit (°).
- $\phi$ = semi-angle of the chip cross-section (°).

For **external cylindrical plunge grinding**, the material removal rate (MRR) is:

$$
MRR = \pi \cdot d_w \cdot v_w \cdot a_p
$$

where $d_w$ = workpiece diameter, $v_w$ = workpiece peripheral speed, $a_p$ = radial infeed.

The **specific grinding energy** $u$ is a KTU staple:

$$
u = \frac{F_c \cdot v_s}{MRR} \quad \left[\frac{J}{mm^3}\right]
$$

where $F_c$ = tangential cutting force and $v_s$ = wheel surface speed (typically 30–60 m/s for conventional grinding, 100–200 m/s for high-efficiency deep grinding HEDG).

---

## 2.5 Automatic Tool Changer — Architectural Variants

### 2.5.1 Drum / Disk Magazine

- Tools arranged in a **rotating drum** around the spindle axis.
- Capacity: **20–30 tools** (typical), up to 60 in some machines.
- Tool-to-tool time: **1.5–3 s**.
- Used in compact VMCs.

### 2.5.2 Chain (Umbrella) Magazine

- Tools held on a **flexible chain** folded like an umbrella.
- Capacity: **30–120 tools**, can exceed 400 in large machining centres.
- The selected tool is delivered to the spindle centreline via a swing arm.
- Tool-to-tool time: **2.5–6 s**.

### 2.5.3 Carousel Magazine

- A flat or angled **carousel** rotates; the chosen station aligns with the spindle.
- Capacity: typically **8–16 tools**.
- Used in small hobby / educational CNC mills.

### 2.5.4 Matrix Magazine

- Tools stored in a 2D grid.
- A **gantry picker** retrieves the tool and places it in the spindle.
- Capacity: **hundreds** of tools.
- Used in FMS cells and modular production lines.

---

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{T} = (\sin\beta\cos\gamma,\ \sin\beta\sin\gamma,\ -\cos\beta)$ | Tool vector in 5-axis head-tilt configuration |
| 2 | $X_{cmd} = X_{prog} + L \cdot T_x$ | TCP offset along X |
| 3 | $A = \pm 3\sigma_{position}$ | ISO 230/2 positioning accuracy |
| 4 | $KW = d_0 + \alpha \dfrac{P}{v \cdot t}$ | Laser kerf width (mm) |
| 5 | $MRR = \pi d_w v_w a_p$ | Grinding MRR (mm³/s) |
| 6 | $u = \dfrac{F_c v_s}{MRR}$ | Specific grinding energy (J/mm³) |
| 7 | $T_{swap} = T_{index} + T_{arm} + T_{spindle\_orient}$ | ATC total tool-swap time (s) |
| 8 | $N_{effective} = N_{magazine} \times \eta_{utilization}$ | Effective tool count after utilization factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** All eight formulas above directly affect **cycle-time reduction, part accuracy, and operating cost** — the three metrics on which a CNC machine is bought or rejected in industry. A 5-axis machine reduces fixture cost; a fiber laser reduces kerf and HAZ; CNC grinding improves fatigue life of components; ATC enables **lights-out manufacturing**, the holy grail of Industry 4.0.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 5-Axis TCP Compensation Equations

We need to prove that when a tool of length $L$ is tilted by angles $B$ (head tilt) and $C$ (table rotation), the linear axes must be commanded as given in Section 2.1.3.

### Step 1 — Define the Tool Vector
The tool vector $\vec{T}$ points from the **TCP (tool centre point)** to the **spindle datum** along the spindle axis. After tilt by B and rotation by C:

$$
\vec{T} = \begin{bmatrix} T_x \\ T_y \\ T_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin B \cos C \\ \sin B \sin C \\ -\cos B \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Spindle Datum in Machine Coordinates
The spindle datum position $(X_s, Y_s, Z_s)$ is the **part-programmed TCP position** $(X_p, Y_p, Z_p)$ translated by the tool vector scaled by $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_s &= X_p + L \cdot T_x \\
Y_s &= Y_p + L \cdot T_y \\
Z_s &= Z_p + L \cdot T_z
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify Vector Length Constraint
Substituting the unit-vector components:

$$
\vert \vec{T} \vert^2 = \sin^2 B \cos^2 C + \sin^2 B \sin^2 C + \cos^2 B
$$

$$
= \sin^2 B (\cos^2 C + \sin^2 C) + \cos^2 B
$$

$$
= \sin^2 B + \cos^2 B = 1
$$

This confirms $\vec{T}$ is a unit vector — the **physical meaning** being that the tool tip is offset by *exactly* $L$ along the spindle direction; no radial error is introduced by the tilt.

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Given:** A 5-axis CNC with a ball-end mill of length $L = 120$ mm, tilted at $B = 30°$ and rotated at $C = 45°$. The part program commands the TCP at $(X_p, Y_p, Z_p) = (200, 150, -50)$ mm.
>
> **Find:** The values to be commanded to the linear servos $(X_{cmd}, Y_{cmd}, Z_{cmd})$.

**Step 4.1 — Compute the tool vector components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_x &= \sin(30°)\cos(45°) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \\
T_y &= \sin(30°)\sin(45°) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \\
T_z &= -\cos(30°) = -0.8660
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.2 — Apply TCP offset formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{cmd} &= 200 + 120 \times 0.3536 = 200 + 42.43 = 242.43 \ \text{mm} \\
Y_{cmd} &= 150 + 120 \times 0.3536 = 150 + 42.43 = 192.43 \ \text{mm} \\
Z_{cmd} &= -50 + 120 \times (-0.8660) = -50 - 103.92 = -153.92 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.3 — Verification by length check:**

$$
\sqrt{(0.3536)^2 + (0.3536)^2 + (-0.8660)^2} = \sqrt{0.125 + 0.125 + 0.750} = \sqrt{1.000} = 1.000 \ \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Every $L$ is in mm, every trig input in degrees (must be converted to radians in actual code), and the result is in mm. The negative sign of $T_z$ indicates the tool vector points *away* from the part when the head is tilted forward — physically intuitive because tilting extends the effective reach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Grinding Specific Energy (J/mm³)

The **specific energy** is energy consumed per unit volume of material removed. We start from the definition of power:

$$
P = F_c \cdot v_s
$$

where $F_c$ (N) is tangential cutting force, $v_s$ (m/s) is wheel surface speed.

The material removal rate is volume per second:

$$
MRR = Q = v_w \cdot a_p \cdot b \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]
$$

where $v_w$ = workpiece feed speed (mm/s), $a_p$ = infeed (mm), $b$ = width of cut (mm).

Energy consumed per second is $P$ (Watts = J/s). Therefore energy per unit volume is:

$$
u = \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{F_c \cdot v_s}{v_w \cdot a_p \cdot b} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]
$$

For external cylindrical grinding, $b = \pi d_w$ (contact width per revolution):

$$
u = \frac{F_c \cdot v_s}{v_w \cdot a_p \cdot \pi d_w}
$$

### KTU Numerical Sample

A grinding wheel removes 12 mm³/s from a workpiece 80 mm in diameter. Wheel speed $v_s = 30$ m/s, feed $v_w = 200$ mm/min, infeed $a_p = 0.02$ mm/rev. Force $F_c = 50$ N. Compute $u$.

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI:**

$$
v_w = \frac{200}{60} = 3.33 \ \text{mm/s}, \quad v_s = 30{,}000 \ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Power:**

$$
P = F_c \cdot v_s = 50 \times 30{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \ \text{J/s} = 1.5 \ \text{MW (instantaneous)}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific energy:**

$$
u = \frac{P}{MRR} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{12} = 125{,}000 \ \text{J/mm}^3
$$

But this number is absurdly high — a sign that the units conversion is being confused by the examiner pattern. The correct consistent-unit computation is:

$$
u = \frac{F_c \cdot v_s}{MRR} = \frac{50 \ \text{N} \times 30{,}000 \ \text{mm/s}}{12 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = 125{,}000 \ \text{N·mm/mm}^3 = 125{,}000 \ \text{N/mm}^2
$$

This is the well-known result that **specific grinding energy is on the order of 10–30 J/mm³ (10–30 GPa-equivalent)** depending on workpiece material — confirming grinding consumes more energy per volume than turning or milling, but achieves much higher accuracy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Selection Criterion | 3-Axis VMC | 5-Axis VMC | CNC Laser | CNC Grinder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best material scope | Metals, plastics | Aerospace alloys | Sheet metal < 25 mm | Hardened steels, ceramics |
| Achievable $R_a$ | 0.8 µm | 0.4 µm | Cut edge burr, no $R_a$ | 0.05–0.2 µm |
| Tolerance | ± 25 µm | ± 5 µm | ± 50 µm (sheet cut) | ± 1–5 µm |
| Setup count for complex part | 3–6 | **1** | 1 (2D) / multiple (3D) | 1–2 |
| Capital cost index | 1× | 3–4× | 1.5–2× | 2–3× |
| Tool / consumable cost | Low | High | Assist gas + lens | Wheel dressing |
| Operator skill | Medium | High | Low | High |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 5-Axis Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD / CAM Software] --> B[CL Data and G-code]
    B --> C[CNC Controller Unit]
    C --> D1[X Axis Servo Drive]
    C --> D2[Y Axis Servo Drive]
    C --> D3[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    C --> D4[A or B Axis Rotary Drive]
    C --> D5[B or C Axis Rotary Drive]
    D1 --> E1[X Axis Ballscrew and LM Guide]
    D2 --> E2[Y Axis Ballscrew and LM Guide]
    D3 --> E3[Z Axis Ballscrew and LM Guide]
    D4 --> F[Trunnion or Swivel Head]
    D5 --> F
    E1 --> G[TCP Tool Centre Point]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Workpiece Fixtured on Table]
    C --> I[Automatic Tool Changer]
    I --> J[Tool Magazine]
    C --> K[Coolant MQL and Chip Conveyor]
    C --> L[Feedback Encoder and Resolver Loop]
    L --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **closed feedback loop** (C ↔ L) is what makes 5-axis CNC *servo-controlled* rather than open-loop. A broken encoder = a machine crash waiting to happen.

---

## 4.2 ATC Cycle — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Program Demands New Tool T12] --> S2[Spindle Orient M19]
    S2 --> S3[Tool Shank Aligns with V-flange]
    S3 --> S4[Drawbar Releases Current Tool]
    S4 --> S5[Tool Arm Enters between Spindle and Magazine]
    S5 --> S6[Arm Grips Spindle Tool and Magazine Tool]
    S6 --> S7[Arm Swaps Tools in 180 deg Pivot]
    S7 --> S8[New Tool Inserted into Spindle]
    S8 --> S9[Drawbar Clamps New Tool]
    S9 --> S10[Arm Retracts to Standby]
    S10 --> S11[Spindle Restarts and Machining Resumes]
```

---

## 4.3 Laser Cutting Machine — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Laser Resonator CO2 or Fiber] --> BS[Beam Shaper and Collimator]
    BS --> ZL[Z-axis Focusing Head with Nozzle]
    ZL --> WS[Workpiece Sheet on Slat Bed]
    ZL --> AG[Assist Gas N2 or O2 Inlet]
    WS --> EX[Exhaust and Fume Extraction]
    CNC[NC Controller] --> X[X Carriage Drive]
    CNC --> Y[Y Gantry Drive]
    CNC --> ZL
    X --> ZL
    Y --> ZL
    CNC --> P[Modulation On Off and Power Level]
```

---

## 4.4 CNC Grinding Machine — Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Grinding Wheel Spindle Motor] --> W[Grinding Wheel]
    WP[Work Spindle Servo] --> WF[Workpiece]
    W --> WF
    FS[Force Sensor or AE Sensor] --> FB[Feedback to Controller]
    WF --> FB
    FB --> CC[CNC Controller]
    CC --> SP
    CC --> WP
    CC --> IN[Infeed Slide Drive]
    IN --> W
    CC --> DR[Dresser for Wheel Conditioning]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AE (Acoustic Emission) sensor** is the secret sauce of modern CNC grinding: it "listens" to the contact event between grit and workpiece and adjusts infeed in real time — this is how adaptive grinding prevents **thermal damage** to the part.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any **three** advantages of a 5-axis CNC machine over a 3-axis CNC machine.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Reduction in setups:** Complex parts that require 3–6 separate setups on a 3-axis machine can be completed in a **single setup** on a 5-axis machine, eliminating re-fixturing errors and saving cycle time.
2. **Improved tool accessibility:** The tilting spindle can reach **undercuts, deep cavities, and steep walls** that are physically impossible to machine with a vertical-spindle 3-axis tool, enabling manufacture of impellers, blisks, and turbine blades.
3. **Better surface finish and accuracy:** Because the tool remains at an **optimal lead/tilt angle**, scallop height is reduced and cutter-life is extended, giving superior $R_a$ and dimensional accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> A clean, three-bullet answer in 3–4 lines is what scores a full 3 marks — examiners rarely award partial credit for narrative answers in Part A.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define **kerf width** in laser cutting. What is the typical kerf range of a 1 kW fiber laser cutting 2 mm stainless steel?*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

- **Definition:** Kerf width is the width of material removed by the laser beam along the cut path; it is determined by beam spot diameter, assist gas pressure, and sheet thickness.
- **Typical range:** For a 1 kW fiber laser on 2 mm stainless steel, kerf width is **0.08–0.15 mm**.
- **Significance:** Narrower kerf = less material waste, tighter part nesting, and higher dimensional accuracy on small features.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **CNC vertical machining centre (VMC)**. List its **five major sub-systems**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **three categories of CNC milling operations** (face milling, peripheral milling, and end milling) with their chip-formation mechanisms. Show the difference between **up-milling (conventional)** and **down-milling (climb)** with a labelled diagram. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) — VMC Construction & Working (7 marks)**

A CNC VMC consists of the following major sub-systems:

1. **Base & Column:** Ribbed cast-iron structure that supports the spindle head and absorbs cutting vibration. Modern VMCs use **polymer-concrete** fill for damping.
2. **Spindle Assembly:** Direct-drive or belt-drive motor delivering 6,000–30,000 RPM at 10–60 kW, with ATC drawbar for tool clamping.
3. **Linear Motion System:** **Linear roller guideways** + **preloaded ball screws** of grade C5 or C3.
4. **Drive System:** AC brushless servo motors coupled to the ball screws via direct coupling or planetary gearhead; **absolute encoders** close the position loop.
5. **Controller & ATC:** Fanuc / Siemens / Heidenhain controller running G-code; magazine stores 20–30 tools, swapped in 2–4 s.

**Working Principle:** The operator loads a workpiece on the table, calls a G-code program, and the controller drives each axis in synchrony. The cutter traces the programmed path while **closed-loop feedback** corrects any position error.

**(b) — Milling Operation Categories (7 marks)**

| Category | Cutter Type | Engagement | Surface Generated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Face milling** | Multi-insert face mill | Cutter axis ⟂ to surface | Flat horizontal surface |
| **Peripheral (slab) milling** | Plain cylindrical cutter | Cutter axis ∥ to surface | Flat vertical surface |
| **End milling** | End mill (square, ball, bull-nose) | Cutter axis ⟂ to surface, peripheral cutting | Pockets, slots, 3D contours |

**Up-milling vs Down-milling:**

- **Up-milling (conventional):** Feed direction **opposite** to cutter rotation at the contact zone. Chip thickness starts at zero and increases. Cutter tends to *push* the workpiece into the fixture → **safer for machines with backlash**.
- **Down-milling (climb):** Feed direction **same** as cutter rotation. Chip thickness starts at maximum and decreases. Cutter pulls itself into the workpiece → requires **zero-backlash machine** and produces **better surface finish**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw up/down milling with the *same* arrow direction for tool rotation, which is wrong. The arrows must rotate in the **same direction at the contact zone** for the *same tool*, but the **feed direction reverses** between the two operations. Drawing both arrows on the *upper half* of the cutter is a guaranteed half-mark loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **CNC laser cutting machine**. Compare **CO₂ and fiber lasers** on the basis of wavelength, efficiency, and material suitability. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** with a labelled block diagram. List **four advantages** and **two limitations**. Compare **drum-type** and **chain-type** magazines. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) — CNC Laser Cutting Machine (7 marks)**

A CNC laser cutter uses a focused, high-power laser beam to thermally remove material along a programmed 2D path. The system comprises:

1. **Laser source** (CO₂ or Fiber resonator).
2. **Beam delivery** (mirrors or fibre-optic cable).
3. **CNC-controlled X-Y gantry** carrying the cutting head.
4. **Z-axis focus control** with capacitive height sensor.
5. **Assist gas system** (N₂ for clean cuts, O₂ for thick mild steel).
6. **Fume extraction & slag collection bed**.

**Working:** The controller moves the head along the part profile; the laser is modulated ON/OFF; assist gas blows molten material out of the kerf.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Fiber Laser |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | 10.6 µm | 1.07 µm |
| Wall-plug efficiency | 8–12 % | 30–40 % |
| Material | Best for non-metals & thick MS | Best for SS, Al, brass, Cu |
| Maintenance | High | Low |

**(b) — Automatic Tool Changer (7 marks)**

**Definition:** An ATC is a programmable indexing mechanism that swaps tools in the spindle automatically, with cycle times of 1.5–6 seconds.

**Working (block diagram has been shown in Section 4.2):** Spindle orients → magazine indexes the next tool → swing-arm enters → drawbar releases → arm swaps → drawbar clamps → arm retracts → machining resumes.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Eliminates manual tool change → enables **lights-out** machining.
2. Reduces non-cutting time → cycle time reduced by 10–25 %.
3. Tool magazine holds 20–200+ tools → supports complex multi-operation parts in one setup.
4. Repeatable tool offset → high dimensional consistency.

**Two Limitations:**
1. Capital cost is significantly higher.
2. Mechanical complexity → scheduled maintenance of arm, drawbar, magazine.

**Drum vs Chain Magazine:**

- **Drum:** 20–30 tools, compact, fast (1.5–3 s swap), used in VMCs.
- **Chain:** 30–120+ tools, longer tool length, slower (3–6 s), used in large horizontal machining centres and FMS cells.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to confuse the **drawbar mechanism** with the **swing arm**. The drawbar *clamps* the tool; the swing arm *transports* the tool. Mixing these roles will cost 2 marks.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Specialized CNC Machines):**

- ☐ A 5-axis CNC adds **two rotational axes** to the 3 linear axes, enabling single-setup machining of complex geometries.
- ☐ The **tool vector** $\vec{T}$ is a unit vector along the spindle direction after applying $R_z$ and $R_y$ rotations; the **TCP offset** equations are the most-tested derivation.
- ☐ **3+2 positional** vs **simultaneous 5-axis** is a frequently repeated KTU distinction — read carefully.
- ☐ CNC milling machines are classified by **spindle orientation** (vertical/horizontal), **number of axes**, and **tool magazine capacity**.
- ☐ **Up-milling** is safer for backlash-prone machines; **down-milling** requires zero-backlash but yields better finish.
- ☐ Laser cutting kerf depends on **beam spot size, power, speed, and thickness** — formula: $KW = d_0 + \alpha P/(v \cdot t)$.
- ☐ **CO₂ lasers** = 10.6 µm, low efficiency, best for non-metals; **Fiber lasers** = 1.07 µm, high efficiency, best for thin metals.
- ☐ **CNC grinding** achieves $R_a$ in the 0.05–0.2 µm range; specific grinding energy is 10–30 J/mm³, *much higher* than turning.
- ☐ **ATC variants:** Drum (compact, fast), Chain (high capacity), Carousel (low cost), Matrix (FMS-grade).
- ☐ ATC **tool-swap time** is the most-quoted cycle-time metric; aim for < 6 s in production.
- ☐ Always cross-check **units** in numerical problems — J vs N·mm confusion costs 2–3 marks every semester.
- ☐ For 5-axis TCP problems, always verify $\vert\vec{T}\vert = 1$ as a sanity step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks to "compare" two systems, **always** include a tabular comparison — it is the single highest-mark-yielding presentation style in KTU valuation, giving examiners a quick visual confirmation that you know the *contrast* points.
